title: BOYS AND GIRLS WHO GO ASTRAY
full_text: BOYS AND GIRLS WHO GO ASTRAY What about our boys and girls who go astray? Certainly it is not christian to lynch and murder them, and yet, thoughtlessness on our part and disinclination to seriously study the problem really conduce to that end. Can we not arrest the tenden- cy in that direction? Is it not pos- sible for Christian men and women to do something to save the boys and girls from perdition? At least we can think on the subject and ser- iously so, But it occurs to us that we need definite information to arouse us to the necessity of doing more than we are for the moral elevation of the young and wayward. The informa- tion can be had along many lines. We are thinking of one special line, and we cheerfully throw out the hint with the hope that good may come out of it, Many of our readers know that there is such a place as 'Cheltenham' a reformatory for incorrigible col- ored boys, and that is about all they do know about this institution. Many of our readers have heard that there is such an institution in Baltimore County, near this city, called the Industrial Home for Col- ored Girls, a reformatory for girls who have gotten beyond the control of their parents, or who have been committed to the institutions on ac- count of their shortcomings. Now what we suggest is, that some one of the largest churches in the city hold a 'special public meet- ing, the church crowded with all in- terested in the improvement of our youth along such lines and that the superintendents of these institutions together with as many of the Board of Managers, of the same who may come, to be invited to address the colored citizens of Baltimore on the respective institutions, giving detail- ed information with respect to the institutions, the inmates, their con- dition and suggest ways of mutual helpfulness. Mr. Dorsey, a few months ago, appointed superinten- dent at Cheltenham, is a most excel- lent man aud is disposed sincerely and honestly to do all that he can along the line of improvement at Cheltenham. The boys already are drawn to him and like him very much. Mr. Dorsey has splendid ideas, and has had much experience; that yet for all this he is disposed to get a valuable hint even from a small child. The colored people want to know and ought to know all about Chel- tenham, and we feel that Mr. Dorsey would gladly respond to such an in- vitationand not only tell what he knows, but kindly invite any criti- cism or suggestion which would have a tendency to improve condi- tions in the interest of the boys at the House of Reformation. By all means let us have such a meet- ing. ! Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: ""THE PUNISHMENTS HAVE BEEN TOO SEVERE.""
full_text: "THE PUNISHMENTS HAVE BEEN TOO SEVERE." "The grand jury committee on the House of Reformation for Col- ored Boys, at Cheltenham, submitt- ed a special report yesterday com- mending the management and condition of that institution, "but stating that the punishments in- flicted on the youthful inmates have been too severe." We regret to say that, in our opinion," the report states, "the punishments have been too severe, better suited for hardened adult criminals than for incorrigible youths. "We are pleased to report that the management have promised to modity this somewhat by doing away with the more severe meth- ods. In this connection one of the great needs of the institution is a strong guard building, where un- controllable inmates may be sately confined under proper police pro- tection. We believe that kind treatment and moral suasion will do more to serve the purposes of the institution than harsh and severe punishments."--Baltimore Sun, Wed. Aug. 24th. The above tells its own story. Here we have an institution exclu- sively for the Reformation of col- ored boys officered entirely by the white race. The Siate and City give thousands of dollars, annually, towards its maintenance, and although Governor Lowndes and Mayor Malster are both re- publicans, voted for by colored cit- izens of this State, yet both of these republican gentlemen appointed white men to act as trustees or directors of this eleemosynary institution for colored boys? Why this? The only plausible and log- ically consistent answer would be, that out of a colored population of more than eighty thousand, not one competent colored person could be found to serve in such a capacity. What other answer could there be to it? So far as Mayor Malster is concerned we can scarcely conceive how that he can justify his position in the mat- ter, for the writer, a tax-payər and a citizen of Baltimore, called upon his Honor and told him how im- portant it was that these poor un- fortunate colored boys, should at least, have one reputable member of their race upon the board of control, and earnestly requested him to appoint some colored man on the board. We left with the impression that the Mayor was so disposed. At the very next meet- ing of the City Council, his first appointee, a white man, declined, and immediately the Mayor sent in the name of a Mr. Doyle, white, a prominent whiskey dealer, so we are informed. We have simply stated the matter to show, that leaving the question of "patron- age" severely alone, and address- ing our attention to charitable and educational institutions, whereby the many are to be benefitted we are no more in it" there than in getting office. ointing of colored men on such boards, in the Southern States, is a very common and ordi- nary thing on the part of demo- cratic Governors and Mayors, and for the life of us, we can not un- derstand why Mayor Malster should thus seek to humiliate the Negro race in Maryland. Will he not explain? Are we Negroes worthy of such attention upon_his part? Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: CHELTENHAM AGAIN.
full_text: CHELTENHAM AGAIN. Some time ago we had occasion to call attention to the treatment accorded to the inmates of the so-called reformatory at Cheltenham, in Prince Georges Co. The matter came to the attention of the State authorities, and at their solicta- tion we sent a member of our staff down there to investigate the conditions. We were told that the cruelties that had been formerly practiced there were done away with and that a new regime had been introduced. Since that time we have heard nothing from the institu- tion until now. In referring to an arti- cle taken from a dispatch to the Even- ing News, it will be seen that if the old time practices were done away with they have been renewed. The dispatch states that a "wee color- ed youth" was found on the streets of Washington with not only a ball and chain to his legs, but hand.cuffs on his wrists. This shows that the ball and chain, which we have called attention to heretofore, has not been done away with, notwithstanding the statements made to the authorities. This Cheltenham is a most degrading institution, and is doing the young Ne- groes of this State more harın, if such a thing is possible, than the Baltimore City Jail. And it would be'a mercy to these boys to commit them to the jail than to such an infernal hole as this Cheltenham. When we published Mrs. Douglass' letter, the city authorities and the au- thorities of Cheltenham were horrified beyond measure, and expressed them- selves so; and now it is shown that the truth was told, notwithstanding their strenuous denials. Of course when the city authorities and the Grand Jury visits that place everything is in good order, but as soon as their backs are turned the same old condition of affairs are taken up again. It is time that sach steps were taken against this institution that will either put it out of basiness or reform it al- together. It can only be reformed ed by turning the whole batch of keepers out of the establishment altogether, They are brutal and the boys of the institu- tion have no reason to remember the institution when they get cut of it. save to curse the day they were incar- cerated within its infamous walls. The idea of putting handcuffs and a ball and chain on such children as the one mentioned is an ontrage, and the society that has the care of this, institu- on should be prosecuted to the full extent of the law for cruelty to chil- dren It has been claimed by the authorities of this institution that the boys cannot be kept from running away unless they are confined in some way, and the ball and chain seems to be their cheadest and best way: Now further down in the same county is a reform school that is worthy of the name "Reform." We speak of the Hart Farm School. To this school the worst boys that cau be found in the District of Columbia are sent, and after a few attempts to get away, which many of them do. they find that it is better to stay, as the treatment received there is so different to what they ex- pected. There boys are treated as if they were human beings. They are well fed and well taken care of, and in the course of time good homes are fonud for them and they get a good chance to have a good start in life. There are no brutal keepers, no whipping, no ball and chain and no fences to keep the boys from running away. The boy is taught the value of good conduct, re- ceive good treatment, and is taught to believe that he is a human being. with all that that implies, The result is that the officials of the District have no hesi- tancy in endorsing the good work that is being done at the Hart Farm School We wish that Cheltenham could be brought under such influences. But right here we will say that nothing can be expected of this demoralizing so-call- ed reformatory until the colored citi. zens of this city shall raise up and take the matter into their own hands and de- mand at the hands of the properly qual- ifed authorities better treatment for the boys committed to this place or that it be abolished entirely. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: CHELTENHAM DIRECTORS.: TWO PROMINENT AFRO-AMERICANS SELECTED ON THE BOARD.
full_text: CHELTENHAM DIRECTORS. TWO PROMINENT AFRO-AMERICANS SELECTED ON THE BOARD. For the Firat Time In Its History Two Colored Mon Are Nelected to Taken Part in the Direction CIA Hairs At This Most Prominent institution For The Reformation of Colored Yonth. Rev. George F. Bragg, of The Afro-tmorienu Steff One of Di. rectors. At a meeting last Friday of the stock- holders of the Colored House of Refor- mation at Cheltenham, by a unanimous vote, the Rev. George F. Bragg, Jr., one of the editors of the Afro-American Lodger was unanimously elected a mem- ber of the Board of Managers for the ensuing year. On Tuesday night. Mayor Hayes sent to the City Council thename of Mr. Harry S. Cummings, a well known lawyer of this city, as one of the managers on the part of the city in this institution. Thus, after one of the hardest battles over fought in this State by a Negro newspaper, recognition was secured on a Board which bas much to do with the future of many of the young Negroes of this State. The election of the Rev. Mr. Bragg, came entirely unsought on his part, and is in recognition of the part he took in trying to secure better treatment for these unfortunate boys, who are confin- od in this institution. Mr. Cummings REV. GEORGE F. BRAGG, JR. appointment was on the recommenda- tion of Mr. Hiram Watty a member of he City Council froin the Fourteenth Ward, the ward in which Mr. Cum- mings live. Both appointments will no doubt give great satisfaction. i Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: A Good Ground of Hope.
full_text: A Good Ground of Hope. In addition to what we have al- ready attained, we have a most reasonable ground for hope, in the way of an increase of the benefits now enjoyed by the race. The natural newness of the Republican party, in coming into power, and the terrible and foul suspicion placed upon it by those of demo- cratic persuasion, with respect to more fully realizing the civil rights and other privileges of the colored race, rendered any great advance, in that direction a thing impossi ble. But in the event of popular indorsement at the coming elec- tion, reinforced by such an auspi- cious attestation of public confi- dence, we have every son to hope for such legislative enactments as will greatly im- prove the moral and material con- dition of the race. The adversary, the Democratic party, has greatly magnified the ignorance, vicious- ness and rowdyism of the colored population, and the Republican party, once more in power, will graciously apply itself to the task of converting this ignorance and viciousness into those excellent virtues which characterize an enlightened and progressive peo- ple. rеа- To accomplish this the leaders of the Republican party are pledged to the establishment of an Agricultu- ral and Industrial College for the Colored youth of the State, the enlargement and re-organization, upon a more satisfactory basis, the Cheltenham Reform School for colored boys, as well as the insti- tution of other means looking to the civic, intellectual and moral advance of the colored race. Now in the face of all this, if we are disposed to vote the Demo- cratic ticket, commonsense would inquire what may we expect, as a substitution, from such a source, to take the place of the present benefits and others reasonably sure? With the situation so clearly and pointedly set forth it is abso- lutely unnecessary to urge colored men to vote the entire Republican ticket, from the top to bottom, as their interests are so obviously and and unmistakeably identified with its success. The few colored men who fail to see the directness and urgency of the situation as above delineated, in all probability, are of that class of the population who are moved only by monetary and other cor- rupting influences. Happily for the race such men are significant- ly few. Let us demonstrate on Tuesday next our capacity of grat- Hitude towards that truly conser- vatism which builds and does not destroy, and we can do this by voting the Republican ticket. title: SATURDAY MARCH 25 1899: THE YOUTHFUL MURDERER AND CHELTENHAM.
full_text: SATURDAY MARCH 25 1899 The Youthful Murderer and Cheltenham. It is with great pain that we note the terrible crime of the col- ored youth of fifteen years, John Berry who killed a Miss Clark in Prince George's county last Satur- day night, and seriously wounded the other sister. The prisoner is in Baltimore City Jail and has al- ready confessed his guilt. We have read. of many exceedingly wicked crimes but in the present case it is hardly possible to persu- ade ourselves, that under the pe- Juliar circumstances, that any sane Individual could have been guilty fsuch an awful crime. It was imply terrible! From the boy's wn.confession there was absolute- nothing to warrant even harsh eatment upon his part of the la. es who had so kindly and gener- usly befriended him and given ma good home. All the parties are Roman Catholics. This crime illustrates the ex- eding sinfulness of sin. It is a le too much for us to under- d. Leaving the poor unfortu- e boy in the hands of the law cannot help thinking of the ny hardened boys of the race tay Cheltenham Re- formatory (?) Insthwith Cowhere Berry wes once an inmate. Is that institution so managed as to secure the best results for the unfortunate omotes as well as for the commn- nity upon whom they are let loose? While granting the purest mo- tives to those who have charge of it we are forced to the conclusion that it is not so managed as to se- cure the best moral results. In the Baltimore Sun of Aug. 4th., may be seen an extract of the report of the Grand Jury concern- ing this institution. It says: "we regret to say that in our opinion the punishments have been too severe, better suited for hardened adult criminals than for incorrigi- ble youths. ** We believe that kind treatment and moral snasion will do more to serve the purposes of the institution than harsh and severe purishments." Here we have the matter in a mut-shell. Under the peculiar environments which surround white and colored people, in a re- formatory institution of this char- acter, at least, a good proportion of the teaching and executive staff conght to be drawn from the race ot which the inmates are members. Only God-fearing, humane and properly equipped colored men and women can succeed in getting into the interior life of these un- fortunate but youthful members of the race. They can most effec- Stively administer "kind treatment And moral suasion" so absolutely necessary for the reformation of these youthful offenders. Their town hearts full of love go out to them. Reserved and suspicious of the kindly interest of white in structors these youthful offenders shrink from completely opening up their trials and temptations to an alien race. The good of the State as well as that of the boys themselves demand the employ. ment of those who can touch them in their inner life. Me. Murphy of the Afro-Amer- the writer made several rips to Anmolis during the a res ons of the the last legislature and rked earnestly upon men who been elected by the suffrages ored men to get them to re- e this institution in the rest of the State and the nt spoilsmen and pro- ticians had but little riwith those who were pleading for the unfortunates uable to help themselves. The State and the City of Bal- timore furnish nearly all the mon ey to run this institution, and in- stead of making it a veritable pri- son we aver that it ought to be made a place to recover to the unfortunate inmates thereof those virtues and amiable dispositions so necessary for the good of all. But whiskey-dealers in Mr. Malster's opinion, are better qualified to ad- vance such interests than the best colored men among us who have given their lives to the study of such intricate problems as the re- forming of the erring members of the race. Ought not the sad 'case' of Berry secure for us a reforma- tion of the House of Reformation for colored boys? Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Is that institution so managed as to secure the best results for the unfortunate omotes as well as for the commn- nity upon whom they are let loose? While granting the purest mo- tives to those who have charge of it we are forced to the conclusion that it is not so managed as to se- cure the best moral results. In the Baltimore Sun of Aug. 4th., may be seen an extract of the report of the Grand Jury concern- ing this institution. It says: "we regret to say that in our opinion the punishments have been too severe, better suited for hardened adult criminals than for incorrigi- ble youths. ** We believe that kind treatment and moral snasion will do more to serve the purposes of the institution than harsh and severe purishments." Here we have the matter in a mut-shell. Under the peculiar environments which surround white and colored people, in a re- formatory institution of this char- acter, at least, a good proportion of the teaching and executive staff conght to be drawn from the race ot which the inmates are members. Only God-fearing, humane and properly equipped colored men and women can succeed in getting into the interior life of these un- fortunate but youthful members of the race. They can most effec- Stively administer "kind treatment And moral suasion" so absolutely necessary for the reformation of these youthful offenders. Their town hearts full of love go out to them. Reserved and suspicious of the kindly interest of white in structors these youthful offenders shrink from completely opening up their trials and temptations to an alien race. The good of the State as well as that of the boys themselves demand the employ. ment of those who can touch them in their inner life. Me. Murphy of the Afro-Amer- the writer made several rips to Anmolis during the a res ons of the the last legislature and rked earnestly upon men who been elected by the suffrages ored men to get them to re- e this institution in the rest of the State and the nt spoilsmen and pro- ticians had but little riwith those who were pleading for the unfortunates uable to help themselves. The State and the City of Bal- timore furnish nearly all the mon ey to run this institution, and in- stead of making it a veritable pri- son we aver that it ought to be made a place to recover to the unfortunate inmates thereof those virtues and amiable dispositions so necessary for the good of all. But whiskey-dealers in Mr. Malster's opinion, are better qualified to ad- vance such interests than the best colored men among us who have given their lives to the study of such intricate problems as the re- forming of the erring members of the race. Ought not the sad 'case' of Berry secure for us a reforma- tion of the House of Reformation for colored boys? . .

title: EX-SENATOR WELLINGTON.
full_text: EX-SENATOR WELLINGTON. The Afro-American Ledger records with extreme gratification and pleas- ure the many courtesies and favors bestowed on this office by Ex-Senator Wellington. When Mr. Wellington was active in politics he never called in vain npon the colored voter. The colored voters of Maryland always gave him hearty support. In spite of the fact that there is such a little in results, accomplished by Mr. Wel- lington during his carerer, for the race, we prefer to believe, though we may be mistaken, that at heart he was really friendly disposed to our struggling race. In this conr ection we may be par- doned for saying that so far as the interests of the Negro are concerned we have been sadly disappointed by Senator McComas, and we do sin- cerely trust that 'ere he makes his exit from the Senate he will find it in his heart to do something for the Negro. Ex-Senator Wellington has our sincere wishes for peace, prosperity and blessing. Before many years roll around we hope to see him at work in the party with his old time enthusiasm, supported by the black voters, as in days of yore when he was State Chairman. GEN. SHYROCK AS PRESIDENT OF CHELTENHAM KEFORMATORY We rejoice very greatly indeed over the good news that Gen. Thomas J. Shryock has been elected by the Board of Managers of the House of Reformation for Colored Boys, as president of that corporation. We are simply delighted at the selection and sincerely wish that the General will find it convenient to accept the office. Possibly, there is no other white Republican in the whole State of Maryland who is more highly esteemed by the colored race than Gen. Shryock. He is one of a very few genuine Lincoln Republicans in this State who believes in giving the black man a fair chance. The fact that he is president of Cheltenham Institution, will do more than any one thing to generate confidence on the part of the colored people in that institution. If Thomas Shryock is president, no one need have any fear with respect to the management of that institution, and the fair and honest treatment of the colored boys sent there. Let our leading men write and urge the General that he will not decline the honor, because of his great business affairs. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: THE REBELLION AT MELVALE
full_text: things being equal, colored persons can do more to uplift, reform and in- spire their own people than can those of an alien race, immune from the inner life of a people, rendered so much the more skeptical of the real interest of white workers in them, by reason of the systematic race per- secution and oppression from with- out. If the white friends who have charge of colored reformatories in this State sincerely desire the refor- mation of colored youth we cannot understand why they should hesitate to do that which seems instinctively natural and normal. We boldly make the assertion and challenge contradiction, that the best moral, material and intellectual results attained on behalf of the Ne- gro have been the fruits of Negro leadership and culture. The greatest Negro institution in this country is monumental evidence of the same. and it is freely admitted on every hand that the Negro preacher is by far the greatest factor in arousing and reaching the moral life of the race. What is needed at Melvale as well as at Cheltenham, is a master mind of the Negro race, with a creative and executive capacity. Easy enough after this to fill out the faculty. And this need is of prime importance both to the city and state, expending thousands of dollars each year for the best moral betterment of the Ne- gro race. The best brain and heart of the race ought to be employed in serv- ing their city and state by drawing out to the fullest extent, the capaci- ties and powers of their own unfor- tunate brethren. THE REBELLION AT MELVALE A few days ago the daily papers gave an interesting acconnt of a very painful occurrance in connection with the Industrial Home for Col- ored Girls, at Melvale, Baltimore County. With respect to the merits or demerits of the uprising in rebel- lion of the colored girls of that in- stitution we are not now chiefly con cerned. Rebellion isa sin. And cer- tainly it is to be sincerely regretted. We must invariably take our stand on the side of law and order. But the very fact of rebellion brings prominently to view an nnwise and injudicious provision dominating all similar institutions in this state for the reformation of incorrigible col- ored persons. We are free to confess that we have but little if any hope for permanent usefulness of such in- stitutions as long as the present pol- icy in this respect obtains. Let it be understood, in the first place that we do not call in question the sincere purpose and benevolent intentions of those who rule and manage these institutions. But we have to do with certain conditions, which as long as they remain must be seriously taken into consideration. In the social life of the two races there is conspicuous absence of any unifying point. The colored people have their own separate schools, churches and other organizations. They are absolutely separated and distinct from social fellowship with their white neighbors. Incorrigible colored boys or girls experience this accentuated separated social order with even more keenness than the more favored of the race. They are arrested by white police officers; they are sentenced to reformatories by white judges or police justices; and finally, in these reformatories they are remitted to the custody of white teachers and keepers. Deprived of their freedom and held practically as prisoners by members of the white race, the effort for any sincere and real reformatory work is handicapp- ed from the start by an impossible approach to the inner life and sym- pathy of prisoners and their keepers. By certain means the appearance of submission is maintained, and a seeming outward reformation at- tained, although the citadel of affec- tions, and the hidden springs of mo- tive are securely barred from those who have no genuine racial affinity and sympathy with those they seek to reform. Under such circumstances the wonder is we do not have more instances of revolution and rebellion. It is far from a question of places for talented colored men and women, but rather whether or not the State and city, supplying the means for the defense of society by the reforma- tion of the criminally disposed, should not have the most efficient and best equipped for this most dit- sficult but most necessary work, It ought to be conceded, without one word of argument, that all Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: MR, GRASTY'S IMPRESSIONS
full_text: MB, GRASTY'S IMPRESSIONS | Mr. Grasty; the Secretary of the | Board of Supervisors of City Cbari- ties, has been visiting in the South, and incidently, he made a visit to Tuskegee. Through the morning papers he gives some idea of how he was favorably impressed at that great school, He seemed to be im- pressed with the idea that the col- ored boys at Cheltenham should, almost exclusively be taught prac- tical agriculture. We had hoped that the wonderful grip of Booker Washington, as well as his judicious executive developement and hand- ling of that great institution, had impressed Mr. Grasty with the need of putting Cheltenham under the management of a superintendent andinstructors taken from the same race as the pupils of that institution This is the paramount need, and everything else is, in a very great degree, useless until this is accom plished. We appreciate good inten- tions, but what the community wants is results, We make the claim and challenge contradiction, that all the education- al and reformatory institutions in the Southern states that are accom- plishing magnificient results both for the community and the race are officered exclusively by teachers of the same race, or by such white peo ple who, practically, make them- selves such. Reformation must come from with- in. The "Kingdom of God is with- in" people, and no one can reach, arouse and stimulate to higher things unless there is the closest con- tact and oneness. But to banish chair-caning, and other industries from Cheltenham is a great blunder. Possibly, nearly one-half of the boys who go to Cheltenham, go from the city and not from the country, and when they become of age there is no power to make them settle in the country. We must deal with facts. Then again, there are only certain months in the year when it is pos- sible to instruct in agriculture and soils, What industries are to en- gage the hands of these boys during the winter months? Mr. Grasty means well, but he is ignorant of many things with respect to colcred people and colored life. The chair- caning industry in this city is not closed against colored boys. There are quite a number of such estab- lishments operated by colored peo- ple in this city as well as in other cities. It would seem, with a population of nearly one hundred thousand col- ared people that patronage for this as well as other industries are abun- dantly wear at hand. Of one thing we are absolutely sure, and that is, the greatest need Cheltenham has is that of a compe- tent colored man as superintendent of that institution and a faculty drawn from the same race. who would work their very hands off of them in demonstrating that those boys can be reformed, uplifted and made useful citizens. Like Sumner, Garrison and others, we believe in this matter with our whole heart, and shall agitate until our white fellow citizens are lead to see the sweet reasonableness and blessing of our contention. title: THE NEGRO AS A FOOTBALL.
full_text: ! the function of a foot ball to the great delight of his Anglo-Saxon neighbors. But that the Negro is growing weary of the job is evi- denced on every hand by the noble and inspiring efforts he is making in every direction to deliver himself from the toils in which he is en- snared. Even the "cake walk" wit- nesseth the avidity with which the white man is characterized in using the Negro as a means of money. making and thus while used as a foot-ball in this direction, at the same time he earns a living by mak- ing a monkey of himself for the de- lectation of the white man who pro- fits thereby. But the foot-ball bus- iness does not stop here. In a very peculiar and real way it is extended into organized moral, religious and philanthropic enterprises. Oftimes reformatories, asylums, and chari- ties of various kinds, ostensibly, in the interest of the poor and unfortu- nate of the race are primarily used as a means of furnishing the oppor- tunity for white persons to gain a livelihood, thus constituting the Ne- gro a foot-ball in this sphere of ac- tivity. We have a respectable number of voters both in Baltimore City and theState of Maryland, and yet so com pletely has been our inanity and im- potency that we have thus far failed to make the influence of our suffrage by such force as to secure a proper concern and care for those of the race who demand reformation, treatment, charitable shelter, or ag- ricultural and industrial training. We are now on the eve of an election and we shall have another election in the fall for Governor and mem- bers of the Legislature, and yet here is Cheltenham Reformatory receiv- ing both state and city support, and far from being what it ought to be, and we unable, with all our po- litical power to secure such a man- agement of that institution where- by it will cease to be conducted, as it seems to be, primarily as an op- portunity to give employment to persons of the opposite race. With a large number, some two hundred and hity thousand colored people in the state of Maryland, and with thousands of acres in the rural dis- tricts that ought to be productive and highly cultivated, we are unable with all our voting strength to de- mand such attention as would real- ize an Agricultural and Industrial) Institution, whereby many of the youth of the race could be trained to lives of usefulness in scientificknowl- edge of agriculture and also in me- chanical pursuits. Inasmuch as we can hardly expect to receive office to any extent it would certainly seem both wise and proper that we should exert what little influence we may have in having our unfortunate colored boys taught and trained at Cheltenham by their own race rather than by those who are aliens to all that is good, noble and inspiring in racial Iffe. title: THE BOARD OF ESTIMATES AND NEGLECTED COLORED CHILDREN
full_text: THE BOARDOF ESTIMATES AND NE- GLECTED COLORED CHILDREN The board of estimates of our city government is composed of Mayor Hayes, Comptroller J. H. Smith, City Enginesr Fendell and Mr. Henry Williams, president of the Second Branch of the City Council. On last Tuesday, in connection with the Cheltenham appropriation matter, the general subject of caring for ne- glected and homeless colored children was freely discussed, There was one thing that was made absolutely clear and unmistakeable, and that was, that each and every one of this Board were thoroughly, intelligent- ly and keenly interested in the prop- er care of all sorts and conditions of the unfortunate or crimina l'y inclin- ed of the colored youth. No intelli- gent colored person could have been present at the hearing and failed to have been specially impressed with the gracious, intelligent and kind spirit which permeated the inquiry with respect to the peculiarities of the situation. President Brackett of the Board of Supervisors of City Charities was present and, incidently, pointed out one of the greatest needs of the city in this direction. It is a notorious fact that many colored boys are sent either to jail or committed to reformatory insti- tutions, who ought not to be sent to such places. But the question is immediately asked, what shall we do with them? The writer is con- nected officially with at least three institutions of a reformatory char- acter, and has enjoyed splendid facil- ities for ascertaining many of the causes of moral delinquencies on the part of colored children. We do not hesitate to most heartily indorse the idea of Dr. Brackett. The need is not only immediate, but in properly supplying such a need, if wisely con- ducted, we shall contribute a great deal towards minimising and reduc- ing criminal tendencies among the colored youth. There is one point in connection with the whole matter, which un- fortunately, is not sufficiently ap- preciated by those in authority, in institucions already established for the reformation of the colored youth. Disregard of its prime importance explains why our success has not been much greater in the reformation of colored youth. If we are not mis- taken, for the most part, whether Roman Catholic or otherwise, the reformatory institutions are operat- ed by those from a race of people alien to the real and secretive life of colored youth. Hence, the members of one race are trying to reform members of an- other race,. and unable to get into the inner life of this race and there- by initiate at its very source any abiding reform, they can only hope to dress the patient, so to speak, up from the outside, with the carnest hope that it may do good. We grant fully and freely that all these persons of an alien race are actuated by the highest and noblest motive, that they are devotedly yielding their best service. And yet for all this the difficulty remains. These unfortunate boys and girls are capable of loving and being loved, they have social natures just like the rest of mankind; they have hopes and fears; they have aspirations, struggles and dejections. When they go from such reformatories they enter "colored society." Not in any particularly social or limited sense, but in the sense that they are asso- ciatedin business, in church, and so- cially intermingle with people of their own color. But little of their inner and real life will touch the white part of so- ciety. Now, from our point of view the greatest need of reformatories, for colored children, are teachers, while in every other respect fully qualified, yet unqualifiedly of the same race as the pupils themselves. It is simply idle to argue the ques- tion whether properly qualified col- ored teachers and instructors can accomplish better results in a color- ed reformatory than teachers of an- other race in the same reforma- tory. The very fact that colored people when left free to choose their own pastors teachers and physicians, in- variably select them from their own race, is evidence most substantial. In the present case it is so much the more absolutely important for per- manent results in reformation that the teachers and instructors should be of the same ráce, In the case of schools, the pupils are fairly moral and sufficiently in- telligent to accept the educational services of teachers of another race although they preſer their own. In the case of criminals, the very fact that they are "in durance vile," and that, objectively, by the sentence of court officers (white) rendersit highly important that they should be emancipated from their general suspicion and distrust of all white faces. Certainly a man who is a Negro, but despite his being a Negro he is much of a perfect gentleman and of general good qualities as to com- mand the respect and esteem of the white community, by reason of his racial identity and his social active- ity in and among the very people of whom he as well as the criminal youth are a part, could by all means be made to touch effectively the in- ner and real spot of life in that col- ored boy's heart, securely barred for- ever from one of alien blood. President Brackett of the Board of Supervisors of City Charities was present and, incidently, pointed out one of the greatest needs of the city in this direction. It is a notorious fact that many colored boys are sent either to jail or committed to reformatory insti- tutions, who ought not to be sent to such places. But the question is immediately asked, what shall we do with them? The writer is con- nected officially with at least three institutions of a reformatory char- acter, and has enjoyed splendid facil- ities for ascertaining many of the causes of moral delinquencies on the part of colored children. We do not hesitate to most heartily indorse the idea of Dr. Brackett. The need is not only immediate, but in properly supplying such a need, if wisely con- ducted, we shall contribute a great deal towards minimising and reduc- ing criminal tendencies among the colored youth. There is one point in connection with the whole matter, which un- fortunately, is not sufficiently ap- preciated by those in authority, in institucions already established for the reformation of the colored youth. Disregard of its prime importance explains why our success has not been much greater in the reformation of colored youth. If we are not mis- taken, for the most part, whether Roman Catholic or otherwise, the reformatory institutions are operat- ed by those from a race of people alien to the real and secretive life of colored youth. Hence, the members of one race are trying to reform members of an- other race,. and unable to get into the inner life of this race and there- by initiate at its very source any abiding reform, they can only hope to dress the patient, so to speak, up from the outside, with the carnest hope that it may do good. We grant fully and freely that all these persons of an alien race are actuated by the highest and noblest motive, that they are devotedly yielding their best service. And yet for all this the difficulty remains. These unfortunate boys and girls are capable of loving and being loved, they have social natures just like the rest of mankind; they have hopes and fears; they have aspirations, struggles and dejections. When they go from such reformatories they enter "colored society." Not in any particularly social or limited sense, but in the sense that they are asso- ciatedin business, in church, and so- cially intermingle with people of their own color. But little of their inner and real life will touch the white part of so- ciety. Now, from our point of view the greatest need of reformatories, for colored children, are teachers, while in every other respect fully qualified, yet unqualifiedly of the same race as the pupils themselves. It is simply idle to argue the ques- tion whether properly qualified col- ored teachers and instructors can accomplish better results in a color- ed reformatory than teachers of an- other race in the same reforma- tory. The very fact that colored people when left free to choose their own pastors teachers and physicians, in- variably select them from their own race, is evidence most substantial. In the present case it is so much the more absolutely important for per- manent results in reformation that the teachers and instructors should be of the same ráce, In the case of schools, the pupils are fairly moral and sufficiently in- telligent to accept the educational services of teachers of another race although they preſer their own. In the case of criminals, the very fact that they are "in durance vile," and that, objectively, by the sentence of court officers (white) rendersit highly important that they should be emancipated from their general suspicion and distrust of all white faces. Certainly a man who is a Negro, but despite his being a Negro he is much of a perfect gentleman and of general good qualities as to com- mand the respect and esteem of the white community, by reason of his racial identity and his social active- ity in and among the very people of whom he as well as the criminal youth are a part, could by all means be made to touch effectively the in- ner and real spot of life in that col- ored boy's heart, securely barred for- ever from one of alien blood. . .

title: CHELTENHAM AGAIN
full_text: CHELTENHAM AGAIN It is now almost a matter of peri- odical expectation that, once in a while, the Grand Juries will in severe language condemn the irregularities and delinquencies of the House of Reformation for Colored Boys at Cheltenham. The late Grand Jury has presented the same old story of dark cells, inhuman treatment and inability of the Reformatory to re- form the boys sent hither. We are not surprised at any of the revela- tions made known. We are, how- ever, surprised that intelligent and thoughtful men of the dominant race continue to persuade themselves of the possibility of accomplishing the very best results on behalf of those boys, as well as in the interest of the `community, by means of a staff of officers and teachers of the white race. There might be some hope of the realization of such blessed results if the very best white men and wo- men, of culture, refinement and influ- ence were ordinarily the teachers and instructors. Only a supreme love for humanity could carry such a class of persons to work among such depraved and unfortunate crea- tures. But as a matter of fact, the small salaries which the managers are compelled to pay almost posi- tively insures the employment only, as a rule, of very ordinary white persons to teach and uplift these de- based Negro youth. If the Managers of that Institu. tion have only the future good of these boys at heart, as well as the improvement of the community, they will forthwith consult with such men as Dr. Booker T. Washing- ton and Hon. John H. Smyth, super- intendent of a similar institution in Virginia, and make a search, a dili- gent search, for the Negro man of sufficient executive powers to take charge of that institution. The find- ing of such a person, to our mind, is the only true and abiding solution of the difficulty. If the best in the Negro race can not get sufficiently close to their own unfortunate mem- bers to reform and uplift them, then we seriously question the ability of those of an alien race to do the same Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: A TRIP TO CHELTENHAM.
full_text: A TRIP TO CHELTENHAM. Mayor Hayes and Other Notables Visit the House of Reformation Eor Colored Boys. Last Saturday, Mayor Hayes, in company with Mr. Joseph Janey, Mr. Orin C. Painter, Rev. George F. Bragg, Jr., directors on the part of the institution; Mr. Harry S. Cum- mings, one of the directors on the part of the city and Mr. Hiram Wat- ty, member of the City Council, paid a visit to Cheltenham and thorough- ly inspected that institution, The Mayor expressed himself as being much pleased with some of the things he saw and was not sparing in his condemnation of some other things which he thought ought not to exist. The company partook of dinner at the institution and afthr dinner, the Mayor addressed the boys in the chapel. The Mayor will endeavor to have the appropriation for the in- stitution increased next year, as their is great need of an increase, if the institution i' to be brought up to a proper standard. Rev. Dr. W. V. Tunnell, warden of King Hall Theological Seminary, Washington; D: C., accompanied the committee and also made an address to the boys. A Costly Thimble. Think of it, a thimble which cost $65,000 in American money! And think of a husband who presents his wife with such a gift! It belongs to the queen of Siam. Thimbles were not in use in Siam until a comparatively recent date. The king, seeing that English and Ameri- can women visiting his court used thimbles, had one made for his wife. The thimble, is of gold, enriched with precious stones. It is shaped like a partially opened lotus flower, each petal bearing the interlaced initials of the sovereign and his wife in ame- thysts. rubies, emeralds and topazes. Around the rim of the thimble can be read the date of the marriage of the royal pair according to the Siamese and European calendars, each number and each, letter being of alternate dia- monds and pearls. Norway, Ireland and Spain have more blind people in proportion to population than any other European countries. Spain has 216 per 100,000, Norway 208 and Ireland 111. Is it strange that the colored people generally have always looked upon this insti- tution with distrust; with being more of a chamber of tortures, than a House of Reformation? How can the managers of this in- stitution have the courage to stand before the public in the garb of be- nevolence and and philanthropy if they fail to make it possible for Maryland to use the best in the col- ored man for the elevation of his un- fortunate brethren? The city of Baltimore and the State of Maryland in return for the money expended on this institution, at the least, expect the highest pos- sible reformation along moral lines, so that when these boys again turn the body politic they may be better and not the worse for having been sent to a House of Reformation. Al- though a Honse of Reformation it is just as possible to make that institu- tion just as successful along certain linesas Tuskegee. But in the midst there must be the guiding and loving heart of a Booker Washington to in- vite and not repel. We do not know of any investment that will pay the city of Baltimore and state of Mary- land, better returns, in industrious and sober colored citizens, than to see to ie, that this school has a Book- er Washington and a body of well qualified teachers and officers of the race, who in morals, in mechanical and agricultural training will dem- onstrate what loving hearts can do for their own unfortunate brethren and consribute their united offering towards lessening the criminal pro- clivities of the racd, BALTIMORE, MAY 17, 1902. THE REFORMATORY FOR COLORED BOYS. Some few years ago, the widow of the late Frederick Douglass visited the House of Reformation for color- ed boys, Cheltenham, Md. The re- port of Mrs. Douglass was publish- ed in England as well as in this coun- try, and it revealed a shameful state of affairs. Shortly afterwards, a representative of the Afro-American- Ledger visited the institution and the results of his investigation were published in these columns. One of the most urgent needs then specified and which exist to this day, was and is a professional nurse for the sick boys of that institution. The sick boys do not receive anything like proper attention, and we do not believe that a like condition of affairs would be tolerated in a similar in- stitution for white boys. Recently a most capable colored woman was in mind for such position, and she was most eager to be permitted to work among the boys of her own race; villing to suffer and to put up with discrimination and even un- kind treatment, if only permitted to to supply a genuine motherly feeling for those black boys. Strange to say, such an offer which should have been seized with avidity, was turned down, simply because the present white teachers and officers working in this Reformatory for Colored Boys did not care to have a colored woman on the place. There can be no longer a doubt with respect to the greatest pressing need of that institution. White per- sons teaching in a Reformatory for colored boys who have such repul- sive feelings towards an educated and refined colored woman that they cannot bear the idea of her presence among them, as a co-laborer, to say the least, are unfit for that work. We fear that the Cheltenham Insti- tution will never amount to any- thing much until there are officers and teachers there at work who are iu sympathy with the boys they would reform. Reformation comes not from without, but from within. The boys must be won by love and kindness, and by means of such, help- ed to overcome their special weak- nesses. Only those persons ought to be allowed to teach them who have faith in them, and who are minded to get as close as possible to them so as to impart the true spirit of self respect and honor. As a matter of simple justice, it should be stated that the majority of the Trustees are not at all indis- posed towards such a radical change, and it is earnestly hoped that this late unfortunate affair of failure to secure a splendid colored woman for such an important spliere of üsefulness in that institution be- cause of her color, will have the ef- fect of hastening the day when an able and well qualified faculty of col- ored teachers and officers shall have charge of that institution. title: MAYOR HAYES AND CHELTENHAM.
full_text: MAYOR HAYES AND CHELTENHAM. Largely through the efforts of Councilman Watty, and Mr. Cum- mings and Rev. Mr. Bragg, members of the Board of Managers of the House of Reformation for colored boys located at Cheltenham, Prince Georges County, His Honor the Mayor of Baltimore, in company with the above gentlemen, and others of tlie committee from the Board visited the above institution on Sat- urday of last week. It was the Mayor's first visit and he was much pleased with it, and is determiaed to do all in his power to increase the appropriation from the city to- wards this institution for its proper and creditable support. The Mayor freely expressed himself with regard to many things in connection with the management of the institu- tion, and enthusiastically expressed his very great interest in the boys. In fact, his earnest address to the boys in the chapel demonstrated that he was not only a man of a great head, but also of a warm, kindly and sympathetic heart: Seldom have we been so impressed with a speech from a public official as we were with the utterances of Mayor Hayes He spoke right out from the heart. The principles of the Mayor with respect to that institution are sound and have the ring, not of flunkeyism, but of genuine manliness, Certainly the colored people of Maryland have a warm friend in the present Mayor of Baltimore. It is far better to have a head to lose than not to have any head at at all. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: INDUSTRIAL FAIR CLOSES.
full_text: Agricultural Department State Industrial Fair. INDUSTRIAL FAIR CLOSES. One Of The Most Successful In The History Of The State Thousands of People Crowd To Examine The Various Ex- hibits.  The State Industrial Fair, under the auspices of the Most Worshipful United Grand Lodge, F. & A. Ma- sons, of Maryland, which opened on Monday, November 25th, in the Centre Market Hall, closed on Mon- day night, when over 2500 people crowded every part of the spacious hall. The came in singles, in dou- bles, in triples and in crowds, until the ticket sellers were worn out selling tickets and had to be relieved by others. It was a record breaking crowd.  There were hundreds of exhibits, many of them of considerable merit, on exhibition. Certainly the Afro- Americans have no reason to be ashamed of the splendid exhibition they have made at this the first in- dustrial fair held in this State for many years. And it is reasonable to suppose that when the next fair shall be held, with proper manage- ment, the exhibit that can be made will surprise even the colored peo- ple themselves. Among those who received prizes are the following: T. Newton Moore for the best figure painting; Charles Gray for the best landscape painting; G. C. Dor- sey for the best sketcli from nature in oil; Roscoe Mitchell, three prizes for figure painting, landscape paint- ing, water colors and pen sketch. Walter Ebb; unique desigus in Harry T. Pratt, charcoal drawing, Nichols and Stevens, crayon por traits; H. W. Johnson, for pyogra- phic work; Miss Emma Martin also received a white ribbon prize for the same kind of work. John W. Davis received first prize for architectural work. All of these were in Depart- ment A.  In Department B, Agricultural. Albert Gantt and J. Holsey, of Cal- vert county, receive blue ribbons for the best display of Maryland tobac- co. J. Holsey also received first prize for the best half-dozen cab- bages. Joseph Garrett, of Arbutus, first prize for the best carrotts; Spen- cer Patterson, of St. Dennis, for field corn, Mace Jones, Sherwood, Keiffer pears; Adam Washington, Anne Arundel county, giant pumpkins; general display of agricultural pro- ducts, Cheltenham Industrial School for Boys. Best display of shirts, em- broidery, bread and preserves, lu- dustrial School for Colored Girls; Melval. Horticulture and Floriculture, Mr. George Siddons received the first prize. Preserves: Mrs. Isreal Gearing, canned peaches; Mrs. John W. Gord man, canned pears; crab apple jelly, Mrs. Hughes, of Loch Raven; pickels in quart jars, H. Catherine Price; largest cucumber pickel, Mrs. Joseph Garrett; grape wine, Mrs. Henrietta Jackson. Department C, Needle and Fancy Work: Best tailor made dress, Met- ropolitan Department Store; best evening dress, Mrs. Mazie Payne; best fancy waist, Mrs. Columbia Butler; Luigene waist, Mrs. Mamie Jones; best embroidered shirt waist, Mrs. Mary Greenstreet, embroider- ed dresser scarf, Mrs. Sophia Holmes; embroidered pictured frame, Elizabeth Brown; table cover, Mrs. Sophia Holmes.  Lace or Drawn Work: Design in hand-made work, Rebecca Simms Bradford; lace hankerchief, Mrs. C. F. Bacus and C. Signora Vau Nockey; lace collar, Miss Maggie Giles; centre piece, Mrs. Cabell Cal- loway; table cover, Miss Mary E. Holland; crazy quilt, Mrs. George B. Kennard; patch quilt, Mrs. John Gordman; knitted quilt, C. Mar riott; knitted shawl, Hannah Foot; foot mat, Joseph J. Demby. Display in millinery, Metropolitan Department Store; general display by a milliner, Mrs. Ella S. Smith; single trimmed hat, Miss Augusta Lewis. Laundry Work: Mrs. Georgianna Booker; best educational exhibit by country school, Catonsville Indus- trial School; best musical composi- tion, Ambrose Briscoe; best hand- made table, Eliner Hunt; best hand- made chair, Charles E. Chester; - three specimens of wood carving, Sherman Briscoe; best exhibit of forged work, James M. Squirrell, of Catonsville; fine work in stell, Mil- ton E. clean_text: Agricultural Department State Industrial Fair. INDUSTRIAL FAIR CLOSES. One Of The Most Successful In The History Of The State Thousands of People Crowd To Examine The Various Ex- hibits. The State Industrial Fair, under the auspices of the Most Worshipful United Grand Lodge, F. & A. Ma- sons, of Maryland, which opened on Monday, November 25th, in the Centre Market Hall, closed on Mon- day night, when over 2500 people crowded every part of the spacious hall. The came in singles, in dou- bles, in triples and in crowds, until the ticket sellers were worn out selling tickets and had to be relieved by others. It was a record breaking crowd. There were hundreds of exhibits, many of them of considerable merit, on exhibition. Certainly the Afro- Americans have no reason to be ashamed of the splendid exhibition they have made at this the first in- dustrial fair held in this State for many years. And it is reasonable to suppose that when the next fair shall be held, with proper manage- ment, the exhibit that can be made will surprise even the colored peo- ple themselves. Among those who received prizes are the following: T. Newton Moore for the best figure painting; Charles Gray for the best landscape painting; G. C. Dor- sey for the best sketcli from nature in oil; Roscoe Mitchell, three prizes for figure painting, landscape paint- ing, water colors and pen sketch. Walter Ebb; unique desigus in Harry T. Pratt, charcoal drawing, Nichols and Stevens, crayon por traits; H. W. Johnson, for pyogra- phic work; Miss Emma Martin also received a white ribbon prize for the same kind of work. John W. Davis received first prize for architectural work. All of these were in Depart- ment A. In Department B, Agricultural. Albert Gantt and J. Holsey, of Cal- vert county, receive blue ribbons for the best display of Maryland tobac- co. J. Holsey also received first prize for the best half-dozen cab- bages. Joseph Garrett, of Arbutus, first prize for the best carrotts; Spen- cer Patterson, of St. Dennis, for field corn, Mace Jones, Sherwood, Keiffer pears; Adam Washington, Anne Arundel county, giant pumpkins; general display of agricultural pro- ducts, Cheltenham Industrial School for Boys. title: A QUESTION FOR THE SUPREME BENCH.
full_text: in a community of more than one hundred thousand colored persons that there should be far more than 30 annual recruits from the army of deserted waifs, youthful criminals and unfortunate ones. It would seem reasonable that adequate and proper provision should be made, especially for color- ed boys between 2 and 10. If chil- dren between these ages were promptly rescued from vile and un- healthy surroundings and placed in a proper institution where they might receive training and direction just at the time when the chances of their salvation would seem most favorable. there would be fewer boys at 14, 15, 16 and the like to be sent from the courts to Cheltenham or the jail, or let loose upon the com- munity. The Maryland Home has been ad- mirably conducted and the children of this institution indicate a very hopeful future, but the benefits and help which the few enjoy ought to be extended to the many. The Board of Managers of this institution rec- ognize all this and have been study- ing this problem for some time, but they are poor men and the supply- ing by them of t.se means sufficient to procure a commodious property suited for manual training and such industries as very young children could operate, is entirely out of the question. Here then is something practical. If the learned gentlemen of the Supreme Bench could induce the charitably inclined of the domi- nant race to cooperate with the au- thorities of this most excellent insti- tution in providing accomodations for greater numbers of the little ur- chins between 2 and 10, who ought to be in such a home, then the num- ber of more maturer years who ap pear before the judges to be sent to Cheltenham, the jail or the peniten- tiary wonld gradually decrease. When we have sufficiently provided for the waifs, bastards and deserted children, and give them a new life, we thereby render it increasingly dif- ficult for the large stock of criminais of maturer years. QUESTION FOR THE SUPREME BENCH. Even such, a learned. justice as Judge Kichie, is, now and then, puz- zled with respect to the punishment to be meted out to certain young criminals of the colored race. The truth is, the whole subject of dealing with colored criminals has received but little intelligent atten- tlon and criticism on the part of our city and state authorities and as a result of this failure we are far from getting the best returns to the com munity in reformation and dimuni- tion of criminal tendencies on the part of the colored youth. Aside from the institutions of the Roman. Catholic Church, there are only two places receiving neglected or crimi ally disposed colored boys. The Maryiand Home for Friendless Col- ored Children receives boys from 2 to 10 years and the Cheltenham in- stitution from 12 and uowards. There are in the city of Baltimore alone, leaving out the rest of the state of Maryland. in the neighborhood of one hundred thous and colored persons. When one considers the peculiar conditions which surround the great majority of colored persons, and the insidious snares and temptations in the way of the colored youth, the annual nor mal percentage of youthful criminals of the race would be sufficient to fill over and again the institutions now an existence. title: A WHITE MAN WHO IS A FRIEND IN DEED
full_text: given a bath and clean under clothe, and supplied with two suits of the regulation uniform, one for daily use, the other for Sunday and spec- ial occasions such as general inspec- tion, is required every Sunday morning during good weather, com- pany drill and parade. The change from a filthy condition to that of cleanliness makes them realize that they are and will be benefitted by coming to this institution. They are closely questioned to determine their mental and moral calibre, their general temperament which governs their treatment, and their inclination to work which regulate their assignment. They are under the care of an officer at all times and must be looked after carefully, restrained or encouraged as may be necessary. Bad habits of the past must be discarded and a new course started. The individuality of each inmate must necessrily be considered in order to wisely ad- minister such discipline as will ob- tain the desired results." In the brief extract which we shall now quote, Mr. Pyles shows himself infinitely more sympathet- ic and human than do some of the Negro mothers at whom the blast is unquestionably aimed. The people of our race will do well to serious- ly ponder such wise and wholesome words. Mr. Pyles says: "This is not an orphanage or a refuse into which heartless and un- faithful parents may send their children. To send such children who simply need care, kindenss and training is a great wrong-a flag- rant abuse of the hospitality and benevolence of the State." And, yet, there is such a thing as a comparatively young woman desiring to re-marry, and to have a "good time," by being freed from the care and rearing of her own child, ready and anxious to make oath to a wicked lie, and murder the moral and intellectual life of her own child by having him sent to such an institution! Our constant regret is, that such few of our better class of colored citizens have ever visited this insti- tution, which, in most respects is more like a great industrial school, along military lines, than a House of Reformation. The Rev. Dr. Gaines, who made his first visit last year, was so exeecdingly pleased A WHITE MAN WHO IS A FRIEND that he almost determined to com- IN DEED There is a white man, in this state, not a politician, but a plain country man, who is doing as much as any other one man in this state for the highest and best welfare of the Negroes of the commnwealth. We refer to Mr. John B. Pyles, the superintendent of the House of Ref- romation for Colored Boys. The present writer has been associated with Mr. Pyles for more than ten years, and, hence, we speak from deliberate personal knowledge and contact. There is no institution in this state serving the interests of our people which is more entitled to the confidence and warm support of the race than the House of Ref- ormation, located at Cheltenham, Md. It is wholly unnecessary for us to dwell upon the personality of Mr. Pyles. Sufficient to say, that both the superintendent and his wife are just as gracious, considerate and kind in this work as could be ex- pected of any sincerely Christian man and wife. It has always been a sincere and genuine pleasure for us to meet and greet the superin- tendent and his devoted wife upon every occasion when we visit the in- stitution. But from the handscime and most artistic biennial report of the institution we desire to give a few extracts which will be of spec ial interest to our readers. In an swering the question "what good is being accomplished?" Superin- tendent Pyles among other things says: "Those who manifest an interest in reformatory work and conse- quently in the future welfare of the degraded, dishonest, vicious or un- fortunate youths who are committed to this and various institutions frequently ask the question what good is being accomplished; are the inmates better at the time they are paroled or discharged than when they are received; has the com- munity received an equivalent for the expense necessary for their sup- port. After being officially connec- ted with this institution its var- ious branches, and as superinten- dent, I am prepared to say that the city and state have been well re- imbursed for the efforts put forth in appropriating to this reforma- tory. It has been the means of taking from the city and the coun- ties hundreds of vicious and incor- rigible boys who were being edu- cated in crime through their daily intercourse in the low haunts of in- iquity by which many of them were surrounded, dishonest and degrad- ing habits being the desire of their hearts to this institution a general history of their past lives is record- ed, with the offences for which they Bre committed. Their unwhole- tome suits are discarded; they are mit some act whereby he might be permanently committed. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: THE DAWN OF THE NEW FREEDOM.
full_text: utter. The defeat of the old Re- publican machine in this state, marked a most decided advance for the race in Maryland. We needed to be emancipated from the servil. ity which has characterised such a large number of colored voters for long time. That has at last been accomplished, and the way is now clear for our entrance into poli- tics. We have never been in politics. Heretofore, we have been used to help win victories for others. The present Governor of Maryland could not have been elected without the aid of colored voters. This assertion even Mr. Golsborough must admit. And, yet, what is he to us, more than Mr. Gorman would have been if he had been elected in spite of our opposi- tion. Judging the younger Mr. Gorman by the practical friendness of his father, the senior Gorman, with respect to helping individuals of the colored race, we would cer- tainly conclude that he could not have been less friendly thad his father. But what of Mr. Golds- borough What has he done to- wards our advancement. Soon after his inauguration, his attention was directed to the educational and charitable needs of the Negro pop- ulation of this state. What move has he made in that direction? His attention was also directed to the Melvale Reformatory, for col- ored girls, and the Cheltenham Re- formatory, for colored boys, and the request made that he appoint a suitable colored man, or woman, on each of the Boards of the insti- tutions mentioned. It is hardly necessary to mention that he has failed to make any such appoint- ments. In the face of all this, we do not hesitate in giving it forth as our deliberate conviction, that were such a man as William Cabel Bruce, or Archibald Taylor or any demo- crat of that type, the occupant of the Gobernatorial chair, that such would not hesitate to appoint well qualified persons to serve on such boards, which institutions being wholly concerned with the needs of the colored race. Yea, more that this, it is to just such men that we must look for larger appropriations for the educational and charitable institutions of the state, designed for our people. This freedom which has begun to dawn upon us will dawn, more and more, and satisfy all our ex- pectations, just in proportion as we cooperate with this class of citizens in securing the best men to go to Annapolis. A new political organization is about to be organized throughout the city, among the men of our race. An oragnization which will control some three or four thous- and votes, pledged to the best in- terests of our people. Here, then, is our opportunity. A certain element of the Democratic party anxiously desires to send to Annap- olis the very best men of brains and character in the state. Is there no way whereby we can help in this matter. What do we vitally need, in this state, in an educa- tional and charitable way? The men among us who have studied the conditions ought to make known, definitely, our needs. And, then, if these good friends, candi- dates for the Legislature, will pledge us their hearty and earnest support, along such lines, ought to vote for them, whether they be Democrats, Progressives, or any thing else. We ought to have enough political sense to vote for the men who will openly pledge themselves to the improvement of such interests as directly concern us. Yes, we need the organization which is projected, and it cannot materilize too rapidly. We are free and independent, and our best interest demands a willingness to cooperate with men of good will. we  THE DAWN OF THE NEW FREEDOM. The result in the state of Mary- Jand, last November, was a distinct victory, and advance, for the col- ored people of this state. title: ATHLETIC NOTES.
full_text: f ATHLETIC NOTES BY W. H. J.BECKETT. Notes for this column addressed to Mr. Beckett, care of this office, will re- ceive careful attention. -Ed. Health is power. The children of the playgrounds are gaining in physical vigor and are fortifying themselves against. many trying diseases, Hon. Harry S. Cummings ad- dressed the children and parents at an unique occasion held last Thurs day at Waesche Street Playground. He said that the playgrounds were supplying a great need. It is evidenced that they are interested in the physical and moral well-being of the boys and girls. "You boys were not made for Cheltenham, or you girls for Mel- vale. All of you can accomplish some good. Learn to do well while you are young." He complimented the Playground Association for the excellent equip- ment and working force. He em- phasized the importance of the pa- rents and community interesting themselves in such an agency, which means an uplift to the race. Games were played and an enjoy- able time was spent. Mrs. Lee and Mr. Bantum deserve great credit for the manner in which the large crowd was haudled. ست Druid Hill Park Playground had a beautiful occasion last Friday. Over forty parents witnessed their children engage in the various ring games. Miss Stockett, the director. of occasions, was present and ex- pressed her delight in effort put forth by the ehildren. Mrs. Green and Mr. Braxton have made the Fark a pleasant place to visit. Any day a lively game can be bad and the boys flock to enjoy the sports. Thursday, August 4th, the High School Playground had a festival from 4 to 7 Р. М. A large crowd was present. A prominent speaker made an address; ring and athletic games indulged in by the children and a pleasant time spent. Mr. Jones director, and Misses Hayes and Scott assistants. Saturday last Mrs. Cooper and Mr. McDaniels, directors of No.. 101 Playground, treated the boys and girls to lemonade and cake. My, but they enjoyed themselves. Jeffer- son Street has an occasion on Aug. 11th. All East Baltimore is wait- ing to make this a fine affair. July 29th-Playground Ball. Playground No. 110, 8; C. H. S. Playground, 2. The game was en- thusiastic and intense interest was manifested when Waesche Street won with flying colors. The High School lads are considered a fast bunch August 3rd-Volley Ball. Playground No. 101, 20; Play- ground No. 112, 15 (small boys). Playground No. 112, 20; Play- ground, No. 101, 4 (large boys). Playground ball-Playground No. 101, 2; Playground, No. 112, 0 (small boys). Playground No. 0; Playground No. 101. 2 (large boys). On last Saturday the "Working Men's Athletic Club" of Brooklyn, administered defeat to the "Smart Set," in a last game of baseball, to the tune of 10 to 9. The children of the playgrounds are gaining in physical vigor and are fortifying themselves against. many trying diseases, Hon. Harry S. Cummings ad- dressed the children and parents at an unique occasion held last Thurs day at Waesche Street Playground. He said that the playgrounds were supplying a great need. It is evidenced that they are interested in the physical and moral well-being of the boys and girls. "You boys were not made for Cheltenham, or you girls for Mel- vale. All of you can accomplish some good. Learn to do well while you are young." He complimented the Playground Association for the excellent equip- ment and working force. He em- phasized the importance of the pa- rents and community interesting themselves in such an agency, which means an uplift to the race. Games were played and an enjoy- able time was spent. title: MARYLAND INDUSTRIAL SCHOOL FOR BOYS
full_text: MARYLAND INDUSTRIAL SCHOOL FOR BOYS Such is the name and title of an institution which has been proposed by His Honor Judge Williams of the Juvenile Court, of the city of Baltimore. The bill which has already been prepared will be in- troduced in the State Legislature. The measure has the heartiest support of the Ministerial Alliance of this city. Judge Williams ap peared before this body. upon its invitation, at the December meet- ing, and outlined the features of the measure, and the Alliance not only warmly indorsed the project, but appointed a committee, repre- senting the various denominations of colored citizens, to communicate with the Governor about the mat- ter, and to cooperate with the Judge in such ways as he should deem most helpful. The bill provides that the in- stitution shall be located in the country, not more than 15 miles remote from this city; it appropri- ates twenty-five thousand dollars for the acquiring of the plant, and ten thousand dollars annually, from the State of Maryland, and five thousand from the city of Balti- a more, towards its maintainence and support. Colored boys between the ages of 8 and 18 may be com- mitteed to the institution by the Judge of the Juvenile Court, or by other judicial authority through- out the State. It needs no argu- ment whatever to establish the necessity of such an institution. We are devoutly glad that it comes from such man as Judge Williams. Apart from his con- nection with the Juvenile Court, and the splendid opportunities thus afforded to judge of the real necessity of such an institution, Judge Williams is a godly man, and a true type of the Christian gentleman, and so impressed were the colored preachers with his sympathetic personality, that were it in their power they would make him Judge of the Juvenile Court for life. And, while writing on this subject, it comes to our mind, from personal knowledge, that there is a large army of such benevolently disposed white citi- zens who share in the same sincere and devoted feelings towards their colored brethren, as our friend Judge Williams. But, the great trouble is, they do not touch the lives of colored citizens at any point, and colored people, generally speaking, are wholly ignorant of their existence. Now is the oppor- tunity for some of the white states- men, at Annapolis, who have, in so many ways, wrought an impression upon colored people, that they are entirely indifferent to anything which pertains to them, save re- strictive legislation, to vindicate the good and redeeming side of their nature. And so we devoutly hope that they may embrace the present opportunity as an earnest of their good intentions. In con- nection with this same subject, it is most timely that we should note the good work of a white country- man, who has proven a real bene- factor of the colored race in Mary- land. And, this we shall do in the article headed "Cheltenham.". Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.
