title: DEATH COMES TO HARRY S. CUMMINGS: SUCCUMBS AFTER ILLNESS OF MORE THAN A YEAR. HIS CONDITION BECAME SERIOUS A FEW DAYS AGO AND DEATH WAS NOT UNEXPECTED. HAD INTERESTING CAREER WAS OLDEST MEMBER OF STATE BAR IN POINT OF SERVICE SECONDED NOMINATION OF THEODORE ROOSEVELT.
full_text: DEATH COMES TO HARRY S. CUMMINGS Succumbs After Illness Of More Than A Year. His Condition Became Serious A Few Days Ago And Death Was Not Unexpected. HAD INTERESTING CAREER Was Oldest Member Of State Bar In Point Of Service Seconded Nomination Of Theodore Roosevelt. Harry Smythe Cummings, senior member of the First Branch City Council in point of service, and long a prominent figure in the councils of the Republican party, died late Thurs- day night at his home, 1318 Drufd Hilf avenue A complication of diseases caused his death.. He had been in ill health for about two years, and was forced to miss many sessions of the City Council. He practically gave up his law practice many months ago. Accompanied by his sister, Miss Ida R. Cummings, he went to Harpers Ferry, W. Va., last month, in the hope of benefitting his health. He return ed a little over two weeks ago, appar- ently benefitted. He was seen on the streets, and many friends congratula- ted him on his apparent change for the better and wished him ultimate recovery. He went out to Roland Park to stay, nearly two weeks ago, and was stricken there on Wednesday of last week. He was brought to his home and remained in a state of coma until the end. He began to sink Thursday morning, and relatives and a large host of friends anxiously await ed news of his condition. DEATH CAUSES REGRET. All over the city, people in every walk of life expressed regret at his death. In the downtown section, where he was well known to promi- nent business and professional men. FIRST COLORED COUNCILMAN The Democratic Legislature in 1890 gerrymanded the city, placing a ma- jority of colored voters in the old Eleventh ward. Rev. William H. Weaver and other leaders, seeing the opportunity of electing a colored man, advised Mr. Cummings to enter the fight. He did and won. He was re- elected in 1891, but was defeated the following year. Thus for a time was lost the services of the first colored man to fill an elective office in this state. During the first year he was in the City Council he appointed Har- ry T. Pratt to a scholarship in the Maryland Institute. He went with the late Ferdinand C. Latrobe, then mayor, on a municipal junketing par- ty to Boston, Mass., anddelivered what is said to have been the best speech delivered by a Marylander at the ban- quet given in that city in honor of the visitors. Coming back from Boston on the train, he secured from Mayor Latrobe a proníise to favor the open- ing of a manual training school for colored boys. The City Council pass- ed the act authorizing the same and the school was opened just twenty- five years ago. At every commencement of the school, before it was merged into the High School, Mr. Cummings deliver- ed the address presenting the medal for steam engineering, offered by the principal of the school, the late Will- iam R. Ryan. HARRY S. CUMMINGS. many tributes were heard. This was especially true around the Courthouse and City Hall, where everybody knew him. full_text: Maryland Home in Need of Soap and Bath. Vist to Home sor Friendless Children Discloses Fact That Force of Workers is Not Ade- quate to Keep Up Institution. A representative of the Afro- American paid a short visit to the Home just outside of Catonsville last week, and took a cursory view of the home, its inmates and workers, and a part of the 32 acre tract.. The part of the tract of land in front of the Home is well laid out in an orchard, and between the trees, a splendid variety of garden crops. The superintendent stated that this farming is done by him- self with the assistance of the boys, whose ages range from ten to fif- teen. A mule, two cows, a calf, and two pigs make up the live stock of the place. Known as the one institution for colored children that stands be- tween the Juvenile Court and Cheltenham, the Maryland Home has a definite part to play in the social reform work of the city and state. It takes boys who are tru- ants, convicted of petty crimes except theft, and aims to give them a start of one or two years until their parents can care for them again, or until some other kind Goins, assistant matron, and a family will offer to rear them cook. title: CHELTENHAM
full_text: CHELTENHAM Cheltenham is situated in Prince George's county, Md. Located at that place, is an institution for colored boys, known as the House of Reformation. The name of Enoch Pratt is not only connected with the public libraries of our city, but also with this institution for colored boys at Cheltenham. The superintendent of the House of Reformation is Mr. John B. Pyles, a white man, and a native of that county. He is a man of a kind heart, but brave and energetic. His is a great task, but he is perfectly at home in the doing of the work devolving upon him. He is, really, a benefactor of the Negro race. just where such bene- faction is most needed. But, we started this article simply to call attention to the fact, that Chelten- ham, in operation, is more of a great industrial school, than a "Reformatory." Some of the many colored boys who enter the portals of Cheltenham receive for the first time a knowledge of the primary lessons in civilization. Good food, warm clothing, school and work; and all under proper discipline. Very recently, a colored tailor, Mr. J. P. Featherstone, of this city, succeeded the former tailor of the institution who was white. We are pleased to learn that Mr. Featherstone is not only giving satisfaction, but is making a good reputation for himself. In a pri- vate letter to one of the members of the Board of Managers, Mr. Featherstone writes, in part, as follows: "The head of my service seems rather much pleased up to the present. I have already turned out 70 blue pants, 50 blue coats, 65 grey pants, 18 grey coats, 36 top shirts, 45 under drawers, with required repairs for entire school. There are 12 boys in my depart- ment;6 machines and 6 bench boys. Two of good behavior, four in moods of behavior, and the other six very troublesome; but 1 am able to keep them, so far, in thorough subjection, and every authority is given me in the com- plete circle of my charges." Mr. Featherstone seems to be the right man in the right place. We are glad of this, because when other vacancies exists, if colored men, of the proper moral and efficient type can be found, they will have vouchsafed them the same opportunity Mr. Featherstone is so well and wisely using. Two things more. From the above sample, the reading colored com- munity can get some idea of the value and importance of the work at Cheltenham. It is a little sur- prising that so few, almost none, representative colored people ever visit this institution. Such not only have a standing invitation, but they are sure to become en- thusiastic supporters of the insti- tution, if once they visit the place. Finally, the only way to learn how to manage such a great institution is by the race availing itself of such opportunities as, now and then, become open as indicated in the filling of the place of tailor. Let the race have the right kind of men for such openings whenever they shall occur. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: MEMBERS OF BOARD VISIT CHELTENHAM
full_text: MEMBERS OF BOARD VISIT CHELTENHAM House of Reformation Found in Admirable Con- dition. Rev. Geo. F. Bragg and Mr. Bernard Taylor, members of the Board of Directors of the House of Reformation in Prince Georges County. Maryland, paid a visit to the institution last Tuesday. They were received by Superin- tendent Pyles, served dinner, and spent a large part of the after- noon in inspecting grounds and buildings and the work of the 353 boys who are inmates there. The committee of the February Grand Jury had already given Cheltenham a clean bill so that the members of the Board were not surprised to find both school work and shop work in full blast and an apparently satisfied set of youths busily occupied. On this last point, Superintend- ent Pyles informed the visiting Board members that despite the fact, that there are no walls around the institution, there are very few boys who attempt to escape, He said that freuently a whole gang is hired out to neighboring farm- ers. and sent away in auto trucks under the charge of some older boys. At night fall however, they invaribly showed up again, and none was missing. Rev. Bragg is one of the older members of Cheltenham Board but Mr. Taylor was making his first visit to the institution since his appointment last year. GOVERNOR DISAPPOINTS Chief Executive Represented at Bethel by Robert E. Lee. Illness of Governor Harrington prevented his attendance at meeting conducted by Rev. Simon Drew of Washington in the inter- est of raising the mortgage on his church: The meeting was held at Bethel Church, and the $75 raised and turned over to Rev. Drew. sum of Mr. Robert E. Lee. (white) copffected with the State Indus- trial. Accident Commission repre- sented the mayor, and in genera commended the work of Dr. Drew. but in particular congratulated the membership of the church on the great drive they had made in reducing the large mortgage. Mr. Lee said that a great many white churches would be glad to point to such a record as Bethel Church had made in the past twenty-one Amonths. Rev. J. W. Winston, and Roy Drew with Dr. Sampson Brooks were the other speakers. Thursday night Mme. Henrietta Vinton Davis entertained a fairly large audience with readings from Dumber and other popular writers and concluded with a travelogue Lin which she took her hearers יום a visit to the West Indian Islands and Panama. John Lewis Morris: Secretary of Inter- Liberia Horward Education for gave rise to the belief that affairs of moment to the little West Afri- can republic were discussed. Mr.. Scott was a member of the Ameri- can Commission that visited Li- benia in the early days of the Taft administration and is keenly inter- ested in affairs there. Beyond stating that they were only renewing old friendships, the gentlemen refused to state wheth- er or not their meeting had any significance. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Mulberry St., Charles Bundy 18 and Robert Young were arranged in part 1 of the crimnal court on last Monday for the larceny of two cases of whis- key from the Seegle Wholesale Liquor House on Lombard St. near Front St., on September 13 and the attempt to repeat the act on September 20. Bun dy had been parolea for the same offense last May, but had violated the parole. The boys claimed in court that they had stolen the whiskey and soid it to Joseph Alexander, who keeps a pool parlor on the corner of Saratoga and Pine Sts. Alexander was arrest- ed for receiving stolen goods but was declared by the court to be not guilty. Bundy was committed to the House of Correction at Cheltenham: Charles Mantley was given six months in jail to take effect from September 20 last; Robinson Young was declared, not guilty by the court. title: MRS. HOOKER IS FRIEND OF BOYS: INTERVENES TO SAVE YOUNGSTERS FROM JAIL WHILE ENROUTE TO CHELTENHAM
full_text: MRS. HOOKER IS FRIEND OF BOYS Intervenes To Save Youngsters From Jail While Enroute To Cheltenham Through the efforts of Mrs. Donald Hooker, Mrs. Hugh.Π. Young and other prominent white women the old custom of detain- ing boys in the City Jail while awaiting transportation to the Colored House of Reformation has been abolished. Instead, the boys are now kept in a room on the second floor of the Northern Police Station while waiting for an official from Cheltenham to come after them. Several were kept in the station this week. "We had heard of the existence of the situation," Mrs. Hooker told the Afro-American Thursday morning," went over to jail to see for ourselves. We were shocked to find that colored boys were kept there with criminals and went to see Police Commissioner Gaither about the matter. When we suggested the Northern Police State he and Judge Wiliams, of the Juvenile Court, gave his ready consent." Mrs. Hooker and a number of ladies are also interested in a movement to have the colorea parental school started. An ap- propriation of $10,000 therefore is now lying idle in the city treasury. CARDINAL'S LETTERS PAVED THE WAY Crackers Respected Local Attor- ney, But Called Others "Niggers" A letter from Cardinal Gibbons proved a boon for Attorney J. Howard Payne, who Was in Raleigh, N. C. on legal business last week. He also had letters of introduction from Judge Rose, the United States District Attorney, Marshall Stockham and State's Attorney Leach, but officials in Raleigh did not want to see these. saying the one from the Cardinal was enough. A Catholic priest in Raleigh in- troduced him to a lawyer there, who took him to Federal Court. One official who took a particular interest in Mr. Payne was asked by him if he were also a Catholic. He replied that he was a thirty- degree Mason. White lawyers there referred to him as "Mr. Payne" and showed him every courtesy, while at he same time refering to colored witnesses in court as "Niggers", etc.. to One white lawyer even saw it that he had a fine place to stay 8 YEAR OLD HURT Archibald Johnson, aged 8 years, of 748 1-2 Saratoga street, was struck by an auto truck Thursday, receiving injuries of the left knee and hip. His mother.refused med- ical aid. BENZINE CAUSES FIRE While Mrs. Bertina Sunlas was cleaning the coat of her son with benzine on the third floor of 5001 Dolphin street last Saturday even- ling, a candle fell into the bottle icontaining the benzine causing a slight fire.. The flames were put out with a blanket.

We were shocked to find that colored boys were kept there with criminals and went to see Police Commissioner Gaither about the matter. When we suggested the Northern Police State he and Judge Wiliams, of the Juvenile Court, gave his ready consent." Mrs. Hooker and a number of ladies are also interested in a movement to have the colorea parental school started. An ap- propriation of $10,000 therefore is now lying idle in the city treasury. CARDINAL'S LETTERS PAVED THE WAY Crackers Respected Local Attor- ney, But Called Others "Niggers" A letter from Cardinal Gibbons proved a boon for Attorney J. Howard Payne, who Was in Raleigh, N. C. on legal business last week. He also had letters of introduction from Judge Rose, the United States District Attorney, Marshall Stockham and State's Attorney Leach, but officials in Raleigh did not want to see these. saying the one from the Cardinal was enough. A Catholic priest in Raleigh in- troduced him to a lawyer there, who took him to Federal Court. One official who took a particular interest in Mr. Payne was asked by him if he were also a Catholic. He replied that he was a thirty- degree Mason. White lawyers there referred to him as "Mr. Payne" and showed him every courtesy, while at he same time refering to colored witnesses in court as "Niggers", etc.. to One white lawyer even saw it that he had a fine place to stay 8 YEAR OLD HURT Archibald Johnson, aged 8 years, of 748 1-2 Saratoga street, was struck by an auto truck Thursday, receiving injuries of the left knee and hip. His mother.refused med- ical aid. BENZINE CAUSES FIRE While Mrs. Bertina Sunlas was cleaning the coat of her son with benzine on the third floor of 5001 Dolphin street last Saturday even- ling, a candle fell into the bottle icontaining the benzine causing a slight fire.. The flames were put out with a blanket.

title: PUT ON BREAD AND WATER: MRS. BARRIET ELLIS ALLEGES THAT SON WAS MISTREATED AT CHELTENHAM
full_text: PUT ON BREAD AND WATER Mrs. Harriet Ellis Alleges That Son Was Mistreated At Chelten-  According to Mrs. Harriett Ellis, 548 St. Mary street, her fifteen: year old son, Wm. Franklin Ellis, was inhumanly treated while an inmate of the Colored House of Reformation at Cheltenham. Young. Ellis was sent to the in- | stitution last July for alleged par- ticipation in a robbery. Fiis moth- er says that he was badly treated and beaten by a colored man whol had charge of a number of boys in one of the departments of the in- stitution. Ellis escaped, but was later recaptured. According to the boy's story, the colored man and a white man severely beat him. He was tienj placed on a bread and water diet. When his mother visited the in- stitution he had been on this bill of fare for eighteen days. Shei says that his eyes were sunken, his! body emaciated and he was so weak that he could hardly stand. She engaged Attorney Roy S. Bond to institute habeas corpus proceedings and Judge Gorter re- leased the boy from the institution, Ellis is under treatment and has regained considerable strength. of his DUNBAR'S BOOKS SELL Publishers Returns Divided Be- tween Poet's Mother And Wife In answer to the question, "What has become of the returns from the publishers for the sale of Mr. Dunbar's books?" which question was contained in an article con- cerning the mother of America's! most distinctive poet in the AFRO- AMERICAN of September 3rd, Dodd. Mead & Company, publish- ers of Dunbar's books write to the AFRO-AMERICAN:- : i ! "We have paid Mr. Dunbar's: mother and Mr. Dunbar's wife very considerable sums twice each year since his death in accordance with his will and publishing contracts.! His books continue to sell, espe- cially the Complete Poems." Rogers. Liberian Educator Here Rev Dr. John H. Reed, former president of Liberia College, Mon- rovia, and now director of the edu- cational work of the M. E: Church in West Africa, was the guest of Dr. Ernest Lyon last Saturday and Sunday. He preached at John Wesley M. E. Church Sunday morning and at Trinity A. M. E. Church in the evening.

title: EX-INMATE TAKES ISSUE WITH RECTOR ON CHELTENHAM: DR. GEORGE F. BRAGG CALLED IT A GOD-SEND; HARRY MACK SAYS IT IS GODFORSAKEN BURIED DEAD BODY SHEET AND PINE BOX ONLY FOR LAD INTERRED IN UNMARKED GRAVE
full_text: EX-INMATE TAKES ISSUE WITH RECTOR ON CHELTENHAM • Dr. George F. Bragg Called It A God-Send; Harry Mack Says It Is God- Forsaken : BURIED DEAD BODY : Sheet and Pine Box Only for Lad Interred In Un- marked Grave Taking issue with Rev. Dr. Geo. F. Bragg. member of the Board of Managers of Cheltenham, who terms the institution a God-send in last week's AFRO-AMERICAN, Harry Edgar Mack, of Riderwood, Md., declares to the contrary that it is God-forsaken. In a letter to the AFRO-AMERI- CAN, he says "Cheltenham in 1917- 18 was a twentieth century slave plantation rather than a house of reformation. "I still remember those words, that Judge T. J. C. Williams ut- tered in early '17. They were 'send him to Cheltenham until he's twenty-one years of age.' Some verdict I'll say charge of a minor without proper care.' Well anyway, was sent there and to tell the truth, it was perfeet torture to0 me. Food consisted of bread, coffee sometimes oatmeal breakfast, with bread, or corn- bread and soup for dinner and coffee or tea, for supper, soup and kale. We used to have sticks and these little "wings" as I call- ed them that blow from maple trees, and some times it looked like dish water. Boys Work On Sunday Work-why there was a plenty to exercise your muscles. Why often in the fall of '18 1 worked 30 days in the month, cut corn Sunday's same as any other day. Sunday too, was our bath day but when I worked other boys too, on Sundays our skin never saw water. So your Sep bodies were not possible, but I never any vermin there. for 1 1 for 1 the cleanesti heard of Cheltenham had a dormitory which I considered a benefactor. as it was really clean with its white beds and linen, Smaller Boys Beaten חו: inch But that's not all. I have seen the officers there beat the small- er boys on thehead with a stick about three fourth's of in diameter until blood ran down their shoulders as water would run from a faucet. I have s٠٠٠ boys half-stripped receiving any number of lashes with a whip until their skin was covered with whelps. These whippings mostly were administered by Mr. Harry Washington, colored who was assistant farmer. I also, with other boys worke on surrounding farms just as we worked at the institution for no more than a meal or two. were compelled to go to bed at seven o'clock in the summer. We i : 1 1 ১ 1 1 11 } 1 U S T h was there and also had to rise early to curry mules or milk th cows, or whatever there was to do. S 1. Buried Naked Body I had the occasion to help buryi a hoy while there. He was placed in a sheet stark naked, and with street, we put him in a wooden box, screwed the lid on, and buried him on the edge of the woods with- out any marking, only twig of a tree which I placed at the head of his grave. This is only 11 short article on this subject, but I could write a book on Cheltenhaทา. call it a God-Forsaken place. T

title: CHELTENHAM NOT AN IDEAL SPOT: E.B. TAYLOR, MEMBERS OF BOARD OF MANAGERS, ANSWERS EDITORIAL IN LAST WEEK'S AFRO POOR CONDITIONS ADMITTED BOYS HAVE TO WORK OUT BECAUSE STATE DOES NOT PROVIDE SUFFICIENT FUNDS
full_text: CHELTENHAM NOT AN IDEAL SPOT E. B. Taylor, Members of Board of Managers, An- swers Editorial in Last Week's Afro POOR CONDITIONS ADMITTED Boys Have To Work Out Because State Does Not Provide Sufficient Funds The House of Correction at Chel- tenham is not an ideal institution any more than the Colored High School, declared E. B. Taylor, mem- ber of the Board of Managers in reply to an editorial in last week's AFRO-AMERICAN. The editorial commented on the lack of any useful educational sys- tem at Cheltenham, the convict plan of leasing boys to work on adjoining farms, and ended by declaring: Two colored members are on the Board of Managers of Cheltenham. No part of the criticism appears to have touched them sufficiently to make a public statement. Is their silence an evidence of their satis- faction with conditions at Chelton- ham? HAS NOT KEPT SILENT Denying that he had kept silent or was satisfied with Cheltenham, Mr. Taylor pointed out that he had recently printed an article in the AFRO-AMERICAN concerning condi- tions there and therefore could not be accused of keeping silent. "I admit," Mr. Taylor said "that only two trades are taught at the House of Correction, that the boys are hired out to work on adjacent farms, and that the schooling does not proper them for good citizen- ship when they leave, but the fault lies with the colored people them- selves. They should be behind the members on the Board, and should appeal to the Governor and other State authorities to give Chelten- ham an appropriation large enough to operate them properly. "If we did not hire boys out to work, there would not be sufficient money with which to run the insti- tution. BOARD INVESTIGATED "A letter in the AFRO-AMERICAN by James Ireland declaring that the boys were mistreated by Superin- tendent John B. Pyles, that he lay in the shade neglecting his duties and compelled boys to fan him, and that one of the boys had killed an- other, was investigated by our Board which spent a day on the grounds. The AFRO-AMERICAN was invited to this investigation." When informed that the AFRO- AMERICAN knew nothing of an in- vestigation and was hearing of it for the first time, Mr. Taylor said he understood from President Janney of the Board that the AFRO-AMERICAN had been asked to be present if it wished. Mr. Taylor said that the investiga- tion proved that the murder was committed and the boy was public- ly tried for the offense. No one could be found, however, who would sub- stantiate charges against the super- intendent. "Anyone," he said, "can claim that there is mistreatment of boys and shad food at Cheltenham, but unless he is willing to come before the Board and press his charges, his claims are worthless. UP TO COURTS Asked to comment upon the pres- ent conditions by which white chil- dren may be committed to institu- tions for 20 or 60 days while all colored children are committed to Cheltenham until they become 21 years of age, Mr. Taylor said this matter is beyond the province of the Board. "It is up to the Courts," he said. "If you want these things changed, it is up to the people to ask judges not to send boys to Chel- tenham until conditions are like you would have them." title: FORUM: ""THE AND CHELTENHAM, TWO DIFFERENT INSTITUTIONS.
full_text: FORUM "The Cat" and Cheltenham, Two Different Institutions.. To the Editor: January 18th, 1913. I would be obliged if you will cor- rect in your next issue the error which appeared in today's paper naming "Warden Lankford" as "sup- erintendent at Cheltenham in Anne Arundel Co." Mr. Lankford is not a warden a: Cheltenham, which, if you mean the House of Reformation for Colored Boys, is not in Anne Arundel County, and has no warden. The reformatory at Cheltenham has the reputation of being a model: for similar institutions in various other parts of the country, as one of the best and most humanely managed semi-penal institutions in the states, and one of the only two containing colored inmates, which has colored representatives on the board. Each Grand Jury has taken occasion to commend its efficient management. Thanking you in advance for mak- ing this correction of an error which I am sure was an inadvertance on your part. in your otherwise very accurate paper, I am, Very truly yours. Ε. Β. TAYLOR Representing the City of Baltime on the Board of Managers, House of Correction. Cheltenham, Prince George's Co., Md. TAKES SLAP AT MINISTER WHO PROPOSED SLATE 1426 Ward St., Baltimore, MC. Mr. Editor of the Afro-American: I read an article entitled "Minister Proposes Siate." The said minister is not a member of the Baltimor Conference. Two years ago he trans ferred to the Philadelphia Conier- ence. He may have his transfer on the road to the Baltimore Conferenc if so he is not a member of any ast yet. He is standing on the outside! between the two conferences. We resent such an article coming from an outsider. We men whe have beeni in this conference for 25. 30 and 4 years do not want instruction from: the Rev.. Mr. Armstrong. Let hin prove himself in our church and con- ference before he undertakes to telli us what to do. I have been traveling in the Baltimore Conference for 28 years, and I would not dare to dic- tate to the Conference who they snould elect to the General Confer- ence. I pray if Rev. Mr. Armstrong has his transfer in his pocket. he will wait until he gets back in the Balti- more: Conference and received by the Conference and then, prove himself. worthy to remaining it. REV.J.R., NELSON. 1426 Ward St, Baltimore : Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: JUDGE SCORES PROCEDURE IN JUVENILE COURT: COMMITTING YOUTHFUL OFFENDERS FOR LONG TERMS ON TECHNICAL CHARGES TERMED OUTRAGEOUS BOY FOUND NOT GUILTY RICHARD JENKINS FREED FROM CHELTENHAM BY CRIMINAL COURT JUDGE
full_text: JUDGE SCORES PROCEDURE IN JUVENILE COURT Committing Youthful Of P fenders For Long Terms On Technical Charges Termed Outrageous BOY FOUND NOT GUILTY Richard Jenkins Freed From Cheltenham By a Criminal Court Judge In rendering a decision of not guilty in the case of Richard Jenkins, age 16, charged in the Ju- venile Court of being a minor with- out proper care, committed to Chel- tenham, and brought before him on appeal, Judge Stanton violently1 scored the practice of the Juvenile Court in sending boys to these in-s stitutions for long terms when they have committed trivial offenses. Richard Jenkins and a number of boys engaged in a fight in which one of the boys was cut with a pen knife. He was tried in the Ju- venile Court and committed Cheltenham but through Atty. Roy S. Bond his case was appealed, and when the testinzony was presented Judge Stanton stated that the practice of twisting these charges to fit the jurisdiction of the Ju- venile Court and incarcerating these boys for long terms was an outrage. It was pointed out that the shortest term that a boy could serve when sent to one of these institu- tions was 17 months, and that in many cases they had to stay until they were twenty-one, and coming into the criminal court with past crime records and cominitting much greater offenses got sentences for 30 and 60 days. men There was another case recently in the courts where a man and a boy were charged jointly with lar- cony. The boy was tried in the Juvenile Court and committed to Cheltenham indetinitel while the man was given a 30-day sentence, The fault lies not in the adminis- tration of the Juvenile Court, it is declared, but in the construction of the law governing its power and jurisdiction. The Juvenile Court can only commit boys to these institu- tions on the technical charge of their being minors without proper care. This blanket charge is applied to every kind of charge coming into the court and the general rule is to send offenders up until they are of age, however trivial the charge. The more fortunate boys, however, are sometimes placed in homes or other- wise paroled. ( 1 i 1 1 This law seems to have been made especially in interest of these insti- tutions and without regard to the . best interest of the boy or girl, : The whole question of dealing with juvenile offenders should receive more consideration from leaders in religion, education and general wel- 1, fare, it was pointed out. title: Took Boys to Omaha
full_text: Took Boys to Omaha Miss Anita Williams, social worker with the local Catholic Welfare Organizations, returned Sunday from Omaha, Neh., where she went to take three hoys to the Father Funnagin's Home for Boys. The boys ranging in ages from eight to 12 were formerly inmates of the St. Elizabeth Home and were sent to the Nebraska insti- tution to avoid having them go to Cheltenham. Miss Williams pointed out the need for an institution nearer here for the proper care of this class of boys. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: LATEST NEWS: SUES COP FOR THIRD DEGREE
full_text: LATEST NEWS SUES COP FOR THIRD DEGREE Bottlesville, Okla., Dec. 20.----Found innocent of the larceny of $400, Roy Burton, hotel chef, has filed suit for $50,000 against chief of police and his assistants alleging ☐ that he was arrested without a warrant, thrown into jail, ☐and subjected to severe beatings in order to make him "confess." INSURES LIFE FOR SCHOOLS Tuskegee; Ala., Dec. 20. - Dr. John A. Kenney, head of Tuskegee Institute Hospital, has taken out a $1,000 policy made payable jointly to Howard University and Meharryi : Medical School. His idea, he said, is a challenge to 5,000 other, professional men also to give these colleges $5,000,000 endowments. I 1 (' f WILL PROBE $10,000 SHORTAGE Newark, N. J., Dec. 20.- Admitting that a sufficient detail of police at the Howard-Lincoln football game held in Philadelphia Thanksgiving Day was not requested in time to handle the game, Dr. W. G. Alexander, graduate manager of Lincoln, told the AFRO-AMERICAN he is after facts upon which to base a rigid investigation of the alleged $10,000 shortage in the receipts of the game. FLOGGING CHARGED AT CHELTENH'M Complaints received here from the boys at the House of Reformation at Cheltenham indicate that the floggings 1. are still being administered. The punishment is from twen- ty to twenty-five blows on the naked body. A teacher in s dormitory two is said to have locked up and flogged one • boy every day recently. j Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: COLORED TEACHERS FOR CHELTENHAM: REV. GEO. F. BRAGG, MEMBER OF BOARD, SAYS THIS CHANGE SHOULD BE MADE EFFORT BEGUN IN 1903 TWO COLORED TEACHERS SENT THERE DID REMARKABLE WORK
full_text: COLORED TEACHERS FOR CHELTENHAM Rev. Geo. F. Bragg, Member of Board, Says This Change Should Be Made EFFORT BEGUN IN 1903 Two Colored Teachers Sent There Did Remarkable Work The first and most important change which should be made at the House of Reformation for Boys at Cheltenham is to give the school colored teachers. Rev. Dr. George F.Bragg, Jr., a member of the Board made this statemem to the Afro-American today. He also declared that a State like Maryland which is com- mitted to the policy of separate colored schools and colored teach- ers should see that the policy is also carried om at Cheltenham. This step might easily be brought about he said providing represen- tative persons make this request of Mayor Jackson and Governor Ritchie. Rev. Dr. Braggs state- ment follows: 20 YEARS ON BOARD I was elected a member of the Board of Managers of the House of Reformation in 1901, and have been re-elected every year since that time. From the very begin- ning I have been under the convic- tion that the several schools there should be taught by colored per- sons. Through my persistency in the matter, 20 years ago, a beginning was made in that direction. Gen. Shryock strongly favored it. I am sending herewith an extract from a personal letter received by me from Mrs. Ance E. Mitchell a col- ored body whom I secured as teacher. Mr. Mitchell, her husband, was placed in charge of the chair caning department. The contents of the letter are most suggestive of the present, notwithstanding the letter was written 20 years ago. The date of the letter is Sept. 5, 1903. NEW SUPERINTENDENT In part it says: "Today's Afro-American states your return: I hope your vacation has been both pleasurable and profitable. Of course, you are aware of the changes. Mr. Pyles is now acting Superintendent. The boys are even now under better control, Gen. Shryock, having been down and one evening called all the boys together and grandly and firmly, announced his intention of running the affairs of Cheltenham, laid down the rules to all officers and boys, and already good results are attained. There are now two schools, with the larger boys, and two with the small boys. Gen. Shryock recommended sort of segregation. I have now the smallest boys, to in number, with the exception of a few retained helpers, so as not to cripple the work. "There had been a noticeable degree of improvement in my school, as a whole. Or within the past two months. Am pleased to undertake the helping of the smallest boys before they become hardened. I find many of them impressionable, and fit months for their intellectual moral and physical advancement. There is a whole chapter of happenings, I will not weary you with detail, but be- ing fully aware of your loyalty and struck for the right preaching of our race being an over-prejudiced people, I wait merely relate to you the parting of Mr. Dorsey had resigned with us. Superintendent. "Extending his hand, and bidding both "good-bye", Mr. Dorsey said: "I wish you in all your future efforts. I must say you have done much for source: you, surely, have raised my estimation of your race, and aroused in me a hope for them that I never had before. I am glad to have known you, and to know that in your care are such people." My reply was "Thank you, Mr. Dorsey, but you probably have been most unfortunate in your con- tact with your race. Among us are many superior to me, farther advanced, in every way, I came here merely do my duty al- ways. CONFESSION OF DISTRUST "Thus ran a conversation that was alike pleasing and sure surprising, with its con- fession of distrust of our race, and appar- ent conversion to behalf and hope. I said to John (her husband) our coming has been worth while: that we have lived to see and hear these words, uninvited, from Mr. Dorsey's lips. God surely is with us, and here is one reward for faith- fulness to His laws. Mr. and Mrs. Dor- sey made us a friendly call before leaving." MAYOR HAYES PLEASED I think the above letter is worthy of re-production at this time. In company with Mayor Hayes, I as well as others, visited Mrs. Mitchell's school, and the Mayor was specially pleased and frankly and heartily expressed himself in favor of colored teach- ers for these boys. From the very first I advocated such a change and all during my membership on the board have never lost an op- portunity to urge the same. That is the one thing in connection with this institution that should be done. As for Mr. Pyles I have nothing but words of praise. I think, un- der all the circumstances, he has bravely striven to do his duty. But it is a situation, and not a theory, which confronts us, and I can con- ceive of no good reason for the re- tention of white teachers in the schools of that institution. Mr. and Mrs. Mitchell did splen- did work, but after some years of service, the isolation was too great for them. Such would not be the case if ALL the teachers were of the same race as the boys of that institution. The boys would be brought into the life of the best of their own race, and, reflecting the same, would be transformed into the same likeness. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: FORUM: ""THE CUT"" AND CHELTENHAM, TWO DIFFERENT
full_text: FORUM "The Cut" and Cheltenham, Two Different Institutions. To the Editor: January 18th, 1919. I would be obliged if you will cor- rect in your next issue the error which appeared in today's paper naming "Warden Lankford" as "sup- erintendent at Cheltenham in Anne Arundel Co." Mr. Lankford is not a warden at Cheltenham, which, if you mean the House of Reformation for Colored Boys, is not in Anne Arundel County, and has n warden. The reformatory at Cheltenham has the reputation of being a'model for similar institutions in various other parts of the country, as one of the best and most humanely managed semi-penal institutions in the states, and one of the only two containing colored inm tes, which has colored representatives on the board. Each Grand Jury has taken occasion to commend its efficient management. Thanking you in advance for mak- ing this correction of an error which I am sure was an inadvertance on your part, in your otherwise very accurate paper, I am, Very truly yours, E. B. TAYLOR Representing the City of Baltimore on the Board of Managers, House of Correction, Cheltenham, Prince George's Co., Md. TAKES SLAP AT MINISTER WHO PROPOSED SLATE 1426 Ward St., Baltimore, Md. Mr. Editor of the Afro-American: I read an article entitled "Minister! Proposes Slate." The said minister is not a member of the Baltimore Conference. Two years ago he trans. ferred to the Philadelphia Confer- ence. He may have his transfer on the road to the Baltimore Conference if so he is not a member of any as yet. He is standing on the outside between the LWO conferences. We resent such an article coming from an outsider. We men who have been in this conference for 25, 30 and 40 years do not want instruction from the Rev. Mr. Armstrong. Let hin: prove himself in our church and con- ference before he undertakes to tell us what to do. I have been traveling in the Baltimore Conference for 28 years, and I would not dare to dic- tate to the Conference who they should elect to the General Confer- ence. I pray if Rev. Mr. Armstrong has his transfer in his pocket, he will wait until he gets back in the Balti- more Conference and received by.the Conference and then prove himself Worthy to remain in it. REYJ. RANELSON, 1426 Ward St., Baltimore

title: Jamaicans Arrive: Seven Young Women Here To Seek Their Fortunes
full_text: Jamaicans Arrive Seven Young Women Here To Seck Their Fortunes. Seven young women from Jamaica were passengers on the Norwegian steamer Machioneal. which arrived in port last Friday with a cargo of fruit. They were Misses Esther McEwan, Gertrude Carmen Tafe, Florence Evelinia Heightington, Una Gertrude Scott, Minna Sutherland William- son. Edna May Atkinson and Doris Isabell Atkinson. Miss. Taffe will enter a higher school in this country. Miss Doris Sutherland went to Boston and Miss Taffe to Montclair, N. J. The others went to New York city. Miss Doris Isabel Atkinson gave her occupation as an accountant, and the remainder, except Miss Taffe, said they were dressmakers. : Ice Boat Latrobe Carries 600 About 600 pupils of the High School and city teachers lof the elementary schools together with the pupilsof the Training School were the guests of the City on a three hour tour around the harbor last Saturday. Charles. Koch, white, Superintendent of Schools, explained the points of interest to the excurionists. Insurance Company Has Novel Stunt Myersdale. Pa., July 1.-A local insurance company which had in- sured the Sheppherd's car,wrecked by falling over a sixty foot lem- bankment. had the car fixed in fohnstown, Pa., and returned it here, as good as new, with this sign on, "This is the Sheppherd's car of Myersdale. Pa.. that was wrecked here June 20, 1920. It is a Hud- son. Insure your car." Released From Cheltenham Through habeas proceedings in- stituted by Attorney W. Norman Bishop, Seymour Diggs, a 15-year old boy, living at 1619 Jefferson, was ordered released from the Colored House of Reformation by Judge Gorter, in City Court, on Friday of last week.

title: BISHOP HURST SCORES HIRING OF CONVICTS: FLORIDA HAS STOPPED IT, MARYLAND KEEPS IT UP AT CHELTENHAM OFFICIALS ADMIT LAXITY CITY AUTHORITIES HAVE NOT VISITED PLACE IN TEN YEARS THEY SAY
full_text: BISHOP HURST el SCORES HIRING OF CONVICTS 1- Florida Has Stopped it, Ma- d : 1- L e - ryland Keeps It Up At Cheltenham OFFICIALS ADMIT LAXITY a City Authorities Have Not e Visited Place In Ten Years n it m They Say "We have stopped hiring out con. 1- victs in Florida, but we keep it up ir in Maryland," declared Bishop John Hurst, referring to the practice of hiring out boys sent to the House of Reformation at Cheltenham, Bishop Hurst led a delegation be. fore Nathaniel Grasty, superintendent of City charities and Senator M. Og. den, who has charge of State appro priations in the Hoen Building, Thurs. day. Bishop Hurst pointed out that in the last two years Baltimore Grand Juries in their reports have disclosed the following facts concerning Chel. ten ham: (1) Lack of schooling. Inmates re- ceive less than two months' instruc- tion each year from undoubtful and uncertified teachers. (2) Lack of facilities for instruc- tion in trades. Except for the broom shop, the 330 inmates get little real training that will serve them in after life. (3) Lashing is administered by the Superintendent or the guard who use leather straps. canes or heayy sitcks. (4) Lack of sanitary arrangements, Inmates use the same wash cloths and towels; clothing ragged and no evidence that bath tubs are frequent. ly used. (5) Superintendent John B. Pyles, after 30 years service is too old and ought to be retired. REFORMS PROMISED Both Senator Odgen and Mr. Gras. ty, who hold the city and State ap- propriations for Cheltenham, promised Immediate relief of ills complained of The delegation then decided not tto visit Mayor Jackson but to leave the matter with them. Mr. Grasty confessed to the dele- gation that, although he is responsi- ble for paying over every year the city's appropriation of $38,000 for the support of Cheltenham, he had not visited the institution for the past ten years. He admitted also, that he had heard frequent compiaints about it. Senator Odgen declared he had nev- er seen reports of the Baltimore Grand Jurles telling of conditions of Cheltenham and asked that they be laid before him. title: NINE YAR OLD BOY SENTENCED TO CHELTENHAM: FRANK ONLEY WHO ESCAPED FROM MANY HOMES IS SAFELY HOUSED AT LAST FOUND THRU AFRO SISTER OF ST. ELIZABETH'S RECOGNIZES NAME OF RUNAWAY HELD AT STATION HOUSE
full_text: NINE YEAR OLD BOY SENTENCED TO CHELTENHAM Frank Onley Who Escaped From Many Homes Is Safely Housed At Last FOUND THRU AFRO Sister Of St. Elizabeth's Re- cognizes Name Of Run- away Held At Station House Nine-year-old Frank Onley is an elusive chap, but he is safe now-in the Colored House of Reformation at Cheltenham, Judge Williams, of the Juvenile Court, having sen- tenced him to that institution on Tuesday. The little fellow, who is described as a "bad boy with a wanderlust," was taken from St. Eliabeth's Home and adopted by Mr. and Mrs. William Davenport, five months ago. He was given a good homel with two other children adopted by the Davenports and sent to School 109, Fremont avenue and King street. He played truant and cut up other didoes and Mrs. Daven- port sent him back to St. Eliza- beth's Home several weeks ago. He was left in the office of one of the sisters, but escaped when her back was turned. the Descriptions were sent out broad cast, but he cluded poilce. He slept under steps, begged pennies and people gave him food. The police of the West- ern District apprehended him last Friday, and he was taken to the Juvenile Court, were he gave a fic- titious name and told the officials that he had been brought here from te country by his parents and gotten lost from them. He was committed to St. Mary's Home, Presstman and Gilmor street. One of the sisters there picked up a copy of the ARFO-AMERICAN last week and read where a boy called Frank Davenport had es- caped from St. Mary's Home. Despite the fact that the boy de- nied that he was the one, one of the sisters went to the home of the Davenports at 298 N. Arlington avenue and ascertained that their ward was the boy wanted. On his promise to do better the boy was turned over to Mrs. Da- venport's brother, John T. Giles, Monday, and he took him to her home. He was washed up, given a change of clothing and a good meal. He was allowed to sit on the front steps with another boy. Tiring of this, he asked to be al- lowed to go into the yard and ran out of the gate. Mrs. Davenport gave chase, and after running four blocks after the boy the police caught him. He tugged and tried to get away, but was taken back home. He was made to put on girl's clothes and placed in an up- stairs room. At night he was taken 10 the Southwestern Police Station, and on Tuesday his sentence to Cheltenham followed. ! Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Jury Frees Boy Tried For Cellmate's Death
full_text: Jury Frees Boy Tried For Cellmate's Death Upper MarYpro, Aug. 10-Stan- Jey Jackson, an inmate, of the House of Reformation for Colored Boys at Cheltenham, Prince Georges County, ws acquitted by a jury of the charge of murdering William Jones, another inmate, at the special session of the Circuit Court here today. During a haz- ing party staged in the cell Jack- son was alleged to have struck Jones over the head with a bucket. Robert W. Wells, defending Jackson, contended that the boys had been confined to the cell for about twenty days and that the condition of Jones, owing to lack of proper food, was the cause of his death. title: CHELTENHAM BROOM FACTORY IS UNDER FIRE: LABOR LEADERSSAY BOYS ARE KEPT BUSY AT EMPLOYMENT THAT IS OF NO USE TO THEM AFTERWARDS EXPLOITATION CHARGED GOV. RITCHIE AND MAYOR JACKSON TO ABOLISH BROOM FACTORY ALTOGETHER
full_text: CHELTENHAM BROOM FACTORY IS UNDER. FIRE Labor Leaders Say Boys Are Kept Busy at Employment That Is of No Use to Them Afterwards பg. EXPLOITATION CHARGED Gov. Ritchie and Mayor Jackson b.ed to Abolish Broom Factory Altogether Local authorities are inves- tigating the case of Earl Crow dy, an inmate of the Maryland House of Reformation at Chel- tenham, said to have been brutally beaten gourds and sent to the hospital to have his wonds dressed. by three V () 1: h A Charging that the broom factory at the House of Reformation for colored boys at Cheltenham is un- fair competition, exploits boys sent there and keeps them from getting any schooling, and employs them at an occupation they cannot use in after life, leading labor officials in a letter to Mayor Jackson and Governor Ritchie urged that the factory be abolished. The statement is signed by Sam- uel Compers head of the A. F. L.. Henry F. Broening, head of the State Federation of Labor, Chas Wiegand, of the, City Federation I of Labor and Will Boyer, secretary s of the International Broommakers Union. title: Only A Few Exceptions
full_text: Only A Few Exceptions Young man don't swear. There is no occasion for it outside of a printing office where it is useful when the paper is behind time. It also comes in handy in proof-reading and is "indispensable" when the ink works bad and the press begins to "buck". It is some- times brought into use when the foreman's mad; and it has been known to entirely remove the tired feeling of the editor when he looks over the paper after it has been printed. ..Outside the printing office it is a foolish habit. * Morton's Cunning * Ferdinand Q. Morton, New York's colored Tammany leader, is the kind of politician who ought to supersede the "Hat-in-Hand" politicians who have infested Capitol Hill for the past quarter of a century. Democrats wanted Mr. Morton to make a speech here in the recent mayoralty campaign in order to get the colored vofers into line to support the Democratic candi- dates. The Tammany leader did not ask "What will I m get out of it," but "What will the colored people of Balti-b more get out of it, if they vote for the Democratic can-fir Adidates?" pe a ga White Democratic leaders were mum. And so Mr. ton stayed in New York, where Tammny believes in sto arding the faithful, and where Charley Murphy does draw the political color-line. *** * Why Cheltingham? * * of ar J: th lo 11 ווז The Prisoner's Aid Society is investigating the brutal h ping ng of an inmate of the House of Reformation at st tenham by three guards. Samuel Gompers, in a letter to Governor Ritchie and Mayor Jackson, declares that the system of hiring boys out as convict labor to private contractors is not in vogue in other enlightened p States and ought to be abolished in Maryland. CL SC 15 Cheltenham is a disgrace to the State. Long has it been run for the benefit of those who draw salaries or do get contracts out of it rather than for the benefit of the he inmates. Instead of sending under-aged youths to school, te it hires them out to private contractors and surrounding farmers. fr 11 No matter what it's motives, the American Federa- tion of Labor deserves the thanks of every right-thinking in citizen for its stand on the convict lease system in vogue there. It brings again to the fore the question of why st the taxpayers' money must be used to keep alive an insti- tution like Cheltenham. * ** * Baltimore Trains Them * * At the rate at which teachers in the Colored High School are appointed and resign, it is estimated that the entire faculty is changed every ten years. h 1 to W ro a tr Graduates from colleges and normal schools come to Baltimore and are appointed to the High School with-it out an examination or any previous experience, get a year or two of training and then go off to better positions in Washington or St. Louis or some of the Southern uni- versities. The resignation of Mr. William Brewer to accept a position in the Dunbar school in Washington is the third this year. Previously, Miss Ora Weaver and Mr. Arthur Turner left this year, preferring to work in Washington. Three other teachers may resign before Christmas. fr 10 h 8 ( a In the past ten years Baltimore has trained at least a dozen teachers who have gone into Washington schools 2 as experienced and capable instructors. Some of the teachers leave for higher pay, others to get rid of the depressing atmosphere of the High School here. Perhaps the School Board is willing to have the High School hold on to its reputation as a training centre for other high schools, but are the parents also willing? * * Garvey At Liberty * * * * 1 1- j Marcus Garvey, head of the Universal Negro Im-5 provement Association and the Black Star Steamship e, is free again. Last week he walked out of the Tombs prison pend- ing the hearing of his appeal in a higher United States court. Three months he has been imprisoned, part of his sentence of five years in Atlanta and $1,000 for using the mails to defraud P r Before his arrest, there was not in America a more skillful financier or a more eloquent one. He had more ways of taking in people's money and holding it than wise old New York ever heard of. Banks, steamship lines, laundries, chain-stores, funds of many kinds and r classes were used by Mr. Garvey to gather in the "bits of silver." Halted in one line, he turned quickly to another, until the long arm of the law reached out for a od stay. The strange part about it is that Mr. Garvey's moral sense does not allow him to admit that he is in error. His arrest, he claims. is due to the hostility of enemies or rival associations. His conviction came as the result of "spite-work," he is a "martyr." Maybe a term in Atlanta will change Mr. Garvey's ideas about collecting money illegally, but certainly three months in the Tombs has not. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: The FORUM
full_text: The FORUM Is The Democratic Party An En- emy of The Colored Schools? Rev. Geo. F. Bragg Asks This Question And Answers It  Mr. Editor, when you were a small boy, in 1895, we had an important election in this city and State. By the most solid vote of Negroes, and thousands of reputable Democrats, a Republican was elected Governor, and a Republican was elected May- The Legislature was Republi- can, and the City Council was Re- publican. The city School Board was also Republican. The colored group asked of the Republican Legislature the found- ing of a state institution, similar to Tuskegee, for Colored People of Maryland. The bill was introdue- ed, and had a chance of passing: but our Republican governor, and his Board of State Education, de- feated it, on the ground, "there is no pressing necessity" for the same. In the City Council, in 1896, the late Dr. J. Marcus Cargill, intro- duced a measure to "eliminate" white teachers from our colored schools. After a Republican City Council had amended nearly all the life out of the bill it finally passed. In the shape in which it passed, if honestly and fairly enforced, the "elimination" would have been ex- ceedingly slow. But, by actual sub- terfuge, with a Republican Mayor, School Board, and Council, every advantage was taken and the mat- ter of elimination delayed Pressure and threats, were brot to bear upon our white Republicans: and, tawo years after the bill had passed the first beginning in the matter of "elimination" was initi- ated, during the fall of 1898, when Henry Grafton Brown, and Miss Nannie Groomes were put in charge of the East Street School, in East Baltimore. Now, as a contrast with the above. In the fall of 1899, Thomas Gordon Hayes, a Democrat, was elected Mayor over W. T. Malster. Despite his infidelity in the matter, Malster received, practically. the united support of the Colored Poeple. Hay- es was under no political obligation to the Colored Race. But soon after his inauguration the. "New chart- er" went into effect. A new School Board, of nine persons, was ap- pointed by Mr. Hayes, and it was this School Board appointed by the Democratic Mayor which wrought a radical change in the Colored Schools. It was this board that gave us Prof. Hugh Brown, and an entire colored faculty in our High School. Mr. Hooper, as Mayor, had the opportunit to appoint a colored man on the Cheltenham Board: but. did not do it. Mr. Malster had the same opportunity, but appointed two white men; and when his at- tention was called to the matter he promised to do better next time. But one of his appointees declined, and he immediately appointed an- other white man, a Mr. Doyle, a whiskey dealer. Now for the contrast, Thomas G. Hayes, a Democratic Mayor, was the first of the Mayors of Balti- more City to make such an appoint- ment. He appointed Gen. Shry- rock and Harry S. Cummings to represent the city of Baltimore on that Board. Timanus, Republi-1 can Mayor, made no appointments of a colored man on the board of that institution. Preston followed the example of the Democratic Hayes, and re-ap-1 pointed Shryrock and Cummings.( Upon the death of these gentlemen, 1 he appointed as their successors.: former Gov. Goldsborough and E. B. Taylor. The late Republican 1 Mayor, following the examples of his predecessors, made no Negro( appointments. I have simply re-1 cited facts. These facts carry their 1 own comment. George F. Bragg, Jr.

title: PROFESSOR FUDGE: THE KICK ON JAZZ MUSIC
full_text: 3 > PROFESSOR FUDGE The Kick On Jazz Music In the White Press there has always been for sometime a sub- dued kick on jazz mus sic. Some have said it was immoral, while others claimed it has no merit whatsoever Nobody pays any Pattention to the kicks, to the contrary, peo ple all over the coun- try are still putting on a shuffle whenever the jazz is heard. It is likely the only rea- son for the complaint is its African origin Everything African or of African descent in this country is be. ing continually kicked on. The Af- rican Chiefs have been squawked out of most all first class Hotels The African Jockeys have been jockeyed off of most race tracks, and in fact, most everything with a streak of African gets the low down" put on it. However, there is always someone to take the joys out of any situation. Just when all the leading musicians in the country. have decided jazz should be prohibited, just as wwhis- key is, along comes a man from Rus- sia who says, so far as he is con- cerned "it listens good." The Boston Symphony As many doubtless know, the Eos- ton Symphony Orchestra is one of the classiest musical organizations in this country. It has had as its di- rector many famous European Mas- ters of Music.. One of its leaders during the war, refused to have the An Important Question t The question of the hour is, shall! we elect Coolidge, Davis or La Fol- lette for President? There are two sides to every question, to this par ticular question there are three. It is claimed Mr. Coolidge as the rep- resentative of the Republican party is the safest man of them all be- cause he believes "silence is gold-1 en." On the other hand, Bishop Hûrst? says. "Down with Coolidge." because? he will not open up on important issues that affect us most, and the Bishop's advice must the considered. ( Then some people claim Coolidge belongs to the Ku Klux Klan and cum his lovandy what sie even will tell privately the hood he wears. Mr. John W. Davis next on the list, is the standard? bearer of the Democratic Party. Mr.I Davis is by far the best looking man 1 of the three, is more than likely the most able of the three, and then his aunt or some relative of his used to live in South Baltimore, which is C an excellent reason for him getting support in this locality. The argu- ment against him brings in the ques- tion of the Southern Democrats. There are people who claim that with all of his influence, his good looks and his ability he will never be able to affect the malignant schemes aimed at the Negroes of the United States by the Southern element. Last Comes La Follette t 2 ε 6 1 I H Lastly we have Senator La Fol- E lette, whose platform has everything t in it not contained in the other two a platforms. Senator La Follette be- lieves in all men up and no men down. He promises to revolution-t Star Spangled Banner on his pro-ize everything and to cure all our gram.. he heing a German For be- Ills: With me his best argument is ing so unpatriotic the American pub- lic at once denied he ever was a competent director, recalled all of the laurels formerly hung about his shoulders and had him "canned." title: Grand Jury Report Hits Celtenliam Reformatory: GRAND JURY URGES CURB ON FLOGGINGS AT CHELTENHAM Body In Report Recommends That Whippings Be Permitted Only in Superintendent's Presence POOR SCHOOLING THERE Boys Said To Be Sent To School Rooms Only When Weather Is Inclement Outside
full_text: Grand Jury Report Hits Cheltenham Reformatory GRAND JURY URGES CURB ON FLOGGINGS AT CHELTENHAM Body In Report Recommends That Whippings Be Per- mitted Only in Super- intendent's Presence POOR SCHOOLING THERE Boys Said To Be Sent To School Rooms Only When Weather Is Inclement Outside Recommendations that would stop indiscriminate flogging of inmates of the House of Reformation at Cheltenham, Prince George's County and would increase the length of the t school term were contained in the report of the Grand Jury submitted to Judge Stein last week. The report urges specifically that in cases where punishment is met ed out, it be done only by the super- intendent, or in his presence. This action followed an investigation in-t to the brutal beating administered Earl Crowdy by one of the guards t at Cheltenham last fall. The boy's knee was injured and he spent over a month in the hospital. Poor educational facilities at Cheltenham are also scored in the report, which makes public the fact that juvenile delinquents sent there from the city and counties, are only f sent to school when the weather in inclement outside. In other state in- stitutions for whites, boys get from two to three hours and more school ing per day. Except in the dead of f winter, no classes at all are held at Cheltenham. The report declares that the institution already has suf- ficient calls rooms and should seek the assistance of the State in secur- ing competent teachers. Tuberculosis Patient Not Isolated i 11 Isolation of a patient at the insti tution who is suffering with tuber- culosis is also recommended by he report, which urges that such pa- tients in the future be segregated that there will be no danger of in-t fecting others or be sent to the hos- pital at Henryton. The use of a com-v mon towel for bathing by the boys whereby skin diseases may le spread is called attention to and individual towels for each boy are urged, Convict System Referred To. The conviet system of hiring out boys committed to Cheltenham to surrounding farmers is mentioned in the report, which states, "the man- agement should be relieved of the necessity of hiring boys out and the handicap of the lack of funds." Su- perintendent Pyles recently declared that he was forced to hire boys out to neighboring farmers because the State did not appropriate enough funds to run the institution without outside aid. Of the 300 boys in Cheltenham at present, 200 of them are from Baltimore City. No Mention of Cruelties. 1 The Grand Jury report declares that buildings and grounds were well kept and the inmates of appear- ent good health, with the exception of one tuberculosis patient. Nol mention was made of the cruel prac tice of compelling Loys in squads of eight and ten under the direction of a larger boy to clean sticks, leaves! and other rubbish from the immense grounds with their bare hands, 1 According to inmates, no rakes are, provided the boys, who go over every! inch of the grounds around the d buildings on hands and knees and f using their fingers as rakes remove every leaf, pebble and stick, which is clawed in a pile and later put in a bag held by the older student. Parents of inmates have asked the question, why boys have the time to rake the lawn, with their fingers, when it could be done in a few hours with rakes and the boys' time be spent in learning to read and write instead. Mental Tests For Melvale The grand jury report on the In- dustrial School for Girls at Melvale, states that the larger portion of the 99 inmates there are employed in the sewing room on power machines. The segregation of inmates who are run down, predisposed toward tuber- culosis, or affected with venereal disease is urged, and the abolish- ment of the community towel for bathing purposes. 1 According to the report, the city! school system should be asked to co- operate with the Institution in mak- ing mental tests of all inmates so that those who attend school might be properly graded, and the large number of mental defectives be placed in classes by themselves. Erection of a portable building for recreation purposes is also recom- mended. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: CHELTENHAM BROOM FACTORY IS UNDER FIRE: LABOR LEADERS SAY BOYS ARE KEPT BUSY AT EMPLOYMENT THAT IS OF NO USE TO THEM AFTERWARDS EXPLOITATION CHARGED GOV. RITCHIE AND MAYOR JACKSON URGED TO ABOLISH BROOM FACTORY ALTOGETHER
full_text: CHELTENHAM BROOM FACTORY IS UNDER FIRE Labor Leaders Say Boys Are Kept Busy at Employment That Is of No Use to Them Afterwards EXPLOITATION CHARGED Gov. Ritchie and Mayor Jackson Urged to Abolish Broom Factory Altogether Local authorities are inves- tigating the case of Earl Crow dy, an inmate of the Maryland House of Reformation at Chel- tenham, sald to have been brutally beaten by three guards and sent to the hospital to have his wounds dressed. Charging that the broom factory at the House of Reformation for colored boys at Cheltenham is un- fair competition, exploits boys sent there and keeps them from getting any schooling, and employs them at an occupation they cannot use in after life, leading labor officials in a letter to Mayor Jackson and Governor Ritchie urged that the factory be abolished. The statement is signed by Sam- uel Compers head of the A. F. L... Henry F. Broening, head of the State Federation of Labor, Chas Wiegand, of the City Federation of Labor and Will Boyer, secretary of the International Broommakers Union. These officials interviewed city and State officers some time ago and were advised to submit their petition in writing. Labor officials charge that the institution is exploiting the boys by hiring out 82 of them the entire year to Ripple Brothers for $4.916.50. This firm aiso gets its heat, light, power and rent free! whereby it 1200 brooms a day at a labor cost of 16 cents a dozen while free labor plants in Baltimore pay $1.50 per dozen to workers. Boys are being kept out of school in order to work in the Cheltenham broom factory says the petition and adds that these boys should be given an education and taught to work at some em- ployment which will enable them to earn a livelihood when discharg ed from the institution. Ripple Brothers operate a free labor fac- tory in Baltimore and not one of the boys learning at the reforma- tory is given employment there, Modern institutions of this cha- acter do not farm out their labor to private contractors, concludes the petition and this method should be abolished by the House of Reformation for Colored Boys. 10 turns o Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Grand Jury Report on House of Reformation at Cheltenham
full_text: Grand Jury Report on House of Reformation at Chelt- enham The Committee on the Maryland House of Reformation for Colored Boys at Cheltenham, visited that In- stitution on November 16th, and was 3 cordially received by the Superinten- dent, Mr. Pyles. This Institution is located on a beautiful farm of 1250 acres of fertile soil, and is housed in several sub- stantial brick structures with a mod- ern barn, and other necessary build- ings for an up-to-date farsa. Some 200 boys are cared for, 200 of which are committed from the city. The majority of these boys are hired; out to various farmers in the neigh- borhood, in order to enhance the re- ceipts to maintain the Institution. About 50 boys are employed by a contractor, manufacturing brooms, and are giving satisfactory service. The buildings and farm are in good Horder, the dormitories, school, hos- pital, etc., being clean, well lighted and ventilated. Nearly all the boys, excepting those 1 at the broom factory, were out at work on the neighboring farms at the time of our visit, but those we saw were apparently healthy, and well satisfied, cheerfully responding to all requirements. The one case of tuberculosis among the inmates is segregated from the dormitories, but not the lavatory, thereby endangering the health of the others. Would suggest that this bet corrected. Owing to the fact that the boys are hired out to work, they only receive] about two mo months schooling in a year, during the winter. Arrnagement has been made with the Maryland Agricultural College to receive practical instructions in scien- tific farming. t We are of the opinion that the $30,-f $20,000.00 received from the city for the care of 200 boys should be in-t creased, as that amount is only about 182.88 per week per boy. i ! The management should be relieved of the necessity of farming the boys out, and the handicap of a lack offl funds. We recommend that the inmatest predisposed toward or suffering with tuberculosis be segregated in dormi-1 tories and where possible, transferred to the State Hospital at Henryton. Further, that each inmate be provided with an individual towel. t We recommend that in cases whereil punishtnent is to be meted out to inmates, it be done only by the sup- erintendent or in his presence. 11 The institution provides at present from one to three months' s' schooling. Boys are permitted to attend school only when inclement winter weath- er keeps them indoors. Especially the h younger boys should be given sixio months' schooling each year. We sug- gest that the Institution ask the co- operation of the State and county au- at teach- thorities in providing competent teach- rs. Each dormitory is already pro- vided with an adequately equipped i class-room so that the biggest iteto is thee provision of a qualified teaching force, d 1 1 0 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: CHETENHAM LAD SAYS HE WAS BEATEN UP: GEORGE WASHINGTON DECLARES GUARD AND SUPERINTENDENT ARE CRUEL STRAP AND CANE USED LASHING TOOK PLACE IN DINING ROOM, AVERS CRIPPLED YOUTH
full_text: CHELTENHAM LAD SAYS HE WAS BEATEN UP George Washington Declares Guard and Superintendent Are Cruel STRAP AND CANE USED Lashing Took Place In Din- ing Room, Avers Crippled Youth House of Reformation, Cheltenham, Md., November 21, 1924. To the Editor: I call your attention to the treat- ment about the House of Reforma- tion. I was beaten up just as bad as can be by Mr. Harry Carroll and Mr. John B. Pyles, November 21, 1924, for nothing. Mr. Pyles took and hit me cross the head with the cane he walks with about 2 or 3 times and it stunt me and also put a knot on my head about just the size of a hen egg. 1 1 1 Mr. Carroll beats me with the strap all across my back and face and after he gets done beating me he also kicks me and put a mark on 1 my face about this long and here) how it looks between this mark. But, Gentlemen, I would not mind: taking the beating but I am telling you the truth, I really got beat for nothing. So I would like for you to come down right away and see me proof of it. 1 ( so I can tell you better and gives I am a crippled boy and have not got no people and have been down here 7 years and 3 months and 13 days and I have never been beat up|] so bad before since I have been here as I got beat up this afternoon1 about 12:30. I cannot put up with t it, getting beat up for nothing. This was done in the boys dining ( room.while at dinner table. To the Editor: I call your attention to the treat- ment about the House of Reforma- tion. I was beaten up just as bad as can be by Mr. Harry Carroll and Mr. John B. Pyles, November 21, 1924, for nothing. Mr. Pyles took and hit me cross the head with the cane he walks with about 2 or 3 times and it stunt me and also put a knot on my head about just the size of a hen egg. 1 1 1 Mr. Carroll beats me with the strap all across my back and face and after he gets done beating me he also kicks me and put a mark on 1 my face about this long and here) how it looks between this mark. But, Gentlemen, I would not mind: taking the beating but I am telling you the truth, I really got beat for nothing. So I would like for you to come down right away and see me proof of it. 1 ( so I can tell you better and gives I am a crippled boy and have not got no people and have been down here 7 years and 3 months and 13 days and I have never been beat up|] so bad before since I have been here as I got beat up this afternoon1 about 12:30. I cannot put up with t it, getting beat up for nothing. This was done in the boys dining ( room.while at dinner table. GEORGE WASHINGTAN, Age 18, No. 3995, No. 2 Building. f . .

title: Youth Is Paroled
full_text: Cave Man Slaps Woman Who Refuses to Dance When Ruggold Dorsey, Catonsville, resorted to caveman methods to com- pel pretty Angeline Dent to dance with him, she appealed to the police. and the former was arrested and charged with assault. Dorsey, after being arraigned in Criminal Court, through his attor- ney, J. Stewart Davis, was released on costs. He will use softer meth- ods next time. 1 Youth Is Paroled Samuel Howard, 23, 1314 Jefferson street, charged with breaking into a stable and taking harness and rain- coat, went on trial before Judge Frank, Tuesday and was paroled to pay cost. Howard was represented by Davis and Evans. C Boy Released From Cheltenham James Jones, 519 Vincent street, committed August 23 to the House of Reformation at Cheltenham for eight months was released Tuesday a d 1 sl g 0 tl IY h a 21 V c S ti on a writ of Habeas Corpus under parole of Probation Officer George Ralph. full_text: CHELTENHAM A REAL HELL Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. ASK MAYOR TO CUT CHELTENH'M APPROPRIATION Citizens Committee, Urges Immediate Action On Grand Jury Report REFORMATORY "REAL HELL" Better To Send Boys To Jail Or House Of Correction, Says Andrews Mayor Jackson was asked this week by a group of cit- izens to put into effect im- mediately the September Grand Jury's report calling for withholding city appro- priation from Cheltenham, The delegation, all members of the N. A. A. C. P., local branch, was headed by Bishop John Hurst. Other members were W. T. Andrews, editor of the Herald-Commonwealth: Joseph P. Evans, Carl J. Murphy, W. Ashbie Hawkins, W. W. Allen, Dr. Harry S. McCard/ S. S. Booker. Geo. B.Murphy, Walter S. Emer- son, and Linwood Koger, president of the branch. The same committee appeared be- fore Supt. Grasty, head of the Bu- reau of Charters, Thursday with the same request. Board of managers of Cheltenham 1 called into special session this Fri- day in order to consider the firing of Supt. Pyles, of Cheltenham, as recommended in the Grand Jury re- port, will also be visited by the del- ) egation. Reaction to the disclosures of cruelty, mismanagement and filth at the reformatory has resulted in a wave of indignation thruout the city and State. According to Mr. An drews, "Cheltenham is a real hell. which, instead of reforming boys, educates them to be the worst type of criminals. That the City of Baltimore should be urged to withhold appropriations from the House of Reformation at Cheltenham, Prince George county, as a means of forcing reforms at that institution, is the outstanding recommendation of a special report of the September Grarfd Jury just given to the public. The Grand Jury also recommend- ed that the courts of the State send no more boys to that institution. W. T. Andrews, a member of that body, recommended that they 'be sent either to the penitentiary or to the House of Correction as an alter- native to being subjected to condi- 3tions as they are at present at Chel- tenham, Find Conditions Bad "Notwithstanding" says the report, "that an announcement of our visit had been made before we arrived, one could easily see that the boys' dormitory is badly kept. The floors were dingy and of unsanitary ap- pearance; the sheets on the straw beds are dingy and some dirty. "The boys are poorly clothed, the suits of many ragged, and some of the boys barefooted." After touching the inhumanity of farming out the boys to outsiders when they should be used on the 1200 acres owned by the institution, the report dealt with education at the institution as follows: "We are informed that boys kept in the institution were given four months schooling. We saw no teachers. If the guards also per- form the duties of teachers, it can hardly be less than a farce. The guards have not the appearance, of men competent to teach school and their utter lack of sympathy for the

title: Anniversary
full_text: Anniversary The AFRO passed ts 33rd birthday September first. Installation of equipment, which makes possible this larger aper we have this issue, was planned for April 15th, last. trikes and unusual labor troubles delayed the work until ow, so that although a month late, the AFRO is able only oday to bring out its Anniversary number. The remodeled plant, the new equipment mean little xcept better service to thousands of AFRO readers. It ught to be said here that all of these improvements were anned by the late J. H. Murphy, and but for his sudden amite would have been carried out before. White Cargo Shocks England "White Cargo, the new drama, startles London, the FRO said last week. Like New York's "All God's Chillun Pot Wings," it deals with inter-marriage of the races and the English hero marries the West African native girl he se- Buced. ☑ England isn't shocked at the revelation that hundreds of Houng men she sends to fill official positions in the colonies Mall in love with native girls. The country has known that hese many years. But marriage. Oughn't the dramatist to lave known that is a tabooed subject. Mixing the races fter dark is all right, but under the sanction of the law, ever. How like our own precious Southland. Cheltenham Up To Ritchie I t 1. r C r I V S t e C a 1 V Governor Ritchie has shown heretofore an agreeable lingness to investigate conditions in State institutions t There mis-management is charged. : r 2 C f For this reason the AFRO commends to him the reports the last four Baltimore City grand juries on the House of Retormation for Colored Boys at Cheltenham. The last! epon especially is remarkable in that the colored member of this body. Mr. Joseph P. Evans, in a supplemental state- hent sets out at some length the lack of schooling, the insani- ation and the poor management of the superintendent, who Srecommended for a pension. r 2 Γ a Cheltenham is only a private institution, but derives its apport from city and State funds. What is needed is a s tate institution to care for these wayward boys, but Gov-r mor Ritchie can remedy the conditions complained of, so ong as the State holds a part of the money bags. A probe of miserably managed Cheltenham is up to the Governor. What will he do? G. O. P. Grand Old Pussyfooters G. O. P., Grand Old Party, has become Grand Old Pussyiooters. The only sincere, sensible speech in the present cam- aign was written but not delivered by Secretary of Navy, libur. at Denver, Colorado. What happened to it? Copies of it were filed first at Jashington, and a fearful "Cal" kept the wires so hot that Thur canceled all speaking engagements and took an oplane for Washington. a 1 C t i Lincoln must have turned over in his grave. Charles! Sumner and Frederick Douglass must have done a double Comersault, and Teddy Roosevelt groaned aloud. ( Here is "Silent Cal" Coolidge afraid to condemn the Ku Klux Klan and its program of mob violence because so many Republicans in Maine and Indiana are Klansmen. He muz- des Wilbur, who wanted to say that the Negro problem is; The main issue in politics and the main question is not the ariff, but whether the South shall be permitted to segregate, Sisiranchise and mob Americans because they are colored. It's a fine spectacle, this G. O. P. in 1924. The initials indoubtedly stand for Grand Old Pussyfooters. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner.
