title: CHELTENHAM DAILY BEEF RATION ONE POUND TO 20 BOYS: MEAT DIET MEAGRE AT CHELTENHAM DIET OF EACH BOY LIMITED TO 1 1-2 LBS. BEEF AND FIVE POUNDS PORK MONTHLY CRUELTY CHARGED CHELTENHAM BOYS COMMITTEE ON FAIR TREATMENT REPLIES TO DR. BRAGG
full_text: CHELTENHAM DAILY BEEF RATION ONE POUND TO 20 BOYS MEAT DIET MEAGRE AT CHELTENHAM Diet Of Each Boy Limited To 1 1-2 Lbs. Beef. And Five Pounds Pork Monthly CRUELTY CHARGED Cheltenham Boys Committee On Fair Treatment Replies To Dr. Bragg That each of the 200 boys at Cheltenham House of Reforma- tion gets one and one-half pounds of beef and five pounds of pork monthly, is clatined in a letter from a "committee of fair treatment" at that place. The letter, which was written response to a communication ap- pearing in these columns recently from the pen of the Rev. Dr. George F. Bragg, sets out in detail the monthly list of provisions bought for the 200 boys in the institution. According to this list each boy gets one and one-half pounds of beef each month. This is the equiv alent of dividing a one pound steak among 20 boys once each day. To this is added a monthly allowance of five pounds of pork meat, each j month or a total meat ration for each boy of one-fifth of a pound each day. The monthly diet sheet sent by this committee includes 1200 loaves of bread, 75 pans of corn bread, 50 bushels of beans. 18 cart loads of cabbages, 25 bags string beans, 24 cart loads of greens, 225 pounds of cats, 1042 pounds of pork. 300 pounds of beef, 180 gallons of milk. 15 hags of potatoes, 15 bags of tur- nips, 30 bags of sweet potatoes, 15 boxes of prunes, peaches and apples. Wants Race Teachers The letter from the committee is as follows: We, the committee on fair treat- fhent, have read Dr. Bragg's letter about having teachers of our own color here. That would be much better than ex-bartenders and old time topers and some one who can- not read themselves, not capable of ruling themselves, auch less. others. What the manager means by a born disciplinarian must be one of the family who is a dive keeper, NO The can hold his job as county poli- tleian and will be recommended by any old bartender or brandy- wine rum slinger. Have two now at the head. They had to be reformed first before they were elevated. What, the Home wants is bright, intelligent and kind bosses at the head and no county brutes. Last week, Freddy Smith, a small boy, always quiet, and good natured, but the nervous and bysterical woman had him lashed 40 lashes but the big stiffs who can kick up h....... only a few lashes. Runny lashed a little boy in the laundry under Mr. Longfelt's bedroom while he was sick and you could hear the child scream all over the Institution. Why don't you brutes try some of that treatment on us large boys. But what can you expect of the unintelligent coun- try cum-slingers? There are some good officers down here who treat us right. Mr. Longfelt is one of thee best and al- ways gives us good advice. He knows how hard we have to work and sees that we get a plenty of wholesome fond. But if one little boy speaks out In the dining room - knocks out the whole school for one viola- tion. Send us a boss, down here who will not work us from dark to dark or hire us out from 4 in the morning to 9 or 10 at night. They regard us as "only niggers." What the Home needs, as Mr. Bragg says, are two good Balti- more men and white men at the head who should be examined as for qualifications, and character. Mr. The county managers don't care a cuss for us, all they care is to get a job for a man to hold his vote: They never come here to visit. -Written by the State Road Com. And Five Pounds Pork Monthly CRUELTY CHARGED Cheltenham Boys Committee On Fair Treatment Replies To Dr. Bragg That each of the 200 boys at Cheltenham House of Reforma- tion gets one and one-half pounds of beef and five pounds of pork monthly, is clatined in a letter from a "committee of fair treatment" at that place. The letter, which was written response to a communication ap- pearing in these columns recently from the pen of the Rev. Dr. George F. Bragg, sets out in detail the monthly list of provisions bought for the 200 boys in the institution. According to this list each boy gets one and one-half pounds of beef each month. This is the equiv alent of dividing a one pound steak among 20 boys once each day. To this is added a monthly allowance of five pounds of pork meat, each j month or a total meat ration for each boy of one-fifth of a pound each day. The monthly diet sheet sent by this committee includes 1200 loaves of bread, 75 pans of corn bread, 50 bushels of beans. 18 cart loads of cabbages, 25 bags string beans, 24 cart loads of greens, 225 pounds of cats, 1042 pounds of pork. 300 pounds of beef, 180 gallons of milk. 15 hags of potatoes, 15 bags of tur- nips, 30 bags of sweet potatoes, 15 boxes of prunes, peaches and apples. Wants Race Teachers The letter from the committee is as follows: We, the committee on fair treat- fhent, have read Dr. Bragg's letter about having teachers of our own color here. That would be much better than ex-bartenders and old time topers and some one who can- not read themselves, not capable of ruling themselves, auch less. others. What the manager means by a born disciplinarian must be one of the family who is a dive keeper, NO The can hold his job as county poli- tleian and will be recommended by any old bartender or brandy- wine rum slinger. Have two now at the head. They had to be reformed first before they were elevated. What, the Home wants is bright, intelligent and kind bosses at the head and no county brutes. Last week, Freddy Smith, a small boy, always quiet, and good natured, but the nervous and bysterical woman had him lashed 40 lashes but the big stiffs who can kick up h....... only a few lashes. Runny lashed a little boy in the laundry under Mr. Longfelt's bedroom while he was sick and you could hear the child scream all over the Institution. Why don't you brutes try some of that treatment on us large boys. But what can you expect of the unintelligent coun- try cum-slingers? There are some good officers down here who treat us right. Mr. Longfelt is one of thee best and al- ways gives us good advice. He knows how hard we have to work and sees that we get a plenty of wholesome fond. But if one little boy speaks out In the dining room - knocks out the whole school for one viola- tion. Send us a boss, down here who will not work us from dark to dark or hire us out from 4 in the morning to 9 or 10 at night. They regard us as "only niggers." What the Home needs, as Mr. Bragg says, are two good Balti- more men and white men at the head who should be examined as for qualifications, and character. Mr. The county managers don't care a cuss for us, all they care is to get a job for a man to hold his vote: They never come here to visit. -Written by the State Road Com. . .

title: DAY BY DAY: WHEN YOUNG LIONS TASTE BLOOD
full_text: DAY BY DAY FRIDAY, OCTOBER 20, 1939. When Young Hearts Prove Real Much is made of love at first sight, but how often does it prove real? In the case of Miss Mary Jane Smith, daughter of Mr. and Mrs. J. H. Smith, of 2000 North Charles street, and Mr. William Henry Jones, son of Mr. and Mrs. H. J. Jones, of 1000 West Baltimore street, it proved to be a real thing. They met at a dance in 1937, and it was love at first sight with both of them. They were married last week. “I never thought such a thing could happen,” said the bride’s mother. “But it did.” The young couple will make their home in Baltimore. * * * “I don’t know,” said Mrs. J. H. Brown, of 2000 North Charles street, “why people are so interested in the fact that I am going to have a baby. I have had five already, and I don’t see why this one should cause so much excitement.” Mrs. Brown is expecting a baby in November. * * * “I don’t see why people are so interested in the fact that I am going to have a baby,” said Mrs. J. H. Brown, of 2000 North Charles street. “I have had five already, and I don’t see why this one should cause so much excitement.” Mrs. Brown is expecting a baby in November. * * * Why Eats Our Watermelons? The moth is a menace to the watermelon crop. It lays its eggs on the vines, and the larvae eat the melons. The moth is a native of South America, and it was first discovered in this country in 1916. It has spread rapidly since then, and it is now found in all parts of the United States. The moth is a serious pest, and it is difficult to control. The best way to control it is to destroy the vines after the melons have been harvested. The moth is a small, brown insect with a wingspread of about one inch. The larvae are white, with brown heads. They are about one-half inch long when fully grown. The moth is a serious pest, and it is difficult to control. The best way to control it is to destroy the vines after the melons have been harvested. * * * Baltimore People Answer Honest If you ask a North Carolinian what he thinks of Baltimore, he will probably tell you that it is a dirty, smoky city. But if you ask a Baltimorean what he thinks of Baltimore, he will probably tell you that it is the best city in the world. The truth is, Baltimore is a city of contrasts. It is a city of great wealth and great poverty. It is a city of great beauty and great ugliness. It is a city of great culture and great ignorance. But Baltimore is also a city of great charm. It is a city of friendly people. It is a city of great traditions. It is a city that is proud of its past and confident of its future. Baltimore is a city that is worth knowing. It is a city that is worth loving. And so, if you ask a North Carolinian what he thinks of Baltimore, he will probably tell you that it is a dirty, smoky city. But if you ask a Baltimorean what he thinks of Baltimore, he will probably tell you that it is the best city in the world. Much worse is a war between nations than a war between individuals. In the latter case, only a few people are killed, but in the former case, millions of people are killed. In the latter case, only a few people are made miserable, but in the former case, millions of people are made miserable. In the latter case, only a few people are impoverished, but in the former case, millions of people are impoverished. In the latter case, only a few people are made homeless, but in the former case, millions of people are made homeless. In the latter case, only a few people are made orphans, but in the former case, millions of people are made orphans. In the latter case, only a few people are made widows, but in the former case, millions of people are made widows. In the latter case, only a few people are made cripples, but in the former case, millions of people are made cripples. In the latter case, only a few people are made insane, but in the former case, millions of people are made insane. In the latter case, only a few people are made criminals, but in the former case, millions of people are made criminals. In the latter case, only a few people are made paupers, but in the former case, millions of people are made paupers. In the latter case, only a few people are made suicides, but in the former case, millions of people are made suicides. * * * Watching Our Birth And Death Rates It is always interesting to watch the birth and death rates. They show us how our population is increasing or decreasing. The birth rate in the United States is now about 18 per 1,000 population. The death rate is about 11 per 1,000 population. This means that our population is increasing at the rate of about 7 per 1,000 population per year. This is a very slow rate of increase. In fact, it is so slow that it is causing some concern. Some people are worried that our population will eventually start to decrease. They point to the fact that the birth rate is declining and the death rate is increasing. They also point to the fact that the average age of our population is increasing. This means that there are more old people and fewer young people. This is a serious problem, and it is one that we must face. We must find ways to increase the birth rate and decrease the death rate. We must also find ways to make our population younger. If we do not do these things, our population will eventually start to decrease. And if our population starts to decrease, our country will be in serious trouble.

title: ASKS SENATE TO MAKE PROBE AT CHELTENHAM: CITIZEN'S COMMITTCE'S PETITION INTRODUCED AT ANNAPOLIS BY SENATOR FROM 4TH DISTRICT TUESAAY CRUELTY IS ALLEGED ATENTION CALLED TO LACK OF SCHOOLING CITED IN REPORT OF SEPT. GRAND JURY
full_text: ASKS SENATE TO MAKE PROBE AT CHELTENHAM Citizen's Committee's Peti- tion Introduced at Anna- polis by Senator From 4th District Tuesday CRUELTY IS ALLEGED Atention Called to Lack of Schooling Cited in Re- port of Sept. Grand Jury Annapolis, Feb. 5.-A pe- tition urging a probe of con- ditions at the House of Re- formation at Cheltenham was introduced in the Sen- ate today by Senator Harry Q. Levin representing the 5th Baltimore City District. The document which calls at tention to the lack of schooling and the cruelty to the boys, who are forced to use their fingers in raking stones and twigs from the fields instead of rakes, was signed by Rev. A. L. Gaines, Rev. W. W. Walker, Mrs. Annie E. Hitchens, Mrs. George Frey, Mrs. A. L. Me- Mechen, Miss Hazel MacBeth, Miss Elsie Mountain, Thomas J. Callo- way, Carl Murphy, Rev. Junius Gray and Rev. W. J. Gray. It was referred to the tinance committee. PETITION TO THE HONORABLE, THE SEN- ATE OF MARYLAND, ANNAPO- LIS, MARYLAND. WHEREAS the State of Mary- land ought to be zealous for the welfare and education of way- ward boys committed to its va- rious reformatory institutions, to the end that the inmates may be afforded the opportunity to become useful citizens and to tessen the probability of their be- coming a detriment to the com- munity, and - Two Months Schooling WHEREAS in the recent re port of a committee appointed by the Grand Jury for Balti- more City for the September Term. 1923, it is charged that little effort is made for the ed ucation of the inmates of the House of Reformation of Col- ored Boys at Cheltenham, in Prince George's t'ounty, except for a total of about two months in the year, at such times when the weather is melement and that at other times when the in- mates should be receiving schooling, because of Leiency of funds provided by the State, the boys are hired out to neighboring farmers, tor su cefits a day, and : Cruelty Charged insur- WHEREAS it is further un- derstood from other sources that part of the time which could be used for schooling purposes is expended in, wasteful practices, one of them to oblig lige many the boys to go over the immens grounds grounds in squads clean "stleks, leaves and other ther rubbish with their bare hands, using their fingers as rakes, es, as we well as other, primeval and unnecessary labor which ein wasily be avoid- ed by modern equipment and tools, and WHEREAS the De said reports by the Grand Jury and from other sources are indiesative that the human standard of the State of Maryland in the treat- ment, care and education of wayward boys is not being main- tained at said Institution and that its polleys and methods should be corrected.. Investigation Asked The undersigned residents of the State of Maryland, petition your Honorable body to investi- gate the conditions above men- tioned and that the Board of Managers and Superintendents of sald Institution be required to report to your body as to the "matters and facts above charged. 1 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

W. J. Gray. It was referred to the tinance committee. PETITION TO THE HONORABLE, THE SEN- ATE OF MARYLAND, ANNAPO- LIS, MARYLAND. WHEREAS the State of Mary- land ought to be zealous for the welfare and education of way- ward boys committed to its va- rious reformatory institutions, to the end that the inmates may be afforded the opportunity to become useful citizens and to tessen the probability of their be- coming a detriment to the com- munity, and - Two Months Schooling WHEREAS in the recent re port of a committee appointed by the Grand Jury for Balti- more City for the September Term. 1923, it is charged that little effort is made for the ed ucation of the inmates of the House of Reformation of Col- ored Boys at Cheltenham, in Prince George's t'ounty, except for a total of about two months in the year, at such times when the weather is melement and that at other times when the in- mates should be receiving schooling, because of Leiency of funds provided by the State, the boys are hired out to neighboring farmers, tor su cefits a day, and : Cruelty Charged insur- WHEREAS it is further un- derstood from other sources that part of the time which could be used for schooling purposes is expended in, wasteful practices, one of them to oblig lige many the boys to go over the immens grounds grounds in squads clean "stleks, leaves and other ther rubbish with their bare hands, using their fingers as rakes, es, as we well as other, primeval and unnecessary labor which ein wasily be avoid- ed by modern equipment and tools, and WHEREAS the De said reports by the Grand Jury and from other sources are indiesative that the human standard of the State of Maryland in the treat- ment, care and education of wayward boys is not being main- tained at said Institution and that its polleys and methods should be corrected.. Investigation Asked The undersigned residents of the State of Maryland, petition your Honorable body to investi- gate the conditions above men- tioned and that the Board of Managers and Superintendents of sald Institution be required to report to your body as to the "matters and facts above charged. 1 . .

title: Cheltenham
full_text: Cheltenham  Cheltenham, the house of refor- mation for colored boys in Prince Seorges County is back in the spot. jight again after a short respite. This time it is because its secretary, Mr. W. H. Janney, refuses to per- mit Mrs. Edith Jones, of Philadel- phia, to see or communicate with her brother committed there by or- er of Baltimore City courts several years ago. The solution of this case is simple nough. Mrs. Jones has only to ap- ply for a writ of habeas corpus through any local attorney and Cheltenham authorities will be com- pelled to permit her to see her bro- ther. In every single case in which habeas corpus has been asked courts have also returned these boys to their parents and guardians when requested. The solution of the whole Chel- tenham problem is not so easy. The Institution is dominated by natives of Prince. Georges County who are not experts in reform themselves and did not lister to the Mayor's committee last year which recom- mended certain changes. As the result, boys there are mis- erably treated, trained little, if at all, and farmed out like convicts to work on neighboring plantations. If there is any place worse than Cheltenham for cildren, we do not know of it.. Here is a matter for the Grand Jury to go into again. The city should be requested to withhold its appropriation to Cheltenham, until its trustees reform it. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: ""Bill"" Pickett With ""101"" Shows
full_text: "Bill" Pickett Mrs. Garvey Here With "101" Shows Mrs. Amy Ashwood Garvey, first wife of Marcus Garvey, who is suing her husband-Provisional President of Af- With Miller Brothers' 101 Ranch rica for divorce, is in the city for a Wild West and Great Far East Show few days, stopping with friends at 531 that comes to Baltimore two days, Preston street. Wednesday, May 26 and 27, at Ed- mondson avenue and Bentalou street, will be "Bill" Pickett, greatest colored plainsman ever known. Pickett's spe- cialty, throwing steers with his teeth, is unmatchable and the marvel of the West. 0 "Y" Arrivals W..D. Winston, Salisbury, Md.; J. J. Banks, Berlin, Md.; O. Williams, Phila- Last year in Albany, during the delphia: Carl E. Darden, Washington; street parade, the driver of a tableau James L. Morton, Washington; William wagon lost control of his horses and Alexander, Washington; Henry Taylor, the five ran away, drawing the heavy Richmond, Va; James Robinson, Spar- wagon down an incline. Pickett, who rows, Point, Md.; C. S. Elder, Rich- mond, Va.; D. E. Brooks, Baltimore. was mounted on a horse, did some spectacular riding, grasped the bit of the leader and saved the life of five [ spectators. The hero suffered a brok- en arm and rib and was incapacitated for months. His injuries have healed and he is now one of the stars of this season's production. There is a col- ored band that is also a feature of the stupendous show. One thousand four hundred persons are required to present the mammoth spectacle, cowboys and cowgirls; 600 elephants,camels and buffalo, (from ranch herds.) Every famous Indian chief and warrior in America is among its contingent of redskins. And ignor- ing the retrenchment policy of the circuses, they bring to town the larg- est and most picturesque street pa- rade ever seen in the land. BOY IN HOSPITAL MOTHER NOT TOLD Cheltenham Authorities Did Not Inform Mrs. Hattie Stewart of Son's Illness D. C. RELATIVE SAW LAD IN FREEDMANS Alonzo Jackson Found To Be Critical After Serious Ope- ration For Appendicitis More of the deplorable con- ditions existing at the boys re- form school at Cheltenham were revealed by Mrs. Hattie Stewart, 1510 Riggs Avenue. She told an AFRO reporter that her son, Alonzo Jackson, age 11, had been confined in the Freedman's Hospital, Washington, with appendi citis for more than a week before she was notified of his condition. She was only informed that her son was confined in the hospital when a relative in Washington hap- pened to visit the institution and discovered the boy. Mrs. Stewart declares that she went to Washing- ton immediately upon being inform:- ed and found that her son had been there some time and had undergone an operation. His condition was de- clared to be serious at the time. Later information from the author- ities declare the boy to be improv- ing however. The boy was committed to the reform school last January she stat. ed. 1 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: LANSEY SCORES CHELTENHAM IN ""JURY"" REPORT: BOYS AT REFORM SCHOOL TAUGHT TRADE THEY CAN'T USE BROOM FACTORY IS CALLED FIRE TRAP HIRING OUT OF BOYS ONLY ANOTHER KIND OF PEONAGE
full_text: LANSEY SCORES CHELTENHAM IN "JURY" REPORT Boys At Reform School Taught Trade They Can't Use BROOM FACTORY IS CALLED FIRE TRAP Hiring Out Of Boys Only Another Kind Of Peon- age In a minority report filed with the annual report of the Grand Jury resulting from their investigations of city and state institu- tions, T. Wallis Lansey, manager of Druid Laundry severely scored existing conditions at the Reform School for Colored youth at Cheltenham, and offered several suggestions for the betterment of the institu- tion. In his report Mr. Lansey suggests ine abolishment of the broom fau- tory, where a large majority of the boys are employed. Very few boys after being dismissed from the in- stitution are able to make a living at this trade, he points out. Money expended in this direction should be used to improve the shoe mak- ing and tailoring shops with compe- tent instructors. If these trades are properly taught it will not only enable the boys to make their own clothes and shoes, thus reducing the expense to the state, but would prepare them to make a living and become useful citizens after their dismissal, he declares. Broom Factory Firetrap -. The broom factory is a veritable firetrap, according to Mr. Lansey. There is but one exit which is very small, and due to the highly in- flamable materials used in the mak- ing of brooms, would result in many deaths before the score or more of boys employed there could make their escape. As long as the broom factory remains, he suggests the building of another stairway lead- ing from the basement where factory is located. Bread Soggy the While the bakery was clean and sanitary, the bread made there was neither wholesome or digestible, he states. A loaf handed to him as he passed through was heavy and soggy. The buildings were well kept. Of the 285 boys only six were employed in the shoe shop at the time of the visit; there was no evidence that the trade was being taught. The equipment was old, scant, and antiquated. Neither were there any signs of instruction in the tailoring shop. The only in- struments were six old sewing ma- chines that revealed little evidence of being used. Calls Farm System Peonage The system of hiring out the boys to neighboring farmers as used by the superintendent is described as being but one step from slavery. The boys get but from $25 to $40 a year for their services it is said. This is collected by the institution and saved until they are dismissed at the age of 21. ex- The management complained of the lack of funds to put the insti- tution on a higher plane, Mr. Lan- cey states, if the boys now hired out to private farmers were em- ployed by the institution, to farm the 754 acres on which it is situ- ated between class hours, they would not only be able to defray a large amount of their own pense but would also be able to hire competent instructors. An as- sistant, who is a specialist in insti- tutional work should be employed who by scientific methods of farm- ing could obtain the desired result. Abolish the Aze Limit The present practice of commit- ting a boy to the institution until he is 21 years of age should be abolished, Mr. Lancey declares, and the judges be empowered to limit the boy's stay in accordance with the misdemeanor committed. Many boys after a year in the reform school realize their error and would come out better citizens. To com- mit a boy of 12 who has made a mistake to such an institution, which does not better his condition, until he reaches his majority is the highest form of injustice. Under present conditions if a boy makes the ten grades through good behavior he is subject to parole. This is optional with th authori- ties, however, and results in very few boys being 'freed. This power should be taken out of the hands of the management and embodied in the judges imposing the sentence, he adds. : Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: GRAND JURY PROBES COMPLAINTS AT CHELTENHAM, MD.: CHELTENHAM MAY BE PLACED UNDER STATE CONTROL GRAND JURY PROBE MAY RESULT IN DRASTIC CHANGES AT BOYS REFORMATORY. INMATES TESTIFY WITNESS BEFORE BODY SAY BREAD AND TEA FORM MAIN ARTICLES OF DIET.
full_text: GRAND JURY PROBES COMPLAINTS AT CHELTENHAM, MD. CHELTENHAM MAY BE PLACED UNDER STATE CONTROL Grand Jury Probe May Re- sult In Drastic Changes At Boys Reformatory. INMATES TESTIFY Witness Before Body Say Bread And Tea Form Main Articles Of Diet. That the House of Reforma- tion for boys at Cheltenham may undergo drastic changes in control and management as a result of an investigation made this week by the Grand Jury is the belief of those interested in the work. Recommendation for stricter State supervision and possibly complete State control may be the outcome, according to some members of the jury. Witnesses Testify The present investigation being made into the affairs of this institu- fon is the result of numerous let- ters sent by inmates from time to ime complaining of conditions here. In a recent letter to the Grand Jury an inmate complained of cruel and inhuman treatment. Besides ack of proper food, long hours of work the letter charged that inhu- ran punishment was administered. Gets Bread and Tea Another witness before the. body testified that bread and tea formed the main diet for most of the time and that the inmates were rarely en meat except from time to time in soup. These complaints, it was pointed out were in line with former charges made to other grand juries in past years.. Is Private Affair Cheltenham is a private institu- tion and under the supervision of a private board of control. Under the pesent arrangements the Juvenile Court sends its wards there under contract. They are given work at the institution and at times farmed out to work for others. Former grand juries have made Investigations and recommended changes but to date none of these recommendations have had any di- rect effect. Dr. George F. Bragg, Jr., a mem- ber of the board of boverners, told a reporter that he had not been made acquainted with the charges brought before the Grand Jury ex- cept what had been published in the papers and woul dmake no comment 1 at this time. title: BOYS CLUBBED IN CHELTENHAM SAY INMATES: FORMER INMATES WRITES AFROAMERICAN-OF BARBARITIES PRACTICED AT REFORM SCHOOL PRISON METHOD USED SOLITARY CELLS, BREAD AND WATER DIET, MADE SKELETONS OF BOYS, IS CHARGE
full_text: BOYS CLUBBED IN CHELTENHAM SAY INMATES Former Inmates Writes Afro- American Of Barbarities Practiced At Reform School PRISON METHOD USED Solitary Cells, Bread And Water Diet, Made Skeletons Of Boys, Is Charge That the worst form of crulety and shameful clubbinig of boys with clubs were practiced at the House of Re- formation at Cheltenham, is the charge made by William M. Creasy, for more than three years an inmate of the institution, to this paper this week. Creasy, who volunteered this infor- Is mation, offers to come here as a wit- ness and testify as to detall happen- dings which took place while he was there. His letter follows: Editor AFRO-AMERICAN: I was reading last week's AFRO, and I noticed your Cheltenham ar- ticle. I was an inmate of the House of Reformation in 1912, and I can frankly say that every word in your article is true. The only thing you have not found out half that goes on on the inside of the place. Bread and tea is certainly the chief diet, and not much of that. Two slices of bread and half a cup of tea to a person at breakfast and supper. At dinner the same bread and tea with a bowl of worm broth which they called soup. Work Without Food When the officers are angry at something they cut the boys out of three and four meals hand running and make them work just the same. They have big straps there which they beat the boys with, and they have handles 2½ feet long and the Ilmber part which boys are hit with is just a little longer. The farm overseer is a big colored man weighing 225 or 250 pounds, and he is the chief beater. Mr. J. B. Pyles, the superintendent, used to give his overseer, whose name is Harry Washington, a quart of whis- key and the strap, and sometimes they would go to all five of the schools and beat all the boys un- mercifully. Dies After Blow Whispering or talking is strictly against the rules, except to an of- ficer or guard. While I was there one boy, John Baker, received a blow over the head with a window stick, and he died a few weeks after getting the blow. E. D. Furgang, the teacher at that time of No. 4 school, was the man who delivered the blow. I was beaten nearly to death be- cause I sobbed too loud when I re- ceived a letter that my mother was.. dead. This was about the third month that I was there. Prison Methods Used When a boy would run away he was confined in a small cell, 8 x 3, from 3 to 6 months. His diet was two thin clices of bread, two cups of water a day, the first thirty days he was confined. The superinten- dent would bring Washington around every day, and the boy who was in confinement would receive a severe beating every day for the first thirty days. By this time these boys would look like walking skeletons. Two School Days In Three Years I was at Cheltenham 3 years, 5 months, and a few days, and I had ' school two afternoons during this time. They would hire the boys out with mean people who hated colored peo- ple and the boys were worked just 3 like slaves: They gave us a fairly good sized piece of meat each Thanksgiving, and this the only time we did get any meat. I was sent to, Cheltenham from this town and the men who sent me are still here. If they are making an investigation of the House of Reformation and need a witness who realy knows the inside of the place I am the man. Very truly yours, WM. M. CREASY.

title: Carol--A Dance or Dance Music
full_text: Carol-A Dance or Dance Music Carol-To Sing in a cheerful or joyous strain to warble, torn dance and sing a dance performed in a circle, also a song accompanying it, a choral dance.- Webster's Dictionary. Yes, if they want to. It is their business and nobody's else. Persons accustomed to regard the Christmas season as a serious and stilled celebration must have gotten a shock from Dr. Hugh Birckhead Sunday when he declared that early Christmas music was written for dance purposes. The rector of Emanuel P. E. Church went further and described German carols as written in waltz time, French in march time, and the Russian carols in polka time. Commenting on the French carols, he added that there was no emphasis on the child Jesus as the son of God, but rather on the Bethlehem Babe as an infant, cunning, beauti- ful and unexpressibly dear. The early church, said Dr. Birckhead, emphasized the dance in connection with Christmas carols. How much danc- ing there was can be seen from the fact that it required three church councils to abolish it. V Today most churches regard the dance as sinful in itself C -a thing to be banished to the stage and the dance hall. St. Mark's Church, New York, advertised a dance interpreting the Scriptures recently, and drew down upon its head the ban of the bishops. How far the church ban goes may be seen also in the fact that no dancing is permitted in the Y. M. C. A.'s and the community houses. Church members dance, members of the pastor's family dance at home or publicly in dance halls, but as for church dancing, the thing simply isn't done any more. Cheltenham Again Grand Juries come and Grand Juries go, but the mis- management of Cheltenham, (House of Reformation for Col- ored Boys) like Tennison's book, goes on forever. Reports of the last three Baltimore City Grand Juries pointed out grave faults in Cheltenham and urged relief. It has been shown that the place is unsanitary, that young boys are given less than three months schooling; that they are hired out like convicts, that they are beaten by guards, and by superintendent. One Grand Jury, of which Mr. Joseph P. Evans was a member, in a special report pointed out these things and in addition, urged that Superintendent Pyles, who is now phy- sically unable to perform his duties, be supplanted. While the institution is privately managed, it receives aid from the City and State and Baltimore delinquents are sent there from the Juvenile Court. Last week witnesses appearing before the Grand Jury testified that the chief diet was bread and tea-seldom meat except in soup. : These are the facts. Certainly Baltimore Judges cannot continue to send delinquents to such institutions as cannot help them to become better citizens. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Cheltenham Worse Than Pen, Relatives Can't See Boys
full_text: Cheltenham Worse Than Pen, Relatives Can't See Boys That Cheltenham is worse than a penitentiary in that relatives are not permitted to see boys sent there, is the complaint of Mrs. Edith James 804 South 48th street, Phildelphia. in a letter to the AFRO-AMERICAN this week. Mrs. James' brother, Paul A. Cook, sent to Cheltenham at his mo- ther's death, doesn't know that his father is dead because authorties there have refused to let his sister see him or write to him. The where abouts of young Cook were discov- ered by relatives through the AFRO. AMERICAN. Mrs. James found out that he was put out to work in October 1917 and will become of age in October of this year. In a letter to the AFRO-AMERI- CAN this week she says that the action of the authorities in keep- ing her from seeing or communi- cating with her brother is an out- rage and asks under what state law the authorities are proceeding. In her letter is enclosed also a communication from W. H. Janney, secretary of the House of Reform- ation at Cheltenham, poorly typed on the official stationery and signed also on the typewriter in which Janney states that young Cook has been hired out on his own request. "We have found that it is bad for boys so placed out to come in contact with their people because these poeple visit them, give them money which they do not need, correspond with them and in gen- eral get the boys, dissatisfied with their surroundings no matter how good they may be, with the result, in every case, that the boys run a- way causing much trouble and ex- pense to recaptare them, and worse than-all, forfeit all the money that has been accumulated in our hands for their benefit. "In the case of Paul Cook we have had considerable trouble along this line. Not that he has run a- way, but I think he was preparing to do so, and I had to send our agent to see him and get him straightened out. This was, I think. caused by some visits to him by a supposed brother, of which visits we knew nothing, but it is evident that there has been some influence brought to bear on the boy to make him believe that he could do much better if he were away from his present home. "Therefore we have declined to tell where the boy is located, be- cause it is always better for them not to be, visited by their people while they are at service, as you will see from the above, and when they become of age, all the infor- mation that we have as to their rel- atives is given them, their money is turned over to them and they are sent to their people." 1 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: MURDER AND SUICIDE CAUSE STILL UNKNOWN: RELATIVES OF GEORGE SMITH UNABLE TO THROW LIGHT ON TRAGEDY WOMAN THOUGHT TO HAVE BEEN MARRIED SISTER OF DEAD MAN NOT CERTAIN WHETHER MARRIAGE DISCOVERY CAUSED ACT
full_text: MURDER AND SUICIDE CAUSE STILL UNKNOWN Relatives Of George Smith Unable To Throw Light On Tragedy - WOMAN THOUGHT TO HAVE BEEN MARRIED Sister Of Dead Man Not Cer- tain Whether Marriage Discovery Caused Act را No reason could be assign- ed by Mrs. Minnie Smith. Har. rison, 908 N. Gilmor strect, in an interview Tuesday as to why her brother, George Smith, of the same address shot and killed Miss Dorothy Carter and then ended his own life in the Wy- man Apartments where they were both employed, Thursday night. Mrs. Harrison stated that she had been introduced to Miss Carter by her brother, who was younger than herself, but knew little of her. She has since learned that she was mar- Fried. Whether the return of the husband had anything to do with the tragedy or not she was unable to say. World War Veteran Smith, according to Mrs. Harri- son, came to the city from Staun- ton, Virginia, in 1915. He enlisted here and served overseas during the World War. Ho again made his home here after his return. Since the installation of dining room service at the Wyman Apart- ment he had been employed as a waiter there. It was there that he became acquainted with Miss Car- ter who was employed as a maid. What transpired between the two prior to the shooting may never be known. Smith was removed to his home in Staunton where the funer- al took place. Calls Cheltenham A Real Hell Cheltenham is a real Hell Attor- ney Josiah Henry told the AFRO- AMERICAN this week following a hearing in which he failed by ha- beous corpus to secure the release of Paul Cook, age 20, from the State reformatory. Cook was brought to Baltimore from Cheltenham by order of his sister who lives in Philadelphia. She said her brother was commit. ted at the age of 15 as a minor without proper care, and that she had provided a home for him now and wanted him with her. The boy who is hired out to a white family and is under the charge of a guide while in the city ap- peared frightened and told the court he didn't want to leave. : "Cook." said Attorney Henry, "is hired out under what is really a slave contract. He wanted to come but was afraid to say, so for fear there might be some slip and the guard would take him back and beat him up. "The real menace of Cheltenham is that it takes away the manhood of every boy incarcerated there." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

Ho again made his home here after his return. Since the installation of dining room service at the Wyman Apart- ment he had been employed as a waiter there. It was there that he became acquainted with Miss Car- ter who was employed as a maid. What transpired between the two prior to the shooting may never be known. Smith was removed to his home in Staunton where the funer- al took place. Calls Cheltenham A Real Hell Cheltenham is a real Hell Attor- ney Josiah Henry told the AFRO- AMERICAN this week following a hearing in which he failed by ha- beous corpus to secure the release of Paul Cook, age 20, from the State reformatory. Cook was brought to Baltimore from Cheltenham by order of his sister who lives in Philadelphia. She said her brother was commit. ted at the age of 15 as a minor without proper care, and that she had provided a home for him now and wanted him with her. The boy who is hired out to a white family and is under the charge of a guide while in the city ap- peared frightened and told the court he didn't want to leave. : "Cook." said Attorney Henry, "is hired out under what is really a slave contract. He wanted to come but was afraid to say, so for fear there might be some slip and the guard would take him back and beat him up. "The real menace of Cheltenham is that it takes away the manhood of every boy incarcerated there." . .

title: Mr. Emerson Starts
full_text: Mr. Emerson Starts Can the Baltimore City Council succeed when the Baltimore Grand Jury and the Mayor have failed. That is the question which proposes itself after City Councilman Walter S. Emerson in- troduced this week a resolution requesting the Board of Managers at the House of Reforma- tion at Cheltenham to replace its white super- visors and teachers with colored. Cheltenham with a larger per capita expen- diture than several other state institutions is yet the most grievously mismanaged reform school in the state. Part of this is due to an- cient methods; part to its private management which is not responsible to the state and city which support it, and part is undoubtedly due to its white staff which has no genuine interest in reforming boys except as it brings a monthly pay envelope. The grand jury for the past five years has reported adversely on Cheltenham. Juries have recommended that more essential trades be taught, that common school instruction be in- stituted; that changes be made so that inmates be kept clean and better disciplined. The mayor of the city interested himself in Cheltenham, appointed an advisory committee which investigated and recommended certain changes which were disregarded by the reform school. Now comes Mr. Emerson with his resolution to change the staff from white to colored. The city of course has no control of the privately owried Cheltenham. However it appropriates something like $35,000. a year for the boys it sends there. If Cheltenham continues refuse to listen to advice, and good counsel, we trust Mr. Emerson will introduce still another resolution-namely that Baltimore city will build and equip at Ca- tonsville on the farm which now houses the Md. Home and Parental School, its own reform school. Such a move is not only in the interest of the youth but also in the interest of economy. Baltimore city can run such an institution cheaper than it can continue to pay somebody else to do it 4 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: AMERICAN LEGION IN ANNUAL CONVENTION: MARYLAND DEPARTMENT HOLDS NINTB ANNUAL MEET AT EASTON, MD. WALTER GREEN POST HITS SCHOOL SYSTEM RESOLUTIONS TAKE SLAP AT COUNTY EDUCATION AND CHELTENHAM
full_text: AMERICAN LEGION IN ANNUAL CONVENTION Maryland Department Holds Ninth Annual Meet At Eas-. ton, Md. WALTER GREEN POST HITS SCHOOL SYSTEM Resolutions Take Slap At County Education And Cheltenham The Maryland Department of the American Legion now in its ninth annual convention at Eas- ton, Md., is expected to act on two resolutions submitted by the Walter Green Post number 14 this week. The resolutions crawn up by Cap- tain Milton Dorsey and Edward H. Marshall are to the effect that the convention go on record as indorsing a uniform length of school year for all children in the counties, adequate establishment of high schools when the law is satisfied, and a uniform salary scale for all teachers. The following grievances are cited in the resolution: a.-The maximum length of the school year for colored boys and giris In our counties is only eight (8) months. b-These children have no high schools in many of the counties, Bal- timore County, for an example. The law, however, in this case states. That a high school may be established wherever there is a minimum of fifteen (15) elementary graduates: Baltimore County had 89 graduates last semester. c-The maximum salaries for coi- ored county teachers is only $70. HIT CHELTENHAM The second resolution points out the evils at Cheltenham Reform School. According the finding of a committee only 30 days' schooling is given to the boys during the year. There are no trades taught these. boys, except baking, broom-making, and farming. The boys are hired out under the convict-lease system to the surround- ing farmers who act as guards with pay in meny cases. No supervision is given the conduct. instruction, and treatment of these boys. No effort is made to reclaim these boys to citizenship. These boys are released under a poor system, since no notification of the release is sent the parent or guar- dian, the transportation is usually left; in the hands of an agreeable passer- by who drops off the released boys anywhere in the designated vicinity. The instiution is privately owned and controlled with the help of a State appropriation of '$53.310. an- nually. The post asks the convention to draw up a petition favoring state con- trol of this institution and recom- mends that measures be taken to rec- tify existing conditions..

title: PRISONERS' AID IS FAMILIES' SANTA: ASSOCIATION DISTRIBUTES BASKETS TO PRISONERS, RELATIVES AND YOUNGSTERS. 20 WOMEN IN JAIL GIFTS ONLY WENT TO THOSE WITHOUT FUNDS
full_text: se bet, ths ar- ice nd W'S, ay. nd WO nt ay г- en he he ng 1s. T st n tu S e e 1 - PRISONERS' AID IS FAMILIES' SANTA Association Distributes Bas- kets To Prisoners, Rela- tives And Youngsters. 20 WOMEN IN JAIL Gifts Only Went To Those Without Funds Christmas cheer, irrespective of race, and in the form of 80 baskets for families under su- pervision, 25 baskets to men and women in six penal institutions, and candy and toys for the kid- dies of the unfortunates, went out from the Prisoner's Aid Asso- ciation offices last week. Upstairs the grim courts were clearing masses of cases ranging from the filing of inventories sentencing men for murder. Downstairs it was Christmas Youngsters of the families who are aided by the association could romp and did romp in the room fitted ur with a brilliantly lighted tree and other geegaw s peculiar to the season. Paper Santas, reindeer, and imitation snow, -all these were or program in the bright spot in the Court House. According to Howard C. Hill, white. secretary of the association, the race was remembered in all the city and state penal institutions. Over a score of women are included in the prisoners at the City Jail, all of whom received baskets, candies and a present. The baskets were liberally heaped with fruits, cake and other holiday goodies. Gifts were made only to men and women without money and friends. Included in the association's treat were the House of Correction for Boys at Cheltenham, the Maryland Penitentiary, House of Correction at Jessup. Catonsville, and those ex- clusively for white children. Many baskets were donated by race churches and individuals, ac- } cording to Mr. Hill. several coming from Sharon Baptist Church, of which the Rev. Beale Elliott is pastor. The larger share of the holi- day treat. however, comes from the association's own fund. The Prisoners' Aid Association is a member agency of the Community Fund, and receives appropriations from the city and state. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: PEONAGE AND CHAIN GANG AT CHELTENHAM SCHOOL: PEONAGE AND CHAIN GANG AT CHELTENHAM REFORM SCHOOL BOYS DIRTY, RAGGED AND HIRED OUT TO SLAVE BATHS ONCE WEEK PASTORS ARE TOLD THREE MINISTERS CONFERENCES NAME CONILLITTECS TO SCE MAVOR AND GOVERNOR
full_text: PEONAGE AND CHAIN GANG AT CHELTENHAM SCHOOL PEONAGE AND CHAIN GANG AT CHELTENHAM Reform School Boys Dirty, Ragged And Hired Out To Slave BATHS ONCE WEEK PASTORS ARE TOLD Three Ministers Conferen- ces Name Committees To Sce Mayor And Governor Virtual slavery, peonage and a chain gang exist at the House of Reformation for Colored Boys at Chel- tenham, Prince Georges County, Md., Dr. Ernest Lyon told the three local ministers' conferences at their various meetings this week. Dr. Lyon said he paid a visit to the institution without notice and there he found 300 boys from Balti- more city and the state of Maryland ragged. and barerooted despite the cold weather, hair uncombed and the older boys hired out in "this Maryland free state". to work on plantations surrounding. Like a chain gang, he said, they stay out all week and come back home Sat- uraday for a bath. Despite the dirty and laborious work these boys have to do, bath- ing facilities are provided only once a week. "The boys are there from the ages of 10 to 21. I went into a room where there were 75 little boys. The teach- er. poorly educated himself, tried to have one of the youngsters stand up and read. But they couldn't. They have had no educational advantages whatever. "The conditions at the institution are shameful. If I weren't a min- ister I would tell what kind of a shame it is." Rev. Bragg Speaks The Rev. Dr. George F. Bragg, for over 25 years a member of the Chel- tenham board, declared that the board at present consisted of 12 per- sons, two members appointed by the governor, two by the mayor and the remaining eight elected by the board itself. The institution is private and is owned by the board of trustees. Most of the members, he said, reside in Prince Georges County and priv- ate interests and politics have pre- vented any change in the personnel. Dr. Lyon referred to the buildings as old and falling apart. The super- intendent. Mr. Pyles, he said. has only one leg. when the institution should have men with at "least four legs" to keep up with the children committed there to be reformed. The A. M. E. ministers named the following committee to cooperate with ministers' meetings of other de- nominations in asking the governor and mayor to withhold appropria- tions from Cheltenham or replace the present personnel with the prop- er administration: The Rev. R. A. Green, the Rev. John T. Colbert. W. L. Fitzgerald. Carl Murphy. Dr. Ernest Lvon, Thad- deous Copeland, the Rev. W. Н. Ма- nokoo, the Rev. C. H. Stepteau, the Rev. W. H. Thomas. Baptist Ministers The Baptist Ministers' committee includes the following the Rev. W. W. Allen. the Rev. L. J. Reynolds, the Rev. Simon Williamson, the Rev. A. J. Payne, the Rev. N. L. Price, the Rev. A. W. Taylor, the Rev. C. R. But- ler, the Rev. J. J. Taylor, the Rev. Geo. A. Crawlev, the Rev. D. G. Mack, the Rev. C. W. King and the Rev. G. Bryan.

title: BIG BROTHERS REACH 350 IN XMAS TREAT: SOME 208 POUNDS TURKEY INCLUDED IN DELICACIES TO KIDDIES IN INSTITUTION. CLUB IN SIXTH YEAR REMEMBER CHELTENHAM BOYS OTHERS GET CASH
full_text: BIG BROTHERS REACH 350 IN XMAS TREAT Some 208 Pounds Turkey In. cluded In Delicacies To Kiddies In Institution. CLUB IN SIXTH YEAR Remember Cheltenham Boys Others Get Cash Just 208 pounds of turkey was one of the items included in the sixth annual treat of the Big Brothers' Club which dis- tributed its Christmas cheer to 250 children in four institutions for children Friday. The club, of which W. C. McCart 1 is president, reached nearly 350 youngsters in its programi this year St. Mary's Mome for Cirls, St. Kath- erine's Home For Girls, St. Luke's Orphanage, and the Maryland Home For Boys at Catonsville received the turkey and all the trimmings for their Christmas dinners. six Included in the treat were bushels of potatoes, three dozen large mince pies, two bushels sweet pota- toes, 80 pounds of hominy, 16 quarts of cranberries, 40 loaves of bread, 40 pounds of sugar, 20 dozen large oranges, 100 pounds of candy, and 20 dozen large apples. Money To Others The home for the Blind at Overlea received 50 pounds of candy. Twenty- five dollars each went to St. Francis Home For Girls, St. Elizabeth Home, and the Mission Helpers on Biddle street near McCulloh. The boys at Cheltenham received 100 pounds of candy. The treat this year cost more than $300, a sum slightly higher than in past years. Contributions were re- ceived from friends of the club, and the secretary reports that a complete list of their names will be published in January. Among the members prominent in the organization are Carlos Jennings. secretary; Carrington L. Davis, Will White, of the Emerson; John Wheeler. Dr. Bennie Brown. Fear- less Williams, Will Venable, Dr. Harry Brown. Truly Hatchett, Dr. A. O. Reid, Williard Allen, Dr. Τ. S. Hawkins. George B. Murphy. Dr. J. C. Woodland, Dr. H. S. McCard, Charles Shipley, Thomas R. Smith, and William Fitzgerald. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

CLUB IN SIXTH YEAR Remember Cheltenham Boys Others Get Cash Just 208 pounds of turkey was one of the items included in the sixth annual treat of the Big Brothers' Club which dis- tributed its Christmas cheer to 250 children in four institutions for children Friday. The club, of which W. C. McCart 1 is president, reached nearly 350 youngsters in its programi this year St. Mary's Mome for Cirls, St. Kath- erine's Home For Girls, St. Luke's Orphanage, and the Maryland Home For Boys at Catonsville received the turkey and all the trimmings for their Christmas dinners. six Included in the treat were bushels of potatoes, three dozen large mince pies, two bushels sweet pota- toes, 80 pounds of hominy, 16 quarts of cranberries, 40 loaves of bread, 40 pounds of sugar, 20 dozen large oranges, 100 pounds of candy, and 20 dozen large apples. Money To Others The home for the Blind at Overlea received 50 pounds of candy. Twenty- five dollars each went to St. Francis Home For Girls, St. Elizabeth Home, and the Mission Helpers on Biddle street near McCulloh. The boys at Cheltenham received 100 pounds of candy. The treat this year cost more than $300, a sum slightly higher than in past years. Contributions were re- ceived from friends of the club, and the secretary reports that a complete list of their names will be published in January. Among the members prominent in the organization are Carlos Jennings. secretary; Carrington L. Davis, Will White, of the Emerson; John Wheeler. Dr. Bennie Brown. Fear- less Williams, Will Venable, Dr. Harry Brown. Truly Hatchett, Dr. A. O. Reid, Williard Allen, Dr. Τ. S. Hawkins. George B. Murphy. Dr. J. C. Woodland, Dr. H. S. McCard, Charles Shipley, Thomas R. Smith, and William Fitzgerald. . .

title: TRAPPE, MARYLAND
full_text: TRAPPE, MARYLAND • TRAPPE, Md.--The collection to $53.35 Sunday. amounted  Church members motored to Williams- burg, Sunday afternoon for their Woman's Day services. Those who took part on the program from Trappe were: Mrs. Minnie Jenkins, Mrs. R. S. Fassett, Mrs. Ida Cam- per, and Mrs. Elsie B. Cooper, solo. A re- cital was given at the church Tuesday evening by the teachers of the county. Those who took part were: Mrs. H. Nixon, M. Ruff, Miss Bess, Miss. McKinney, P. A. Cooper, Thos. Kemp, Miss Jefferson, Miss Dobson and Miss Armstrong. A repection was tendered the teachers and. Dr. and Mrs. J. W. Jefferson in the Knights Hall by the W. W. Club. Receipts from the reception were $23.64. Mrs. Annie Banks entertained at dinner Sunday. Her guests were the Rev. and Mrs. J. A. Fas- sett. Anthony Fassett. and Miss Emma Brown. Mrs. E. J. Raikes is visiting friends and relatives in Germantown, Pa., and Salem, N. J. Mrs. Georgeanna. Pinkney spent a few days in Ridgely visiting her daughter. Mrs. Lulu Henry. A Mock Conference will be held at the Church, December 29th. Mr. and Mrs. John Waters of Oxford. were the guests of Mr. and Mrs. Wilson Wells. Sunday afternoon. title: PEONAGE TO END AT CHELTENHAM GRAND JURY TOLD: HIRING OUT OF BOYS WILL BE DISCONTINUED AFTER SEPTEMBER 15 WOULD WITHDRAW FINANCIAL SUPPORT STATE SHOULD EITHER CONTROL INSTITUTION OR LEAVE IT ALONE
full_text: PEONAGE TO END AT CHELTENHAM CRAND JURY TOLD Hiring Out Of Boys Will Be Discontinued After Sep- tember 15 WOULD WITHDRAW FINANCIAL SUPPORT State Should Either Control Institution Or Leave It Alone The State of Maryland and the City of Baltimore were called for a showdown at the Cheltenham Reform school in the report of the grand jury filed last week. A special committee appointed to investigate the conditions there in their recommendations say in part that the state should either ap- propriate funds sufficient for the car- rying on of the institution and then see to it that it is properly done or else withdraw all support and estab- lish a State school which will not be operated by private interests. A lack of keen and intelligent in- terest on the part of the city and Hi state, inefficient management and absolute methods in educational and f-institutional work are given as the short comings of the home at the present. No Justification The committee in referring to the practice of hiring boys out say: The by-laws, rules and regulations of your institutions stress the value ON of sound, practical education, school instruction being esteemed as one of the chief means of reclamation. Since our entry into the World War, this part of the work of the Institution has been lost sight of. VO while a pernicious system of hiring out the inmates to farmers in Prince; George's and neighborhood counties has been enforced. The alleged justi- fication for this is that it was neces- sary to help out the farmers during the war. because of the lack of labor. while from that time on the claim is that the ncessities of that insti- tution have been such that the en- forced labor of these boys, hired out to the farmers at 50c and 60c a day in direct competition with higher paid free labor has continued up to the present time. To Discontinue This committee is thankful to state that it is informed by the Superm- tendent and by the Secretary of the Board of Mangers that after ten years this most objectionable method of benefiting the farmers by furnishing them with cheap Negro labor will cease absolutely on September 15. 1927. These officers have admitted that in very many of these cases the boys let out under this system of practical peonage have not receiv- need proper food or lodging and that some of them have been mal-treated. It is a vicious system and should nev- er have been permitted to exist. All Work Of the 275 boys at the institution on Novmber 30. 1926, 121 are listed as general outside workers (presum- ably those farmed out and over whom the institution could have no real oversight): 4 were employed in the broom factory under contract with an outside manufacturer: 40 were p working on farms: 10 were emploved as in the shoe and tailoring shops; 12 in the laundries, and the remainder were utilized in the general work of the institution. Apparently no industrial training of any value is provided and a boy, on leaving the institution. is almost or quite as unfitted to face the world as he was when commit- ted. No Education At All The broom work is done by ma- chinery, every boy doing only a small part of the complete operation, and the work is crude. The boys, who have been farmed out. have received practically no education and those remaining at the institution have re- ceived no more. Our opinion. from what we were able to see. is that there is no edu- cational system whatever. but that the teaching of even the smallest Obous is irregular, desultory and non- productive. We feel that the great opportunity afforded this institution for the reformation and training of the boys has been neglected. State Anpropriations The institution is a private corpo- ration, managed be a board of 16 members. Since 1922. the state has appropriated annually for the sun- port of the institution $15.000. while Baltimore City's annual contribution has ranged from $30.000 to $37.000. For the year ending November 30, 1926. the total receipts. including the above appropriations, were $08.797.51. of of which $6.924 came from the labor of the hous rented out to the farmers: 67.718 from the broom factory, $7.438 from the sale of carden produce, and 3.000 from the enforced sale of live stock to meet present demands. Should Withdraw Support This committee feels strongly that the state and city should support this work adequately, seeing at the same time that it is efficiently con- ducted. If they are unwilling to do so while the institution is in pri- vate hands, they should establish a proner state reformatory for colored boys and finance it properly with- drawing entirely fom the Chelten- ham institution the half-hearted and insufficient support. Dr. Ernest Lvon was the only col- ored member of the investigating committee.  Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

An- thony Cole, age 12, 566 Numsen St.. admitted that he had poured gas- Holine on the floor of a barn in Num- sen St. and ignited it to bring out the engines. The youths gave their names as Richard Jackson, age 9. 513 Numsen St.: Blis B. Bacon. 10. 534 Green- willow St.; Joseph Lee. 10. 918 Pear Alley; Charles Snowden, 565 Oxford, St. WIFE SCREAMS, COPS COME, HUSBAND FINED Officers Find Mrs. Teresa Shearn In Condition From Unconscious Spouse's Blow. A woman's cries for help took po- lice officers to the house at 1103: Mosher Street. where they found! Mrs. Teresa Shearn. Iying in an un- conscious condition on the floor. having been struck in the head with, some unknown instrument by her. husband. James Shearn, Sunday night. Mrs. Shearn was removed to the Colonial Hospital where she was treated for a deep laceration of the scalp and face. The husband was apprehended sometime later and ar- raigned before Magistrate Johann- sen. Monday morning where he was fined $25 and cost. title: M. E. PREACHERS FIND CONTRACT LABOR BAD AT CHELTENHAM: MINISTERS ALSO SURPRISED TO FIND ""TOTS"" UNDER EIGHT YEARS PHYSICAL CONDITION' DESCRIBED AS GOOD LACK OF EDUCATIONAL FACILITIES WILL BE POINTED OUT IN REPORT
full_text: M. E. PREACHERS FIND CONTRACT LABOR BAD AT CHELTENHAM Ministers Also Surprised To Find "Tots" Under Eight Years PHYSICAL CONDITION DESCRIBED AS GOOD Lack Of Educational Facili- ties Will Be Pointed Out In Report Good physical condition, but bad conrtact labor sys- tem, presence of little 'tots' under eight and practically nothing being done to train the inmates for useful lives, was the reaction of several ministers in a group which visited the House of Refor- mation at Cheltenham Mon- day. The delegation of ministers, head- ed by the Rev. R. A. Green, will sub- mit a detailed report of their visit to the regular session of the preach- ers meeting next Monday. Convict Labor One of the worst features of the system, declared the Rev. Mr. Green. is the contract labor wherein the boys are farmed out to work for out- siders and during which time they get no training as should be given in an institution of this kind. But the most surprising thing we met was the presence of mere "tots" un- der eight. who had been sent there. The chairman also stated that while they found the physical cons ditions excellent, the educational sys- tem was practically nihil. "I cannot see." he said. "how any one could expect the lastitution to help the hous sent there in so far as it is designed to train them for useful- ness." Lacks Funds The Rev. W. A. Manokoo stated that while he did not care to give out any detailed statement before the committee set forth its findings he thought the institution needed funds with which to inaugurate a better administration. Those there at present are courteous and seem- ingly conscientious, but they lack funds with which to provide proper training for the boys. The whole delegation got a fav orable impression from the care of the plant itself. he said. but they found little being done for the boys in the way of training. Many Away At Work Commenting on conditions there the Rev. C. H. Stepteau said: "Upon going to the institution I found that many of the older boy's were off at work and I could not learn some things in detail. "The favorable thing that I notic- ed was that the beds were clean and well made up and the dormitories in a tidy state. "The superintendent was courteous and expressed a desire that more visitors should come down to see the! exact state of affairs so that their criticism could be made from per- sonal knowledge. "My adverse criticism would be that there are small boys there around the ages of seven, ten and twelve. Several said they were there because they were regarded as bad and there was no other place to send them. "Many of them do not know how long they are to stay there. Some will be kept until they are 21. "Some of the larger boys who are hired out earn money for themselves! by working overtime. One boy told me that he earned $11 last week. He was about 19 years old. "Considered as a whole. the criti- cism that I have is of small matters." Recommendations The committee will make definite recommendations. said the Rev. Mr.n Green, and will make some further co Investigation as to the presence of the very young boys they found at the institution. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: 20 Cent Loot Sends Boy To Cheltenham
full_text: 20 Cent Loot Sends Boy To Cheltenham With three previous records against him, Ernest Thompson, 15, 610 Ma- deria street, was com.mitted to Chel- tenham School of Reformation when arraigned before Judge Williams, in Juvenile Court, Thursday. Thompson, according to testimony. is a member of a gang of young des- peradoes who have committed nu- merous major crimes in the Eastern District. Late Sunday evening, in company with three other older boys he held up George Capper. 126 Pratt street, at the point of pistols and knives while he was standing at the corner of Pratt and Albermarle street. Then the boys took twenty cents and a car check from the man which was all he had on his person at the time. Police attracted to the scene, chased-the youth and cap- tured Thompson. He will remain in the reformatory until he is 21 years of age.  Afro Visitors Luke McCulloh. Greenville, N. C. Mrs. William E. Thrower, Ridgewood, N. J. Dr. Chas. T. Lansford, Rochester, N. Y. K. Lloyd Bright, New York City. Ted McIntyre. New York City. Ruth Grandeau, New York City. Fay T. H. Casselle, New York City. Inez Bellman Ross, Westminster, Md. Edyth D. Murray, Philadelphia, Pa. Alton C. Berry, New York City. Southern Plantation Singers The Rev. M. L. Cherry, manager: Miss Lizzie Robinson, Miss Beulah Jackson, Miss Jessie M. Wright, Miss Mabel Wright, Miss Ella Waldron, all of Clearwater, Fla.. Book Dates Now For New Grove Mr. Samuel H. Watters announces to the public that he is prepared to book dates for his beautiful grove, Middle River, where church and fra- ternal organizations, private and public parties. may enjoy themselves. Large pavillion for religious meetings or dancing. Boating, fishing and crabbing. For further information write. SAMUEL H. WATTERS Stemmer's Run, Baltimore Co., Md. tf.

title: Youths Held Here
full_text: - Youths Held Here Two youths. James Owens, 19, and Ernest Cannon, 18, 1015 Linden ave- nue, are being held in the Northwest- ern police station, charged with be- ing fugitives from justice, having es- caped from the school of reformation at Cheltenham. The two boys are members of a cang of eight who escaped from the institution in one of the largest wholesale deliveries and escapes of the school in the past three years. title: Cheltenham: When Mayor Malster Named A Whiskey Dealer On Board Of School Deigned To Reform Wayward Boys.
full_text: e ', و Cheltenham When Mayor Malster Named A Whiskey Dealer On Board Of School Deigned To Reform Wayward Boys. To the Editor: No longer a member of the Board of Mana- gers of the House of Reformation, I desire, as I may have leisure, to review some of the phases of the work of that institution. In this. I shall only indicate the particular incident which first fixed my attention upon that institution. During the year 1898 I was the publisher and Editor of "The Ledger," a weekly publica- tion of this city. At that time both the city and state were under Republican rule. Lloyd Lowndes was the Governor, and William T. Malster, was Mayor. In the issue of "The Ledger" March 12, 1898, there was this Editorial: * * THE MISTAKE OF MAYOR MALSTER Some weeks ago the Mayor of Baltimore ap- 3pointed two white citizens as the city's mana- gers of the Board of the Cheltenham House of Reformation for Colored Boys. As soon as the appointments were announced, through the press, the attention of Mayor Malster was called to the seeming humiliation which such an act nec- essarily implied with respect to the many qualifi- ed and capable colored citizens of this communi- ty. The Mayor left the impression upon the gen- tlemen who directed his attention to the matter, that he would most gladly rectify the mistake at some convenient time. To our great amaze- ment and surprise, we were informed during the present week. through the daily press, that one of the appointees had resigned, and immediate- ly following this information is the statement that the Mayor had appointed a prominent white politician in his stead, if we mistake not, a gentleman who is a WHISKEY DEALER. WHISKEY DEALER : ( It may not, of course, be unlawful to appoint ( a white man, a whiskey dealer, as a manager of a House of Reformation for Colored Boys, but we feel sure that the sentiment among the Col- 1 ored People of this city is certainly adverse to such an appointment. But our main objection is not that that the man appointed is a whiskey dealer but we do maintain, most earnestly, that the best interest of the city of Baltimore, as well as that of those detained in that Refor- matory institution, seem to require some colored men on the Board of Managers. ת น 0 V There is no good reason in the world why colored men should be left off such a board sole- ly because they are COLORED. The work of the institution is decidedly moral. The moral na- tures of the unfortunate colored boys of that institution can be more successfully reached by it good, reliable honest and reputable colored men than is possible by white men. INSULT 1 ! Whether Mr. Malster intended it or not yet his action is a direct insult to the intelligence 1 Is and capability of the many able, refined and worthy colored men of Baltimore City." n n 1, * i All this was in the days when the masses: d of our people were blindly attached to the pic-1 ture of Abrham Lincoln on the Republican ticket. Two years later, although the masses of our group still stuck to Malster, there was a small tremnant of us who helped to defeat him, by voting for Hayes, Wilmer. and Smith. But, just a few months before the beginning of the Hayes administration, the Board of Managers, them- 1 selves, fully conversant with the views of this writer, upon the recommendation of the late Mr. Golsborough Griffith, unanimously elected him t to membership on the Board, and he was the first member of the colored group to sustain membership on that board. 1 1 1 TWENTY-SEVEN YEARS Π 0 11 S fi G For twenty-seven years this writer has re- mained steadfast in the views expressed in the a editorial above quoted. He has lost no oppor- t! tunity in making manifest such views before b the members of that board. But during that time there have been great changes in the perso- di nality of that board, and a less liberally dispos- n ed sentiment obtains towards our group. Thus, gl it was to be expected that the discordant ele- se ment would be eliminated. ti When, a few months after we had become a W member of the Board, Mayor Hayes determined a to move along the line indicated, he was stren- uously opposed, he was resolute and determined. ar MAYOR'S LETTER m To quote from Mayor Hayes' letter, Decem- of ber 9, 1901: "I am in receipt of your favor of the 6th in- ha stant. It gives me a great deal of pleasure to its read your letter, and see that you agree with the position I take in reference to the Board G of Directors of the House of Reformation for Colored Children. I was determined to do it, notwithstanding the attempted effort to frighten me away from it. I shall send names in to- pa night." The nmaes sent in, and promptly confirmed. Sn were Gen. Thomas J. Shryock, and Harry S. Jo Cummings. GEORGE F. BRAGG. bu S t Sn (

title: CHELTENHAM BOYS NOW WORK ON STATE ROAD: AUTHORITIES WITHDRAW CHARGES FROM FARMS; INAUGURATE NEW PEONAGE NEW RECOMM'ND'TIONS IN GRAND JURY REPORT BROOM SHOP IS FIRE TRAP; MELVALE GETS OKEY
full_text: CHELTENHAM BOYS NOW WORK ON STATE ROAD Authorities Withdraw Char- ges From Farms; Inaugu- rate New Peonage NEW RECOMM'ND'TIONS IN GRAND JURY REPORT Broom Shop Is Fire Trap; Melvale Gets Okey That the promises of the directors of the Reform School for Colored Boys at Cheltenham to discontinue hiring boys out to private farmers in Prince George County has merely lead them to take a more obr jectionable form of peo- nage, is revealed in the re- port of the Grand Jury, handed in to Judge Gorter, Saturday. So much howl was raised by the press, members of the city council; and others interested in the humane treatment of boys, committed to the institution, over the method of hir- ing the boys out into veritable slavery that the management promised that this procedure would no longer con- tinue, and more stress would be placed on the educational develop- ment of the inmates. ! Now On Roads Many of the boys were recalled from the farms in accordance with the promise. but the fact that they were only let out in a worse form of servitude is shown in the recent re- port which says that of the 390 boys supposed to be in the institution less than 100 were found on the grounds on the occasion of the visit of a special committee sent by the Grand Jury to investigate conditions. Mr. W. R. Janny, secretary-treas- urer of the reformatory, informed. them that the boys were working on the state road, the report says. Broom Factory Fire Trap In spite of the fact that each grand jury for the past three years has recommended changes in the broom factory where a large number of boys are employed, the present committee again brands the place as a fire trap, which has been the complaint of all previous commit-: tees. The committee, like their predeces- sors, recommend the retirement of Suprintendent Pyles, who because of age and infirmities, is unable to properly supervise the institution. The place was found to be unsani- tary and the classrooms were pгас- tically empty. Maryland Home For Boys Conditions found at Cheltenham were quite different from those re- ported by the committee which vis- ited the Maryland Training School for Boys at Lochraven, where white incorrigibles are sent. E "We found boys in different grades: and classes at their studies and int the vocational department," the re- port roads. We were pleased with the appearance and general behavior of the boys. Upon visiting the cot- tages where the boys make their home we were impressed with the art rangement of the furniture. Every- thing was in good taste. t | "The boys form' untis in the diff- erent cottages in a military fashion which are commanded by officers elected by themselves. Of the 230 C boys at the school not one was in C the hospital, which we found in ex- cellent condition. a S "The high degree of health speaks well for the authorities who by gir- ing wholesome food and providing [ proper recreation and sanitation show that they have the welfare of the boys at heart." a J Melvale Given O. K. The Melvale School for Girls also received praise because of the whole- some conditions which prevail. The place was said to be clean and the c girls were pleasantly occupied and apparently happy, the report says. P f The Grand Jury recommended that the practice of holding state's witnesses in jail pending the trial of an accused person be discontinued because it was unfair to the witness i as in many cases the prisoner wast out on pail. A more vigilant inspection of the-c atres by health authorities was also t recommended. The practice of y subletting stalls in city markets was asked to be discontinued. The ma"- I ket authorities should lease stalls direct to the stallkeeper instead of to individuals who in turn lease the property and necessitate the stall-d keepers charging higher prices. a c 1 1 t C a Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.
