Welfare Organizations Branded As Meddlers In Domestic Affairs For the first time in the history of the institution the Grand Jury returned a favorable report on school work at the Reform School for Colored Boys at Chel- tenham. According to the report of the committee which visited the institu- tion on March 1, all boys under the age of 16 were found in class rooms here and were being instructed in regular-organized classes by appar- ently competent teachers. "The instructors seemed gentle- manly and efficient and lacked all semblance of the 'hard-boiled' type generally found in such institutions," the report says. Learn Trades Older boys were found at work in the tailor shop under a practical tailor and seemed contented in their work. Other youths were found in the shoe shop, where they were be- ing ably taught. The committee recommended the abolishment of the broom factory and advised that a practical carpen- ter be placed in the carpenter shop. which should be more modernly equipped. Skimmed Miik Only There were 276 boys placed at the institution during the year, three of which died from tuberculosis. In spite of the fact that there are 30 cows on the farms, the boys get very little milk and that only of the skimmed variety, the committee was informed. It was recommended that more nurishing food be given and that the inmates be given stews of beef or mutton at least once a day. Women Attendants Bread, which is baked in the school oven. was found to be of a very in- ferior character. It was recommend- ed to the Grand Jury that women 1 'be employed in place of some of the men to care for the smaller boys and while the plan was well thought of.i the body did not endorse the change. The jury returned a favorable re-1 port on the House of Good Shepherd i and the Training School For Girls at Melvale. I Score Alimony Practice The Grand Jury looked with dis favor on the methods employed bys welfare organizations in handling a non-support cases. 2 "While few cases have been dis- umissed without due consideration," the report reads, "we find that there t As a tendency among young women to parade their husbands in court on the slightest excuse. And while those 1 1. who appear before the body always e plead poverty and distress, very few L are found to be in that predicament. t g "There is too much interference r front outside organizations and peo-s ple who take it upon themselves to s settle other people's troubles." Foreign Born Menace The foreign born element is a source of lawlessness and because of ti their distorted views of life which a they put into practice, they are as menace to law-abiding citizens. C The ignarance found in this ele-ir ment makes them fit and fruitful re-h cruits for the formation of organized F criminal gangs. The Grand Jury also advised the granting of fewer paroles and more rigid treatment in the penitentiary because under present conditions criminals have no fear of returning to the institution. st Di Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: Mr. Langfield is Suggested for a Place On the Board of Managers at Cheltenham.
full_text: Mr. Langfield is Suggested for a Place On the Board of Managers at Cheltenham. To the Editor: I just returned from Europe. I see they still have rum brutes at Cheltenham Reform School. After my brother and I wrote to the judges and managers about the brutes and their method of treatment, we had been inmates and had worked on farms down in southern Maryland and know how these officials would go to Smith's sa- loon three and four times a week, fill up and sleep their drunk off at the bar. Yet the Board of Managers think they are good enough to re- form boys. They need reforming themselves. Ninety per cent of the boys now discharged from there go to jail or penitentiary. Boys receive no education and little or no Christian instruc- tion. When I was there we had a priest of the Marl- boro Church, a priest of the Episcopal church and a Methodist preacher to come and preach and give religious teachings and Pyles got rid of the priests and only hired his preacher of the Methodist church so the pay would go to help pay the preacher's salary. I hear he got rid of Mr. Langfield. one of the best men ever there. What I am, and brother also. we credit to Mr. Langfield's good counsel and treatment. But officers were jealous and spiteful toward Mr. Langfield because he was offered the assistant superintendency. What we want on the Board of Managers is men like Mr. Langfield, who has had fifty years' experience and would help to improve the school. We have plenty intelligent colored men capable to run the place as it should be run. What good are the county managers? They come on the place only at election times or to get boys to work their farms. What does am McGruder know about the place and how it is managed. My consin carries a scar on his head where an officer known as "Brandywine," Whiskey, Tar Pot, struck him with a monkey wrench. The fellow never was fit to be an officer at the school. Too fond of his rum. Too much of a brute. Perhaps it will be well to build a new reform school, say, on the Eastern Shore. Let all church- es and sell Cheltenham to the government for a place for bootleggers or for insane people. It is a failure as a reformation for our boys. Fifty and one hundred lashes on a boy only hardens him. That is why so many turn out bad and go to jail or penitentiary. Good men who desire to help boys to lead a better life are not desired under running officials. One who can use brute force is most desirable and praised. We are going to write to every judge in the county in Maryland and tell them the boys can receive better treatment at the penitentiary than at the ouse of Reformation. WILLIAM FREEMAN. TOM FREEMAN. Nelson And Rainey Qualified To Head Up Na- tional Work Of Democratic Campaign Com- .mittee. New Yorkers Should Put Aside Their Hammer. To the Editor:- Just a word for Robert J. Nelson of Wil- mington and Washington and Julian D. Rainey of Boston. I have known both very well, Nel- son for many years, and Rainey ever since he quit Norfolk. From super Harlem has come the usual "squawk" of Presidential years against "outsiders" managing a Presidential campaign from New York headquarters. Hitherto, it has been about Negroes invited to help run Repub- lican campaigns. Now it also is against Nelson and Rainey, Democrats, and "Al Smithites." Mr. Nelson is the only Negro extant who ever ran a campaign from the satrt to defeat a Con- gressman for deliberately offending Negro citi- zens and succeeded in his purpose. A U.S. Sen- ator went down in the crash, as Nelson warned him would be the case. Hence the elevation to the upper Branch of Congress of Thomas F. Bayard of Delaware, second of the Bayards to serve in the Senate, and the end of Congress- men Layton's political career. Shoulder to shoulder has Nelson fought seg- regation in the Government departments here with Neval Thomas and Arch Pinkett. They are the triple-Nemses of Cabinet officers in whose jurisdictions segregation flourished. It was as head of the Elks' Civil liberties Bureau that Nelson put the power of that greatest or- ganization behind the anti-segregation fight. Additionally, he has made a Smith Democrat of Neval Thomas, best asset and "bally-ho" among the forthcoming campaign orators. As for Rainey: In 15 years this boy has come from a dining car through law school and the Great War (which he entered as a private and from which he emerged a lieutenant) to a lucra- tive law practice in Boston-the "William H. Lewis" of his generation. Despite his hankering after public office in law-school days, Rainey could not afford to accept an assistant district attorney-ship from the District Attorney he help- ed elect. He has taken into his office from a dining car a young, graduate lawyer, whom he recently sent into the U. S. Court at Boston to win a deportation case against the Government; in 1926 he managed the colored end of the guber- natorial campaign of the late noble and wealthy William A. Gaston, who was defeated, and of David I. Walsh, who was not As Nelson is "Bob" Nelson to thousands of voters, so Rainey is "Jule," to numberless young voters. The Harlemites contend, nevertheless, that only a New York Afric is entitled to manage a Democratic Presidential campaign, as they are original Tammany Democrats. Carried to logi- cal sequence, ultimately, such a contention would forbid Gov. Smith naming Mr. Raskob, hitherto not a Democrat, as Chairman of the Democratic National Committee. But Mr. Raskob is Chair- man! New Yorkers react to the provinces and pro- vincials in politcs just as they do in professional baseball-14 other big league ball clubs exists only to carry out schedules to fetch the penants and the World's series to New York-so national campaign managers must be New Yorkers, des- pite the fact that they cannot carry their own precints. When Aldermen Moore and Hawkins were elected to the Board of Aldermen as Re- publicans from Harlem the fact automatically eliminated Harlem's Afric Dems from National consideration in the matter of managing political campaigns. A leadership whih cannot elect an Alderman certainly cannot elect a President. RIENZI B. LEMUS. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Chicago Judge Is 56
full_text: Chicago Judge Is 56 mAlberta Ballert. Georgian Albert Bailey George, judge of the since 1924, who will be fifty-six years old, Wed- nesday, October 23. He was born in Washington, D.C., in 1873 and educated at Miner high school and the Spencerian Business College, both in Washing- ton. and North- western univer- sity. He mar- ried Miss Maude J. Roberts and there is one child, Albert Roberts. Judge George began his career as clerk in the office of James B. Raymond, alderman in Altoona, Pa., in 1895.  STABBED IN DICE GAME Emory Taylor, 28, 328 N. Bond street, is in the St. Joseph Hospital in a serious condition suffering from stab wounds of the head and chest inflicted by Leon Jackson, alias "Bloodgood," 1300 block Jefferson street, during an altercation over a dice game in front of the former's hcme, Saturday.  CHELTENHAM RUNAWAY James Crowner, 16, 822 N. Fremont avenue, who was arrested on descrip- tion, is being held in the Northwest- ern police station charged with being a runaway from the Cheltenham Re- form School for boys. 10 BOY STRUCK BY HIT-AND-RUN MACHINE; INJURED Theodore Ford, nine, 1809 Madison avenue, had his left leg and knee in- jured when he was struck by a hit- and-run driver while crossing Myrtle | avenue and Saratoga street, Sunday. title: WINNIE WINKLE GIVES PROGRAM
full_text: WINNIE WINKLE GIVES PROGRAM They say that the first impression is lasting. Well, we are quite sure that the Winnie Winkle made a very favorable Im- pression when they rendered their first program at Sharon Baptist Young People's Union Sunday. The participants consisted largely of Douglass High School students and also members of Enon Baptist Church. Ruth Watts. Dorothy Douglass and Doro- thy Green were participants from the club. Dorothy Douglass and Mary Hall presided. 0 Boy Released From Cheltenham On Writ Sent to Cheltenham reformatory on charges of being a minor without proper care, Robert Arkers, 723 Har- lem avenue, was released from the school after a hearing in Criminal Court on the charges, Monday. The boy, testimony showed, was committed by Judge Williams of Ju- venile Court on that charge although the law was repealed several years ago. He was represented by Attor- ney Josiah Henry. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

full_text: VOTE FOR WILLIAM P. JACKSON FOR NATIONAL: REPUBLICAN COMMITTEEMAN STATE PRIMARY-MONDAY, MAY 7, 1928 Under Mayor William F. Broening, the following colored men have been placed in positions which have brought prestige and honor to the race: ARTHUR E. BRISCOE, Assistant City Solicitor. WILLARD W. ALLEN, member of the Zoning Commission. T. WALLIS LANSEY, member of Charities Board. DR. HOWARD E. YOUNG, member of Jail Board. JOSEPH P. EVANS, member of Cheltenham Board The reform school for colored boys. The following named have been made foremen in city departments: JOHN W. BADGER, CHARLES C. HALL AND SAMUEL JOHNSON. These men have approximately one hundred men working under them, in addi- tion thereto, one hundred colored men have been placed in the street cleaning depart- ment of the city. The above named gentlemen endorse and ask your suffrage at the polls Monday, May 7th for Wm. P. Jackson, who is running for National Republican Committeeman from the State of Maryland, and the delegates pledged to support him. Published by aut hority of R. FRANK SMITH. Broening, the following colored men have been placed in positions which have brought prestige and honor to the race: ARTHUR E. BRISCOE, Assistant City Solicitor. WILLARD W. ALLEN, member of the Zoning Commission. T. WALLIS LANSEY, member of Charities Board. DR. HOWARD E. YOUNG, member of Jail Board. JOSEPH P. EVANS, member of Cheltenham Board The reform school for colored boys. The following named have been made foremen in city departments: JOHN W. BADGER, CHARLES C. HALL AND SAMUEL JOHNSON. These men have approximately one hundred men working under them, in addi- tion thereto, one hundred colored men have been placed in the street cleaning depart- ment of the city. The above named gentlemen endorse and ask your suffrage at the polls Monday, May 7th for Wm. P. Jackson, who is running for National Republican Committeeman from the State of Maryland, and the delegates pledged to support him. Published by aut hority of R. FRANK SMITH. title: Winston-Salem Man Sought, for Estate
full_text: Winston-Salem Man Sought, for Estate Local policemen were reuested to help locate Ellwood Murriel, who has been in Baltimore a little over five years. Relatives are seeking the man to settle an estate of a relative who died recently. Any one know- ing his whereabouts will please no- tify his sisters, Miss Kate Murriel, 1470 N. Main street, Winston-Salem, N.C. (0 WEIGHTS NOT SEALED Three storekeepers, Charles Thom- as, 52, 918 Calhoun streets; Wash- ington Jones, 34, 1100 N. Carey street; Howard Gibson, 53, 1520 Riggs ave- nue. were fined $10 and costs each on charges of using scales not sealed or tested when arraigned in the Northwestern police court, Friday. () CHELTENHAM BOY, RUNAWAY Augustus Fleet, 19, 27 N. Register street, is being held in the North- eastern police station charged with being a runaway from the Chelten- ham Reform School. title: Cheltenham Lad Takes Life with Shot Gun
full_text: Cheltenham Lad Takes Life with Shot Gun ONION BRIDGE. Md. -Robert An- derson, 16, farmed out from Cheiten- ham Reform School to Guy Carlisle. white, committed suicide by shooting a hole in his chest with a shot gun. He fastened the gun in the wall and fired it with a stick. The boy was an crphan. Whether the cruel- ties of Cheltenham still rankled, can- not be learned. Virginians Wed in D.C, WASHINGTON. D. C.-Nine per- sons from Virginia were granted li- censes to wed here last week. They were: Samuel Page, 25, and Miss Mary . E. Porter, 22, of Charlottesville, Va., by the Rev. 17. D. Jarvis. Joseph Washby, 33. and Miss Victoria Johnson, 19, of Alexandria. Va.. the Rev.! W. Westray officiating. John Morris, 35, and Miss Annie Eubanks;; 28. Richmond, Va., the Rev. Wm. D. Jarvis officiating. Edgar D. Bass. 26. this city and Miss Marie Fields, 21, of West Point. Va., by the Rev. W. A. Jones. Fasco D. Maylor, 42, and Miss Roha A. Beverly. 36, of Manassas, Va. () DePriest at Princeton PRINCETON, N.J. - Congressman Oscar DePriest told an audience of 300 composed of both races that America should give the. Negro not only equal opportunity but equal rights. The meeting was held in the Alex- ander Hall of Princeton University Out-of-town guests included: Mr. and Mrs. John Lewis, of Morristown. Pa.: Mrs. Charles B. Johnson, and Mrs. Serena Shackleford. of Atlantic City; W. A. Valentine, Bordentown; N.J.: Dr. C. Defrinta, of Plainfield. Mr. and Mrs. William H. Young and Mrs. Herritt Carr. of Hackensack: Mr. and Mrs. W. M. Richardson and Mrs. Millie Brown. of Philadelphia: and. Captain W. W. Carr, of New York. Mrs. Elizabeth B. Mention is, head of the Women's Club which spor ored the meeting. t DePRIEST IN JERSEY JERSEY CITY. N.J.- Congress. man Oscar DePriest delivered an ad- dress at St. Mark's A.M.E. Zion Church Monday night, the Rev. E. S. Hardge, pastor. After the meeting the distinguished visitor, accompan- ied by his wife, were dinner guests C. Bion Jones and Mrs. Ethel Field, and guests of honor at a re- ception given by the Citizens Club.

title: GOOD MORNING JUDGE
full_text: GOOD MORNING JUDGE Some women may be sweet enough to eat but it cost James Wesley, of Annapolis, $25 and costs for a bite of his sweetie. Miss Julia Walker, 221 S. Bethel street, Thursday. An officer passing in front of the young woman's home heard her scream and went to her rescue. At the Eastern police station they told the story. "Judge." declared Wesley, "we did have a little argument when I came up to spend 'Thanksgiving with her and found that another fellow was to take her to a party, but we made up when I bit her; it was such a lit- tle bite." "No. Judge, there wasn't any trouble much, only he took a bite out of me for love," claimed Miss Walker| in defense of her lover. "Just the same." declared the offi- cer. "it took a stitch to close the wound." "She may be a sweet girl," return- ed the magistrate, but you can't bite ed the magistrate, "but you can't bite these sweet women here. You tock a $10 bite but I am going to fine you $25 and costs, which includes payment for everything except Julia's Iying and she is going to pay $2.45 for that." 0 BIG BROTHERS MAKE APPEAL FOR ORPHANS Solicit Contributions For Annual Christmas Dinner to Orphans The Big Brothers Association is making an appeal at this time to Baltimoreans to contribute toward their annual Christinas treat to or phans of the city, and to the boys at Cheltenham. Along with the other various wel- fare agencies of the city the Big B Brothers give Christmas treats to the orphanages. Last year they contrib- 3 uter toward the Christmas dinner at St. Francis Academy, on Chase street, - St. Elizabeth's home at Overlea, the Mission Helpers at Biddle and McCul- loh streets, giving $25 to each institu- tion. The boys at Cheltenham re- ceived 100 pounds of candy and the - little blind children at Overlea 50 pounds. They are asking the public to give contributions toward this year's treat which they hope to make larger than ; that of previous years. Dr. Benjamin B. Browne is chairman. George B. Murphy, treasurer, and Carlos C. Jen- nings, secretary. ; 0 Truck Slips on Hill; Breaks Show Window The show window of the drug store, operated by Dr. Henderson Kerr, 723 George street, was con- pletely demolished when a coal truck of the Emerson Morgan Company, 5 which skidded back into the store ,while attempting to climb the George street hill, Monday morning. t Woman Is Overcome 1 by Escaping Gas 3 Mrs. Attie Wiles, 35. 1715 Laurens street, was overcome by gas, which 1 escaped from a leaky heater, while asleep in her home, Monday. 1Mrs. Wiles was found in the gas- -filled room by her daughter, Amelia Jackson. Mrs. Wiles was taken to the 1 West Baltimore General Hospital, - where she was treated and allowed 3 to return home. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: ASK LEGISLATURE TO PROBE CHELTENHAM: MELVIN L. FINE, 4TH DISTRICT DELEGATE, INTRODUCES RESOLUTION IN HOUSE WANTS STATE CONTROL ABUSES AND INEFFICIENCY AT PRIVATE REFORMATORY CITED
full_text: ASK LEGISLATURE TO e or may take such action and rec- ommend in his budget such amounts as will be necessary and advisable to put the institution on an efficient PROBE CHELTENHAM working basis. Melvin L. Fine, 4th District Delegate, Introduces Reso- lution In House WANTS STATE. CONTROL Abuses And Inefficiency At Private Reformatory Cited Citing long: nding inefficien- cy, lack of facilities and abuses, and asking that the State take over the control of the institution and conduct it in the interest of the inmates, Delegate Melvin L. Fine, from the Fourth District of Baltimore, introduced a resolu- tion asking a searching investi- gation of the House of Reforma- tion for Colored Coys at Chel- tenham, Tuesday. The resolution which asks that a committee be appointed also asks that it report back to the legislature before March 18, 1919, 'hat the Gov- Cites Abuses In his resolution Mr. Fine cites abuses and bad management of the institution covering a long period as revealed by various Grand Jury re- ports., Special mention is made of the fact that the original object of the institution - that of giving the boys such "school instruction" and education as would fit them to take their rightful place in life - was be- ing sadly neglected. It also called attention to the "pernicious system" of hiring out the boys on farms, and declares that under the past regime the boys have left the institution in worse condition than when they en tered. 4. Although appropriations of $15,000 made by the state and from $30,000 and $37,000 by this city are reported each year, the institution under priv- ate management has been inefficient- lv handled and Mr. Fine's resolution asks that the State take direct charge and supervision in order that a re- sponsive management may be insti- tuted.

title: One Race Delegate at W.C.T.U. Meet: Baltimorean Attends National Convention in Indianapolis.
full_text: One Race Delegate at W.C.T.U. Meet Baltimorean Attends National Con- vention in Indianapolis. The National W.C.T.U. convention met at Indianapolis at which time there were 1050 bona fide delegates. one of whom was Mrs. Violet H. Whyte, of this city, national field worker among Negroes. Prizes were awarded to members! whose records showed the most el- ficiency in their work. Two prizes went to Mrs. Whyte one medal for securing a hich ner cent of Negro members during the year; one ten- dollar check for outstanding achieve.. ment along lines of social imorality. Addresses 5000 During the conference, Mrs. Whyte addressed audiences at the churches, the Murat Theatre. Crispas Attucks High School and at the convention hall where it was estimated that she reached over 5000 people of both. races. Statements of work accomplishe-l while in attendance in Indianapolis: Addresses. Simpson M.E. Church, on Lavy Observance: Mt. Perrin Baptist Church, on W.C.T.U. Iocals: Murat Theatre, on Educational Wor's Amon? Negroes: Crispus Attrucks High, on the Meaning of Temperance and How You May Help: Mass Meeting at Club House, on Our New Mehods: and addresses at points suburban to the city. A summart of the work shows: sis adresses, twenty confer- ences with workers and forty-tyo homes visited. 0 KNOCKED DOWN BY AUTO Mrs. Henrietta Johnson, 39, 833 Aisquith street. sustained minor lac- erations and contusions of the face and body when she was knocked down by a bicvale at Lexington and Park avenue, Friday ()- BOY. 7. title: RITCHIE ASKS $20,000 FOR CHELTENHAM: MESSAGE TO LEGISLATURE, HOWEVER, SILENT ON EQUALIZING SALARIES AVERAGE NOW $620 FOR RACE TEACHERS WHILE AVERAGE FOR WHITE TEACHERS IS $1,150 IN ELEMENTARY SCHOOLS
full_text: RITCHIE ASKS $20,000 FOR CHELTENHAM - Message To Legislature, However, Silent On, E- qualizing Salaries AVERAGE NOW $620 FOR RACE TEACHERS While Average for White Teachers Is $1,150 In Elementary Schools Appropriations of $20,- 000 for the House of Re- formation for Colored Boys at Cheltenham and $10,000 for the girl's home at Mel- vale were recommended in the message of Governor Albert C. Ritchie to the opening session of the State legislature. Other apropriations recommended included new buildings, heating plant and water supply at Crowns- ville to cost $210.000; increased ap- propriation for Henryton and other state institutions. Equilization Of Salaries Although attention is called in the report to the increase in average elementary state school salaries from $359 in 1920 too $620 in 1928. nothing is promised with reefrence to equalization of white and colored teacher salaries. During the same period from 1920 to 1928 white salary averages in- creased from $631 to $1.150. the mes- sage says. During this period the average daily atendance in colored schools increased practically tvo full months and the percentage of teach- ers having had four years high school work plus two years of normal school instruction has increased from 20er cent to 82 ner cent. Interracial Report It is expected that the Governor's Inter-Racial Commission will make an independent report to the legis- lature. Wo:. at Mo an College is touch- ed upon and commended. title: Say Youth Looted Paper Cash Boxes
full_text: Say Youth Looted Paper Cash Boxes  Police are keeping a watchout for a gang of youths who are said to have tampered with and robbed 90 honor system paper boxes, last week. The youth who are known to police are believed to be working under the direction of older boys.  Authorities of the Cheltenham Re- formatory notified police to appro- hend Bernard Robinson, alias “peaches” and hold him as an escapee from that institution. The youth is described as being 15 years of age, 5 feet one inch tall, 115 pounds in weight, gingercake complexion, brown eyes, and with a scar on his upperlip. At the time of his escape the boy was wearing the customary blue uniform pants with a brown jumper, black shoes and no hat.

title: GRAND JURY RE-VISITS REFORM SCHOOL: BODY WOULD ASK CHANGES AT CHELTENHAM GRAND JURY COMMITTEE FORMULATES REPORT FOLLOWING VISIT. STATE FAVOR COLORED TEACHERS AND OTHER CHANGES.
full_text: GRAND JURY RE-VISITS REFORM SCHOOL BODY WOULD ASK CHANGES AT CHELTENHAM Grand Jury Committee Formulates Report Following Visit. STATE CONTROL' Favor Colored Teachers and Other Changes. Another grand jury report will recommend sweeping changes at the House of Re- formation for Colored Boys at Cheltenham, it was indicated in a committee hearing of that body Tuesday evening in the grand jury room. The conference, into which was called a number of citizens to consid- er the matter, was headed by Grand Juryman C. M. Falconer, who headed the special committee which visited the institution on an inspection trip last Wednesday. Some Improvements While a number of improvements were cited by the chairman, including the abolishment of the old practice of farming out boys for work, and the achievement of better physical conditions, it was stated that under the present management it was fail- ing to meet the problems of a reform school and that sweeping changes were needed. The chief criticism brought out at the meeting was to the effect that teaching and industrial activities there were not preparing the boys for citizenship. Statistics show that a large percentage of the boys leaving Cheltenham keep straight on to jail and the penitentiary under the pres- ent regime. It was also brought out that this is the result with boys taken there, not as delinquents, but be- cause their families break up or die and they become wards of the com- munity. Politics was also blamed for failure to get a change of management at the institution. Race Teachers The discussion indicated that the Grand Jury report would recommend among other things, race teachers, state control of the institution, or the shutting off of municipal funds until conditions are remedied. The Grand Jury Cheltenham com- mittee comprises: C. M. Falconer, chairman; James E. Preston, Jr., Herbert A. Gillespie, William H. Crane. Frederick P. Steiff (all white), and W. Emmett Coleman. Called into the conference were Dr. Howard Young, Miss Georgianna Fields. R. Maurice Moss, Probation Officer George Ralph and Willian. N. Jones. 0 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: MAN LEFT SICK WIFE; SHE GETS DIVORCE: WAS IN BED ILL WHEN SPOUSE LEFT HOME, WOMAN DECLARES. HE IS IN NEW YORK MRS. FLOSSIE MONROE OBTAINS DECREE IN LOCAL COURT.
full_text: MAN LEFT SICK WIFE; SHE GETS DIVORCE Was in Bed Ill when Spouse Left Home, Woman De- clares. - HE IS IN NEW YORK Mrs. Flossie Monroe Obtains Decree in Local Court. Testifying that her husband, Albert W. Monroe, Brooklyn, deserted her while she lay ill in bed, for the love of another woman, Mrs. Flossie M. Monroe, 2411 Etting Street, was granted an absolute divorce in the circuit cour', Friday. In her statement to the examiner, Mrs. Monroe declared that she was married in Brooklyn, N.Y., on De- cember 18, 1926. During the period they lived together, her husband treated her with exceeding cruelty, and would stay away from home for several days at a time. She was taken ill on February 26, 1927, and on learning of her illness he stayed away five days. When he returned on March 1, he took his clothing, declaring that he was in love with another woman. Relatives who testified for Mrs. Monroe corroborated her statements. There were no children. Mrs. Monroe was granted the resumption of her maiden name, Flossie M. DeLoach. 0 STABBED BY UNKNOWN According to a report he filed at the Western police station, John Tal- lor, 1012 Morton Street, was stabbed in the left leg, Sunday, by an un- identified man while walking through Lexington Market near Eutaw Street. 0 Cheltenham Boy Held Said by police to have escaped from the House of Reformation, Solomon King, 20, 1031 W. Saratoga Street, is being held in the Southwestern po- lice station pendnig an investigation by welfare workers. 0- KICKED MAN IN FACE WASHINGTON., - Pueara Brock- man, 22. of 2025 Thirteenth Street, is suffering a broken jaw as the re- sult of a fight with Rema Robinson. Sunday afternoon, when he was kick- ed in the face. Brockman was treat- ed at the Emergency Hospital for the fracture and a warrant for Rob- inson was secured. title: Letter to the Editor 5 -- No Title
full_text: Same Cheltenham Reports, But No Action, Says the Reverend Mr. Bragg; and He Suggests a Plan. To the Editor: According to the Baltimore Sun of August 24, 1898, adverse criticisms of the management of Cheltenham have been made, dating back to that early period. The Sun of that date, said: 1 The grand jury committee on the House of Reformation for Colored Boys, at Chel- tenham, submitted a special report yester- day commending the management and con- dition of that institution, but stating that the punishments inflicted on the youthful in- mates ha have been too severe. "We regret to say, that in our opinion," the. report states, "the punishments have been too severe, better suited for hardened adult criminals than for incorrigible youths. We are pleased to report that the management have promised to modify this somewhat by doing away with the more severe methods. "In this connection one of the great needs of the institution is a strong guard build- ing, where_uncontrollable inmates may safe- ly be confined under proper police protec- tion. "We believe that kind treatment and moral suasion will do more to serve the purpose of the institution than harsh and severe pun- ishments." For more than a third of a century these criti- cisms have been made, and yet the whole situa- tion is entirely unsatisfactory. Now, my suggestion is just this: Let our preachers' meetings and other organizations pass resolutions requesting the Hon. Wm. Cabell Bruce, and the Hon. M. A. Soper, to take the leadership in this matter, and direct it in the most effective way of accomplishing results. In a matter of this kind Senator Bruce will do for us gladly what we could not pay him to do for us as an attorney. In a matter of this character we need the lead- ership of men of the class we have named. And that class of men would willingly do more for us if they could only feel that such services would be duly appreciated by the colored race. As a matter of fact, in a recent personal letter received from Senator Bruce, among other things, he said: I am, indeed, eager that full justice should. be done, in every respect, to my colored fel- low citizens, and I can not see how any pub- lic man who has the slightest pretensions to the character of a statesman could be other- wise. I do not have to go outside of my own household to find illustrations of the high level of moral worth on which the lives of some of my colored fellow citizens move. Let our public spirited men and women, in- terested in improved conditions for our delin- quent colored boys and girls, address a line to Senator Bruce and Judge Soper, conscripting them for such public service on behalf of those I of the community who need it most. Baltimore. GEO. F. BRAGG, Jr. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Two Bad Spots
full_text: hav gyms and none has a yard big enough for an athletic field or a running track. This is the ugly side. However, reform and improvement are around the corner. The Urban League, which asked Mr. Atwell to survey the city's recreation facili- ties, or lack of them, has sponsored also an in- terracial committee which includes among its members, Mrs. Marie Bauernschmidt and Mrs. John Wesley Brown, white, City Councilman Walter S. Emerson, and Dean John W. Hayward of Morgan College. They have assumed the job of changing the attitude of the city towards the free-time ac- tivities of the average boy and girl, and their successful handling of this situation means that Baltimore will have fewer delinquents to send to Cheltenham and to Melvale. Two Bad Spots Baltimore has two undeveloped spots for which It must continually apologize. Strangely enough, both of them involve the city's relationship to children. In the Cheltenham House of Reformation. in Prince Georges county, the city has two hun- dred boys, the most of them hired out to a manufacturer of brooms. They are living in dirt and disease, with little chance for education or reformation. A similar situation exists at Melvale, the re- form school for girls, where a power sewing machine shop takes the place of a broom factory. A city which mistreats its wayward boys and girls usually neglects also its normal children. How true this is in the case of Baltimore, Mr. Ernest T. Atwell, associate director of the National Playground and Recreation Association, reminded us last week when he said that the Playground Athletic League of this city, with a budget of nearly $250,000 a year, spends less than $3,000 a year in work among Negro child- ren. This is the reason there is but one full time P.A.L. worker handling colored work here. This is the reason there is not a single year- :ound play-field or playground for Negroes in Baltimore. title: Baltimore Should Protest Against This Nuisance.
full_text: Baltimore Should Protest Against This Nuisance. To the Editor: Investigation will prove that a nuisance is prevalent in Baltimore, which does not exist in many large cities-North or South. The nuisance is the littering of the streets and vestibules of colored people's homes with hand-bills and all other cheap form of advertisements. In nearly every city, there is a city ordinance against this procedure. Two towns in California have legislated against this nuisance in the past three months. The postman in Northwest Baltimore, in many cases, must move advertisements from our mail boxes in order to deposit our mail. The city council would render invaluable service to the tax-payers if an ordinance were passed which would do away with this nuisance. A community cannot be sanitary if the alleys, gutters, doorways and streets are littered with paper blowing every direction, scattering the filth in gutters. Let's fight the nuisance. HENRY F. ARNOLD, No Booing at Lincoln Debate. Pennsylvania Men Defeated Fisk as Well as West Virginia State. To the Editor: I notice on the front page of this week's AFRO- AMERICAN an account of the debate between teams from the West Virginia State College and Lincoln University, in which it was stated that Lincoln's victory by a two to one decision of the judges was "booed" by the students here. As chairman of the faculty committee on debating, might I be permitted to correct this very erron- eous statement? Our president, a number of the faculty, and a fine representation from the student body were present at the debate, and all of us can testify that there was applause, but no booing (either inside or outside the auditorium) when the de- cision was rendered. On the contrary it seems to be the very general opinion here that the decision should have been unanimous in our favor. I say this only to contradict in the most emphatic manner the false report of your correspondent, whoever he might be. I regret all the more the unfortunate impres- sion which this article will make, because one of the most promising of our debaters is one of your own Baltimore boys, Clarence M. Mitchell. For his sake as well as for the sake of the others on the team, would you be willing to correct the misrepresentation made regarding them and the attitude of their fellow students toward them? Let me also say that two of this same team, Messrs. Chapman and Mitchell, met this week in New York a finely equipped team from Fisk Uni- versity in one of the best debates it has been my pleasure of listening to; and in this case also they won a two to one decision of the judges. ROBERT M. LABAREE. Dept. of Social Science, Lincoln University. Travelling in Jim Crow Dixie Which Promises, But Rarely Gives, Equal Pullman Accommoda- tions. To the Editor: There are many interesting, though annoying paradoxes that confront one in the South to-day. Though I was able to get Pullman accommoda- tions out of New York to Atlanta, where I slept in the same car with white passengers, men and women, I was not permitted to eat in the dining car with white passengers, as though there was some great and fundamental difference involved. It is customary, according to some of the Negro leaders in the South, for Negro passengers to be given Lower 13, which is the drawing room, in order to keep them out of the body of the car, so as to avoid contact with the white passengers. But on this trip, I had Lower 6 which is in the body of the car-going to Atlanta. No regular, invariable rule with respect to accommodations for Negroes seems to prevail. Even coming out of Augusta to Atlanta, while considerable juggling and insistence was re- quired to get a berth, finally Lower 1 was secured. The previous week, Dr. Channing Tobias, na- tional colored Y.M.C.A. secretary, had secured sleeping car accommodations out of Augusta, but was given Lower 13 or the drawing room. It may be interesting to know in this connec- tion, too, that traveling from Augusta to Atlan- ta is intra-state, which renders the securing of sleeping car accommodations more unusual. New York City. A PHILIP RANDOLPH. Judge Waxter is the Grandson of the Late Gen- eral Thomas J. Shryock. To the Editor: Judge Waxter, of the juvenile court, is a young man. His work on behalf of our group should not fail of the highest appreciation upon our part. When others of his kind discern how sincerely such efforts are appreciated by the thoughtful class of colored people, they will more and more be inclined to join in the fight for justice and fair treatment of their colored brethren. The grandfather of the judge, General Thomas J. Shryock, a generation ago, was one of the best and staunchest friends we have ever had I knew him well, and worked with him. Lying be- fore me is a letter of his to me, under date of March 28, 1901, in which he uses the following language: "I can assure you that I have held you in the highest regard since our work together in behalf of the re-election of Governor Lowndes. You are one of those with whom I came in contact whom believe one can tie to." Under the presidency of General Shryock, the first general improvements were made at Chel- tenham. One colored teacher, and two colored men, heads of departments, were appointed dur- ing his administration, and he was minded grad- ually, to extend the same kind of service through- out that institution. He was of old "Abolition stock," and a man that demanded a square deal for all. I am glad that his grandson has interested himself in this work, and I trust that through his efforts other white men of his standing may be interested, and finally, that we may have such institutions for the care and training of colored delinquents as will be a credit to the State of Maryland. GEORGE F. BRAGG, Jr. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Letter to the Editor 3 -- No Title
full_text: Same Cheltenham Reports, But No Action, Says the Reverend Mr. Bragg; and He Suggests a Plan. To the Editor: According to the Baltimore Sun of August 24, 1898, adverse criticisms of the management of Cheltenham have been made, dating back to that early period. The Sun of that date, said: The grand jury Committee on the House of Reformation for Colored Boys, at Chel- tenham, submitted a special report yester- day commending the management and con- dition of that institution, but stating that the punishments inflicted on the youthful in- mates have been too severe. "We regret to say, that in our opinion," the report states, "the punishments have been too severe, better suited for hardened adult criminals than for incorrigible youths. We are pleased to report that the management have promised to modify this somewhat what by doing away with the more severe methods. "In this connection one of the great needs of the institution is a strong guard build- ing, where uncontrollable inmates may safe- ly be confined under proper police protec- tion. "We believe that kind treatment and moral suasion will do more to serve the purpose of the institution than harsh and severe pun- ishments." For more than a third of a century these criti- cisms have been made, and yet the whole situa- tion is entirely unsatisfactory. Now, my suggestion is just this: Let our preachers' meetings and other organizations pass resolutions requesting the Hon. Wm. Cabell Bruce, and the Hon. M. A. Soper, to take the leadership in this matter, and direct it in the most effective way of accomplishing results. In a matter of this kind Senator Bruce will do for us gladly what we could not pay him to do for us as an attorney. In a matter of this character we need the lead- ership of men of the class we have named. And that class of men would willingly do more for us if they could only feel that such services would be duly appreciated by the colored race. As a matter of fact, in a recent personal letter received from Senator Bruce, among other things, he said: I am, indeed, eager that full justice should be done, in every respect, to my colored fel- low citizens, and I can not see how any pub- lic man who has the slightest pretensions to the character of a statesman could be other- wise. I do not have to go outside of my own household to find illustrations of the high level of moral worth on which the lives of some of my colored fellow citizens move. Let our public spirited men and women, in- terested in improved conditions for our delin- quent colored boys and girls, address a line to Senator Bruce and Judge Soper, conscripting them for such public service on behalf of those of the community who need it most. Baltimore. GEO. F. BRAGG, Ir. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: URGES SCHOOL FOR FEEBLE-MINDED: PROBATION OFFICER POINTS OUT NEED FOR CORRECTIVE INSTITUTION HERE. CASES ARE CITED SMALL BOY COMMITED TO CHELTENHAM FOR LACK OF HOME
full_text: URGES SCHOOL FOR FEEBLE-MINDED Probation Officer Points Out Need for Corrective Insti- tution Here. CASES ARE CITED Small Boy Commited to Chel- tenham for Lack of Home The crying need for a home for feeble minded boys was shown again this week, when two small boys, suf- fering from low mentality, appeared in juvenile court and one was cont mitted to Cheltenham for lack of a better institution. : Joseph Thomas, who was brought into juvenile court by his parents, was committed to the reform school after he had run from home for the eighth time. According to juvenile court records, the boy was first picked up on the street, on May 1, 1928, again on October 10, December 5 and De- cember 12, 1928. On February 8, 1929, he again ran away from home, was sent back, but disappeared again on February 21, 1929. On this occasion he was committed to the Cheltenham School for Boys where he stayed but a little while and ran away from it. He was again picked up on January 23. 1930, and again on May 3. According to the boy's parents, he was struck by a truck when a child, and since that time has shown him- self to be of a feeble mind, and his greatest fault is running away from home, although his parents claim that he gets the best of treatment. Mr. George Ralph, probation offi- cer, declares that it is not to the lik- ing of the juvenile court to commit boys of this type to Cheltenham, as there are no facilities there for their Improvement. full_text: Accidents Dog Attacks Young Woman While passing the house at Jose- phine and Arch Streets, Miss Grace Moore, 22, 632 Josephine Street, was seriously bitten by a dog on the right leg. Friday. She was treated and then examined for rabies. Boy Hit by Auto Running into the street in front of an automobile, Maud Chester, seven, 888 Booth Street, was knocked down by the machine resulting in her sus- taining cuts and bruices of the face and body, Thursday. Bit By Stray Dog While skating in the 1600 block of Vine Street, Marie Roberts, nine, 15 N. Gilmor Street, was atacked and bitten on the left leg by a stray dog, Thursday The girl's wounds were cauterized at the Franklin Square Hospital. Chased in Path of Auto Chased into the path of an auto- mobile by anothe, girl at Myrtle Ave- nue and Saratoga Street, Annie Brassler, 10, 713 W. Saratoga Street, received cuts of the face and body, Thursday. Boy, 4, Has Narrow Escape Clarence Williams, four, 707 S. Greene Street, sustained cuts of the face and body when he ran from the sidewalk in front of his home and was struck by an automobile, Friday. Many Destitute, Sent to Institutions Here Commitments to various institu- tions were issued by police magis- trates for twenty persons, including one woman, who were arrested and convicted of being without homes. this week. The lone woman to be cited on charges of vagrancy was Mrs. Rose Ashburn, 221 Herring Court, who was found wandering the streets in the vicinity of Lexington and Pine Streets. The woman told officers that she was without home. having been ejected when she was unable to pay rent after the death of her hus- band three weeks ago. She was sent to the City Hospital for three months. The others, all men, were sent to penal institutions after it was dis- closed that they had migrated here in search of work and were unable to find any work, and were without means of support. Attempts Suicide in Lovers' Quarrel (Reprinted from Last Week's City Edition) Despondent because of an argu- ment with her boy friend, Miss Ma- rie Mosley, 22, 621 W. Biddle Strect. swallowed an ounce of iodine and a mixture of another poison at Penn- sylvania Avenue and Biddle Street, Sunday, in an attempt to commit suicide. According to records, the young woman purchased the poison at Lem- ler's drug store at Pennsylvania Ave- nue and Biddle Street. On reach- ing the sidewalk the young woman put the container to her mouth and swallowed its contents. She was tak- en to the Maryland General Hospi- tal where she was treatc: and held for observation. In a statement to the police, the young woman said that she had en- gaged in a quarrel with her lover and she was despondent and wanted to kill herself. She refused to dis- close the identity of the young man. 0 Escaped from Reformatory; Youth Held as a Fugitive (Reprinted from Last Week's City Edition) Said to have escaped from the Cheltenham Reform School, Ran- dolph Cook, 18, Linthicum Heights, who was arrested when he arrived at his home, Monday, is in the Wes- tern police station awaiting the ar- rival of officers to return him to the institution. The boy gained: temporary freedom while working for a farmer near the school who had secured his services from the officers of the institution. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: Mayor Is Quizzed On Board Appointment Promise: 3 VACANCIES ON SCHOOL BOARD IN MARCH Broening also Has Yet to Make Jail Board Appointment. MAYOR TALKS New Member for Cheltenham Advised.
full_text: Mayor Is Quizzed On Board Appointment Promise 3 VACANCIES ON SCHOOL BOARD IN MARCH Broening also Has Yet to Make Jail Board Appointment. MAYOR TALKS New Member for Chel- tenham Advised. The terms of three mem- bers of the Board of School Commissioners expire in March. They are, Mrs. John Wesley Brown, Mrs. Louis H. Levin and John H. Duncan. Friends of Dr. B. M. Rhetta, local physician, who have twice placed his name before Mayor Broening-once in his first administration and again last year when Dr. Frank J. Good- now, Theodore E. Straus and War- ren S. Seipp were reappointed-have indicated already that they expect to continue the fight to place a colored representative on the school board. Other Cities Have Colored Advocates of this program point out that under the law, in the Dis- trict of Columbia, three of the nine school board members must be col- ored. Atlantic City and New York are other cities which have had colored school commissioners, and on Janu- ary first, Mrs. A. H. Martin, a for- mer Cleveland (Ohio) school teach- er, was sworn in and took her seat as the first elected colored member of the Cleveland school board. Mayor Gave Promise Politicians point out that the may- or in his pre-election speeches made promise of appointment of colored on city boards. After election, he appointed Dr. Howard E. Young to the Jail Board and Willard Allen up- on the Zoning Commission. To the Board of Education, to which his promise was directed, no appoint- ment was made. The Mayor Talks When asked what was the possi- bility of a colored member on the school board this year, Mayor Broen- ing said: "While I have not yet fully made up my mind and am giving the school board and other matters of this kind my serious consideration, I have had so much pressure brought to bear to retain the old school board in office that I can give no definite answer to this question. There would no doubt be consideration of an ap- plicant should a vacancy arise, but there is an agreement to keep the school board out of politics and not make class appointments. "I am being opportuned to keep the present board in office during the development of the program they are putting over. My greatest de- side is to see that the best is done for both colored and white children." Jail Board Welfare workers have an eye on the Jail Board, upon which there has been a vacancy. Parker Emer- son, white, was appointed by the mayor, but has failed to qualify. Effort will be made to have mayor appoint a colored woman to this board. They point to the effective service now being rendered by Mrs. Grace Hartnett, white, as an indi- cation of what could be done by a colored woman member. Cheltenham Another appointment which the mayor will be asked to make this year is a new city representative up- on the board of the House of Refor- mation at Cheltenham. Bailey Con- way and Joseph P. Evans are the mayor's appointees on this board at present. One of the first moves of the board several months ago was to oust Dr. George F. Bragg, who had been a member of the board for two dec- ades. Under the law, Governor Ritchie is authorized to name annually in February two members of the Chel- tenham board. Both of the govern- ors's anointees are white. abstract: The terms of three members of the Board of School Commissioners expire in March.
clean_text: Mayor Is Quizzed On Board Appointment Promise 3 VACANCIES ON SCHOOL BOARD IN MARCH Broening also Has Yet to Make Jail Board Appointment. MAYOR TALKS New Member for Chel- tenham Advised. The terms of three mem- bers of the Board of School Commissioners expire in March. They are, Mrs. John Wesley Brown, Mrs. Louis H. Levin and John H. Duncan. Friends of Dr. B. M. Rhetta, local physician, who have twice placed his name before Mayor Broening-once in his first administration and again last year when Dr. Frank J. Good- now, Theodore E. Straus and War- ren S. Seipp were reappointed-have indicated already that they expect to continue the fight to place a colored representative on the school board. Other Cities Have Colored Advocates of this program point out that under the law, in the Dis- trict of Columbia, three of the nine school board members must be col- ored. Atlantic City and New York are other cities which have had colored school commissioners, and on Janu- ary first, Mrs. A. H. Martin, a for- mer Cleveland (Ohio) school teach- er, was sworn in and took her seat as the first elected colored member of the Cleveland school board. Mayor Gave Promise Politicians point out that the may- or in his pre-election speeches made promise of appointment of colored on city boards. After election, he appointed Dr. Howard E. Young to the Jail Board and Willard Allen up- on the Zoning Commission. To the Board of Education, to which his promise was directed, no appoint- ment was made. The Mayor Talks When asked what was the possi- bility of a colored member on the school board this year, Mayor Broen- ing said: "While I have not yet fully made up my mind and am giving the school board and other matters of this kind my serious consideration, I have had so much pressure brought to bear to retain the old school board in office that I can give no definite answer to this question. There would no doubt be consideration of an ap- plicant should a vacancy arise, but there is an agreement to keep the school board out of politics and not make class appointments. "I am being opportuned to keep the present board in office during the development of the program they are putting over. My greatest de- side is to see that the best is done for both colored and white children." Jail Board Welfare workers have an eye on the Jail Board, upon which there has been a vacancy. Parker Emer- son, white, was appointed by the mayor, but has failed to qualify. Effort will be made to have mayor appoint a colored woman to this board. They point to the effective service now being rendered by Mrs. Grace Hartnett, white, as an indi- cation of what could be done by a colored woman member. Cheltenham Another appointment which the mayor will be asked to make this year is a new city representative up- on the board of the House of Refor- mation at Cheltenham. Bailey Con- way and Joseph P. Evans are the mayor's appointees on this board at present. One of the first moves of the board several months ago was to oust Dr. George F. Bragg, who had been a member of the board for two dec- ades. Under the law, Governor Ritchie is authorized to name annually in February two members of the Chel- tenham board. Both of the govern- ors's anointees are white. Despite the public clamor against Cheltenham which has resulted from "nfavorable Grand Jury reports, and from refusal of judges to commit boys there, no changes have been made by the board in the white per- sonnel of the reform school. . .

title: WAXTER WOULD CLOSE MELVALE: WAXTER URGES NEGRO WORKERS AT INSTITUTION COLORED PERSONNEL CAN HANDLE THE PROBLEMS BETTER CLOSE MELVALE INSTITUTION IMPOSSIBLE, JUDGE SAYS
full_text: WAXTER WOULD CLOSE MELVALE WAXTER URGES NEGRO WORKERS AT INSTITUTION Colored Personnel Can Handle the Problems Better CLOSE MELVALE Institution Impossible, Juvenile Judge Says A complete Negro person- nel from top to bottom for Cheltenham, and a proposed institution for delinquent girls were urged by Juvenile Court Judge P. J. Waxter, speaking at the "Y" Anniversary at Bethel A.M.E. Church, Sun- day, May. "And whatever body the legisla- ture might be induced to appoint to map out a program for delinquent and feeble minded Negro boys and girls in the state, should have on it a goodly number of Negro men and women," the judge declared. Cheltenham Speaking of Cheltenham, Judge Waxter declared: "Cheltenham has 12,000 acres of land, a fine substantial building, and the state pays good wages, but there is one thing lacking there that every institution should have, that is, the proper attitude the child has toward the institution. Is he contented? Is he getting something out of that school? At Cheltenham they don't have that. "Give them the elements of educa- tion, take them out of the illiterate class. There should be a colored per- sonnel who come into contact with the children who come into the insti- tution, to give them some background, to teach them to understand why they should be right-show them the reason why they are there and the job of a trade to be able to go out and earn their living. "We should have men who are really going to see their problem, men who are going to see their point of view. After all, it is up to you, be- cause it is primarily your problem. "In the whole state of Maryland, there is not one single institution to which the colored feeble minded and delinquent children may be sent. We of the Juvenile Court feel that we should have an addition to Crowns- ville. Melvale Impossible "The two great problems that are facing us are, first, as to the delin- quent child. We do have reformatory schools in which we can take care of them, but there is no institution in the state where the feebleminded can be sent. "The truant girls are sent to the Melvale Industrial Home, but Melvale is absolutely impossible at the pres- ent time. The reports are to the ef- fect that conditions at that place are impossible-the house is in very bad condition, there is no equipment with which to work, the girls have had no knives and forks, but at the present time have spoons with which to eat. The moral condition is terrible. There is no supervision over the building and the girls are not being properly clothed. It is impossible to do any- thing at that place as it now stands. "We have gotten together about from various sources which we to spend as soon as we have ized a definite plan of proced- "We hope to get the backing of Gov. Ritchie. Of course we can't send all children to institutions. We try to keep them out of the institu- tions. Even the best institution in God's world is a bad place for a child. So we have to find some place other than an institution for as many as we can. We take the child out of the home and we try to find a foster parent. With that thought in mind, we are sending one man throughout the counties this summer to try to find homes for the colored children- persons who will adopt them and give them a decent home, and help them get a decent hold on life. "We feel that if we can get enough colored people who understand and will work-that if we can get that kind of colored people interested - we will be able to work and do more for the colored children and will be able to get something done. "Our problem is not dealing with the 5,000 children. In other words, with the 5,000 children we can do a certain amount of work called re- search work. We can find out first of all, the section of the city the child comes from, and can go into the schools with the consent of the Continued on Page Seventeen  MORE MELVALE (Continued from Page One) school board and find out why that child is delinquent and why the teachers are not trying to keep that child out of the court. "The problem there is that the better things of the city are closed to both the child and the parent, and he is thrown more and more back on himself for his recreation. We should spend more money in recreational facilities. City Should Act "Now we feel that first of all, to do these things, the city of Baltimore should set aside a program or map out a plan whereby the colored chil- dren of Baltimore who are delin- quent and feebleminded could be taken care of. We should build a home for such children, get rid of Melvale and put in its place an insti- tution as good as there is for the same class of white children. Equip the institution, and get the highest type of officers over same, so that the child can get his bearing." Other Speakers Other speakers at the anniversary meeting included Dr. Harry S. Mc- Card: Chairman William Anderson of the Committee of Management; Executive Secretary William C. An- derson and the Rev. John T. Colbert, pastor of Grace Presbyterian Church and chairman of the committee in charge of the anniversary. Music was rendered by the Coppin Normal School quartette and by Har- old Bryant, baritone.

title: Low Mentality Shown by Tests at Cheltenham
full_text: Low Mentality Shown by Tests at Cheltenham BOWIE, Md.-Intelligence test given by Nicholas Orem, white superintendent of pub- lic schools in Prince Georges County, and scores tabulated and graphed by the principal and faculty at Bowie State Normal School indicates that the 172 inmates of the Md. House of Reformation at Chel- tenham are of average low in- telligence. The boys, whose ages range from eight to seventeen years, have an average age of 14.4 years, but an av- average mental age only 9.3. Tests would seem to indicate that 93 boys with ages varying from 11 to 14 have still the mental equipment of a boy of 9. With 100 per cent regarded as nor- mal, the 172 boys showed a median intelligence quotient of 70.2. Only one boy of normal intelligence was found. Schools and Colleges Alphas Stage Go-to-High Go-to-College Week Eighty-five chapters of the Alpha Phi Alpha, a college fraternity, open their national "Go-to-High-School, Go-to-College Week," May 4th. One hundred speakers with the aid of stereopticon slides, circulars, bul- letins, personal letters, and placards are behind the cmapaign to fight il- literacy by urging school children to stay in school. State directors are New Jersey, Dr. Ferdinand G. Williams; Maryland, P. D. G. Pennington; North Carolina, L. H. Trigg; New York, Myles A. Paige; Pennsylvania, R. Maurice Moss; Delaware, Dr. Francis T. Jami- son; Washington, D.C., Woodley E. Lawrence; South Carolina, Sidney R. Williams; West Virginia, J. A. Shel- ton; Virginia, L. S. Palmer. 0 At Wilberforce WILLS MONEY TO WILBERFORCE WILBERFORCE, O.-Rev. Joseph Stiles, Ja- maica, L.I., for several years pastor of Allen Chapel A.M.E. Church, bequeathed third of his $75,000 estate and a legacy of $300 for a memorial window in the musi- cal conservatory, to Wilberforce University, according to his will which was admitted to probate Monday. President Jones announced in chapel Fri- day a recent lagacy of $1,000, the gift of Mrs. Emily J. Howland, Auburn, N.Y. DR. JONES "GUIDE RIGHT" SPEAKER President Gilbert H. Jones, of the univer- sity, will be the principal speaker at the "Guide Right" exercises of the Cleveland Alumni Chapter of Kappa Alpha Psi Fra- ternity, Sunday afternoon, May 4, at St. Paul A.M.E. Zion Church, Cleveland. OHIO PEACE ORATIONS Wilberforce was represented at the fifth annual Ohio Peace Association Oratorical Contest, Friday afternoon at Capital Uni- versity, Columbus, Ohio, by Andrew Hughey, senior in Payne Theological Seminary. UNIVERSITY HEALTH WEEK Mrs. Mary Carterwright of the State department of health and Judge S. C. Wright, of the juvenile court, Xenia, Ohio, telked to student groups of the university, Wednesday, as the features of University Health Week. At Lincoln "U" BALTIMORE PREACHER HERE On Wednesday morning, Rev. Euclid Phillips, of Baltimore, visited the campus. During his stay he honored students of the Theological Seminary of the university with a lecture on "Recent Translations of the Bible." GLEE CLUB CONCERT The annual concert, promenade and dan- sant will take place on the evening of May D, at the chapel and Livingstone Hall. This event is a swanky affair that draws celebrities from far and near. The Glee Club is directed by J. E. Dorsey, former Tuskegee jubilee singer and now professor of music here.. RECEPTION FOR ATHLETES The Athletic Association of the university will entertain visiting athletes to the annual Lincoln University Track and Field Meet, to an informal reception and dance on May 10, after the track meet. CAMPUS SPOTLIGHT The Lincoln News, a student publication, says: "We colored folk may or may not question the superiority of the white man. That is left to the individual's own way of thinking. But we cannot deny that we be- long to the great body of the oppressed, and that we are subject to the exploitation of the powers that be. To us it is no question of Nordic superiority, rather it should be a question of reaching a definite millenium, in reaching a greater racial harmony. This does not imply subservience. "Some say that the Negro is not a social animal; that he cannot fit into the social scheme of the superior group. Neither is that social group a social animal in denying the Negro a chance to equip himself for that social scheme." ! Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: DESERTION CLAIMS I WIN TWO DECREES: WIVES GAIN FREEDOM AFTER MATES QUIT THEIR HOMES

Mrs. Clara Brown, 726 Ensor Street, was granted an absolute di- vorce in the Circuit Court, Monday. The couple were married August 12, 1912, testimony showed, and with- out any provocation on the part of the wife Brown abandoned his home. Mrs. Brown, who is a maid at the Johns Hopkins Hospital, was grant- ed the use of her maiden name, Clara Deller. Roy S. Bond represented Mrs. Brown. Mrs. Nancy Bailey, 2500 Madison Avenue, was granted an absolute di- vorce in the Circuit Court, Friday, from Howard W. Bailey. The couple were married in Phila- delphia, December 15, 1910, and lived together until November 3, 1919, when he quit their home after a minor domestic rift. Mrs. Bailey was also granted + : us of her maiden name, Nancy Α. Stoner. She was represented by Roy S. Bond. Eden Street, and Miss Anne Adams, 1240 Edythe Street, from serious in- juries when they engaged in a knife duel. Sunday. Both women, however, received cuts of the arms during the fracas, which is said by police to have been caused by jealousy over a young man. 0 Girl and Man Sought when Babe is Found The body of a new born male baby was found by a policeman on the dump opposite the Clifton Park Lake, Monday. Police who are investigating the finding of the body learned that a young woman, 20 years of age, five feet, four inches tall, brown skin, 130 pounds. was seen with a young man whose description was not given, walking near the dump. The young woman wore a white and blue dress, blue coat and white sum- mer hat. The boy gained: ; temporary freedom while working for a farmer near the school who had secured his services from the officers of the institution. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: GIRLS EAT WITH THEIR HANDS AT MELVALE: JUDGE WAXTER TERMS REFORMATORY WORST IN COUNTRY. 65 INFECTED ABNORMAL SEX LIFE IS TAUGHT NEWCOMERS.
full_text: GIRLS EAT WITH THEIR HANDS AT MELVALE Judge Waxter Terms Reformatory Worst in Country. 65 INFECTED Abnormal Sex Life is Taught Newcomers. A picture resembling the notorious "hell hole of Shan- ghai" was painted by Judge J. S. Waxter of the juvenile court. in describing conditions at the girls' reformatory school at Melvale, in a talk to the Interdenominational Min- isterial Alliance of white and colored ministers at the Cen- tral Y.M.C.A., Monday. Speaking on "Delinquent Youth and the Church," the judge declared! that conditions at this school were the worst of any such place in the entire country. There are about 100 girls there, ac- cording to Judge Waxter, and 65 per cent of them have some form of so- cial disease. Most of these cases, he says were contracted in the institu- tion, statements by the girls showed. Sex Abnormality In nearly every case the girls in- dulge in homosexual practices, he said. Each girl who comes to the institution is initiated into the prac- tice by older inmates. He found in one extreme case one of the girls had been cut by another with a razor as a result of homo- sexual practice. Another who was burned, said she enjoyed it as a sex experience. Seventy-five per cent of the cases brought in 'show that offenders are from three to four years retarded mentally. Eat With Hands At one visit made by Judge Waxter he found them eating their meal of liver and gravy with their hands. This is the practice there, he was told. There is no school and the one teacher there admitted that she had no books with which to teach so that only six or seven girls get any school- ing at all. None of the girls has sufficient clothing nor enough and proper bed clothing. The condition of the toilets was almost ndescribable. said the jurist, and at one visit that he made, only one was in proper working condition. There were rat holes and tremendous filth. he says. Should Fight Judge Waxter declared earnestly that a fight should be made against these conditions. In describing conditions at Chel- tenham. Judge Waxter declared that they used to be abominable there, but were now slightly improved. Even there, he said, they do nothing con- structive for the inmates. Untrained Staff There are only two people on the staff at Cheltenham who have suffi- cient training to be there, he said, and there is no vocational training nor proper recreation. Judge Waxter attributed most of the bad moral condition at these in- stitutions to lack of proper recrea- tion. Girls at Melvale all work in a factory during the day and after eat- ing their evening meal go to their dormtory and to bed. There, in the absence of proper recreational outlet. th degenerate into sordid practices! to amuse theinselves. Juvenile Court The local juvenile court is making ar effort to raise the standard of juv- enile treatment. Social, physical and psychiatric examinations are being made af all cases brought in. Seventy-five per cent of children from three to four years of age have retarded mentality. Sixty-five of the delinkuent cases among girls are sex cases. Mostly Protestant The judge finds that the Protest- ant churches are not dealing as suc- cessfully with child life as the Catho- lics are. Most of the girls who come | in are from Protestant homes. Catho- lics, he said, get in early training and plant holy fea" into children. In commenting on the disclosures of Judge Waxter, the Rev. George F. Bragg. Jr., declared that when he was on the board he advocated the appointment of colored women. The white women there were not of a high type, he said. Dr. Ernest Lyon asked if conditions at the institution could not be remedied through officials handling the funds appropriated to the school. The intervention of politics, how- ever, was discouraged by the minis- ters. A committee headed by the presi- dents of the white and colored In- terdenominational Ministers' Alliance and which will be composed of both ministers and laymen will be ap- pointed to investigate the Melvale situation and offer suggestions for bettering conditions there. This ac- tion came through vote of the meet- ing. Monday. Matron Admits Charges In a statement to the AFRO- AMERICAN, Tuesday. Mrs. Sarah Medinger, now. in charge of the Re- form School for Colored Girls at Mel- vale, admitted that Judge Waxter's Continued on Page Two ! MELVALE GIRLS EAT WITH THEIR HANDS (Continued from page one) statement was about right when she went there last January. "The girls," she said, "have no knives and forks because they are considered a menace. They have plenty of food and use spoons. The clothing is better now than it was when I came here. The building, which is an old one and inadequate, is kept as clean as possible. Lack of funds is the chief obstacle. "Additional lavatories have been placed but homesexual practices still continue. While the school facilities have improved some, there are still lacking books. Recreation is not sufficient. We have a victrola and piano and the girls can sing and dance. They have no ambition to study and must be driven to take part in what educational opportuni- ties we have to offer. Chapel ser- vices are held each week. George M. Griffith, white, member of the board, who told the AFRO- AMERICAN that he has been ill for some time, stated that he had been to Melvale but twice in the last two years. "I cannot make any statement regarding the conditions there," he said. Cheltenham Board Joseph P. Evans, Bailey Conway, H. B. Claggett, Upper Marlboro; Clar- ence M. Roberts, president, Landover; Enoch Harlan (named by Mayor Broening vice president), W. H. Jan- vey, secretary, and superintendent John B. Pyles. constitute the Chelten- ham Board. The last named five are white. The institution is privately owned by whites. | The school estate, of 1250 acres, is located in Prince Georges County. Efforts to get the state to take over Cheltenham have repeatedly failed. State appropriations for the institu- tion amount to $15,000 a year and city appropriations amount $37,000. to Of the inmates, 200 boys are sent from Baltimore city and 100 from the counties. Melvale The Industrial Home for Colored Girls at Melvale is also a private in- stitutio.. to which delinquent grls of the state are committed. The Melvale board is composed of the following persons who include one city councilman: George M. Griffith. Dr. Alfred T. Gundry, Allen L. Smith, J. Harry Williams. Wm. H. Kirkwood. Mrs. Frank LeMoyne and City Councilman J. Warren Burgess. All are white. City Councilman Burgess said he has been recently appointed to the board, and that there had been only one meeting since his appointment. He has made no investigations of the Melvale institution. The state appropriation is $4,000 a wear to Melvale and the city, $14,000.

title: Cheltenham's Board
full_text: if the census taker comes by day, and are out if he comes by night. New York City illustrates the difficulties of an accurate census. For example, the 1920 U.S. cen- sus showed a population of 80,118 colored women and 72,351 men; total, 152,469. In 1900, New York had 60,666 colored folk; and in 1910, 91,700. If the usual, growth of the city has kept up since 1920, New York today has 210,000 colored people. The New York Tattler "guesses" however that the number should be 254,000. Other New York papers have estimated as high as 275,000 or 300,000. They may be correct; but when the census figures are made public next year and show 210,000 Negroes in New York City, the rest of the country can well marvel how so many Har- lemites succeed every ten years in ducking census enumerators. A Snob University "In some of our most popular universities, un- dergraduates are simply products of stuffing machines, and the degree of A.B. becomes a mere label-a standard bonded label on a bootleg bottle," writes Dean Edward Wilber Berry, white, of the College of Arts and Sciences of Johns Hopkins University in a current magazine. Dean Berry thinks that the average college teaches "simply the accumulated debris of the past three or four hundred years of hit-or-miss instruction." Defenders of the college as it is, may well wonder how much of this opinion can be regarded as an effort to better college teaching and how much is a sneer at colleges in general. Dean Berry was privately educated. He holds no de- grees. Yet neither Dean Berry, who is 55 years old and was born in Newark, N. J., nor Johns Hop- kins University can afford to sneer at many other colleges. Nowhere is snobbishness, prejudice, race hatred and intolerance better taught and better ex- emplified in this Christian(?) land, than at Johns Hopkins. Named for a philanthropist who left his wealth. to heal and educate both black and white, it has been turned into a university of snobs for the white aristocrat only. Dean Berry can well afford to confine his sneers to his own college. Cheltenham's Board Two out of sixteen members of the board of trustees of the Maryland House of Reformation for Colored Boys at Cheltenham are colored. Of the sixteen members, two are named by Governor Albert C. Rtchie in February and two by Mayor Broening in March, and the board it- self chooses twelve. The mayors of Baltimore have always made a practice of naming one white and one colored member. The governors of Maryland have al- ways named only white persons. It seems to be a strange thing that this pro- cedure has so long continued, yet it has, despite the fact that Maryland and Baltimore have al- ways been committed to a separate school system, Jand Cheltenham has been adversely reported on by grand juries for the past five years. Since all the inmates at Cheltenham are colored, both mayor and governor should have enough courage and sufficient confidence in the colored population to name colored men and women to these boards and entrust to them' the problem of making Cheltenham the kind of reform school the state ought to have. It is not enough for the chief executives of the city and of the state to say that Cheltenham is a private institution and nothing can be done. This can be done-responsible colored persons can be placed by them on the Cheltenham board. Until this is done, neither state nor city has done its duty by the colored people. N. Y's Number, 210,000 There is always a large number of persons whose names never get on the U.S. census books, Some do not think it advisable to have the police know where they live. Others have no permanent domicile, and still others are asleep Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.
