title: Virginia Reform School Got Colored Instructors Through Booker T.--Arnold: It is Strange Educator Should Reverse Himself in Regards to Cheltenham, Writer Points Out.
full_text: Virginia Reform School Got Colored Instructors Through BookerT.-Arnold It is Strange Educator Should Reverse Himself in Re- gards to Cheltenham, Writer Points Out. By HENRY F. ARNOLD An interview which is alleged to have taken place between the late Booker T. Washington and Mr. Nathaniel G. Grasty, white secretary for the Super- visors of City Charities, appear- ed in the AFRO-AMERICAN on April 23 under the caption, "Booker T. Advised Against Col- ored Teachers for Cheltenham" which, if allowed to go unchal- lenged, would be a slap at the honesty, sincerity of purpose, and ideals of the man whose life was spent in placing Ne- groes in every nook and corner of America where it was pos- sible. During the life time of Dr. Book- er T. Washington, both the white and colored enemies of the man brought accusations of every de- scription against him for the pur- pose of stifling and precluding his progress as an educator and build- Such accusations, in most cases, were out of jealousy and per- sonal hate. None has been more damaging than the above charge. er. To my way of thinking, the ac- cusation brought by Mr. Grasty was for the purpose of trying to prove to Negroes in Maryland why there are no Negroes connected with Cheltenham, is because Mr. Washington opposed colored teach- ers, and the white people accept- ed his preachment. In the body of the alleged Grasty interview, we find these words:- "Booker Washington was consulted concerning the placement of col- ored teachers. Washington ad- vised the trustees not to employ colored teachers, saying that the instructors would not teach in iso- lated schools." A Strange Apparition One of the first things Mr. Wash- ington did after starting work on Tuskegee's grounds, was to investi- gate a farm in Alabama where col- ored boys were being held in semi- slavery under white instructors, as it is alleged is the case at Chelten- ham. Mr. Washington immediately took up the matter with the Gover- nor of Alabama and some of the leading white citizens of the state, and a reform school known as Mt. Meigs was built and placed under colored teachers. The school is: still in existence, and colored in- structors are on the job. was During this period, there living in Richmond, Va., a man whose name was Henry W. Smythe, who was running a school for de- linquent colored boys. Mr. Smythe made an enviable reputation among the white people of Virginia as a result of his interest in colored youth, and hence a move was start- ed to have a colored school which would teach the boys trades. Baltimorean Mr. Smythe and Booker Wash- ington became friends. Dr. Smythe. who was born in Baltimore, had had tremendous influence with the late railroad magnate, Robert Cur- tis Ogden, who gave Ogden Hall to Hampton Institute. Dr. Wash- ington and Mr. Smythe induced Mr. Ogden to come to Richmond and meet several of the city's out- standing white and colored men, for the purpose of interesting Mr. Ogden in the turning over of a twenty-five hundred acre tract of land which he owned, to be used for a reformatory for colored boys. This tract of land was situated two and one-half miles from Hanover in Hanover County. Dr. Washington assured Mr. Ogden that the land was idle and that hundreds of colored boys, who were getting into trouble, were victims of circumstances, and that a large number of such boys could be saved if they were sent to school and given proper training under colored instructors. Mr. Og- den agreed with Dr. Washington and Mr. Smythe, so it was decided that Mr. Ogden's attorney and an attorney representing the state of Virginia, draw up an agreement to be signed by both parties. Drew up Agreement Th eagreement read as follows: "I, Robert Curtis Ogden, agree to turn over 2,500 acres of land, locat- ed in Hanover County, which is two and one-half miles from the County Court House at Hanover, Va., to be used as a reform school for colored boys. "Said refrom school is to be placed under colored instructors, and one-half of the board of gov- ernorship must be white and the other half colored. The state of Virginia must pay a certain amount pro-rata daily for each boy incar- cerated in the school, and the Bi- enniel Session of each legislature must vote a certain sum of money not to exceed two hundred thousand dollars for new buildings and such things which go to maintain a school above the common prison. "The time limit for each boy in- carcerated must not exceed two years unless it is found that the boy has not improved. A school must be maintained by the state for the purpose of giving each boy, who is uneducated, a fourth grade education. Said school must be operated under well-trained teach- ers day and night so as to give such boys who are working daily on the farms of the said land to grow food for teachers and boys, an op- portunity to receive not less chan a fourth grade education. "It is further agreed that noth- ing but colored instructors shall ever be placed over the boys. It is further agreed that if this con- tract is violated, the said 2,500 acres of land shall revert back to my es- tate or to a contingent fund set up by the administrators of said es- tate." The land was turned over to the state, and the Virginia Manual La- bor School started. The Virginia Manual Labor School is still in op- eration, and is said to be the most oustanding reform school in the world. 30 Instructors After the demise of Mr. Smythe, a young colored woman from Washington, D.C., (Miss Ship- pings), was placed in charge of the the State of Virginia. About six years ago, Governor Ritchie sent a delegation of white school. Five years after taking the and colored citizens to look over presidency, this young woman the school. It is said that the died. Governor had in mind the placing Mr. Thomas J. Edwards, a rela- of colored instructors over Chelten- tive of mine who was teaching at ham. It is also alleged that whites Tuskegee, was then induced by Dr. in southern Maryland bitterly Washington to take the presidency fought the suggestion that colored of the school. title: IN AND ABOUT BALTIMORE: THE NEW STREET
full_text: IN AND ABOUT BALTIMORE By K. W. THE NEW STREET Coming to this office Friday after- noon two young women hailed me at Druid Hill Avenue and St. Mary's Street and blandly asked me where was Jazzbo Street. At first I thought they had discovered I was from the country and were out to have some fun, but suddenly I thought of Jas- per Street, but a square above. I told them, and that was IT. * WHAT'S THE NUMBER? It was told in Tuesday's edition of the AFRO of Norfolk's num- bers determination from prices dis- played in provision stores, but the Virginia City has nothing on Balti- more when it comes to the playing of lotteries in the Jewish neighbor- hood stores of the Northwestern sec- tion. These merchants collect thousands of dollars weekly from players and pay the bets to the winners. There is no attempt at concealment, for I went into two last week in which I had never been. The wagers were made in rear rooms, but a general conversation was carried on in the stores before me whom they had never seen. I daresay mey have no fear of the law or assume all co' ed people play the digits. In one of the stores, at the time I was there, was a small boy who came to purchase a yo-yo, and to receive free the ground-work for a pre-numbers education which will admit him to the gambling colleges where craps and cards will lead to his post-graduate course in the higher institutions, Cheltenham, the jail. House of Correction, and peni- tentiary. Jews are the colored peo- ple's best friends? Oh yeah? .. DON'T BE FUNNY * My Nordic fellow columnist and brother Baltimorean, Carroll Du- laney, has evidently imbibed of the draught of prejudice and bias which makes him throw all regard to de- cency and usual sense of fair play into Bodkin Creek. Here's how he feels about it: "I have seen only one intelli- gent white man die at the end of a rope by order of the court, and I believe it was the humiliation of the thing which hurt him most. "The others whose sudden, but not unexpected, departure from this world I had the questionable honor to witness were dumb, brutish human beings. And all of them, white and black, seemed deeply sensible of the fact that they were the heroes of the oc- casion. "Certainly, of the negroes, not cne would have exchanged places with the Sheriff. They had been worked up to a high pitch of re- ligious emotion, and all were sure of a safe landing in some prom- ised land where work was un- known, and life was grand round of gin, and chicken din- ners. a "I am convinced that each and every one of them choked a final prayer of thanksgiving for a civi- lization that warrants such a taking off." So there you are. The same dam- nable concept, but the difference is that it is spread by a man who knows better. He is aware that ALL colored people do not drink gin, shoot craps, and, I'll add, do the Charleston. For whom did the old-time chefs prepare fried chicken in the restaurants of Baltimore with such excellence that the fame of the dish is known around the world? clean_text: IN AND ABOUT BALTIMORE By K. W. THE NEW STREET Coming to this office Friday after- noon two young women hailed me at Druid Hill Avenue and St. Mary's Street and blandly asked me where was Jazzbo Street. At first I thought they had discovered I was from the country and were out to have some fun, but suddenly I thought of Jas- per Street, but a square above. I told them, and that was IT. * WHAT'S THE NUMBER? It was told in Tuesday's edition of the AFRO of Norfolk's num- bers determination from prices dis- played in provision stores, but the Virginia City has nothing on Balti- more when it comes to the playing of lotteries in the Jewish neighbor- hood stores of the Northwestern sec- tion. These merchants collect thousands of dollars weekly from players and pay the bets to the winners. There is no attempt at concealment, for I went into two last week in which I had never been. The wagers were made in rear rooms, but a general conversation was carried on in the stores before me whom they had never seen. I daresay mey have no fear of the law or assume all co' ed people play the digits. In one of the stores, at the time I was there, was a small boy who came to purchase a yo-yo, and to receive free the ground-work for a pre-numbers education which will admit him to the gambling colleges where craps and cards will lead to his post-graduate course in the higher institutions, Cheltenham, the jail. title: Held for Stealing 15 Pounds of Beef
full_text: Held for Stealing 15 Pounds of Beef Suspected of stealing a round of beef from a stall in Lexington Mar- ket, a 60-year-old man was com- mitted for the action of the grand jury, Saturday. Arraigned before Magistrate Bauer in Western police court, the man, Charles Colbert, 60, of 2420 Stock- ton Street, pleaded not guilty of the charge. The operator of the stall, Frank Knell, reported to Western district police that the beef had been stol- en from his stall. Colbert was arrested on suspi- cion, and later charged with the theft of the fifteen pounds of meat valued at $5. The lock on the stall had been broken during the night.. Bail for the accused was set at $500 and his committment ordered after he was unable to arrange bail. 0- BOY SENT TO REFORMATORY Said to have disrupted the moral of the St. Marys Home and to have been incorrigible, Louis Hall, 11, a former inmate of the home, was ordered sent to the Cheltenham Re- formatory after a hearing in the ju- venile court, Friday. Despite the EPHRAIM PERRY, a police officer of Philadelphia, who found the dead body of Dorothy Lutz, white, in an abandoned house. William Brown, 16, was convicted for the murder of the child. POLICE BOARD LETS COLORED TAKE EXAM Examining Board Breaks Prejudice Bar in Giving Application Blanks for Tests. In conjunction with the recom- mendation of the recent grand jury that colored men and women be appointed to the police force of Baltimore members of the Board of Police Examiners expressed willing- ness Wednesday to accept the appli- cations of all qualified persons who wanted to join the force. The police examiners are prepar- ing to hold an examination for headquarters clerks, station house clerks and signal and telephone operators. The qualifications neces- sary for a person to receive an ap- plication blank is that the appli- cant be twenty-five years of age, a citizen of the State of Maryland, and a registered voter. Applicants must have a high school education, never have been arrested, indicted or convicted of any violation of the law. The requirements for the position of clerk requires that the applicant be able to take short hand accu- rately and operate a typewriter speedily. The first colored to quali- fy for an application to take the examination was Levi H. Jolley, a reporter on the AFRO-AMERICAN, who received blank No. 854. boy's age, he has been arraigned in the court on previous occasions for being incorrigible. title: Three Boys Ordered To Reform School
full_text: Three Boys Ordered To Reform School Facing accusation of violating state felony and misdemeanor laws, three boys were ordered sent to the Cheltenham Reformatory, this week. Arraigned in the Juvenile Court before Judge Waxter, the boys gave their names as: Oscar Allen, 14, 1 Port Deposit, Md.: Ernest Johnson, 14. 1142 N. Carrollton Avenue, and Arthur Clark, 927 McDonough. SIFTING THE NEWS By RALPH MATTHEWS RELIGIOUS RACKETEERS Baltimore readers who have been following the trials and tribula- dons of Father Divine might be interested in knowing that the good prophet did not descend from heaven at all, but was at one time a Baltimore hedge-cutter. It was in his home here in Baltimore that he got the idea that he would like to be "God," it has been revealed by the Rev. St. Bishop, The Vine. In a statement to the press this week by the Rev. St. Bishop, he has the following to say: Not only did I teach Divine. but I taught Steamboat Bill- you know, Joe Worlds, Divine's old running mate. It was I who first pronounced the theory that God was within man. I made that statement in 1912 when I was staying at Divine's house in Baltimore, and Divine-he was only George Baker, a hedge-cut- ter, at that time-grabbed it and started out preaching that it had come to him from Heaven. It ain't right, if you know what I mean. This merely proves that even the business of getting people into heaven has been reduced to a racket and the racketeers are stealing each other's thunder. OKLAHOMA TEACHES BANDITS Bank bandits who attempted to rob the Farmers and Merchants Bank at Boley, Okla., found that the idea some seek to create about the Negro's inability to conduct business, was erroneous. Two of the bandits, one white and one colored, are dead from the bullets of a colored bank teller. Many are afraid to entrust their money to our banks. H. C. McCormick, the fearless teller, who shot It out with the bandits before he let them carry off the bank's money, proved that when it comes to risking one's life, the colored teller is all there. title: Six Boys Sent tot Cheltenham
full_text: The Laugh Is on Us Readers of the early edition of the AFRO for the current week were informed of the Scriptural contradic- tions discovered by the Rev. Dr. Harry Emerson Fosdick, who points out that only 5,500 Hebrews were freed in Egypt instead of 600,000; and instead of one set of Ten Commandments, there were two. Dr. Fosdick, after a trip to the Holy Land, observes that a traveler is immediately impressed with the fact that it would have been a physical impossibility for the number of people mentioned in the Biblical narrative to have lived in the land of Goshen. Although well founded, Dr. Fosdick's contradictions will have little effect upon alleged Christians, because few churchgoers of either race take religion or the Bible or anything connected therewith seriously any more, since everyday life as practiced by the world at large is itself a complete contradiction of everything the Bible sets forth. Devout churchgoers whoop it up for brotherly love and champion the cause of segregation, discrimina- tion and Jim Crow. Pious charlatans weep crocodile tears over Pha- raoh's oppression of the Hebrew children, while they squeeze the life blood out of their own employees with long hours, intolerable conditions and low wages. Mealy-mouthed hypocrites mourn and lament over the crucifixion of Christ while they turn a deaf ear to the wails of agony of innocent men and women who are meet- ing similar fates at the hands of modern lynchers. While it is highly probable that the early chroniclers, unassisted by modern tabulating equipment, may have been a little off in their arithmetic, we can imagine what a laugh they would get picking out the contradictions in practices of the supposedly Christian nations after 1933 years in which to evolve some degree of perfection. The laugh is on us, not the Bible. title: HELD FOR COURT ON NUMBERS CHARGE
full_text: HELD FOR COURT ON NUMBERS CHARGE Charged with accepting money as bets on numbers, William Henry, 41, was ordered held for action of the Criminal Court after a hearing in the Northwestern police court on Monday. According to the policeman, who made the arrest, Henry was sitting on a bootblack stand in a shoe re- pair shop in the 1400 block Gilmor Street, when the money was given to him. The patrolman alleged that Hen- ry accepted the money and gave the bettor a slip as receipt. A book. said by the policeman to have been used by Henry, was shown at the hearing, before Magistrate Pohl- haus. The alleged number writing is said to have occurred on April 15. Bail was set at $500. 0 Four Boys Sent to Reform School Arraigned in the Juvenile Court on charges preferred by policemen, Your boys were ordered by Judge Thomas F. Waxter, to be commit- ted to the Cheltenham Reforma- tory, this week. The committments were for pe- riods ranging from one year to four years. The boys who were sent to the school were Gordon Giles, 15, 1119 McCulloh Street, Marshall Dorsey, 11, 927 Stricker Street; Ir- vin Gordon, 14, 707 Edmondson Avenue, and Robert Garrett, 14, of 1155 Carrollton Avenue. The boys' records show that they were con- victed of having committed felon- ies. Two of the boys were second cffenders and at large on parole. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: Shaking Hands With the Scottsboro Boys
full_text: Shaking Hands With the Scottsboro Boys Ralph Matthews's interview with the Scotts- boro boys published in last week's AFRO-AMERI- CAN, presents a human side of this case that has not been emphasized. They have been represented as nine innocent boys, wrongfully imprisoned for three years and condemned to death. In two trials in lower courts, and in two ap- peals to higher courts, these boys have simply been human types without individuality, rarely referred to by name and usually grouped with others as the "Scottsboro Boys." Mr. Matthews certainly begins the process of Individualization. Haywood Patterson is the lead- er and handles the fan mail. He wants to get out and see his people, to go to work and raise a family. Patterson wants to be a bell-boy; Ossie Powell an office boy: Eugene Williams wants to learn typewriting; Allie Montgomery would be Pull- man porter. Down the list it goes. Each has his own eager- ness to get out of jail and each his own plan how to build a future out of a bad beginning.  Boldly, Honestly and Without Equivocation Harry W. Nice, Republican, who needed less than a hundred votes to defeat Albert C. Ritchie for Governor several years ago, has his hat in the ring again. Item number three in his recent "political. talk" to fellow Republicans, calls upon them "TO MEET ALL ISSUES BOLDLY, HONESTLY AND WITHOUT EQUIVOCATION." If that is good medicine for the party, it is also good medicine for the candidate. So we ask Mr. Nice a few questions: Mr. Nice, if the people elect you Governor of the State, would you: 1. Wipe out the race lines in the State laws governing pay of county school teachers? 2. Pledge your party to erase the Jim Crow car laws off the statute books? 3. Substitute for Cheltenham a State reform school for boys equal in equipment and promise to the new Melvale Girls' Reformatory, built by Governor Ritchie? 4. Pledge your party to provide equal facilities for college and university education for all citi- zens instead of just for whites as at present? 5. Pledge yourself to open every phase of State employment to all citizens and see to it that Negroes are employed in proportion to their party strength? These issues are simple, wise and just, Mr. Nice. Do you dare meet them "boidly, honestly and without equivocation?"  The Tuskegee Murders In all that has been reported in these columns concerning the three recent murders at Tuske- gee Institute, there has been no expressed or im- plied criticism of the administration of Dr. R. R. Moton, the principal, except in one particular. Goodness knows, our college and university heads have enough troubles without burdening them also with gratuitous criticism from a non- educational source. Dr. Moton has done a job which few people thought could be done. He filled the shoes of Booker T. Washington, not only consolidating the gains made by Mr. Washington, but adding eight millions in endowment and improvements in plant, personnel and course of study. Unfortunately, into that happy picture are flung three killings, all of them brutal. In two of the cases, the killer or killers escaped. In the third case he was a crazy man, whom many people knew was demented months before the killing. Unfortunately, also, murder is not a private affair. It is a crime for which our laws provide a definite procedure. Individuals may think that the interests of the schcol, or of a community warrant them in inter- fering with orderly processes of grand jury in- dictment and trial before a court. But no one has a right to think himself beyond the law. If Tuskegee has made any error at all, it seems to have made it in assuming that a murder case could be hushed up. It can't. It is as true today as 6,000 years ago, "Murder will out."  Kidnaping Must Stop Or- Beginning with one kidnaping in 1931, the number increased to seven in 1932 and in the first half of 1933 to twelve. The end of prohibi- tion makes it necessary for bootleggers and rack- eteers to seek a new field. They turned to kid- kidnaping. Ransoms demanded vary from $250,000 asked by the kidnapers of John O'Con- nell in Albany, N.Y., to $30,000 paid for release of Mary McElroy in Kansas City. Except for one case in Atlanta and another In Virginia, all kidnapings have happened in the North. Lynchings are preceded by kidnaping. and the South is especially skilled in all phases of lynchings. If the North has more kidnapings, it is because it has more rich people able to pay ransoms. The United States Department of Justice an- nounces a campaign to wipe out kidnap gangs. It is a little Cate. It would be much further ahead had it acted directly after the stealing of the Lindbergh baby, which, incidentally, was the only kidnap case which has ended in murder. National horror at the outcome of the Lind- bergh case has probably caused quick compromise in all eighteen kidnapings since. Victims have been willing to pay, and the racketeers have avoided killings. We have demonstrated how to violate the Prohibition Law and get away with it; how to avoid income taxes and get away with it; how to lynch Negroes and get away with it, and how to violate all insurance and banking laws and get away with that. Our country today represents the sad spec- tacle of wholesale conspiracies to beat the law. If the kidnaping racket can be added to this roll of national sins, we are that much closer to ruin and decay. If kidnapers can succeed, sooner or later, we are all sunk. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

Goodness knows, our college and university heads have enough troubles without burdening them also with gratuitous criticism from a non- educational source. Dr. Moton has done a job which few people thought could be done. He filled the shoes of Booker T. Washington, not only consolidating the gains made by Mr. Washington, but adding eight millions in endowment and improvements in plant, personnel and course of study. Unfortunately, into that happy picture are flung three killings, all of them brutal. In two of the cases, the killer or killers escaped. In the third case he was a crazy man, whom many people knew was demented months before the killing. Unfortunately, also, murder is not a private affair. It is a crime for which our laws provide a definite procedure. Individuals may think that the interests of the schcol, or of a community warrant them in inter- fering with orderly processes of grand jury in- dictment and trial before a court. But no one has a right to think himself beyond the law. If Tuskegee has made any error at all, it seems to have made it in assuming that a murder case could be hushed up. It can't. It is as true today as 6,000 years ago, "Murder will out." Kidnaping Must Stop Or- Beginning with one kidnaping in 1931, the number increased to seven in 1932 and in the first half of 1933 to twelve. The end of prohibi- tion makes it necessary for bootleggers and rack- eteers to seek a new field. They turned to kid- kidnaping. Ransoms demanded vary from $250,000 asked by the kidnapers of John O'Con- nell in Albany, N.Y., to $30,000 paid for release of Mary McElroy in Kansas City. Except for one case in Atlanta and another In Virginia, all kidnapings have happened in the North. Lynchings are preceded by kidnaping. and the South is especially skilled in all phases of lynchings. If the North has more kidnapings, it is because it has more rich people able to pay ransoms. The United States Department of Justice an- nounces a campaign to wipe out kidnap gangs. It is a little Cate. It would be much further ahead had it acted directly after the stealing of the Lindbergh baby, which, incidentally, was the only kidnap case which has ended in murder. National horror at the outcome of the Lind- bergh case has probably caused quick compromise in all eighteen kidnapings since. Victims have been willing to pay, and the racketeers have avoided killings. We have demonstrated how to violate the Prohibition Law and get away with it; how to avoid income taxes and get away with it; how to lynch Negroes and get away with it, and how to violate all insurance and banking laws and get away with that. Our country today represents the sad spec- tacle of wholesale conspiracies to beat the law. If the kidnaping racket can be added to this roll of national sins, we are that much closer to ruin and decay. If kidnapers can succeed, sooner or later, we are all sunk. . .

title: RITCHIE BUDGET TO LEGISLATURE
full_text: RITCHIE BUDGET TO LEGISLATURE ANNAPOLIS, Md. - Governor Al- bert C. Ritchie's 1934-35 budget, re- duced by $9,000,000, was sent to the legislature Tuesday. While several new items appear in the list of appropriations for the next two years, cuts are noted all down the line. carried on a dual love affair with th Bowie Normal The governor's allowance for Bowie Normal School was $49,030, & cut of $1,620. Under this appropriation this school will carry 15 employees which are expected to be colored and will care for girls now committed to Melvale School. title: Two Boys Sent to Reform School
full_text: Two Boys Sent to Reform School Convicted of criminal offenses, two boys were ordered committed to the Cheltenham Reform School from juvenile court, this week. The boys were sentenced from one to three years by Magistrate Thom- as Waxter. They gave their names as Elmore Bailey, 13, 1051 W. Fay- ette Street, and James Reid, 13, 1007 W. Fayette Street. title: Youth Is Held As Cheltenham Runaway
full_text: Youth Is Held As Cheltenham Runaway Arrested and identified as the youth wanted on charges of being a fugitive from justice, Russell Mor- gan, 13. 1003 Edmondson Avenue, was held for Cheltenham Reforma- tory officials, Friday. The boy es- caped from the school June 6, past. He was arrested at his home by Northwestern District police. AFRO Editor Named on Md. N.R.A. Body President Roosevelt Names Carl J. Committee to co- ordinate the recovery program in the State and work with the Federal and State authorities in furthering the program. Others named on the committee were, W. title: SCHOOL ROBBER GANG GOES TO CHELTENHAM: SAY BOYS BURGLARIZED 24 PUBLIC SCHOOLS IN BALTO. SEE INCREASE IN DELINQUENCY MORE JUVENILE ARRESTS THAN IN 1932.
full_text: SCHOOL ROBBER GANG GOES TO CHELTENHAM Say Boys Burglarized 24 Public Schools in Balto. SEE INCREASE IN DELINQUENCY More Juvenile Arrests Than in 1932. Arraigned in the juvenile court to face charges of participation in a series of misdemeanors, or being minors without proper guardian- ship, nine boys were ordered sent to the Cheltenham Reform School by Judge Thomas E. Waxter, this week. Two of the boys, John Dixon, 10, 702 Woodyear Street, and Herman Johnson, 12, 551 Hoffman Street, were accused of entering 24 public schools, in every section of the city, and stealing supplies, money and other articles from teachers' desks, and of tearing down pay telephones in the schools and robbing the boxes. Increase Noted The other boys who were impli- cated in violations were given terms at the school ranging from one to two years. Statistics of the court show that during the first quarter the present year there has been an increase of 21 juvenile arrests in comparison with the same period of 1932. Those sent to the school were: James Hammond, 13, 863 Vine; Samuel Murray, 13, 1425 Brunt; John Perry, 17, Alexandria, Va.; James Floyd, 1105 Madison; Wm. Stokes, 16, 300 W. 20th; John Dix, 702 Woodyear; Herman Johnson, 12, 551 Hoffman; Harry Brown, 14, 633 Central; Raymond Homer, 15, 1002 Carey. 0 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

The Lamottonians among the graduates were: Ada Mann, Leola Jackson, Kathryn Williams, Lena Reed, Marian Fripps, Sarah Williams, Frank Walker, Raymond Tripplett, Charles Vaughn, Noah Fields, John Lang, James Smith, John Ashton and Joseph Stokes. Miss Helen Lee, 538 Phipps Avenue, Wil- low Grove, Park school teacher, observed the second grade class of the Martha Washington School, 44th and Aspen Sts., Tuesday, June 27. The class was taught by Miss Goldie Irvin. Miss Lee also visited Miss Roberta Robinson, Cheyney graduate, who is a teacher at the school. title: Two Escaped from Cheltenham
full_text: Two Escaped from Cheltenham E.LTIMORE, Md.- Local police were instructed to keep a lookout for the two boys who are reported to have escaped from the Cheltenham Reformatory, Thursday. Officials of the institu- tion reported the boys to be James Carter, 17, 5 feet 3 inches, 120 pounds, light brown skin. Roland Roland Sewell, 17, 5 feet 4 inches, 120 Ipounds, and brown skin. title: RITCHIE ASKS $47,500 TO AID INSTITUTIONS: $8,000 INCREASE FOR THE CHARITY RELIEF WORK IN STATE. PROVIDENT MAY GET $17,000 $5,000 MORE THAN WAS GRANTED IN 1932.
full_text: RITCHIE ASKS $47,500 TO AID INSTITUTIONS $8,000 Increase for the Charity Relief Work in State. PROVIDENT MAY GET $17,000 - $5,000 More than was Granted in 1932. A total of $47,500 for colored in- stitutions has been recommended! to Governor Albert C. Ritchie to be included in the state budget for 1933 by the Board of State Aid and Charities, it was learned this week. This amount carries an increase of $8,000 in the recommendation for the awards this year over the amounts granted last year. Of this amount, $17,000 has been asked for Provident Hospital and if included in the state budget and then passed by the state legisla- ture, the institution will receive the amount recommended. This is an increase of $5,000 over the $12,000 granted by the state to Provident Hospital last year. Cheltenham Gets $20,000 The boys' reform school at Chel- tenham, Md., will receive the highest amount if the recommen- dation of $20,000 stands the test of fitting into the budget. No in- crease for this school has been ask- led. It was recommended to the board in a letter that a colored social worker be placed at Cheltenham. The matter will be discussed in a later meeting, it was said at the offices of the board, Thursday. The Good Shepherd Hospital has been recommended a sum of $1,500 with no increase asked over last year. Asks Three Increases In the two other cases of colored institutions the St. Anthony Or- phanage will receive a total of $4,000, an increase of $1,000 and the James Cardinal Gibbons In- Istitute has been recommended to receive a total of $5,000, with a $2,000 increase. title: Taking A Chief Down
full_text: Taking A Chief Down Chief Tshekedi was deposed as ruler of the Bamangwato tribe last week by order of Vice Admiral E. R. G. R. Evans of the British navy, acting as high commissioner for the Bechunaland, protectorate, The chief's crime was that he had presid- ed over a native court which some time ago sentenced a white man to be flogged for living in adultery with a native woman. British laws forbid trial of a European by native court. The chief, who was edu- cated in England, contended that the white man had become a native by taking a com- mon-law native wife and that no European should be allowed to take advantage of the law to flout morals and outrage decency, even in Africa. Fifteen thousand native subjects stood around and watched the British vice ad. miral depose their chief, whose sole recourse now is an appeal to the British crown. Two hundred fully armed British marines ac- companied the vice admiral to impress upon naked black men that British laws are sacred even in tropical interiors of Africa. From this distance, it appears that che English authorities had determined upon the deposition of Chief Tshekedi in advance. Two admissions support this theory. One, the testimony of the European who was flogged, to the effect that he submitted him- self voluntarily to the native court and did not wish to prosecute the case. The other was the pronouncement of the vice admiral that Chief Tshekedi had pre- viously flouted the British administration. The decision of the court would indicate that the chastity of African women and na- tive laws regarding it need not be respected by Europeans, The New Melvale School for Delinquent Girls With the appointment of Miss Sadie George and thirteen assistants to the new state girls' reform school and announcement that the new $150,000 plant will be ready for occupancy by October, Maryland is in a fair way to start work on the proper hand- ling of delinquent cases. It has been a long way from the inade- quate institution at Melvale to a modern re- form school, and even those who have been impatient at the long delay may rejoice that a new day is at hand. Grand jury after grand jury recommend- ed a new plant for Melvale, but until Juve- nile Court Justice J. S. Waxter, Mrs. Marie O. H. Bauernschmidt, and interested friends sold the plans to Governor Ritchie and the state legislature, nothing was accomplished With the new school in operation and the state engaged in the job of making good citizens out of delinquent girls, it ought not be too difficult a job to convince the Gov- ernor that what he has done for the girls at Melvale ought to be duplicated for the boys at Cheltenham. During Governor Ritchie's administration the state has erected a new tuberculosis hospital at Henryton and a new girls' reform school to take the place of Melvale. All good things go by threes. A new Cheltenham ought certainly to supplement the other two good forward steps. Furthermore, the outgoing grand jury recommended it. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Former Cheltenham Inmate Says It Is True Guards Club Boys There.
full_text: Former Cheltenham Inmate Says It Is True Guards Club Boys There. To the Editor of the AFRO: I noticed in your issue of this week's AFRO, September 22, a picture of boys coming from work at Cheltenham House of Refor- mation, and also noted that John Pyles, white, superintendent, says it is all right for guards to hit boys. In 1925, when I lived in Oak- land, Md., I wrote your paper a letter concerning conditions at the Cheltenham reformatory and at that time, according to your paper and the Baltimore Sun, Pyles was supposed to be fired. It seems as though he is still in charge. I am a former inmate of the institution, and it is true boys are clubbed. When they are not work- ing to suit the guards and in the hottest weather are allowed one- half of cup of water. They are cut out of meals hand running three and four at a time. This is all for this time, but I can give you plenty of informa- tion about Cheltenham. WILLIAM CREASY Pennsylvania.

title: The Whirling Hub
full_text: The Whirling Hub By HERBERT TUCKER and ELLIOTT FREEMAN BOSTON-There is a pinch-hitter in our midst! Our dear friend Elliott has decided that a much-needed rest is the best thing for him, and that there was no better time than Yuletide, so that he can sit and wait patiently by his fireplace for good old Santa. WHERE TO GO * * * * * The T.I.D.'s are making a stab to come back into prominence by giving a shipwreck party at 65 Munroe Street, December 21. You are supposed to have been on the ship when it was wrecked. Whatever you had on at the time, is what you are supposed to wear to the party. I'll see you there. The Mizpahs, a club formed long before my time and your time, is entertaining the public with a dance at the Ritz Plaza during the Yuletide season. DID YOU KNOW THAT? * * * * John Tyler, one of the presidents of the United States, sold his white daughter into slavery because she fell in love with a slave and eloped with him? The colored population of New York is proportionately a third more intelligent than the white population? A colored man invented the first clock made in America in 1754? The city of Chicago was founded by a colored man in 1779? Jan Matzeliger, a Dutch colored man living in Masachusetts, invented the first machine for sewing the sole of shoes to the uppers? (It was his invention that revolutionized the shoe-manufacturing business.) The characters portrayed in the Bible are largely colored? The fastest bicyclist of the world was a colored man by the name of M. W. Taylor? A lowly Louisiana colored man, posing as Prince Apache of Arizona, became the social lion of the elite of New York and Phila- delphia about twenty years ago? Hair-straightening is not a mod- ern idea? Blacks of Egypt straightened their locks about five thous- and years ago? * * WHAT YOU OUGHT TO KNOW * * Louis Davis, formerly of this city but now of Washington, has returned to her native city for an indefinite stay. Her return may mean the renewal of her childhood romance with Herbert Chelten- ham. Millie Braxton of Everett received a telephone call the other day that sent her reeling. To whom the voice on the other side of the wire belonged. I was not able to learn. Whoever it was, must be a blip to have told her something that made her forget all about the dinner she was eating at the time. Such is love! Eva Moulton is said to be making trips to the Snowden house- hold, now that it is time for the great O. Philip to return. There is nothing like having two on the string, is there, Eva? (Aside to O. Philip: your mother says for you to write to her, telling her your plans for the holidays. Are stationery and stamps so scarce down there?) Curtis McLain has elected himself president of the mythical "Back to Normal Club of Boston." Mac seems to feel that the social standards of Boston have gone down too much. He feels that with four colleagues he can bring Boston's social standards back to normal. The former Jean Bonner of U Street, Cambridge, gave birth to a diminutive baby not long ago. It is an incubator baby weighing less than five pounds. Congratulations to you. Jean. Current news has it that Bus Nurse will middle aisle it shortly with Beauteous Erie.. What's sauce for the goose is often sauce for the gander! Con- grats and rats from the gang. (Who is jealous?) That the Brown Marlene is too lucky in numbers, is an event long to be remembered, since she gives tips only to the femmes. It would be wise to send your gals over sometimes, youse guys. You might get rich quicker than you expect! Through byways and highways it is flashed that a certain cor- respondent has learned that the ex-boy friend still likes her a little bit. Friends in Medford would like to see them reconciled. Gladys Bracy was seen in a new make car with plates bearing Rhode Island registrations. The question is: who was her escort? These Cam- bridge girls certainly get around; don't they? It seems that Gladys Wiggins still has a weak spot for her ex- flame, if one were to judge by the way they were talking together the other night. Gladys must have had a rather nice time at the party on Thanksgiving night. So did tots of others, including Alice Mills. Eva Wiggins is seen making weekly treks across the bridge to West Springfield Street now. Evidently it is too cold for the boy friend to make his way across the Charles. or he isn't that much interested. We are led to believe that Teddy Anderson, member of the junior debs, is giving Jimmy Butler, of Depression Hill, the gate. Joe Grey, who is now in Arizona, is asking a certain Arlington Street lass to act more naturally. Kitty Gaines seems to be having plenty of competition, if we are to believe the latest bulletin. Wags have it that a very sweet brown-skin minister and his wife's best girl friend are that way about each other. The girl friend's hubby has a hot temper, but everything is still O.K. because he is still in the dark. Brother, you had better come out in the light before it is too late. Flash!! Saturday night the owl positively will appear at sev- eral rent parties on the Hill. * WHAT YOU WOULD LIKE TO KNOW * What is the Owl and where can he or she be found? What has happened to E. Brown these days? This is asked especially for the benefit of a certain University City lass. Who is, and who is not, going to receive invitations to the Junior League party during the holidays? Why is it that Elaine Roberts, a girl with more charming grace than most of the girls on the Hill, must consistently attend the movies all by herself? What is Parson Charles Gabriel's story? He has declared a moratorium on women through this column al- most weekly and yet each time he is seen out he has a different girl with him. We all thought the parson was a man of his word. That's what thought does for you. Jean Westmoreland, a school girl from D.C., says that Boston fellows are the silliest she has ever met. Why is it that she continues to go out with them? You can't tell me that that old adage about a bird in the hand being worth two in the bush, has any effect on her. * * * * PERFECT MATCHES A certain correspondent of this burg sent in the following matches. In making these pairs he kept in mind the old universal law that opposites attract and likes repel. Here is hoping that no one takes this to heart and accuses yours truly of foul-play. First, we have the perfect match of the Brown Adonis and that most prominent member of the Little Women, our own Inez Russell, of Sargent fame. Next comes the no less perfect match of Kenneth McLain and that belle of Rhode Island, Josephine Edmonds. While in the family, we must stop to consider little Curtis with the petite and alluring Dibby Wilkins. Wallace Roundtree comes to the front with Eleanor Smith, Ted Gould steps into the limelight with Mar- garet O'Banion. William Johnson takes a liking to a great many, but those in particular are Vera Sport, Virginia Ridley, Clarice Cưr- tis and Dorothy Tell. Edward Hutchins is destined to be with Doro- thea Lindenburger. John Tucker seems to prefer Yvette Keelan. Jesse Williams paired with Olga White, Parson Charles Gabriel shows preference to the buxom and debonair Eva Moulton. Ted Louden is pre-destined to be with Myrtle Green, Craig York is matched with his secret passion, Catherine Watson. Herb Chelten- ham is hard to match, but the recent arrival of Louise Davis from the District of Columbia solves the question. Connie Davis, without a doubt, chooses the dapper Vinvent Wilbur. clean_text: The Whirling Hub By HERBERT TUCKER and ELLIOTT FREEMAN BOSTON-There is a pinch-hitter in our midst! Our dear friend Elliott has decided that a much-needed rest is the best thing for him, and that there was no better time than Yuletide, so that he can sit and wait patiently by his fireplace for good old Santa. WHERE TO GO * * * * * The T.I.D.'s are making a stab to come back into prominence by giving a shipwreck party at 65 Munroe Street, December 21. You are supposed to have been on the ship when it was wrecked. Whatever you had on at the time, is what you are supposed to wear to the party. I'll see you there. The Mizpahs, a club formed long before my time and your time, is entertaining the public with a dance at the Ritz Plaza during the Yuletide season. DID YOU KNOW THAT? * * * * John Tyler, one of the presidents of the United States, sold his white daughter into slavery because she fell in love with a slave and eloped with him? The colored population of New York is proportionately a third more intelligent than the white population? A colored man invented the first clock made in America in 1754? The city of Chicago was founded by a colored man in 1779? Jan Matzeliger, a Dutch colored man living in Masachusetts, invented the first machine for sewing the sole of shoes to the uppers? (It was his invention that revolutionized the shoe-manufacturing business.) The characters portrayed in the Bible are largely colored? The fastest bicyclist of the world was a colored man by the name of M. W. Taylor? A lowly Louisiana colored man, posing as Prince Apache of Arizona, became the social lion of the elite of New York and Phila- delphia about twenty years ago? Hair-straightening is not a mod- ern idea? Blacks of Egypt straightened their locks about five thous- and years ago? * * WHAT YOU OUGHT TO KNOW * * Louis Davis, formerly of this city but now of Washington, has returned to her native city for an indefinite stay. Her return may mean the renewal of her childhood romance with Herbert Chelten- ham. Millie Braxton of Everett received a telephone call the other day that sent her reeling. To whom the voice on the other side of the wire belonged. I was not able to learn. Whoever it was, must be a blip to have told her something that made her forget all about the dinner she was eating at the time. Such is love! Eva Moulton is said to be making trips to the Snowden house- hold, now that it is time for the great O. Philip to return. There is nothing like having two on the string, is there, Eva? (Aside to O. Philip: your mother says for you to write to her, telling her your plans for the holidays. Are stationery and stamps so scarce down there?) Curtis McLain has elected himself president of the mythical "Back to Normal Club of Boston." Mac seems to feel that the social standards of Boston have gone down too much. He feels that with four colleagues he can bring Boston's social standards back to normal. The former Jean Bonner of U Street, Cambridge, gave birth to a diminutive baby not long ago. It is an incubator baby weighing less than five pounds. Congratulations to you. Jean. Current news has it that Bus Nurse will middle aisle it shortly with Beauteous Erie.. What's sauce for the goose is often sauce for the gander! Con- grats and rats from the gang. (Who is jealous?) That the Brown Marlene is too lucky in numbers, is an event long to be remembered, since she gives tips only to the femmes. It would be wise to send your gals over sometimes, youse guys. You might get rich quicker than you expect! Through byways and highways it is flashed that a certain cor- respondent has learned that the ex-boy friend still likes her a little bit. Friends in Medford would like to see them reconciled. Gladys Bracy was seen in a new make car with plates bearing Rhode Island registrations. The question is: who was her escort? These Cam- bridge girls certainly get around; don't they? It seems that Gladys Wiggins still has a weak spot for her ex- flame, if one were to judge by the way they were talking together the other night. Gladys must have had a rather nice time at the party on Thanksgiving night. So did tots of others, including Alice Mills. Eva Wiggins is seen making weekly treks across the bridge to West Springfield Street now. Evidently it is too cold for the boy friend to make his way across the Charles. or he isn't that much interested. We are led to believe that Teddy Anderson, member of the junior debs, is giving Jimmy Butler, of Depression Hill, the gate. Joe Grey, who is now in Arizona, is asking a certain Arlington Street lass to act more naturally. Kitty Gaines seems to be having plenty of competition, if we are to believe the latest bulletin. Wags have it that a very sweet brown-skin minister and his wife's best girl friend are that way about each other. The girl friend's hubby has a hot temper, but everything is still O.K. because he is still in the dark. Brother, you had better come out in the light before it is too late. Flash!! Saturday night the owl positively will appear at sev- eral rent parties on the Hill. * WHAT YOU WOULD LIKE TO KNOW * What is the Owl and where can he or she be found? What has happened to E. Brown these days? This is asked especially for the benefit of a certain University City lass. Who is, and who is not, going to receive invitations to the Junior League party during the holidays? Why is it that Elaine Roberts, a girl with more charming grace than most of the girls on the Hill, must consistently attend the movies all by herself? What is Parson Charles Gabriel's story? He has declared a moratorium on women through this column al- most weekly and yet each time he is seen out he has a different girl with him. We all thought the parson was a man of his word. That's what thought does for you. Jean Westmoreland, a school girl from D.C., says that Boston fellows are the silliest she has ever met. Why is it that she continues to go out with them? You can't tell me that that old adage about a bird in the hand being worth two in the bush, has any effect on her. * * * * PERFECT MATCHES A certain correspondent of this burg sent in the following matches. In making these pairs he kept in mind the old universal law that opposites attract and likes repel. Here is hoping that no one takes this to heart and accuses yours truly of foul-play. First, we have the perfect match of the Brown Adonis and that most prominent member of the Little Women, our own Inez Russell, of Sargent fame. Next comes the no less perfect match of Kenneth McLain and that belle of Rhode Island, Josephine Edmonds. While in the family, we must stop to consider little Curtis with the petite and alluring Dibby Wilkins. Wallace Roundtree comes to the front with Eleanor Smith, Ted Gould steps into the limelight with Mar- garet O'Banion. William Johnson takes a liking to a great many, but those in particular are Vera Sport, Virginia Ridley, Clarice Cưr- tis and Dorothy Tell. Edward Hutchins is destined to be with Doro- thea Lindenburger. John Tucker seems to prefer Yvette Keelan. Jesse Williams paired with Olga White, Parson Charles Gabriel shows preference to the buxom and debonair Eva Moulton. Ted Louden is pre-destined to be with Myrtle Green, Craig York is matched with his secret passion, Catherine Watson. Herb Chelten- ham is hard to match, but the recent arrival of Louise Davis from the District of Columbia solves the question. Connie Davis, without a doubt, chooses the dapper Vinvent Wilbur. Julia Wormley and Rollins Ross are mutually put together. Beautiful Louise Scott now prefers the more mature type of boy friend, in the person of Oswald Jordan. Corrinne Howe and Edward Ricker constitute a pair. And how! Enough said! Georgette Foster and Wallace Owens, of Ever- ett, seem to be solemnly laying their racket. Wallace seems to have supplanted little Bussy in Georgette's heart. It seems that the ro- mance between Evelyn Harvey and Norman Hornsby is quite in bloom. What Franklin Street lass is keeping that Christmas mail packed? Ten letters daily is what she sends through Uncle Sam. . .

title: Note from Inmate Cites Brutality at Cheltenham: Boy Charges That Clothing Is Taken, and Food Is Unfit for Dogs.
full_text: Note from Inmate Cites Brutality af Cheltenham Boy Charges That Clothing Is Taken, and Food Is Unfit for Dogs. (Reprinted from late editions of last week) The charges that clothing is tak- en, brutal beatings administered, and poor food is served were lodged this week against Chelten- ham, the reform school for boys, by an inmate whose name is being withheld by the AFRO. The contents of the letter telling of conditions are: "I am an inmate at the Chelten- ham reformatory. "People of Maryland don't know what the boys go through down here. Last week a boy died after being beaten and another was beaten over the head with a club. "The food that is cooked for us is not fit for dogs, but when high officials come down we can't tell them the real way we get along because we are beaten when they leave if we do. Boys' Suits Taken. "If a boy comes here with a suit that is worth $25, it is taken away from him and the first boy to go home gets it. The same thing is done with shoes. "When the boys are drilling, if one of them drops out he is left under a tree until he comes to. "The officers had a still down here, and some of the boys who found it were beaten with sticks and locked up on bread and water. "A boy was writing a story to your paper and another boy told on him. He was badly beaten. If I get out of here, I'll be good, all- right. Some Fall in Water. "In the winter we cut ice on the water and some fall in, they get beaten but when people come down here they don't see any- thing. "I am not telling who I am or how I got this letter out of here, but I am begging the people of Baltimore and the State of Mary- land to see about the boys in Cheltenham. "We were bad boys at home but thouse of reformation is just as bad." Judge Waxter Speaks. Judge Thomas J. S. Waxter, of the juvenile court, was allowed to read the letter. Some parts of it were unintelligible because of the way in which it was written, but he asserted that conditions were bad at Cheltenham. He declared that a preliminary report on a survey that is being made had disclosed many interest- ing things, and he also stated that the next session of the legislature should make the place a state institution. Judge Waxter believes that the institution should be manned largely by a colored personnel, and he stated that the report so far is "loaded with dynamite." This report is being assembled by a representative of the Child Welfare League of America. Grand Jury Urged Change. In the report of January 4, the grand jury recommended that col- ored officers be placed at the re- formatory, and that the state take over the control of it. It was also pointed out that the officials had a difficult job to per- form in the taking care of boys who are physically unsound, feeble-minded, tubercular, and delinquent. The jurymen declared that the food, at a noon meal which was witnessed, was limited in variety although of a wholesome quality.

title: Other 59 -- No Title
full_text: | DREAM DUST GABBIN By THE GHOST NEW YORK CITY The first and largest Easter partee was that given by Cecella and Mabel Dudley. The second was that of Norma Potts, and last, but not least, Eddie (Comites) Parkus. Have you met Barrett Johnson's two cousins, the Smith boys?,? Frances (Am- sterdam News) Ball, maybe you forgot to mention that Betty (Jr. Deb.) Arrington refuses to go anywhere without Warren Hollings, and Billy Garvin does not go to Columbia, and Frankie (Comites) Bourne is not an Elite. THINGS I CAN'T SEE Why Evelyn Bryant sticks to David Brown when he's just gaga over Eurtha Edwards. Why Julia Summersett pulled that fast one in the Café Royal and then took a week-end trip? Why Muriel Southerland refuses to have a boy friend'and spends most of her time staying at home reading? Why the Junior Debs are only taking in three members, which, by the way, is a violation of their quota. MYSTERIES AND OTHER TALK : Philip (Budy) Hilton moved to bachelor quarters. I have to smile whenever I see Leonard (Comites) Jones clown...He's good. Leo Terry has been nicknamed "Mouse- o" by the boys. I wonder if Teddy Mays has a new heart beat? Clement Carrington is in South America. The Issac Jacksons (Evelyn Wiggens) have just greeted Sir Stork. And Dame Rumor has it that Buster Jacobs is engaged to Grace Peyton. The 29 Club of Brooklyn, called so be- cause it has twenty-nine members, is throwing its annual shake in May. Howard Blackston, àce relay man for Boys' High, spent the whole week-end in the nation's capital. Johnye-Pope Mitchel and Winnie Mason are doing their best to make that stage- coach party a success. Ernie Stoken came in for the holidays to take Elizabeth (Jr. Deb.) Johnson places. Jimmie (Brown) Kingsland came into town and then went down to D.C. A few of the Guardsmen are preparing to go South, to a formal to be given by the Baltimore chapter in that vicinity. Didi Walterding will not go places when her heart-throb, Buster Davis, is not in town. Arlene Barker gave a nice freeble at her house on Wednesday last, and this brought about a new romance between Constance (Brooklyn Deb) Clark and Harry (Skeeter) Hollings. The boys call Aida (Red Pepper) Bear- den. "Lady Dunkwell." And Doris Carter, "Duchess Mothball." That Doris Bly-Edgar Cheltenham affair looks serious. I wonder what's happened to Dorothy Douglas, Edna Mitchell, and Doris Madden. Donald Carrington doesn't know it yet but he nearly broke up a perfect- friend- ship between two guys and two gals. Wyetta Hill could save a lot of trouble by explaining why she didn't come up for the holidays. Jimmy (Guardsmen) Adams plans to at- tend N. Y. U. when he finishes Lincoln. Ask Elaine Walterding to show you that new set of photos she had made. Hope Davis has been seeing a lot of Leonard Jones and it's all done when Ann Lewis Motley isn't looking, too. Kenneth James of Manhattan comes over this-a-way a lot and Mary Burwell is the cause of it all. Barney Hairston and Vera Trice are slated to be married in June. * THINGS I'M SURE ABOUT Ford Dabney dates up Roberta (Bert) McLain every day but Sunday. Frank (Toekwood) Kirkland takes charge of them. Arthur Burwell and Unis Presley have returned home after spending the week in D. C. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: 8 BOYS SENT TO REFORM SCHOOL
full_text: 8 BOYS SENT TO REFORM SCHOOL Given hearings on charges rang- ing from disorderly conduct to burg- lary, eight boys were ordered sent to the Cheltenham Reformatory, following hearings before Judge Thomas Waxter in Juvenile court, last week. With seven charges of burglary placed against him by Northern District police, William Wills, 14, said by officers to be the leader of youthful gang, 2827 Simpson Street, was sentenced to the school for a period of two years. The other boys who were sent to the school for a varied number of viclations were: Paul Clayton, 17, Easton, Md.; William Wright, 12, 1302 Mulligan Street: Fred Logan, 15, of 419 Bethel Street; Bernard Palmer, 11, of 910 W. Franklin Street; Howard Ornder, 13 of 712 Sharp; Ivan Queen, 16, of 109 W. Fayette Street and Daniel Scott, 515 N. Calhoun Street. 0 COSTONIE PRAISES CHAIN STORES Kiwah Costonie had only words of praise for chain stores Friday in discussing some results of the picket movement. The A. and P. stores now have 37 colored clerks and the Ameri- can stores. 22 he said. | Reads Drug store also gave him a list of 75 colored employees, in- cluding 65 fulltime workers, Mr. Costonie told the AFRO. 0 TOMS RIVER By MISS NANCY WORTHY 10 Centre Street TOMS RIVER, N. J.-The Rev. George Crawley preached at the Second Baptist Church, Sunday night. Mrs. Mary Alexander left Thursday for her home in Philadelphia where she will spend the winter. The Ror. T. Tillman preahced at the Well's Chapel, Sunday night. Miss Bennie Lee Meadows, Forker Riv- fer, spent the week-end with her brother, Albert Meadows, of Centre Street. Mrs. Mack Worthy, who has been ill is recovering. The Daughter Elks gave their annual memorial program at the Well's Chapel Sunday night. Rev. I. Tilman preached the sermon. Annie Payne was mistress of ceremonies. There were solos by Miss Myrtle Pinkney and Mrs. Julia Young. Mrs. Mac Roe and Isaac rendered piano solos, also remarks b the daughter ruler, Mrs. Lizzie James. 0 White Woman in Death Plot Must Die FRANKLINGTON, La. (ANP) - Thirty-eight-year-old Mrs. Alice Mae Purvis, white, who entered in- to a plot with her houseboy to kill her husband, was found guilty here Saturday. Conviction carries ja penalty of death by hanging. i

title: BLAME LOW WAGES FOR CRIME BOOST: Y. SOCIAL WORKER TELLS CIVIC LEAGUE DEPRESSION HITS KIDS. CHELTENHAM 1S GIVEN RAP FARMING CONTRACTS ALMOST LIKE PEONAGE.
full_text: BLAME LOW WAGES FOR CRIME BOOST N. Y. Social Worker Tells Civic League Depression Hits Kids. CHELTENHAM IS GIVEN RAP Farming Contracts Al- most Like Peonage. BALTIMORE, Md.- That the increasing crime rate could not be ascribed to any one cause, but to several mutually inter- dependent causes, Mrs. Armamita W. Anthony, of the department of crime prevention of New York City, told members of the Co-Operative Women's Civic League, Saturday. Mrs. Anthony spoke from theli subject, "The Work of the Crime Prevention Bureau Among Negroes in Harlem," at the social service conference, held by the league at the Booker T. Washington Junior High School. Poverty Blamed Present day crime is characterized by poverty, low economic status, low wages, lack of work and overcrowd- ing of homes, which form a back ground for the large percentage of criminals she said. "Even in prosperous times colored mothers, more often than others, are employed outside of the home. All of us are familiar with the youngster wearing his door key about his neck. What then is to be expected but that children deprived of this parental supervision should get much of their training from the streets? "Although this lack of parental supervision due to poverty is preca- rious enough, it cannot be mcre se- rious than the poverty brought on by unemployment. "With welfare agencies taxed to their utmost to furnish the bare necessities, there is nothing left for recreations, or other things that children consider necessary to their afe. Need Recreation "Movies, for instance, have be- come almost as necessary to the av- erage child as food, clothes ad shelter. Children will steal in or- der to get what adults may consider pure luxuries. "Large families forced into over- crowded rooms and tenements are obviously a menace. Indiscriminate herding of both sexes lays the foun- dation for future immorality. "Then many children are being. forced to leave school and are thus inadequately prepared to find work. Even when they are prepared, they cannot, at all times, find work. Hence they seek excitement which often proves hazardous. A stolen car, a ride with a stranger, or a questionable dance may be the be- ginning of a criminal career. "Children in poor or crowded sec- tions often fail to get that sympa- thetic understanding necessary to formative years. "The work in crime prevention in Harlem has shown that children's behavior isn't always due to pure cussedness, but is a result of some profound maladjustment, physical or mental handicap or bad inheri- tance. Many times these can be prevented if observed at an early age, thus preventing criminal atti- tudes. "Thus, no single cause can be pointed to as being the factor in any criminal situation. With ten- tacles reaching far into the com- munity, correlated factors wind and rewind themselves into resulting misconduct." In explaining the work of her de- partment, Mrs. Anthony pointed out that the moment cases came under the observance of that department, they ceased to be ploice cases but became individual problems of mal- adjustment. To prevent embar- rassment, no uniformed officer is sent on the case. In many instan- ces, Mrs. Anthony said, children were most co-operative, and in some instances brought friends to the de- partment whom they thought in danger. Mrs. Sarah Collins Fernandis. president of the league, presided at the social conference session. The discussion following the ad- dress by Mrs. Anthony was opened by Edward S. Lewis, executive sec retary of the Urban League, who pointed out the lack of colored so- cial service workers under the muni- cipality of the city of Baltimore. Mr. Lewis urged the league to lend its support to the effort to secure workers, and colored policeman and policewomen. Boys Under Peonage In a report on the Cheltenham School for Boys, Mr. Lewis decried the lack of up-to-date trade train- ing equipment. The broom factory, he said, was tuberculosis breeding because of its lack of air cleaning facilities, and that many of the in- mates were in a state of literal peonage to white farmers. "We must have laboratory studies made by members of our own group for we have problems which no white person can ever reach," Mr. Lewis said. Mr. Lewis also pointed out that the type of supervision at Chelten- ham does not make for betterment of the inmates. The people in charge are not of the fine scientific type, he said, but the kind who de- pend upon the "big stick" for-dis- cipline. A talk on the work of the Child Study Group in the city was given by Clarence Roberts, principal of School No. 128. Mr. Roberts re- ported that Schools Nos. 110, 118, 102, 115, 125, 129, 101 and 113 had branches of the Child Study Group. "Realizing that our biggest need is for improved citizenship," Mr. Roberts said, "and for appreciation of the finer things in life, the group has set itself to work to counteract slip-shod training. The work of the group consists of studying the ac- tions of children to note whether these actions would lead to fuller lite or to juvenile court and de- linquency and to study the methods of coping with such actions." Two delightful vocal numbers, "What's In the Air Today?" and "The Birth of Dawn," were rendered by Miss Georgiana Lawrence, tralto. con- Miss Lawrence was accompanied by Mrs. Herndon White, pianist. abstract: That the increasing crime rate could not be ascribed to any one cause, but to several mutually interdependent causes, Mrs. Armamita. W.
clean_text: BLAME LOW WAGES FOR CRIME BOOST N. Y. Social Worker Tells Civic League Depression Hits Kids. CHELTENHAM IS GIVEN RAP Farming Contracts Al- most Like Peonage. BALTIMORE, Md.- That the increasing crime rate could not be ascribed to any one cause, but to several mutually inter- dependent causes, Mrs. Armamita W. Anthony, of the department of crime prevention of New York City, told members of the Co-Operative Women's Civic League, Saturday. Mrs. Anthony spoke from theli subject, "The Work of the Crime Prevention Bureau Among Negroes in Harlem," at the social service conference, held by the league at the Booker T. Washington Junior High School. Poverty Blamed Present day crime is characterized by poverty, low economic status, low wages, lack of work and overcrowd- ing of homes, which form a back ground for the large percentage of criminals she said. "Even in prosperous times colored mothers, more often than others, are employed outside of the home. All of us are familiar with the youngster wearing his door key about his neck. What then is to be expected but that children deprived of this parental supervision should get much of their training from the streets? "Although this lack of parental supervision due to poverty is preca- rious enough, it cannot be mcre se- rious than the poverty brought on by unemployment. "With welfare agencies taxed to their utmost to furnish the bare necessities, there is nothing left for recreations, or other things that children consider necessary to their afe. Need Recreation "Movies, for instance, have be- come almost as necessary to the av- erage child as food, clothes ad shelter. Children will steal in or- der to get what adults may consider pure luxuries. "Large families forced into over- crowded rooms and tenements are obviously a menace. Indiscriminate herding of both sexes lays the foun- dation for future immorality. "Then many children are being. forced to leave school and are thus inadequately prepared to find work. Even when they are prepared, they cannot, at all times, find work. Hence they seek excitement which often proves hazardous. A stolen car, a ride with a stranger, or a questionable dance may be the be- ginning of a criminal career. "Children in poor or crowded sec- tions often fail to get that sympa- thetic understanding necessary to formative years. "The work in crime prevention in Harlem has shown that children's behavior isn't always due to pure cussedness, but is a result of some profound maladjustment, physical or mental handicap or bad inheri- tance. Many times these can be prevented if observed at an early age, thus preventing criminal atti- tudes. "Thus, no single cause can be pointed to as being the factor in any criminal situation. With ten- tacles reaching far into the com- munity, correlated factors wind and rewind themselves into resulting misconduct." In explaining the work of her de- partment, Mrs. Anthony pointed out that the moment cases came under the observance of that department, they ceased to be ploice cases but became individual problems of mal- adjustment. To prevent embar- rassment, no uniformed officer is sent on the case. In many instan- ces, Mrs. Anthony said, children were most co-operative, and in some instances brought friends to the de- partment whom they thought in danger. Mrs. Sarah Collins Fernandis. president of the league, presided at the social conference session. The discussion following the ad- dress by Mrs. Anthony was opened by Edward S. Lewis, executive sec retary of the Urban League, who pointed out the lack of colored so- cial service workers under the muni- cipality of the city of Baltimore. Mr. Lewis urged the league to lend its support to the effort to secure workers, and colored policeman and policewomen. Boys Under Peonage In a report on the Cheltenham School for Boys, Mr. Lewis decried the lack of up-to-date trade train- ing equipment. The broom factory, he said, was tuberculosis breeding because of its lack of air cleaning facilities, and that many of the in- mates were in a state of literal peonage to white farmers. "We must have laboratory studies made by members of our own group for we have problems which no white person can ever reach," Mr. Lewis said. Mr. Lewis also pointed out that the type of supervision at Chelten- ham does not make for betterment of the inmates. The people in charge are not of the fine scientific type, he said, but the kind who de- pend upon the "big stick" for-dis- cipline. title: Arrest Guard in Shooting at Cheltenham: Aubrey Bronson of Baltimore in Freedmen's Hospital Seriously Wounded. BOYS MUST EYES DOWN AS WHITE WOMEN PASS Morale at Low Ebb; Boys Escaping Daily.
full_text: Arrest Guard in Shooting at Cheltenham Aubrey Bronson of Balti- more in Freedmen's Hos- pital Seriously Wounded. BOYS MUST EYES DOWN AS WHITE WOMEN PASS Morale at Low Ebb; Boys Escaping Daily. (Exclusively to the AFRO) CHELTENHAM, Md. Rigid si- lence on the part of officials at the boys' reformatory here causes mystery to surround the shooting of Aubrey Bronson, 19, by a white guard named John Hurley, Sat- urday. Barefooted inmates of the re- formatory would reveal no facts concerning the gun play, and visi- tors are permitted to go about the grounds with understanding that they will not question any of the boys. The state's attorney, Allen Bowie, claims to have made a per- sonal investigation of the matter, and has released the guard from arrest on a bond of $1,000. Mr. Bowie's Version Mr. Bowie's version of the shooting is: "A bunch of the boys were go- ing to be fed and he (Bronson) re- fused to get in line. "Later, Bronson went into a room where the guard (Hurley) was sitting and asked for a note of some kind about the matter. In a resulting argument, the guard attempted to strike him (Bron- son) with a cane. "Other boys started to the aid of Bronson and a guard named Grier came to separate Bronson |Continued on Next Page| Arrest Guard in Shooting at Cheltenham [Continued from Page One] and Hurley, who were struggling for possession of the stick. "In a following scuffle, a bullet was fired from Hurley's gun and Bronson was hit, but I don't know where." Further questioning of the attorney revealed that what had been called a cane was really a small sized club. Mr. Bowie, according to John B. Pyles, superintendent, has left instructions for the authorities of the reform school to keep their mouths shut about the matter. Conditions Getting Worse In the meanwhile, conditions are rapidly going down at the in- stitution, according to the super- intendent, and Saturday's shooting has not helped matters much. Mr. Pyles stated that the ap- pearance of AFRO news stories about the conditions of the insti- tution has helped to foster unrest. All inmates have been instruct- ed to keep their eyes on the ground when any of the women ascend steps on the grounds. These women are said to include the wife of Superintendent Pile. Miss Hattie M., his secretary, and a third who has charge of the din- ing room. Punishment of the boys who fail to keep their eyes lowered is set at fifty lashes on their bare backs. Supt. Pyles denied having any knowledge of whippings adminis- tered to the boys, who are inmates, and displayed anger when ques- tioned about a rule which pro- hibits boys from raising their eyes when women are going up the steps of the institution. "I am trying to make them well mannered," he said, "and I tell them to turn their backs when women up going up stairs because it is impolite to look." Rule Necessary When told that his idea of good manners was not practiced any- where, and persons coming up the steps behind a woman are not necessarily paying attention to her, Mr. Pyles admitted that such was the case but he said that the rule was necessary at Chelten- ham. He also stated that the visiting hours at the institution have been limited because of frequent es- capes. Mr. Pyles stated that four boys escaped last Sunday and have not been apprehended. Autoist Gives Ald One boy, according to Mr. Pyles, was picked up in front of the ad- ministration office by an autoist. The superintendent admitted that the guards and boys have been having frequent clashes of late, and he also declared that the trouble between Bronson and Hurley might have been brewing for a long time. Two Guards Hurt - Two guards were seriously in- jured by those boys no longer than the other day," said Mr. Pyles. "What was the nature of their injuries?" he was asked. "Yes, sir, they were some se- rious," he returned and started to change the subject. "Can you describe those injur- ies?" he was asked. "Well, one of the guards was cut about the face in a close-quar- ter fight." he stated slowly. He failed to find a way to de- scribe how the other guard was hurt. Version of Relatives The version of the shooting given by the relatives is: "Bronson was shot by a guard who was yelling at a group of boys and thought that the victim said something 'smart.' "The guard had attempted to strike Bronson with a club, but the youth succeeded in wresting the cudgel from his assailant. "Another guard seized the man in an attempt to stop him from hurting Bronson, but the latter drew a gun and shot over the shoulder of the would-be peace- maker. "No official notification was given of the shooting, although Bronson was sent to the hospital and it was known that he was suffering from a punctured lung." Office Hadn't Heard The Baltimore office of the House of Reformation, queried by the AFRO on Tuesday morning, had had no report of the shoot- ing and learned of it for the first time from the AFRO. Cheltenham is a private school to which the city and state send male delinquents under 21. Near- ly every Baltimore grand jury since 1918 has condemned the place as unfit and unprepared to give correctional training or make good citizens of the boys who are sent there. State Scored The state has also been scored for sending colored boys to a pri- vate school, conducted and oper- ated by white persons who have only monetary interest in the work, while white boys are sent to well-manned reform schools operated by the state. Cheltenham guards are said to be jittery since three boys escaped from the institution Sunday be- fore last, and four more ran away last Sunday. In one case, a visiting woman is said to have helped the boys flee by hiding them in her car. She was caught at the Hanover Street bridge, Baltimore, and forced to carry them back to the school. So many boys are escaping that Sunday one visitor was told that visiting hours will be restricted to the second Sunday in the month. Visitors Sunday were not per- mitted to walk about the grounds with the boys as usual. Privi- leges were curtailed and boys saw their visitors in the hallway near the superintendent's office. Wear White Coats Boys in white coats and white aprons were pointed out to visi- tors Sunday as special waiters at the tables of the white employees, which included three white wom- en. The boys are barefooted so that they will make no noise at meals.
