title: Bronson Says Guard Threatened to Pine Box Him
full_text: After Riot Smoke shop at 625 Erie Street, Niagara Falls, Y., which was shot up when mob resented Reds' defense of lad accused of assault- ing of a Polish girl. Bronson Says Guard Threatened to Pine Box Him WASHINGTON.--(AFRO Би- reau)-Aubrey Bronson, 21, of 220 N. Caroline Street, Balti- more, Md., is in Freedmen's Hos- pital, suffering from a bullet wound in his chest, said by him to have been inflicted by a guard at the House of Reformation, Cheltenham, Maryland, Bronson is a ward. where According to Bronson, one of the guards came on duty last Sat- urday, acting as if he were under the influence of liquor and start- ing yelling at the boys, declaring himself not to be feeling good. Shortly after the guard came on duty, Bronson said, it was time for supper, and all the boys [Continued on Next Page] Bronson Says Guard Threatened to Kill Him [Continued from Page Onel got in line-laughing and talking loud. Hurley Pointed Him Out Hurley, the accused guard, ac- cording to Bronson, pointed him out and informed him that unless he stopped laughing he would be going out in a pine box. Hurley also gave Bronson orders to sit at table number five, Bronson said. That table is designated for inmates who, for various offenses, are made to sit without eating. Pine-Box Threat "I don't like your looks." Bron- son said the guard gave as his reason for the order, whom he charged made a second threat to send him out in a "pine box." "You are not tough, just rough, and, like sandpaper, you can be smoothed out," Bronson quoted the guard as remarking. "Because he seemed to be drunk, I didn't take him serious- ly," Bronson said, "and I went on to my table and ate my meal. After supper, Guard Hurley came back to the boys' dining room to see whether I had eaten at table number five, and when he learned that I didn't, informed me that he would see me later." Convinced Bronson said that when he went to the dormitory, he was convinced that Hurley was drunk, and, being afraid that he might hurt him, he asked Hurley to give him a permit to see the superin- tendent, John Pyles. This re- quest was refused after asking Hurley twice, Bronson charged. Fired Over Guard "I then turned to go to the school room," Bronson said, and as I did so, Hurley raised a stick to hit me, and I caught it. At that time, Guard Greer stepped in between us and took the stick away from Hurley. Hurley then said, 'Let me at him,' and, reach- ing over Greer's shoulder. fired one shot into my chest," Bronson told the AFRO. After he was shot, Bronson said, he ran down stairs, refus- ing to come back at the request of the two guards. Later, he said, one of his fellow inmates carried him to the reformatory hospital. Brought to Freedmen's He was given emergency treatment there and then trans- ferred to Freedmen's Hospital in a private car, accompanied by three guards and the reformatory physician. At Freedmen's Hos- pital, Bronson was treated by Dr. J. N. Mason of the hospital stafi, No Operation Performed No operation has been per- formed yet to remove the buiiet, according to Eronson. Hospital authorities, when questioned about that, and the extent of the injuries of Bronson, refused to make any comment. Bronson still has the bullet in him. No guards are stationed around him. He posed for an exclusive AFRO photo. Bronson is serving his third sentence in the House of Refor mation, he told the AFRO. He was sent there in 1924 for stay- ing out late at night, for which he served three years. In 1923, he served a year for playing hookey, and in 1929, he was re- turned to the reformatory on a charge of disorderly conduct. Last February he ran away from the home, he said, and was captured and taken back last June. Bronson said he is 21 years of age, and is due to be released from the reformatory, but the of- ficials, he said, are ignoring his age and refusing to allow him to leave.

title: Why Ritchie Was Defeated
full_text: Reprinted from Late Editions of Last Week Why Ritchie Was Defeated Harry W. Nice, Republican, was elected governor of Maryland by some 5,543 votes over Governor Albert C. Ritchie, Democrat. Had 2,772 of those votes been cast for Mr. Ritchie instead of Mr. Nice, they would have given Mr. Ritchie a fifth term in the state house at Annapolis. Strange, isn't it, that Governor Ritchie, who had been elected to this same office in previous years by majorities of forty to sixty thousand should, in thi sfinal plunge, bite the dust by a mere 2,772 votes? Yet it isn't strange to experienced politicians and for that reason the AFRO, now that the election is over, takes a minute to tell why the 2,772 votes were missing. Here they are: In fifteen years the Democratic party, under Governor Ritchie's leadership, has steadily refused: 1. To appoint a single colored Democrat to a state political office. 2. To name colored policemen for Baltimore. 3. To repeal the jim crow car law. 4. To equalize the salaries of county school teachers. 5. To appoint colored staffs for Crownsville State Hospital for the Insane, or Henryton T. B. Sanitarium. 6. To aid Morgan College adequately or establish a state university. 7. To establish a state school for delinquent boys in- stead of the privately owned House of Reformation at Cheltenham. While Governor Ritchie vacationed in Europe, Mr. Nice visited every hole and cranny in the state making friends and soliciting support. While the Democratic state platform contains no single word of promise or encouragement to the colored voters, the Republican platform, at Mr. Nice's directions, pledges definite reforms in several of the items mentioned above. The Democratic party with a two-to-one registration over the Republicans, has proceeded for fifteen years un- der the assumption that it could win without its 15,000 registered colored voters in the state. So it can in many elections. But the time came Tuesday when it needed 2,772 votes and it could not put its hands on them. That is a lesson for the Democratic party which won every important post on the state ticket except the gover norship. That is a lesson for Herbert O'Conor, who is flying high and wide with a majority of 100,000 in his race for the post of attorney-general of Maryland. It's a lesson for Mayor Jackson of Baltimore, who faces a probable primary next May. Not a single one of the seven reforms mentioned above would have menaced the credit of the state, or alienated white supporters of the party. To the contrary they constitute progressive steps which have been taken in many states further South than Maryland. The election is over. To use another expression, the water is under the bridge. However, we repeat here, the Democratic party can win elections without the col what is today perfectly evident to everybody, namely, that ored Democrats, but there are occasions when 2,772 votes are needed. That is a lesson for Herbert O'Conor, who is flying high and wide with a majority of 100,000 in his race for the post of attorney-general of Maryland. It's a lesson for Mayor Jackson of Baltimore, who faces a probable primary next May. Not a single one of the seven reforms mentioned above would have menaced the credit of the state, or alienated white supporters of the party. To the contrary they constitute progressive steps which have been taken in many states further South than Maryland. The election is over. To use another expression, the water is under the bridge. However, we repeat here, the Democratic party can win elections without the col what is today perfectly evident to everybody, namely, that ored Democrats, but there are occasions when 2,772 votes are needed. . .

title: State May Pay Morgan College $100,000 Bill: Nice Favors Payment of Debt Contracted in 1890 for Academy. UNIVERSITY OF MD. BROKE AGREEMENT Two Physicians May Get Appointments.
full_text: Reprinted from late editions of last week State May Pay Morgan College $100,000 Bill Nice Favors Payment of Debt Contracted 1890 for Academy. - UNIVERSITY OF MD. in BROKE AGREEMENT Two Physicians May Get Appointments. Morgan College may collect a debt that has been owed to it since 1890 by the University of Mary- land and is said to now be approxi- mately $10 $100,000, when the next session of the legislature convenes in January, 1935, according to in- formation received from Harry W. Nice, governor-elect, this week. During an interview, Thursday, Mr. Nice stated that he is in favor of paying the money to Morgan at the rate of $25,000 a year. The bill was incurred when the University of Maryland sought to get Federal funds made available by the Morrell Act. The officials learned, however, that they could get no money unless they provided educational opportunities for col- ored citizens as well as white. Three alternatives were offered to the University of Maryland. These were: admittance of col- ored students to the university; founding of a state institution for colored students, or making an agreement with a college within the state boundaries under which colored persons would be edu- cated at state expense. The University of Maryland chose the last of these three, and the Princess Anne Academy at Princess Anne, Md., was turned over for the teaching of agricul- tural and mechanical courses. No Sale Made No actual sale was ever made, but the agreement was considered a gentleman's agreement. In 1919 another agreement was made under which the Univer- sity of Maryland is required to ask the state legislature to buy Prin- cess Anne Academy, whenever Morgan College requests that this be done. In the last three sessions of the legislature Morgan has been trying to get the University of Maryland to live up the the terms of its agreement, but has not been suc- cessful on any occasion. Other Changes Expected .. Other changes are expected to take place in civic circles by the time the legislature is in ses- sion. Among these changes will be the taking over of Cheltenham by the state. The board of this institution, which is devoted to the reform of delinquent colored boys, has strongly objected to state control. It is reported that continued op- position to state control of the in- stitution by the board will cause the legislature to withdraw a con- tribution given each year by the state. Further reports indicate that two colored physicians will be given posts in state institutions under the Republican guberna- torial administration. A shake-up in the personnel of several institutions that serve col- ored citizens is also predicted. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Evangelist Out on $2000 Bail on Girl Charge: Girl's Mother Tells Crowded Court He Abused Her 15-Year-Old Daughter. COURT ROOM FILLED WITH CHURCH MEMBERS ""Pray for Me,"" Evangelist Says, ""I'm Framed.""
full_text: Evangelist Out Fugitive and Why He Escaped on $2000 Bail on Girl Charge Girl's Mother Tells Crowd- ed Court He Abused Her 15-Year-Old Daughter. COURT ROOM FILLED WITH CHURCH MEMBERS "Pray for Me," Evangelist Says, "I'm Framed." Special to the AFRO-AMERICAN BOSTON Charged with hav- ing a love affair with a 15-year-old girl, who is expected to become a mother in November, the Rev. Wilbur W. Henry, 22-year-old pastor of the Holy Trinity Bap- tist Church, and a former mem- ber of the famed Henry Brothers' evangelist team, pleaded not guilty, when arraigned before Judge Timothy Ahearn in the Roxbury Court, Monday. His case was continued until the 19th. The former evangelist was released in $2,000 bail. According to the police, a war- rant was issued for the arrest of the handsome "boy-pastor" when a complaint was made by the mother of the allegedly abused girl. The abuse is said to have occurred on February 19. Followers Fill Court The large corridors of the Rox- bury court-house was filled with loyal members of the accused pas- tor's flock. Female and male parishioners surrounded the deb- onair Rev. Mr. Henry, expressed their confidence in him, and pre- dicted his acquittal. The youthful former evangelist, with tears rolling down his eyes, made the following statement for the AFRO: "I am a victim of a frame-up. This is the second attempt made by my enemies to ruin my suc- cessful, youthful ministerial ca- reer. It is all the result of care- ful plotting of my enemies, who are jealous of my success at the Holy Trinity Baptist Church." "Scarcely Knew Girl" Trembling with uncontrollable emotion, the "boy-pastor" declar- ed that he scarcely knew the girl, and only remembers her as a recent addition to his growing number of parishioners. In the tone of voice which made him famous as an evangelist, the Rev. Mr. Henry calmly added: "Sometime ago, an unidentified informant told me that my enemies were out to get my scalp, and shake me down. This at- tempted shake-down was to be made because it is the consensus of opinion that my family is wealthy, which is not true. But I will fight this case to the highest courts of the land." Prayers Requested Making a request to the "kindly AFRO." the former evangelist said he wanted, "his friends throughout the country to pray for him that he will be able to conquer the devil, who is making a diabolical fight to overthrow him and destroy his youthful ministerial career." "Pray for me," he requested, "that I might be able to stand firm on solid rock with Jesus Christ." The arrest of the Rev. Mr. Henry created a furore in church circles here. Several months ago he ran afoul of the law when he was arrested by Officer Parker A. Coleman of the Roxbury Cross- ing station on a drunkenness charge, but his case was subse- quently placed on file. According to the police, the youthful pastor put up a stiff bat- tle with Officer Coleman and po- lice clubs were brought into ac- tion to subdue him. He was char- acterized by the police as "fighting parson." the Escaped youth from Maryland House of Reformation at Cheltenham, who hailed motorists on Crane highway Tuesday and asked for a lift. He said he wanted to get to Annapolis, his home. The boy declared he was beaten last winter by a white guard and had been trying for several months to get away. He exhibited a broken foot and showed where his teeth had been knocked out as proof of his story. At right is photo of the schad, a three ply leather strap which is used to whip unruly boys at the institution. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: His Plight at Cheltenham Is Recalled by Inmate in '84.
full_text: His Plight at Cheltenham Is Re- called by Inmate in '84. To the Editor of the AFRO: I am in favor of abolishing Maryland's Cheitennam Reform- atory, established in 1872. Its motto was to reform the boys sent there and to make them good citizens. The institution made a bad start, and if conditions are as bad there now as they were in the beginning, it should be cut out. I was sent there from Baltimore on August 1, 1884, when I was 11, and stayed until May 1, 1889. I was sent for running away from home. The guards there used a stick or anything to beat the boys. The average boy sent there did not seem bad, so I feel that many were sent to Cheltenham because their parents did not want them. I wrote a story to the AFRO in 1896, telling about conditions of the place. CHARLES E. JACKSON. 239 Norfolk Street, Newark, N.J. Matthews, Miss Elliot Are Called Traitors to Ethiopia. To the Editor of the AFRO: The letter of Carrie Wilkins Elliot in the July 27 issue of your paper was evidently inspired by the propaganda of Ralph Mat- thews and white chiselers with their declaration, "Ethiopians will look down on colored Americans as their inferiors." She asks: "Does Ethiopia re- gard colored Americans as kin- dred?" Prince Malaku Bayen, nephew of the Ethiopian emperor, has just returned to his royal family with a colored American wife. A prince with a "looked-down- upon" wife? Traitors and Mr. Matthews, please explain. This society desires to warn traitors to Ethiopia that their children will spit on their tomb- stones and curse them for hav- ing aided in selling Africa down the gutter. AFRICAN UNDERCOVER DRAGON SOCIETY. Honor Due Miss Barbour. To the Editor of the AFRO: The 1935 Douglass High School year book is beautifully gotten up, is of historical value, and re- flects great credit on all respon- sibile for its production. Especially, do I want to direct attention to the extreme appro- priateness of its dedication. The community can scarcely realize its indebtedness to Miss Fannie! L. Barbour. At a time when the great mass- es of our people had but faint appreciation of education, and the white people, generally, were indifferent, her wonderful tri- umph, along with that of Joseph H. Lockerman, proving the ability of our people to qualify as teach- ers, paved the way for a com- plete revolution with respect to the educational interest of her race. (The Rev.) GEORGE F. BRAGG, JR. 827 N. Arlington Avenue, Baltimore. Instead of an Interracial Church. To the Editor of the AFRO: During the present series of discussions concerning the or- ganizing of interracial churches it is specifically important that we realize that to possess Christianity, it is not necessary for anyone to go to any church. The true church so far has never been founded, else the de- nominational issues would not arise. We need no more churches, but let those we have put Christianity into practice. What the masses must learn to do is to organize militant organi- zations which will force equality of decent wages for work done, and everything which pertains to the necessities of life, including hap- piness. God put everything here. Man must use his brains to obtain it we must use the ballot. T. E. SMITH. 513 Cherokee St., Bethlehem, Pa. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: WED 15 YEARS, ASKS DIVORCE
full_text: WED 15 YEARS, ASKS DIVORCE Charging that her husband, Wil- liam Henderson, 1136 Myrtle Ave- nue, was excessively cruel and abusive. Mrs. Mary A. Henderson filed suit for an absolute divorce in the circuit court, Tuesday. In her statement, filed through the law offices of Roy S. Bond, at- torney, Mrs. Henderson declared that she was married in Philadel- phía, May 10, 1910. and lived with her husband until May 1, 1925, when his treatment forced her to leave. Prior to, and since the separation, her husband failed to contribute towards her support, the wife charged. William A. Johnson filed a suit against his wife, Mrs. Lorraine B. Johnson, in the circuit court Tues- day. The couple were married on March 15, 1923, and separated on February 1, 1925. Clarke Frazier, attorney, represents the husband. 0 TWO ASK COURT FOR FREEDOM Married at Fayetteville, N.C., on March 30, 1910, and deserted De- cember 27, 1923, was the statement of William H. Monroe in his suit for an absolute divorce, filed in the circuit court against Mrs. Geneva Monroe, of New York, Wednes- day. | In his testimony, Monroe de- clared that his wife deserted their home while he was at work, taking all the furniture. Roy S. Bond, at- torney, represented the husband. Clarence Contee, 1135 Harlem Avenue, was granted an absolute divorce in the circuit court against Mrs. Rachael Contee, of Washing- ton. The couple were married on August 14, 1914, and separted on November 30, 1921. Roy S. Bond represented the husband. -0 NINE BOYS SENT TO CHELTENHAM The largest group convicted in the Juvenile Court and committed to an institution were the nine boys who were sent to Cheltenham Reform School, this week. The boys were arraigned before Judge Thomas Waxter. They were cited on misdemeanors as second offenders. The length of con- finement ranged from one to three years. The boys who were committed were: Robert Smith, 14, 1032 Gillis Court; Charles Morgan, 13, 1500 N. Dallas; Robert Ward, 13, 2211 Hunter; William Thompson, 13, 1606 W. Lexington; James Harvey, 15. 1805 Eagle; Frederick Thomas, 2219 Hunter; John Thomas, 12, 2413 Etting: Felbert Cook, 12, 2435 Francis; Eli Loboard, 14, 856 W. Lexington. Demanded to See Mayor to Get a Job, Drunk Jailed George Summers, 42, 1437 E. Monument Street, was fined $5 and costs when arraigned in the Central police station on charges of creating a disturb- ance in the City Hall when he insisted on seeing Mayor How- ard W. Jackson while intoxi- cated, and ignored the requests of attaches to keep quiet. He was yelling that he wanted a job. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: THE WHIRLING HUB
full_text: THE WHIRLING HUB By ELLIOTT FREEMAN (O. Phillip Snowden substitutes this week for Elliott Freeman, now on vacation.) It was about this time last year that I substituted for Elliott Freeman. If I remember correctly, I began the column by quoting these beautiful lines from Keats's "Ode to Autumn." As autumn is fast approaching, it would not be inappropriate, it seems to me, to begin in similar wise: Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness, Close bosom-friend of the maturing sun; Conspiring with him how to load and bless With fruit the vines that round the thatch-eves run; To bend with apples the moss'd cottage-trees, And fill all fruit with ripeness to the core; To swell and guard, and plump the hazel shells With a sweet kernel ***** These fair strains of poesy are as true today as yesterday, and will be perennially so. Like the sages, of old, let us reflect for a moment upon this thought and recall that though every- thing seems in a state of flux, though governments may change, though the harmony of world peace is threatened at every turn, and though fortunes are lost over-night, that there are, after all, certain abiding and permanent values which we are apt in un- settled times to overlook. ***** WHAT PEOPLE OUGHT TO KNOW William Dean, prominent on the faculty at Atlanta University. has returned to complete work on his doctor's degree at Harvard University, which he hopes to receive in February. Two of Boston's most prominent sons, Moreland Fort, former stellar Boston University athlete, and Marion Fort, Tech graduate and brilliant chemist, will trek altar-ward before Father Time ushers in September. Erskine G. Roberts, former M.I.T. graduate, who has been serving in the capacity of superintendent of mechanical mainten- ance at Tuskegee Institute, for the past two years, has been appoint- ed to the faculty of the engineering school of Howard University. Elwood McKinney, Harvard University sophomore, having received a grade of "A" in all his courses the past year, is head- ing Phi Beta Kappa-ward. Maybe McClane will have company. In answer to the question of many, as to who is the most outstanding Bostonian at Howard University, Stephen Davis and Marion Martin are successfully supporting Boston on its cultural pedestal. Betty McCree, having attained a scholarly record at Roxbury Memorial High School, intends to enter Fisk on a scholarship awarded by the university. Herbert Wilkins, student instructor for the past three years at Lincoln University, in physics, will continue his education at the graduate school of M.I.T., in September. Edith Brown, teacher at the Roxbury Memorial High School, was delegate from Epsilon chapter at the recent convention of the Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, held in California. W. Thomas Fuller, graduate of Colby College, will enter the insurance business in Atlanta, Ga., this fall. May he be suc- cessful. Edward Dixon, for years connected with the General Electric Company in Lynn as draftsman, is the first non-white to receive an appointment in one of Boston's boys' high school. He will take up his duties at the Boston Trade School. ***** MEMOIRS OF A FADING SUMMER Buster Hopkins, known to the little girls as Uncle Bus, during his brief stay in Boston, "lined" all femmes from nine to ninety at his beck and call. Many a wet handkerchief did he leave, but only with the promise that he'd be back. Ted (Stepin Fetchit) London spoiled a perfect summer romance by falling into the arms of Morpheus in the presence of the lovely Kay Taylor of Philly. Since that fatal evening, Kay has for- bidden Ted to cross her threshold and won't even offer an antidote for a broken heart. What attractive young college junior had her evening spoiled by having her pater come to tell her it was her bed-time, at the most popular beer garden on the Hill? Say what, Eleanor? Juliet Octavia Lane has come and gone, but her melody lingers on. The little Sugar Hill lasses have just recuperated from trying to follow in the steps of the veteran-in the beer garden. While the cat's away the mice will play. While Kenny Mac rides the waves, little Jeanne is very busy-but not worrying whether or not the boat will sink. If you don't believe me, ask the physicist. When is this trio going to be broken up into a duo? The only Jitter Bugs party this summer, with Orange. Blossoms flowing freely was terminated at midnight, because the guests arose too far from the ground. What is this thing called love? How it does make us do things! Frank (D.C.) Reeves journeyed over five hundred miles to spend only one day with his college sweetheart, Marie (Howard U.) Stephenson. I wonder if Milton Reddick knows Dot Howard will take the vows the first of September? Can Frank Pierce have been the reason for Bobbe Webster's staying in New York all summer? This is quite a relief on the postmaster. The flying parson, Charles W.. Gabrouel, who always seems to break in the news, claims that he has found a new love. She might be only a foot taller than he, but he says that makes little difference when there are step-ladders handy. If appearance means anything, the romance of Johnny Parnell and Kay Craft is the real thing. Doris West, Cappie Ferguson's grammar-school sweetheart, has asked every Bostonian visitor at Camp Atwater about his whereabouts. Why don't you see the girl, Cappie? Olive Freeman and Louise Scott deserted their college boy- friends for two youngsters from Virginia. Was it because the kids had a pocket full of nickels? Feets Kennell, "the perfect host," has the peculiar charac- teristic of inviting friends to his home to a party and not showing up himself. Anna Reba Cox, M.Ed., Boston University, 1935, left Boston, a single woman. On her return, brought a husband with her. See what can happen in two months? The female visitors who were the cynosure of all male eyes this summer were: Vascar and Elaine Barnett, Lynchburg, Va.; Maxie Ferebee, Norfolk, Va.: Nellie Butcher, D.C.; Julia Wormley, N.Y.C.; Lucille Harris. Pittsburgh, Pa.; Bea Walker, D.C.; Marian Pettiford, Yonkers, N.Y.; Juliet Lane, Baltimore, and Ruth Perry of South Carolina. They all were feted royally at parties, dinners and so forth, by hospitable Bostonians. ***** The most attractive visitor, Vascar Barnett; the most enter- taining visitor, Juliet Lane; the most seen, Buster Hopkins; the quietest, Elaine Barnett; the best bridge player, Maxine Ferebee; the "night-clubiest," Julia Wormley; the most tennis-minded, Ham Johns; the cutest, Ruth Perry: the beau brummel, James Coles; the most inquisitive, Marion Pettiford; the hardest to please, Nellie Butcher LOOKING FORWARD ***** The New England Open Tennis Tournament, on Labor Day week-end, is expected to be the successful finale of a ne'er-to-be- forgotten summer social season in good old Boston. The tourna- ment will be held at the Harvard University courts. The "on-to-school" party will be a greater social success than ever, this year, because of the greater influx and reflux of stu- dents in Boston. As usual, the affair will be given by the Three Q's-Alpha, Beta Gamma. Boston Welcoming Committee, composed of our most promi- nent young bachelors, are anxiusly awaiting the arrival of the beautiful femmes who are coming here to look into our schools this fall. They have made a resolution between themselves that none of the committee shall trespass on one another's territory. They believe in the old adage, "First come, first served." EXTRA! EXTRA! Kenneth Smith, better known as the Weasel (?), is expected in Boston soon, with a car-load of so-called beautiful women from Washington and points South. "There isn't a biffer in the car-load," says the Weasel. IT MAY BE SO, BUT- ***** where old man winter will find Satan's disciples: Herbert ! Tucker will enter Northeastern Law School. Edward (Feets) Ken- nell, Jesse Williams and Betty McCree will make the long trip to Fisk. Gene Green will take the Massachusetts Pharmaceutical Board. Frances Watkins will enter Howard University. Kenneth McClane will enter Boston University Medical School. Yvette Keelan will begin her freshman year at Radcliffe. Her- bert Cheltenham enters Northeastern Law School. Richard John- son, back to Boston University. Kenneth Smith makes another change this time, it's Howard. When Oberlin opens its doors, Willie Johnson will be there, bag and baggage, ready to resume his studies. John Parnell, out for football at Boston University. Marion Martin, Eleanor Trott, Rena Johnson, Marie Stephenson will take it back to Howard U. To my friends, the columnists, Malcolm Fulcher, Bill Small- wood, Elliott Freeman, Walter Winchell, Ted Yates, and the rest of you guys: when the last letter is struck on my typewriter for this column, I resolve never to ATTEMPT to put myself on the same level with you as far as writing is concerned. You have too far a lead on me. This is your racket and you can have it. To you laymen, let me say this: It wasn't easy for me to write this column as I thought it would be. My attempts at journalism compare with the plumber who tries to perform an operation for appendix. From now on, I'll be contented to read what is already written. O. P. clean_text: THE WHIRLING HUB By ELLIOTT FREEMAN (O. Phillip Snowden substitutes this week for Elliott Freeman, now on vacation.) It was about this time last year that I substituted for Elliott Freeman. If I remember correctly, I began the column by quoting these beautiful lines from Keats's "Ode to Autumn." As autumn is fast approaching, it would not be inappropriate, it seems to me, to begin in similar wise: Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness, Close bosom-friend of the maturing sun; Conspiring with him how to load and bless With fruit the vines that round the thatch-eves run; To bend with apples the moss'd cottage-trees, And fill all fruit with ripeness to the core; To swell and guard, and plump the hazel shells With a sweet kernel ***** These fair strains of poesy are as true today as yesterday, and will be perennially so. Like the sages, of old, let us reflect for a moment upon this thought and recall that though every- thing seems in a state of flux, though governments may change, though the harmony of world peace is threatened at every turn, and though fortunes are lost over-night, that there are, after all, certain abiding and permanent values which we are apt in un- settled times to overlook. ***** WHAT PEOPLE OUGHT TO KNOW William Dean, prominent on the faculty at Atlanta University. has returned to complete work on his doctor's degree at Harvard University, which he hopes to receive in February. Two of Boston's most prominent sons, Moreland Fort, former stellar Boston University athlete, and Marion Fort, Tech graduate and brilliant chemist, will trek altar-ward before Father Time ushers in September. Erskine G. Roberts, former M.I.T. graduate, who has been serving in the capacity of superintendent of mechanical mainten- ance at Tuskegee Institute, for the past two years, has been appoint- ed to the faculty of the engineering school of Howard University. Elwood McKinney, Harvard University sophomore, having received a grade of "A" in all his courses the past year, is head- ing Phi Beta Kappa-ward. Maybe McClane will have company. In answer to the question of many, as to who is the most outstanding Bostonian at Howard University, Stephen Davis and Marion Martin are successfully supporting Boston on its cultural pedestal. Betty McCree, having attained a scholarly record at Roxbury Memorial High School, intends to enter Fisk on a scholarship awarded by the university. Herbert Wilkins, student instructor for the past three years at Lincoln University, in physics, will continue his education at the graduate school of M.I.T., in September. Edith Brown, teacher at the Roxbury Memorial High School, was delegate from Epsilon chapter at the recent convention of the Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, held in California. W. Thomas Fuller, graduate of Colby College, will enter the insurance business in Atlanta, Ga., this fall. May he be suc- cessful. Edward Dixon, for years connected with the General Electric Company in Lynn as draftsman, is the first non-white to receive an appointment in one of Boston's boys' high school. title: They Don't Like This Life Much
full_text: They Don't Like This Life Much A few of the four hundred boys at the House of Reform- ation at Cheltenham, Md. One of their group was shot by a white guard last Saturday because he is alleged to have said "something smart." Their is much unrest among older inmates at the institution. Although officials forbid any questioning of the boys, some of them openly admitted to AFRO reporters that they "didn't like down here much." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: IS WITNESS AT BEER HEARING
full_text: IS WITNESS AT BEER HEARING With the Rev. Ernest Lyon, pas- tor of Ames M.E. Church, acting as the star witness, Clarence Webb, 32, 1235 W. Lafayette Avenue was held for violating the new beer law, Sunday. Webb was arrested by Sergeant Edward Hitzelberger at 1:25 a.m. after the minister had complained to the Northwestern district about the noise in the rear of his home. Officers testified that when the house at 828 Woodyear Street which is operated by Webb, was raided, the man was seen selling beer to number of persons. a Webb, who is well known in the northwestern section, was held on charges of selling intoxicating bev- erages without obtaining a license. The six persons who were arrest- ed in the place and later dismissed were: Clarence Webb, 1235 Lafay- ette: James Luckey, 1619 Mount; John McBride, 647 Calhoun; Frank Hobbs, 1235 Lafayette; Norris Brown, 714 Stockton, and Emma Brown, 714 Stockton. SIX BOYS SENT TO CHELTENHAM Facing the accusations of police that they had committed misde- meanors, six boys were sent to the Cheltenham Reform School after trials in the juvenile court, this week. The boys who were accused and sent to the school were: Frank Johnson, 14, 1650 Woodyear Street; Raymond Cross, 12, 612 Fremont Avenue; Howard Thomas, 14, 1531 Woodyear Street; Sylvester Wil- liams 14. 1636 Bruce Street, and James Boone, 16, 1609 Mulliken Street, and Prince Edward, 17, Flor- ence, S.C. Woman Held by Cops As Shoplifter Charges of shoplifting were made against Mrs. Mildred Sessons 23, 1012 Madison Avenue when she was arraigned in the Western Police Court, Saturday. Mrs. Bertha Jellers, a female de- tective at the May Department store stated that she saw the woman take a pair of garters. When she was searched two dresses, two pairs of socks, two pair of hose, a can of Kleenall and the garters were found in a bag. Mrs. Sessons was held for the action of the grand jury. title: Cheltenham Is Not Fit for Humans, Ex-Inmate Says: George Clark Tells of Beatings and Vermin in Food. AUDIENCE REVOLTED BY MAN'S STORY Abolition of the School Is Sought.
full_text: Cheltenham Is Not Fit for Humans, Ex-Inmate Says - George Clark Tells of Beatings and Vermin in Food. : AUDIENCE REVOLTED BY MAN'S STORY Abolition of the School Is Sought. BALTIMORE-Gasps of amaze- ment rippled through Musicians' Hall, Saturday, as George Clark, 28, unfolded a story of alleged in- human treatment suffered by the inmates of Cheltenham. Clark, who spent the years from 1920 to 1930 in the institution, ex- hibited scars and pointed to bumps on his head which he claimed to have received from the white guards at Cheltenham. He ap- peared before a special meeting of the Abolish Cheltenham, Commit- tee, called by Mrs. Maud Bell. Aubrey Brunson, recently shot by one of the guards, was to have spoken, but did not appear. How- ever, those persons seeking infor- mation as to the reputed abuses which the young boys undergo were not disappointed: was incarcerated. "Cheltenham is a place not fit for a dog," declared the former inmate as he launched into his nar- ration. "A dog can get water when he wants it, but those boys can't get any water until the guard is good and ready. "People are told that the boys have school, but they don't. The men out there tell the people most anything." No School, Is Charge At this point, Clark told of his blasted hopes for an education, which he had hoped to receive at Cheltenham. "All I saw there was work," he said. Speaking of punishment, Clark told of the leather straps, four and one-half feet long, which he said are used to beat the boys. "When they are ready to whip you," he continued, "there are two men; one! has this long strap and the other has a stick or cane. Forced to Stoop "They make you kend over with your hands touching the floor. Then they hit you. If you don't bend over, the men will hit you all over the face, head and any- where. Just for talking or any lit- tle things, the guards send you to No. 5 table," the speaker continued. "You just look at the other boys eat. If you say anything to any- body, you go right back on No. 5 at the next meal. "If you keep it up," Clark said further, "they'll beat you up and throw you in a cell for thirty-five or forty days, on bread and water." The speaker told of a beating he claims to have received in 1926. "When the guard struck me with a stick," he said, "I threw up my hand to defend myself. I was then knocked down, struck in the head, kicked in the ribs-onc being brok- en. I stayed in the hospital for six months." Clark denied statements of the superintendent that the youths are taken to the office when they are whipped. He claimed that when- ever a guard takes a notion to beat up one of the boys, it is done. Vermin in Food A revolting story of the food served the youths was related. "Bugs, dirt and vermin can be found in the food," Clark stated. "The boys in the hospital get the same food as the guards, but they have to be nearly dead before be- ing admitted," Clark charged. The former inmate also told of a tobacco-chewing white cook, who allowed the saliva to drip from his mouth into the soup or other food he happened to be preparing. Ob- jection to the man's filthiness or any other things would only bring about a severe lashing, Clark told those assembled. Editor Pays Visit How officials at Cheltenham in- timidate the boys to prevent them giving out information to visitors was also related by Clark. The speaker recalled visits of the grand juries and how the reputed injus- tices were kept hidden from them and other visitors. He also told of a visit made by Carl Murphy, edi- tor of the AFRO-AMERICAN, 1925. in The speaker told of fear of talk- ing with Mr. Murphy, but stated that he promised to write, which he did, sending six letters. Front- page stories of the charges were carried in the AFRO-AMERICAN. He related how the officials tried to find the person who gave out the information. Clark said that one of his fellow inmates knew that he had written the letters, and that he warned him not to tell about it. The speaker said that he knew if the officials learned that he gave out the information they would just about kill him and that he made up his mind to kill the boy should he tell. Bernard Ades, Miss E. Briscoe, and Michael Gold, all white, of the International Labor Defense, were Mr. Ades suggested as a legal also speakers. remedy that: Interested persons have any of the inmates of Cheltenham brought before a judge in a writ of habeas corpus. He stated that proof of the exist- ing conditions could then be pre- sented. Mr. Ades said that should one judge deny the writ, then the matter could be carried further un- til a sympathetic judge could be found. He visualized that in case of suc- cess of having one or two persons liberated by this method, changes would be made. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Most Projects of State Executive Pass Legislature: Made Friends in Order to Break Down Democratic Opposition, He Says. 5 SENATORS TRIED TO GUM UP WORKS Administration Lists 19 Major Achievements.
full_text: Reprinted from Late Editions of Last Week Most Projects of State Executive Pass Legislature - Made Friends in Order to Break Down Democrat- ic Opposition, He Says. 5 SENATORS TRIED TO GUM UP WORKS Administration Lists 19 Major Achievements. ANNAPOLIS, Md. (Special) - Taking into account that he had a legislature with only 24 Republi- cans and 94 Democrats in the House and 11 Republicans and 17 Democrats in the Senate, Governor Harry W. Nice felt that he had done very well this week, as the legislature adjourned. Twenty-four Republicans in the House voted selidly for his 17 ma- jor bills, he said. But in the Senate five members did everything to defeat the Re- publican program. It was not done maliciously, he said, but it was done. The employment agency bill, carrying an appro- priation of $40,000 which would have been supplemented by a Fed- eral grant, he said, was defeated by Senator Phoebus (Rep. Somer- set County). It would have meant four posi- tions in every county and 25 in Baltimore City. Auto Tax Reduced Despite the $4,000,000 deficit left by ex-Governor Albert C. Ritchie, the governor said he had been able to keep the tax rate at the same level and to reduce the automobile tax from $8.30 to $5,20. The Democratic legislature kill- ed the proposition that would make $2 tags possible. Chauffeurs will not be required to renew their driving licenses every year jas formerly. The governor listed among his 19 objectives obtained the follow- ing: 1. Increased appropriation of $20.000 for Morgan College. 2. Ten thousand dollars to take college and professional courses in or outside the state by persons who are denied attendance at the Uni- versity of Maryland. 3. Six thousand dollars for a commission to study means of making higher education available to all people. Coroner Appointed 4. Appointment of Dr. George S. Allen as coroner in Baltimore, and appointment of several jus- tices of the peace. In the case of Mrs. Lula Powell, white, who was objected to as a justice of the peace, the governor said, he asked that her name be withdrawn and he will give her a recess point- ment this week. The governor also stated: 5. "The bill to equalize teachers' salaries was lost, but I hope for at least a five per cent increase for colored as well as white teachers from the 'Cushion Fund.'" Only 5 Rejected 8. "Of 3,000 appointments only five were rejected." 7. "The bill to take over the House of Reformation for Colored Boys at Cheltenham as a state in- stitution went through. Senator Melvin Fine (Rep., 4th Dist.) certainly worked hard on it." 8. "I got a sum for the repair of the executive mansion." 9. "A sum to make a survey of state institutions; 10. "A law establishing pensions for the aged; 11. "A three-man conservation board; 12. "A fireman accident com- mission. 13. "An engineering department in the State Roads Commission." Other accomplishments were: 14. Recodification of military laws of the state. $100,000 to Morgan 15. A law authorizing the state to purchase Princess Anne Acad- emy from Morgan College at a cost of $100,000, to be paid in annual installments of $25,000. 16. Twenty-four bills recodi- fying the banking laws. 17. "A yellow dog law" for or- ganized labor. 18. Separation of state police from auto commissioner's office. 19. Law establishing the right to appeal de novo and to give ad- ditional testimony in awards by accident commission. Made Friends Asked whether he had expect- ted to get this far with a so-called hostile legislature, Governor Nice said he certainly had, that he had not found it difficult to make friends in the legislature, and through these friends to smooth out difficulties. The governor said that at the end of this term he did not ex- pect to run again. When it was suggested that he was now tired out by day and night conferences ture and might change his mind during the 90 days of the legisla- after he was rested, Governor Nice said: "My mind is made up now. I Jam not going to run again." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: No operation Yet on Cheltenham Boy Shot Last Week
full_text: No Operation Yet on Cheltenham Boy Shot Last Week WASHINGTON-Aubrey Brun- son, 21-year-old Baltimore boy, who was shot by a guard at the House of Reformation, in Chel- tenham, Md., last week, is still in Freedmen's Hospital recovering from the bullet wound in the up- per abdomen. According to Brunson's state- ment to an AFRO reporter this week, no operation has been per- formed yet to remove the bullet. Attending physicians would not give out any information con- cerning the treatment that had been given. Brunson, appearing to be weak, said that he frequently suffered sharp pains in his abdomen and side. He is being attended by Dr. L. Walberg, of the hospital staff. Issue was taken with reports carried in last week's issue of the AFRO that all whipping was done in the superintendent's of- fice. Brunson declared that any of the guards administered cor- poral punishment whenever and wherever they wanted to, and that the superintendent was not necessarily around when the pun- ishment was being administered. Punishments such as bending over and touching the toes for seven hours a day for a week; solitary confinement; standing on one foot for long hours at a time, were described by Brunson as "only a few of the many hor- rible punishments we get." Since being in Freedmen's Hos- pital, Brunson has been visited by his mother and sister and brother. No officials from the re- formatory are known to have vis- ited him since he was brought here last week under heavy guard. 0 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Joe Louis Train s for Levinsky Bout Afro Clean Block Campaign on Its Way: BALTIMORE GETS CLEANED UP AS BUCKET BRIGADES START WORK DURING THE CLEAN BLOCK CAMPAIGN BY THE JUNIOR DEPART MENT OF THE AFRO-AMERICAN
full_text: Joe Louis Trains for Levinsky Bout▾▾ Afro Clean Block Campaign on Its Way BALTIMORE GETS CLEANED UP AS BUCKET BRIGADES START WORK DURING THE CLEAN BLOCK CAMPAIGN BY THE JUNIOR DEPART MENT OF THE AFRO-AMERICAN MAUDE WATERS 1602 Cal- houn Street, scrubs woodwork of her home during Clean Block Campaign. THE CELLAR WINDOWS-Get a bath too at the hands of Ethel Edwards, who lives at 1610 Calhoun Street. Her brother is doing the steps. This was one of the first blocks to sign up in the AFRO Clean Block Campaign. SWEEPS SIDEWALK - Dirt must go, says Bernice Waters, of the 1600 block of Calhoun Street. Hundreds of children agree with her. WHOLE BLOCK AT WORK-A scene in the 1600 block of Calhoun Street, last week as youngsters opened the Clean Block Campaign in Baltimore by making dirt fly. The junior department of the AFRO-AMERICAN expects to organize clubs in every block in the city during the campaign. AFRO VISITORS FROM ILL. The Rev. Frederick Douglas, pastor of Cosmopolitan Church, Baltimore, and his guests. Left to right, Miss Etta Gleghorn, Mrs. Bertha Branford, Mrs. Pressic Lince, Mrs. Seabelle Hudson and Emmerson Claghorn. ALL MAIL CARRIERS ARE NOT MALE-Miss Helen B. Gib- son, whose four-year contract expired July 1st. She served R.F.D. route between Marietta and Cutler, Ohio, and holds the distinction of being the only woman in rural mail service in the United States. GETTING HIS DYNAMITE CHARGE READY AGAIN-The imposing figure of Joe Louis, the Detroit Bomber, who started training in Chicago, last week, for his bout with King Levinsky, white, at Comiskey Park, next month. Joe's handlers expect him to explode again, making more ring history and taking a step nearer to the heavyweight title. VERY COOL-Miss Helen Torrence, Sparrows Point, Md., fresh from a dip at Edgewater Bathing Beach. BOSTONIAN ENGAGED-Miss Lola M. Wilson. Her fiance is Cornelius Crosson. Announcement came from the mother, Mrs. Matilda Wilson. ABOLISH Cheltenham Cheltenham ABOLISH ABOLISH Cheltenhan BULLYRAGGED BY COPS-Members of the Abolish Cheltenham Council who marched in front of the Baltimore headquarters, last week. title: State Department Locates Missing Minister in Berlin
full_text: State Department Locates Missing Minister in Berlin WASHINGTON. - (ANP) -As- surance as to the safety of the Rev. Herbert M. Smith, dean of.. the school of religion of Bishop College, Marshall, Texas, who was reported kidnaped in Europe. recently, was received by Secre- tary of State Cordell Hull from the American consul in Berlin, last week, and relayed to rela- tives of the minister. Attaches of the American em- bassy at Berlin reported that they had been directly informed by the Rev. Mr. Smith that he had at- tended the Baptist World Alliance meeting in Berlin and the Passion Play at Ober-Ammergau, Ger- man. Russian Trip Planned It was further reported that the Rev. Mr. Smith planned to leave soon for Russia, where he intends to make a study of Rus- sia's attitude toward Christianity and the colored race. The Rev. Mr. Smith is the son of Charles C. Smith, of Scranton, Pa., and a brother of Miss Ruth. Gwendolyn Smith, teacher at Southern University, Scotland- ville, La., and Mrs. Andrew S. Fdwards of Kingston, Pa. Accused Bishop Sims Still in South Africa- CAPETOWN, South Africa.- (Special to ANP) -Bishop D. H. Sims, of the African Methodist Episcopal Church, told the adult school at the Unitarian Church here recently that "Education is progressive participation in com- plete living. The A.M.E. prelate, who heads the African division of his church by authority vested in him by the main body in America, is at pres- ent the cynosure of all eyes be- cause of the publicity turned on him following accusations of de- sertion and grave charges of im- moriality, filed against him by his wife with the A.M.E. tribunal in the United States, recently. Indications are that the bishop will not obey the summons of the A.M.E. board in the U.S. to re- turn to face the charges against him. It was pointed out that the American churchman has won quite a large following in South Africa since he has been here. Where Guard Used Gun on Boy Main building at the House of Reformation at Chelten- ham, Md., where Aubrey Bronson, inmate of Institution, was shot last Saturday because, witnesses say, he said "something smart" to a white guard. Cheltenham is a private school for boys with all white staff, to which State incorrigibles are sent. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: Brutal Beating Laid to Guards at Cheltenham: Inmates Say Steel Knuckles Were among Weapons Used by Three Guards. SUPT. SAYS IT'S OK FOR GUARDS TO HIT INMATES Disclosure Follows Recent Shooting of Boy.
full_text: Brutal Beating Laid to Guards at Cheltenham Inmates Say Steel Knuckles Were among Weapons Used by Three Guards. SUPT. SAYS IT'S OK FOR GUARDS TO HIT INMATES Disclosure Follows Recent Shooting of Boy. CHELTENHAM, Md. Reports that youthful inmates of the Cheltenham Reformatory were beaten frequently and unmerci- fully by white guards at the in- stitution were denied Tuesday by Superintendent John Pyles, white, following an investigation by representatives of the AFRO. Only last week, news was pub- lished of the serious shooting of Aubrey Brunson, Baltimorei youth, by one of the white guards at the institution, precipitated, according to reports, by an argu- ment over the boy's disobedience of a minor order. The investigation was prompted by written accounts of an alleged beating of Louis Waters of Balti- more, said to have been admin- istered recently by three white guards, in a basement room at the institution after the youth was reported to have disobeyed the orders of one of the guards. Eye-Witnesses Give Account Eye-witnesses, when interview- ed by an AFRO reporter, said that the Waters boy was locked in a cell for punishment, instead of being taken to the superintend- ent's office, after he retaliated when a guard, under whose di- rection he was working in a to- bacco barn, struck the youth. Inmates, in their description of the incident, declared that a schada (whipping implement made with several plies of leather), steel knuckles, a taped stick and fists were used to beat the youth un- til his head was a "mass of blood.' Blinded, Led from Cell They stated that Waters was unable to see, when finally re- leased from the cell, and had to be led to the office before being taken to a hospital. When shown a diagram, drawn by one of the inmates as his ver- sion of the affair, more than a dozen other inmates declared that it was a perfect description of the manner in which the boys are handled. They also stated that the in- struments of punishment are used freely by the guards. Use of Weapons Denied Superintendent Pyles declared that no brass knuckles nor black- jack weapons were used at the institution. as he carefully looked at diagram. He showed reporters the schad and said it was the only thing used in punishing the boys, and it was always used in his presence in the office. He also stated that the law for unruly boys is that they be brought to his office by guards for investigation. If he finds that they deserve punishment, he de- clared, it is administered there. Ο.Κ. to Hit Inmates When reminded that if those were orders, guards apparently were assuming additional respon- sibility when they struck boys, he said it was all right for them to do so when they saw fit. He failed to explain why Waters was carried to the basement before being brought to his office. The superintendent's version of the Waters affair was as follows: Waters disobeyed a guard or did not pile the tobacco in the manner the guard wanted. Angered, the guard slapped Waters and Waters picked up an stick to defend himself. Two guards then took him to the cottage, where he attempted to jump out a window. A guard headed him off from the outside and two of them pulled him back through the window. Waters, according to Superin- tendent Pyles, scratched one of the guards, and in return the guard, who had Waters's head clutched in his arm, used his fist to free Waters's hold. Mr. Pyles said that all the blood came from Waters's nose. Boys Still Look Down A white woman came up the steps behind reporters and, know- ing the rule that boys must look down, the reporters watched her. As she reached the top step, where a boy was sitting with his head down, she inquired: "Are you asleep?" Another Boy Escapes Returning to Baltimore Tues- day, the reporters were stopped along the road by a frantic youth who said he had been beaten up last winter by a guard. He ex- hibited a broken foot and indi- cated where several teeth had been lost, allegedly as a result of his beating. He said he had been bounced against the concrete floor and had been seeking a chance to get away for months. When shown the diagram of the Waters incident, which included the name of one of the guards, the escaping youth said: "That man has got them knuckles in the back of his car." He added: "They drink whiskey, too." The youth revealed that he had been sent to Cheltenham seven years ago and was sup- posed to stay until he was 21. He is only 16 now. He said that he was sent there because he would not stay at home, but would stay now rather than return to the re- formatory. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Nice Renews Pledges Made in Platform
full_text: Reprinted from Late Editions of Last Week Nice Renews Pledges Made in Platform The pledge that he plans to car- ry out all phases of the platform of the Republican was renewed Wednesday by Harry W. Nice, gov- ernor-elect of Maryland, in a state- ment to the AFRO-AMERICAN. Mr. Nice refused to make a com- ment that would refer to any race or individual, but he stated that his chief concern is to devise a program of relief that will prevent suffering in the state. If Mr. Nice carries out the plat- form, as he has promised, how- ever, it will automatically mean that Morgan College will be taken over by the state and made into a university. The reform school for boys at Cheltenham, Md., will also be in- vestigated and manned with a colored staff, according to pre-elec- tion promises of the Republican party. Voting Act to Go An act known as the Declara- tion of Intentions Act, which pre- vents many persons from exercis- ing their right to vote, and which the Republican party officials say hits colored voters hardest, will possibly be repealed. The phase of the platform re- lating to this is: "We advocate the complete re- peal of the Declaration of Inten- tions Act and repeal of the Affi- davit of Removal Act. "These pernicious and un-Ameri- can laws have prevented thousands of citizens from voting which im- pose restrictions on the free exer- cise of suffrage. "They not only hamper and re- strict, but in thousands of cases absolutely prevent voting by per- sons otherwise entitled." Minority Group Recognized Mr. Nice expects to have mi- nority representation on all boards and commissions which he shall appoint during his administration. At one time Mr. Nice publicly declared that he was against the appointment of colored police, be- cause he did not want to do any- thing that would arouse racial an- tagonism in Maryland. It was rumored that this state- ment was an effort on the then candidate's party to curry favor with the Eastern Shore voters. Shoreman Wants Police Harry Phoebus, white, senator from Somerset County, on the Eastern Shore, said that Shoremen are not opposed to the appoint- ment of colored officers. Mr. Phoebus, who was present with a number of other persons from Princess Anne and adjacent sections, stated that he thinks it is right and proper that colored en- forcers of the law should be. ap- Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: Morgan Receives $45,O00 State Aid in Budget
full_text: Reprinted from Late Editions of Last Week Morgan Receives $45,000 State Aid in Budget (By Staff Correspondent) Excitement ANNAPOLIS. burned at a fever heat in this city, Wednesday, following the re- lease of the governor's budget of $60,000,000 that includes an allot- ment of $45,000 for Morgan Col- lege. _ This amount marks an increase of $21,600 in the fund provided for the college, but is $30,000 short of the $75,000 requested. Persons close to the governor assert that the shortage can be explained by agreements that are to be made with Lincoln Univer- sity in Pennsylvania and Howard University in Washington, where- by the tuition of Maryland stu- dents will be paid until a state uni- versity can be set up. $59,000 for Henryton It has also been indicated by the administration that the sum of $59,000 requested for new build- ings at Henryton, for the housing and care of tuberculosis victims, will be taken care of in the pro- posed bond issue of $855,000. The proposed budget includes an appropriation of $20,000 for Cheltenham, but activity is now under way to make this institution entirely under state supervision which will mean a necessary in- crease in the appropriation. On Wednesday a special com- mittee from the Cheltenham board visited the governor here, and its members were told that the insti- tutino must be taken over by the state. Members Dubious The committee presented itself following intimations by some of the board members that the state might not be willing to assume control of the institution-as a number of organizations and civic groups have demanded. The Maryland School for the Blind, which requested an appro- priation of $13,000, was granted its wish, and this amount repre- sents no increase over that given last year. No increase was given the N. M. Carroll Home of the M.E. Church, which receives its customary al- lotment of $5,000. The sum of $500 was allotted to the Shelter for the Aged and In- firm, which failed to ask for and did not receive an increase in its appropriation. A.M.E.'s Get $1500 Another static figure in the bud- get is the sum of $1500 for the A.M.E. Home for the Aged, which did not ask for an increase over the amount that it received last year. St. Elizabeth's Home sought an increase of $500 over its appro priation of $12,000 received last year which was granted. Other institutions failing to ask for or receive increases in their appropriations are St. Mary's Home for Boys, $1250; St. Cath- erine's Home for Girls, $1250; and the House of Good Shepherd, $4,000. Princess Anne Cut Princess Anne Academy and Bowie State Normal School were given upsets. The former asked that its allowance be increased from $15,672 to $17,672, but was given a decreased amount of $15,- 513. The latter sought $44,385 instead of its last year's sum of $38,069, but was given $37,411. Crownsville, seeking $245,000, receives $123,950, but will be re- duced to $115,700 in 1937. The Good Shepherd Hospital, a new institution on the list, will re- ceive $5,000. The Henry Watson Children's Aid Society, seeking $15,000 in- stead of its allotment of $8,000, re- ceived $9,000, while the St. Peter Claver Industrial School, request- ing a $500 increase in its allotment of $3,000. had its wish granted. Asylum Gets Increase St. Vincent's Infant Asylum, seeking a $500 increase in its al- lotment of $14,500, was given that amount, which brings its total to $15,000. Provident Hospital, which faced a huge deficit because of the failure of state funds to meet the amount of money expended on state pa- tients, was refused a request for $16,000. The amount alloted, which was $14,000, is an increase of $2,000 over previous allotments. n Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: New Md. Governor Sworn in New York Gets Colored Marshal ""Fire"" Mother Faints at Funeral
full_text: New Md. Governor Sworn in New York Gets Colored Marshal▲ “Fire” Mother Faints at Funeral WBAL MD. GOVERNOR'S INAUGURAL "When you think I am right, commend me. When you think I am mis- taken, tell me. Unemployment relief must be provided. People must not be allowed to starve or go unclothed. I urge you to have faith in me." title: Judging Religions.
full_text: Judging Religions. To the Editor of the AFRO: If our religions were to be judged by each other. there would be no religions right: if we judged our own, there would be no religions wrong. We differ, but the divine object brings the human family into one accord, God is Supreme. God is all. (Mrs.) GERTRUDE WILLIAMS 228 Fourth Street, Lackawanna, N.Y. Replace Cheltenham. To the Editor of the AFRO: After reading of the treatment afforded our boys at Cheltenham. I have been wondering whether it would be a good plan for us to get together and do something our- selves to alleviate the situation. Why can't we get some of our lawyers, doctors and business men to purchase a building and take care of these boys? Our Governor is a good, just man, and I am sure he will help. Baltimore, Md. Likes De Priest; AAA. To the Editor of the AFRO: Μ. We need a man like Oscar De- Priest; he is a good man and I would like to meet him. I think that he will do good work for us. I am also sorry that the AAA was declared unconstitutional and hope that some day soon something will be found to replace it. The farmer will be lost without it. GEORGE W. WHITAKER Box 351 Tarboro, N.C. Students in Theological Seminary Dorms To the Editor of the AFRO: In a recent report of the Middle Atlantic Seminary Conference car- ried in the AFRO this statement was made: "Students from General Theological Seminary would take no part in the discussion, although colored students there are advised not to live in the dormitories." I, and three other colored stu- dents, live at the seminary. When I applied for admittance there was no mention made of my staying anywhere else. In fact, when I asked about commuting from my home I was advised against it be- cause the distractions involved would be harmful to my life here as a student preparing for priest- hood. I might also say that my life here has been most satisfying and inspiringly pleasant. We all have the feeling that we are preparing for the same work in the Catholic Church, embracing people of many races and countries, which causes the petty feeling and prejudices. that you imply, to sink into the background. CHARLES E. HARRISON. 175 Ninth Ave.. New York City

title: No Menacing Reds.
full_text: No Menacing Reds. To the Editor of the AFRO: Fruit has been borne from the seeds sown by your editorial of some months ago, entitled "No Menacing Reds," which said in part: "In fact, there is more real Christianity among white Com- munists than in the white YMCA, the white Christian Endeavor So- cieties or the white so-called Christian churches." Since this editorial was pub- lished, the Communists have been instrumental in organizing both colored and white share- croppers into a union which now has a membership of ten thous- and and which has helped in bet- tering working conditions and wages. The case of Angelo Herndon, the Scottsboro case, defense of Ethiopia, etc., are just a few of the many undertakings. S. CARMAN 1204 E. Baltimore Street Baltimore, Md. 0 Red Cross Snubs Ethiopia. To the Editor of the AFRO: The International Red Cross has refused to give Ethiopia its ser- vices, and will leave the wound- ed of Abyssinia to rot and die in the jungles of Africa. Non-combatant, neutral, medi- cal, surgical and nursing units are recognized as necessary forces of mercy for soldiers wounded and sick in war zones and are accepted civilized forces. DR. JOSEPH T. THOMAS. 2406 E. 40th Street, Cleveland, Ohio. F. D.'s Alarm Clock. To the Editor of the AFRO: In a letter to the President re- cently, I said: "It seems so inconsiderate for those seventy erstwhile, and pos- sible future, enemies of our land and people to come in here on a visit and then try to make a con- trast of the German-Jewish situ- ation with that of the races in our America. "Just why, too, are Mr. Hitler's seventy hired henchmen-doctors traveling by numbers instead of names? Are you asleep?" MOSES ST. MATTHEW ASHLEY Long Island City N.Y. School to Replace Cheltenham Is Urged. To the Editor of the AFRO: Since white officials have re- fused to allow the State of Mary- land to take over control of the Cheltenham Reformatory, I sug- gest that religious, civic, and fraternal organizations unite in the erection of an institution for delinquent boys. This school could be organized with the proper backing of our leading citizens throughout the State. I have an ideal site, 209 acres, adjoining Crownsville State Hospital. I am willing to have a school and cottages erected here, so that misguided boys may have proper spiritual, educational, and indus- trial training to prepare them for good citizenship. MAUD EDGELL. Waterbury, Md. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Man Is Freed in Torch Murder
full_text: Reprinted from Late Editions of last week) Girl, 6, Charges Man Tried to Assault Her BALTIMORE- Police of the Northeastern dis- trict are searching for the uniden- tified man who attempted to rape a six-year-old girl, Thursday, in the 1400 block of E. Fayette Street. The child, daughter of a tailor, was accompanying the man to his home to get a suit that was to be pressed by her father., Reprinted from Late Editions of last week Oliver Quits Job at Cheltenham BALTIMORE- Claiming dissatisfaction with conditions at the Cheltenham School of Reformation, Basil B. Oliver, physical educational di- rector, resigned from the teaching staff of the school, Wednesday. Oliver, a native of Burkesville, Va., and a former football star at Virginia State University, stated that he planned to accept the post of educational director at the Din- widdie, Va., CCC Camp. The former All-CIAA tackle was appointed to Cheltenham on January 15, 1935. Other than stating that he was dissatisfied with conditions, Mr. Oliver de- clined to elaborate on his resig- nation. He graduated from Va. State in 1933. Reprinted from Late Edition of Last Week Revoke Licenses of Six Motorists BALTIMORE- Six motorists, including Moses E. Jackson of 1110 Laurens Street, who is under indictment for fa- tally injuring a white boy, had their operator's licenses revoked by the commissioner of motor ve- hicles, this week. Jackson, records show, was in- dicted by the grand jury last week on charges of manslaughter. He is said by police to have been a hit- Reprinted from Late Editions of last week and-run motorist and to have re- fused to take the injured white boy to a hospital. Man Is Freed in Torch Murder BALTIMORE- Charged with first degree mur- der in the torch death of his al- lege sweetheart, Miss Ethel Rich- ardson, George Scott was found not guilty by Judge Duke Bond in the criminal court, Wednesday. Officers in their testimony said that the man poured a gallon of gasoline on the young woman, with whom he lived at 1423 May Street, on January 19. Defense ob- jections to the woman's alleged death-bed statement which were sustained, aided in Scott's being freed. The other five persons whose permits were revoked are: James Watkins of 811 N. Carey Street; Frank W. Mills of Hebron, Md.; Charles H. Smith of Delmar, Md.; Delmar. Md. George Banks of 1155 N. Carey Street and Edward Edwards of 411 N. Carrollton Avenue. title: Nice Names Five to Reform School Boards
full_text: Reprinted from Late Editions of Last Week Nice Names Five to Reform School Boards Appointments of five persons to the boards of two Maryland cor- rectional Institutions were an- nounced this week by Governor Harry W. Nice. Joseph C. Briscoe, principal of the vocational school, and the Rev. Frederick Douglas, pastor of Cosmopolitan Commur.ity Church, are named to the board of man- agement of the Cheltenham Re- formatory for boys, the other eight members being white. On the board of the Maryland Training School for Girls at Glenburnie, Governor Nice ap- pointed Miss Adeline Green and Mrs. Howard E. Young, both of Baltimore, and Charles Oliver of Annapolis. The positions carry no salary, according to Marse Callaway, Fourth District Republican leader. The appointments of the mem- bers of the Governor's Commis- sion on Higher Education were also confirmed this week. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Governor's Stand Spells Doom to Boys' Reformatory
full_text: Governor's Stand Spells Doom to Boys' Reformatory BALTIMORE take it over." - "I purpose to Thus spoke Gov. Harry W. Nice, Monday, in reiteration of his pre- election promises in regard to al- leviating unsatisfactory conditions at the Cheltenham Reformatory, against which repeated complaints have been registered by numer- ous organizations and individuals. The governor's declaration was made following the announcement that a special committee of the retiring grand jury had recom- mended that the institution be taken over by the state. Hits Farming-Out Systen. "I said in my campaign that I believed the institution should be taken over by the state," he de- clared, "and I purpose to take it over. The boys there are wards of the state, and I do not believe the wards of the state should be farmed out as is done in this case." Nice said he was not prepared to discuss the report of the grand jury committee, but expressed fa- miliarity with ger.eral conditions at the reformatory and mentioned a survey, now in his possession but not yet made public, made through the Board of State Aids and Charities. Gov. Nice called attention, in his statement, to the fact that Cheltenham is "one of the last institutions of its kind." Although a number of institutions for chil- dren were originally private, the State of Maryland has taken over practically all of them except Cheltenham; the Melvale School for Girls having gone under state control in 1931. Want Pyle Replaced The grand jury report, submit- ted to the judges of the criminal court on Friday, complained of overcrowding, inadequate medical facilities, lack of recreational de- velopment and the probable influ- ence of older vicious boys on the younger inmates. It was recommended that Su- perintendent Pyle, white, be re- tired and that he be replaced by a trained executive. Although the report failed to discuss the treat- ment of inmates at the reforma- tory, against which so many com- plaints have been lodged, much emphasis was placed on health conditions. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Goldsboro S. Griffith.
full_text: Goldsboro S. Griffith. To the Editor of the AFRO: No white man in Maryland for many decades after the Civil War labored more earnestly and faithfully for the colored race's betterment than did Golds- borough S. Griffith, whose slogan was: "It is a luxury to do good." Mr. Griffith was born in Havre de Grace, Md., in 1814. It was through his efforts that the Industrial Home for Colored Girls, and the House of Refor- mation for Colored Boys were established. The Rev. George H. Nock, in his, "The Story of a Great Life." says: "Mr. Griffith's sentiment and feeling could not be more terse- ly expressed than in the follow- ing words quoted from one of his reports: "The man who curses an- other because he is black, curses the God that made his so and challenges the infinitude of his wisdom. "The man who says, I hate the Negro', and calls himself a Christian, should read 1 John 4:20: 'If a man say I love God, and hateth his brother, he is a liar, for he that loveth not his broth- er, whom he hath seen, how can he love God whom he hath not seen.' "Brethren, the time has come when we as the strong- er race are called upon, not only by the demands of our religion, but by the exigen- cies that exist, to pray that the Spirit of God tear away from our hearts every ves- tige of prejudice that hin- ders our deep and practical and helpful sympathy from entering into the needs of the colored people of this city and state. They ask our help they need it-they must have it." - Mr. Griffith was all that the above sentiment implies. The writer of this article enjoyed an intimate acquaintance and fel- lowship with him. Thirty-five years ago, upon the nomination of Mr. Griffith, this writer was elected a member of the board of managers of the House of Reformation, commonly known as "Cheltenham." (The Rev.) GEORGE F. BRAGG 827 N. Arlington Avenue. Baltimore, Md. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Search for Three Who Escaped from Cheltenham
full_text: Reprinted from Late Editions of Last Week: Search for Three Who Escaped from Cheltenham Three youths who escaped from the Cheltenham reformatory are among the eight persons hunted by police and detectives of the missing persons bureau, this week. The boys escaped from the school during the night and were not missed until several hours later. Officials of the school state that the boys have been missing since Tuesday. Leonard Collins, 18, is described as being 5 feet, 7 inches tall, 150 pounds, light brown skin. Arthur J. Shoeman, 18, is said to be 5 feet, 6 inches tall, 125 pounds and light brown skin. Clarence L. Howard is 14, 5 feet, 2 inches tall, 100 pounds and dark skin. Other persons reported missing are: Carl Weaver, 18, of 911 Arlington Ave- nue, 5 feet, 9 inches, 140 pounds, brown skin; wore grey overcoat, blue pants. black sweater. John Ricks, 33, of 649 Bankard Lane; 5 feet, 10 inches, 170 pounds, light brown Iskin. Howard Dillard, 27, of 1403 Madison Avenue; 5 feet. 10 inches, 180 pounds. light brown skin; wore brown coat, ov- eralls, and cap; has scar over left eye. John Smith. 38, 640 Vine Street; 6 feet, 3 inches. 170 pounds, dark skin, small mustache; wore blue overalls and shirt, brown cap. A letter from Miss Mamie White of 859 Hobson Street. Tallahassee, Fla.. re- quests that a search be made for Hugh McCoy White. who formerly lived on Druid Hill Avenue, and is employed at the Bethlehem Steel Company's plant. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

JUST ANOTHER DAY FOR THEM-With books in their belts, these kids whistle away their cares on first day of school in Balti- more. Left to right, David Rainey, William Pritchard and Robert Boyd. title: 3 H.U. Men Named for Cheltenham
full_text: In Money i JULIAN BLACK, one of Joe Louis's managers, who accompanied the Brown Bomber to Washington last week, where he refereed a boxing match at Griffith Stadium. Tuskegee Head at Williams It WILLIAMSTOWN, Mass. is a fallacy to expect the colored group to be at its best and to make its greatest contribution without equal educational and cultural op- portunities, Dr. Frederick D. Pat- terson, president of Tuskegee In- tsitute, asserted, Tuesday, before 1,000 persons at the Williamstown Institute of Human Relations, held at Williams College. "If the group's standards of liv- ing are the lowest, it is because as a group it is the most under- privileged and exploited," he add- ed. Another fallacy is the belief that the color of one's skin is a badge of racial inferiority. The development of the race in the past seventy years has exploded this theory." 3 H.U. Men Named for Cheltenham BALTIMORE-Chancellor Wil- liams has been named principal of the academic work at the re- form school at Cheltenham, Md., at $100 a month. Purcell Banks and James F. Harmon are the new teachers at $75 a month. All three are Howard graduates, recom- mended by the Prince Georges County supervisor of schools. John H. Beckwith, white, has been named supervisor of boys at $1500 a year. The Cheltenham board met Thursday. He Leads 10,000 Dr. A. W. Brazier, of New Or- leans, national grand sire of the Independent Or- der of Good Sa- maritans and Daughters of Sa- maria, compris- ing 10,000 mem- bers. The 88 year-old lodge closed its tenth biennial session in Washington, this week. Dr. A. W. Brazier Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner.
