title: Found Reefers in Tavern, School Boy Tells Court
full_text: Reprinted from Late Edition of Last Week Cop Sługging Charge to Be Investigated BALTIMORE- An order for an investigation into the slugging of Charles Tyler, 30, of 11 S. Regester Street, by Pa- trolman Louis H. Schultz of the Northeastern district on Sunday, was ordered by Inspector Thomas Moon- ey, Tuesday afternoon. The officer reported that he placed Tyler under arrest at 1530 Ashland Avenue, for a minor disorder. While en route to a patrol box, Tyler struck him in the face, knocking him to his knees, the officer stated. To protect himself, it was necessary to use his espantoon, the officer declared. Tyler was taken to the Johns Hopkins Hospital for treatment of lacera- tions of the scalp.. Found Reefers in Tavern, School Boy Tells Court BALTIMORE- Charged with the possession of narcotics when six reefers were found in his pockets, Edward Sampson, 17, Douglass High School student, of 700 700 Dolphin Street, was held for the grand jury, Friday. The youth, police say, was found in a tavern with the doped cigar- ettes in a small tin case. His ar- rest was made after a plainclothes officer saw him pass something to another youth, it was testified. Under Suspicion For several weeks, officers stated, the youth has been under suspicion as a member of a gang that peddled the reefers. Patrol- man John Brown testified that Sampson has been arrested previ- ously for a similar offense. The high school student testi- fied that he found the cigarettes while in a Pennsylvania Avenue tavern. He denied statements that he passed cigarettes to anoth- er youth. Reprinted from Late Edition of Last Week Spanish War Vet on Missing List BALTIMORE-Seventeen per- sons including a Spanish-Ameri- can War veteran and a youth who escaped from the National Train- ing School for Boys, were the ob- jects of searches conducted by of- ficers of the missing persons' bu- reau, this week. Mrs. Maria Preston of 1749 Eleventh Street, Northwest, Wash- ington, requested officers to search for her husband Firman Preston, 67, Spanish-American War vet- eran, who formerly lived at 1638 N. Gilmor Street. Escaped from School William Brown, 16, who escaped from the National Training School for Boys in Washington, is being sought. The youth is described as being 5 feet 7 inches, brown skin; wore khaki shirt and blue over- alls. On the request of Mrs. Lucy A. Hollis of Route 4, Canadayua, N.Y., police are hunting Mrs. Essie John- son, a relief client, who formerly lived at 1618 Carlos Terrace. Mrs. Lula Christian of 817 Thir- teenth Street, Philadelphia, re- quested that Mrs. Eva Pope Cobbs be sought. Reprinted from Late Edition of Last Week 5 TO CHELTENHAM Convicted on charges of mis- demeanors and felonies, five boys ranging from 14 to 16 years of age were committed to the Chelten- ham School of Reformation after hearings in the Juvenile Court, this week. The youths, police testified, were second offenders and each was on probation when arrested recently. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: 4 Cheltenham Inmates Freed for Holidays
full_text: Reprinted from late editions of last week 14 Cheltenham Inmates Freed for Holidays BALTIMORE- A Christmas present in the form of freedom was given to four in- mates of the Cheltenham Reforma- tory, Saturday, according to Mrs. Maud Bell, secretary of the Abolish Cheltenham Committeee, which is besieging city officials with a bar- rage of efforts meant to improve the alleged inhuman treatment of the youths confined in the institu- tion. The hopes of another boy to be free on Christmas Day were blasted when officials could not find his parents, Mrs. Bell said. He was returned to the institution. Those released were: John Thomas Brown, son of a Baptist minister; Otis Dickerson, Robert Nettles, and Fitzhugh Briscoe. The youth whose parents could not be found was Robert Wiggins. The father of Brown is the Rev. Joseph Brown, pastor of the Prince of Peace Baptist Church. He has two brothers and four sisters. The youth, who now claims he is sixteen years of age, was sent to the institution three years ago. title: The League Has Failed.
full_text: The League Has Failed. To the Editor of the AFRO: The League of Nations, pre- dominated by England and France, has proved to the world its utter failure as an arbitrative body in carrying out the program of its responsibility of safeguarding the rights of the weaker nations sub- scribing to its convenant. As a black man I see its failure when Italy is allowed to run riot in Ethiopia in defiance of justice and Christian Good-will, thus violating the sacredness of peace. J. B. HOWARD Charleston, S.C. -о- Advice to Ethiopia. To the Editor of the AFRO: I plead to the Ethiopians again. They are slowly but surely fall- ing. If they don't mend their ways and turn to God, great will be the fall. The Lord is asking that the 10th of April be set aside as a day of worship. It is bettter to chey God before it is too late. PROPHET MOSES. Route 1, Box 293, Pleasantville. N.J. -0 - Α.Μ.Ε. Contest To the Editor of the AFRO: L. L. Berry and the Rev. Carl F. Flipper have both been named to the post of secretary of the missionary department of the AME Church. In many respects this contest will eclipse in interest any other election that will take place. There is all ready a split among the bish- ops as to the laws governing gen- eral offices. This will doubtless come to a focus. JOHN HENRY ADAMS Waco, Texas. Flowers for Cheltenham Principal. To the Editor of the AFRO: Much has been said and written about the Cheltenham Reform School, but few people know of the monumental work being done there by Chancellor Williams, the young principal who took charge last September. Mr. Williams, a graduate of Howard University, has been attacking the various problems. in spite of all handicaps, with the dauntless courage of an experienced administrator. He reorganized the school system from top to bottom. He abolished the cottage system of instruction and classified the boys upon the basis of achievement and intelli- gence tests. He introduced the Dalton Laboratory Plan of instruc- tion and the Big Brothers' Hour. He is publishing this month the first edition of the Cheltenham Boys Messenger. Courses in Negro histo:y have been included in all upper grades. In this work, Mr. Williams is being assisted by three other Howard men: James E. Har- rison, Jr., Howard P. Banks, and L. S. Wilson. HAROLD_N. SLAUGHTER Washington, D.C.

title: Maryland Reform School Boys Rioted against Alleged Brutal Guards Until They Fled
full_text: Maryland Reform School Boys Rioted against Alleged Brutal Guards Until They Fled Here is Cheltenham din- ing hall, restored to order after the inmates rioted, hurling tin cups, tin plates and other utensils at guards who handcuffed a boy Fri- day night of last week. The guards fled with minor in- juries. One guard had his forehead split. Windows are used for dense growing ferns which shut out one- third of the light in this room. Ferns are more important than the children. A group of boys on the porch of the main building at Cheltenham, Md., Reform School in Prince Georges County, which is run by private interests. Despite snow, ice and freezing weather, these boys are clad only in cot- ton overalls and cotton underwear. Some are hired out to white farmers. Their wages go to the school and the boys are given 50 cents a week. Christmas, some of these boys, barefooted, were sent to the woods to cut logs. The institution has been condemned by every grand jury for 15 years. Welfare surveys recommended the in- stitution be taken over by the State, but the private board of directors refused the offer of Governor Nice to take it over, preferring to hold on to it because it receives over $100,000 appropriations from the city and State annually and provides political patronage. (See picture on front page.) Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Is New Mrs. Maxey with Cab's Band in Hollywood?: Cab Calloway's Drummer Faces Fireworks Mrs. L. Maxey Calls Hubby's Companion Imposter. PROCESS SERVER SPILLED BEANS Wanted Her to Pay 'Other Woman's' Bill.
full_text: Is New Mrs. Maxey with Cab's Band in Hollywood? Cab Calloway's Drummer Faces Fireworks Mrs. L. Maxey Calls Hubby's Companion Imposter. PROCESS SERVER SPILLED BEANS Wanted Her to Pay 'Other Woman's' Bill. By RALPH MATTHEWS NEW YORK CITY--There will be no hi-de-ho in the soul of dapper Leroy Maxey, drummer in Cab Calloway's band, when he returns to Harlem from Hollywood, if Mrs. Sadie Maxey, a beauti- cian and former entertainer, has her way. Mrs. Maxey, who says that she is indirectly responsible for the creation of the Callo- way orchestra, has filed claims for back alimony, which she says will run into big money. Her husband was ordered some- time ago to pay her $15 per week from his salary of $100 per week. Ever since the organization left the East more than three months ago, and long prior to this date, she says she has been neglected. She did not bother, she said, when her husband sent her a re- ceipt for nearly $600 which he had spent on his teeth. Posed as Wife The straw that broke the cam- el's back, however, was a story she read in a Western paper, an- nouncing the arrival of Mr. and Mrs. Maxey on the Coast, and she investigated and learned that an- (Continued on Next Page) Not a Georgia Prison Cage but the dark cell of the Cheltenham, Md., Reform School, where boys rioted, Friday, over harsh treatment of guards. Here are alleged ring leaders who have been placed, like criminals, in this dark hole for 30 days on bread and water. (Other pictures on inside page.) Cab Calloway's Drummer Faces Fireworks (Continued from Preceding Page) other popular Harlem entertainer was allegedly traveling with her husband, posing as Mrs. Maxey. Given Summons To make matters worse, she states, a process-server called at her home and attempted to serve her. with a summons in connection with unpaid furni- ture bills and household arti- cles purchased in the name of Mrs. Theresa Maxey, who was her rival and not herself. She had to produce witnesses to prove her own identity to avoid appearance in court. Helped Make Band Mrs. Maxey, who was herself an entertainer and the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. George Treadwell, one of the old-line families of Flushing, Long Island, met and was married to her husband in 1923. She met Maxey while playing with Lew Leslie's "Dixie to Broad- way" company, first starring ve- hicle of Florence Mills. Maxey was the member of an orchestra known as Robinson's Syncopators in St. Louis, Mo. Upon returning to New York, the then Miss Tread- well used her influence to have the orchestra placed in the Cotton Club at a salary in- crease of from $40 per week to $100 per week per man. The couple were then married. Went on Strike After some time in the club, the band went on strike for $150 per week, and it was then that Irvin Mills snatched the obscure Duke Ellington and his five-piece band from a smaller club, increased the organization and placed them in the Cotton Club. The St. Louis boys were then without their name of Cotton Club Orchestra, and assumed the name of the Missourians, the name which they carried until Cab Cal- loway was placed in control and finally the name, "Cab Calloway and His Missourians" became just Cab Calloway's Orchestra. In "Africana" Before this transition took place, however, Mrs. Maxey again re- sorted to all of her New York con- tacts to place the band again and had them spotted with "Africana," produced by Earl Dancer, with Ethel Waters as the star. This was in 1927. All of these things Mrs. Maxey did, she said, out of love for her husband, and ev- erything was rosy until her husband outgrew her and wrote on the back of their marriage license. "I do not want my wife, Sadie Tread- well Maxey, any more." $3,000 Drums Even after this, she still played the part of a friend, she said, and it was her father who went on the note when her husband purchased his $3,000 set of trap drums, which is said to be one of the finest rhythm sets in Harlem music cir- cles. When Mrs. Maxey learned that her husband had a traveling com- panion, she immediately burned the wires to California, demanding an explanation. Denies Report She received a wire saying: "Don't believe what you read in jig papers. They don't get anything right." Nevertheless, Mrs. Maxey and all Harlem are waiting the return of the prince of hi-de-ho and his crew, who are scheduled for a date at the 125th Street Apollo Theatre within a few weeks' time. 0-

title: NAACP Fires Opening Gun in Jim-Crow War: Protest to Retail Merchants Marks Local Campaign
full_text: NAACP Fires Opening Gun in Jim-Crow War Protest to Retail Merchants Marks Local Campaign BALTIMORE -The local NAACP fired the opening gun on Sunday in its cam- paign to end bans against colored patrons in certain Baltimore stores by calling the discrimination officially to the attention of the Re- tail Merchants' Association. The drive was launched at the association's mass meeting at ; Sharp Street ME Church, and will continue with increasing vigor, Mrs. Lillie M. Jackson, president of the NAACP unit, said. Governor Harry W. Nice was commended in a telegram sent him by the branch during the NAACP meeting. The governor was praised for his stand on the equalization of salaries for all teachers in Mary- land and also for his interest in Cheltenham School for Boys by seeking the resignation of the present board. He was further asked to make two justices of the peace, George W. McMechen and William I. Gosnell, active. Should Know Facts It was also brought to the gov- ernor's notice that the local liquor ☐☐ board was not protecting the col- ored citizens of Baltimore by per- mitting disorderly taverns to operate in respectable neighbor- hoods near churches, schools, the YMCA and other such centers. A telegram was also directed to Joel Hutzler, president of the Re- tail Merchants' Association of ☐ Baltimore protesting against the un-American policy of those Bal- timore department stores that re- fuse to serve colored people. - Speaks on Law More than 800 people attended the meeting presided over by Carl Murphy, president of the AFRO AMERICAN and heard Judge Rowland K. Adams of the Baltimore Supreme Bench de- clare that men in office who do not respect the law cause much trouble in the community. "When men lose respect for the law, then comes disorder and chaos," he said. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Boy Murders Girl Killer: Tragedy Takes Place in State Reform School.
full_text: Boy Murders Girl Killer Tragedy Takes Place in State Reform School. BALTIMORE. Another chapter in a tragic trail of juvenile delinquency and blighted youth was written Friday when Roland McClellan, himself a girl-killer, died at the Freedmen's Hospital with a fractured skull and concussion of the brain. When admitted to the hospital, it was said, attend- ants were told that he re- ceived the injuries when he fell down a flight of stairs at the Cheltenham school of reformation. Another Version Another version, which came to the AFRO-AMERICAN, was to the effect that young McClellan received his fatal injuries as the result of being hit on the head with a baseball bat by another in- mate, James Tynes. The boys quarrelled, it was said, and Tynes used the bat in the fight that resulted. Tynes is reported to have been committed to the institution last July. He was charged with the theft of an automobile and with running over and fatally wound- ing a girl. Killed His Sweetheart McClellan was committed to Cheltenham in May after he had been convicted of killing 15-year-old Lillie May Cherry on April 21 when she visited his home. Testimony at the trial was to the effect that he and Miss Cherry quarrelled over his atten- tions to a Miss Dorothy Wilson, a friend of the slain girl. Miss Cherry went to his home on her way to work at 7:00 a.m., wit- nesses said. He testified that Miss Cherry approached him with a knife and he grabbed a shot gun and fired. The killing at the time caused considerable interest because of the sordidness of life it revealed. Orphaned since 1929, the Cherry girl had lived a precarious life with relatives, it was said. Testi- mony by police brought out that before she was 15 years of age she was visiting the home of Mc- Clellan as his sweetheart. Relatives fought a court battle over the possession of her body and insurance benefits. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: A waiting Initiation, Lodge Member Dies
full_text: Initiation, Awaiting Lodge Member Dies Collapsing while attending & CHELTENHAM SENDS regular meeting of a local fra- ternal organization, James An- 12 TO GRAND JURY drew Lloyd, 26, of 519 Hoffman Street, was pronounced dead at Provident Hospital, Sunday. Following an examination of the body, Coroner Cornelius Hamm announced that death was due to a heart condition brought on by the heat. Rumors that Mr. Lloyd was a candidate for membership and was awaiting initiation when he died were emphatically denied by officials of the lodge. However, Mrs. Lula Wilson, with whom Lloyd lived, informed the AFRO that the man had left home that evening to be initiated. Mr. Lloyd is survived by a widow, Mrs. Lizzie Lloyd, who accompanied the body to North Carolina. He had been employed by a local anilk company, it was learned. title: CHELTENHAM TO GET BUILDING FOLLOWING $15,000 BLAZE
full_text: CHELTENHAM TO GET BUILDING FOLLOWING $15,000 BLAZE BALTIMORE According to the Rev. Frederick Douglas, a mem- ber of the executive board of the Cheltenham School for Boys, a new building will replace the institution's $15,000 hospital building which was destroyed by flames on Sunday. At the meeting of the committee early in January, plans and specifications will be taken under advise- ment. Plans are expected to be drawn up by State building engineers. Others on the executive committee are Joseph Branford, Howard Claggett, John McCormick, and Willard W. Allen. A defective flue is said to have caused the fire at the institution. Sigma Delegate RALPH H. JONES, president of the Nu Sigma chap- ter of the Phi Beta Sigma frater- nity, Philadelphia, who was se- lected as a delegate to the na- tional conclave to be held in De- troit during Christmas week. Juette Garth was appointed alternate. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Ofay Won't Die for 2 Murders
full_text: Ofay Won't Die for 2 Murders JACKSON, Miss.---The sentence of William Clark Mitchell, 54- year-old white man, scheduled to die on December 17 for the mur- der of Lewis Bryant, farmer, and his son, Coot Bryant, was com- muted to life imprisonment by Governor Hugh White last week. Had Mitchell gone to the gal- lows, he would have been the second white man in Mississippt to die for the murder of a col- ored man. Cheltenham Loses Hospital in Fire UPPER MARLBORO, Md. A $15,000 fire which volunteers of three towns battled in sub-freez. ing weather with ice-caked hose lines, destroyed all but the walls of the two-story hospital at the Cheltenham School for Boys, Monday. Four young patients at the in- stitution were rescued safely and removed to other buildings. The firemen were aided by youthful inmates in confining the blaze to the hospital. 72-YEAR-OLD MAN FACES ASSAULT CHARGE ASBURY PARK. N.J.---George Statford, 45, was in Fitkin Me- morial Hospital, this week with a bullet wound in his right arm while William Morrison, 72, who allegedly fired a double-barrel shotgun at him faced a charge of assault with intent to kill. Debutante to Wed MISS JULIA L. RICHARDSON, Philadelphia debutante, whose parents, Mr. and Mrs. Malley Richardson, announced plans for her marriage to Earle M. Brown on December 18, by the Rev. Sydnor Thomas of St. Barnabas Church. The ceremony will be followed by a reception at the bride's parents home. Following a short honeymoon, the newlyweds will be at home, 1320 N. Park Avenue after January 1. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Cheltenham's Staff to Be All Colored: Nice Indicates to Delegation Board Faces Change, Too
full_text: Cheltenham's Staff to Be All Colored Nice Indicates to Delegation Board Faces Change, Too BALTIMORE-A colored personnel and ultimate col- ored board of directors were indicated when a delegation of citizens led by Mrs. As- bury Smith, chairman of the Citizens' Committee for Col- ored Boys, called on Gover- nor Harry W. Nice to urge immediate action in adjust- ing affairs at the Chelten- ham School on Monday. So certain was the delegation that immediate board changes are inevitable, that it handed the governor a list of possible ap- pointments. Backs Up Executive In presenting the plea of the delegation, Mrs. Smith stated that in view of the governor's proven interest in action at Cheltenham, the committee had come only to indicate a desire to back him up in the courageous attitude he had taken. Calling attention to possi- ble political pressure on one side, the group wanted to as- sure him that he had the support of the community in his determination to wipe out the disgraceful conditions which existed for such a long perlod at the reform school. Adding to what Mrs. Smith said, Sidney Hollander, a mem- ber of the State Welfare Board, reviewed the long fight which those interested in the institution had made. Turning to Governor Nice, he said: "Until you were elected governor, we were not able to get to first base in the matter." Favor Colored Workers Both Mrs. Smith and Mr. Hol- lander advocated acquiring well- trained colored workers or, some white workers who understand what running such an institution means. When a survey was made some time ago, Mr. Hollander said, "Not one employee at Chelten- ham was qualified to do the joo he was expected to do there. "Now that the institution has been taken over by the State, the next job is to get a proper per- sonnel and the kind of board of management that will back up a trained staff and proper pro- gram." Wants All Colored Governor Nice told the dele- gation that he was thoroughly committed to a policy of a whol- ly colored board as well as whol- ly colored staff at the institution. 1 ( ( 1 ! title: Rev. Douglas Offered to Resign Post
full_text: Rev. Douglas Offered to Resign Post BALTIMORE The Rev. Frederick Douglas told the Cheltenham Board of Management and Gov. Harry W. Nice that he would gladly resign his post as chief parole officer, it was learned this week. In a statement immediately fol- lowing consideration of Commis- sioner Jones's report, the Rev. Mr. Douglas stated, that he ac- cepted the position because he was asked to do so, and in the spirit of being of service to the institution during its reorganiza- tion period. Didn't Seek Appointment He did not seek the appoint- ment, he said, and had no idea at the time that it was in conflict with any State law. The Metropolitan pastor, however, refuted the assertion that the parole office was without proper records. He pointed out that the work of the office had been highly praised and that the status of paroled boys had been great- ly improved. Many citizens discussing the Cheltenham situation, this week, were inclined to feel that the en- tire board shared the responsibil- ity out of which the appointment of its own members as employees developed. Old Guard in Control Those who have analyzed the situation declare that the control of the board is still in the hands of the old faction which had kept a strangle-hold on affairs there. The recently appointed mem- bers, it was said, have had pro- gressive ideas, but have been un- able to put them into execution. It is known that practically all of the new members put on some time before the State took it over, are favorable to an efficient col- ored staff. : It was the old influence on the board which maneuvered the leg- islative provision that would put all present employees under civil service and thereby secure them their jobs until their deaths or re- tirement. t 1 1 ( The members of the board are: Enoch Harlan, Howard C. Hill, John E. McCormack, B. R. Miller, Joseph H. Blandford, H. B. Clag- i gett, J. Amos Davis, William H. Gibbons, H. Hampton McGruder, Willard W. Allen, Joseph C. Bris- coe, the Rev Frederick Douglas, Dr. George McDonald and Dr. John O. Spencer. 1 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Hall, Echols Missing as Va. State Preps for Opener Against Bluefield
full_text: Hall, Echols Missing as Va. State Preps for Opener Against Bluefield PETERSBURG, Va. "Things are looking brighter," was all that big Harry Jefferson, coach at Vir- ginia State, said this week as he surveyed the nicest crop of fresh- men huskies that has been seen in the Trojan camp during his six years as head football mentor, during which he has produced two championship teams. Last year, State won six games, tied one, and lost none to win the CIAA Conference top honors. It was Bluefield who played State to a 0-0 tie. On October 7, State opens against an unchanged Blue- field team, which last year was manned by sophomores for the most part. Hall, Echols, Missing The grim spectres of gradua- tion, poor scholarship, and finan- cial difficulties have reared their ugly heads to cause havoc in the Trojan camp this season. First se- vere blow came with the an- nouncement that Jumping Joe Echols would not return to har- rass the opposition. Most recent set-back is the rumor not yet confirmed that Joe Hall, speed merchant from Philadelphia, would be among the missing. Of the 44 men reporting, an un- lucking 13 were veterans, while 31 were freshmen Although there undoubtedly will be a steady stream of men reporting through- out the week, it is doubtful that many of these will be veterans. Coach Jefferson has already pre- dicted that the Trojan machine of 1939 will be the greenest that State has put on the playing field in many a year. With few, if any, of the return- ing linemen being anything to write home about, there will be a mad scramble for regular line berths. Already the natural abili- ty of several of the newcomers has attracted the critical eyes of the coaching staff. Three freshmen look particular- ly good in the light drills-Charles Lee, Kenneth Whitlock and Ted McCain. Lee is a Charlottesville boy, who is looked upon as one of the best prospects of the new timber, while Whitlock, a Penn- sylvania boy, was a three-letter man in football, basketball and track while in high school. Both are better than average kickers. McCain, a Boston boy, has all that a good passer needs. To Stress Passes With Coach Jefferson planning to develop an overhead game to offset a shoddy line, passers and kickers will bask in the limelight as keymen this season. It will be Dicky Burr, diminutive quarter- back, who looks like All-Ameri-| can timber this year, who will play the stellar role of kicker par excellence. Dicky's toe will bear the major share of the burden if the Trojans kick their way to another CIAA championship. New Men Shine Other newcomers who looked good in the first drill of the sea- son were: John Jackson, who comes from Youngstown, Ohio, and will be sweating for a back- field berth; James Burton, who starred at end for Braddock High in Pennsylvania; Shirley Jordan, a Norfolk boy who comes well- recommended from Booker T. Washington High School; Coppin Cartwright, from Phindelphia's Cheltenham High School; and Earl Holmes, a Hampton boy, who has hopes of landing a backfield post, Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. "Things are looking brighter," was all that big Harry Jefferson, coach at Vir- ginia State, said this week as he surveyed the nicest crop of fresh- men huskies that has been seen in the Trojan camp during his six years as head football mentor, during which he has produced two championship teams. Last year, State won six games, tied one, and lost none to win the CIAA Conference top honors. It was Bluefield who played State to a 0-0 tie. On October 7, State opens against an unchanged Blue- field team, which last year was manned by sophomores for the most part. Hall, Echols, Missing The grim spectres of gradua- tion, poor scholarship, and finan- cial difficulties have reared their ugly heads to cause havoc in the Trojan camp this season. First se- vere blow came with the an- nouncement that Jumping Joe Echols would not return to har- rass the opposition. Most recent set-back is the rumor not yet confirmed that Joe Hall, speed merchant from Philadelphia, would be among the missing. Of the 44 men reporting, an un- lucking 13 were veterans, while 31 were freshmen Although there undoubtedly will be a steady stream of men reporting through- out the week, it is doubtful that many of these will be veterans. Coach Jefferson has already pre- dicted that the Trojan machine of 1939 will be the greenest that State has put on the playing field in many a year. With few, if any, of the return- ing linemen being anything to write home about, there will be a mad scramble for regular line berths. Already the natural abili- ty of several of the newcomers has attracted the critical eyes of the coaching staff. Three freshmen look particular- ly good in the light drills-Charles Lee, Kenneth Whitlock and Ted McCain. Lee is a Charlottesville boy, who is looked upon as one of the best prospects of the new timber, while Whitlock, a Penn- sylvania boy, was a three-letter man in football, basketball and track while in high school. Both are better than average kickers. McCain, a Boston boy, has all that a good passer needs. To Stress Passes With Coach Jefferson planning to develop an overhead game to offset a shoddy line, passers and kickers will bask in the limelight as keymen this season. It will be Dicky Burr, diminutive quarter- back, who looks like All-Ameri-| can timber this year, who will play the stellar role of kicker par excellence. Dicky's toe will bear the major share of the burden if the Trojans kick their way to another CIAA championship. New Men Shine Other newcomers who looked good in the first drill of the sea- son were: John Jackson, who comes from Youngstown, Ohio, and will be sweating for a back- field berth; James Burton, who starred at end for Braddock High in Pennsylvania; Shirley Jordan, a Norfolk boy who comes well- recommended from Booker T. Washington High School; Coppin Cartwright, from Phindelphia's Cheltenham High School; and Earl Holmes, a Hampton boy, who has hopes of landing a backfield post, . .

title: Illegally Held Youths Home for Christmas
full_text: Illegally Held Youths Home for Christmas BALTIMORE - Through legal procedures by which it was shown they were being held at Chelten- ham illegally, two youths were released in time to celebrate Christmas with their parents, last week. The boys were: John Johnson,. 12. of the 400 block Colvin Street, and Albert Robinson, 13. of 866 Vine Street. The legal papers were filed by John's mother, Mrs... Martha Johnson, through George W. Evans, attorney, and by Al-.. bert's father, George Robinson, through George L. Fornoff, white attorney. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. title: Cheltenham Boys Barefoot: Shiloh Baptist Church Members, on Visit to School, Find Boys Shoeless, Even on Sunday
full_text: Cheltenham Boys Barefoot Shiloh Baptist Church Members, on Visit to School, Find Boys Shoeless, Even on Sunday BALTIMORE saw two boys with patches on although I didn't see any of them barefoot. But, my son, who went with me, said that he saw some of them barefoot and when he asked one if he was cold, the boy said no." Insufficient clothing, rotten food, barefoot boys, with band- ages covering head wounds were described this week by Mrs. Mazee Stephens, 1433 Edmond- son Avenue, on her return from a visit to the Cheltenham Schoo! for Boys. a Mrs. Stephens reiterated statement by her husband, David II. Stephens, carried in Tues- day's AFRO, which said that the young boys at the school wear no underwear and appear cold and underfed. The Stephenses are members of Shilch Baptist Church's Tuesday evening Bible class which, to- gether with the Sunday school, visited the institution on Sunday to take ice cream and cake and hear the boys play in the band and, sing. Two bus loads made the trip. Mrs. Stephens said that the group was entertained by the school band and chorus and that 3, for the program they were all 1. dressed in blue suits and wore shoes, 'S g e t !, r 1 Greens, Nice, Fat Worms "But underneath," said Mrs. Stephens, "I could see it was all; a put on. Sunday was a cold day and as soon as the program was over the boys went back to: their barracks and put on old blue shirts and overalls and thick shoes. Some had no shoes. They said they weren't allowed to wear their suits except on Sun- day. "Some of the boys told us that they had had bread and milk for breakfast and for dinner had sprouts with green worms ard potatoes. For breakfast they say they have oatmeal but because it has so many bugs they call it buggy meal. "Two or three of the boys had bandages on their heads and told me they had been beaten with rubber hose and blackjacks. While we were there, the at- tendants stayed close and it was hard to talk to the boys. Salts Is the Cure-All "One boy told us that if any 1 one of them gets sick he is sent s to the infirmary and given salts. - If he doesn't improve he is told to 'go on back to work as you're not sick.'נ Mrs. Stephens said that she learned from one of the boys that many of them wear no I shoes and work in the fields barefoot. She said that she noticed them shivering from the cold. She also stated that she was sur- •prised at the number of young boys at the school. 1 7 t 11 1 B. B. Sunderland, super- intendent of the school, who grected them, according to Mrs. Stephens, always found something to do in his vest pockets when greeting a visitor and never extended his hand despite the fact that many extended theirs in greeting. So noticeable was this, according to her, that her 6-year-old grandchild noticed it. ! Leonard Coleman of 521 Ar- lington Avenue, a deacon in the church and president of the i Bible class, stated that he found conditions at the school "better: than I expected," but he also: I knew that it was a camouflage. He said that those who made the trip had heard so much about! the school that they wanted to 1 see for themselves. Have No Underwear 1 1 "Half of the boys appeared to be hetween 5 and 12 years old." ас-1 cording to Mr. Coleman. "All of them had on blue uniforms for the program but afterwards! went back to the barracks and changed to blue jears and shirts. They had on no underwear. I saw some with bandages on." 1 ( M. Ada Layton. another member, who conducts a store atif 315 Carreliton Avenue, said: 1 "Everyone who made the trip i came back with a sad heart. If Cracked Heads Mrs. Mary Hudnell of 1319 Mosher Street, whose husband, Howard, is also a deacon in the church, made similar statements regarding the conditions at the school. In addition she stated that she asked one of the boys why his head was bandaged and he answered "it is busted." Be- sides bandages on the heads, she stated she also saw some with bandages on their toes. On the other hand, George S. White of 1003 Lanvale Street. president of the Shiloh Church choir, stated that the boys ap- peared well-fed and clothed, and Mrs. Elizabeth Cole of 1220 Mosher Street, who also made the trip, said that everything at the institution was "just fine." She admitted, however, sezing "one boy with a patch on his head," which she said he told her he received "in a fight with an- other boy."  Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Tells of Beating at Cheltenham
full_text: Tells of Beating at Cheltenham "BALTIMORE-A 15-year-old this inmate, he is taken to the vupil who was released from the Cheltenham School for Boys, in October, told the AFRO recently that the reports brought back by members of Shiloh Baptist Church who visited there in No- vember were true. The young ex-inmate, now in the sixth grade at Elementary School 107, said that it is not unusual for Cheltenham boys to get beat with rubber hose at the slightest provocation, "We are made to stand on one foot and hold the other up, and one hand must touch the toes and the other hand is held raight out. If we don't keep this awkward position, we get beat more," he said. Says Food Is Inadequate Like the Shiloh Church vis- itors, the young school boy de- scribed the clothing as very thin and inadequate and the food as fit only for pigs. He stated that all food is boiled and that usually the in- mates get the scraps-the guards getting the best part if any. : hospital and given salts and if he refuses to return to work, he is compelled to do so, as guards say he is not seriously injured but "is trying to dodge work." He told of the cases of two boys. one of whom had pneu- monia and the other, appendicitis. The officials waited so long in each case that as soon as the in- mates were taken to hospitals they died. ! Another revelation on the part of the inmate was the fact that residents of Marlboro and Upper Marlboro are offered $5 a head. for any escaping inmates they catch and return to the school, Hidden from Parents He also stated that it is a common thing for inmates to get beat so badly that when their parents or visitors come and in- quire for them, officials say they have escaped, when in reality they are in solitary. They are not allowed to read articles which appear in the AFRO concerning the school and if their mail contains these ar- ticles or any money, it is usual- ly missing when it gets to them. If any candy is sent to them. As for the clothing, he said! they wear the same thin shirts, it is also retained in the office and pants in winter as in sum- and sold to them. If their con- mer and on Sunday they change duct is not up to par in the to grey uniforms. When shoes guards' opinion, according to this wear out, a requisition is re-inmate, the honors and grades quired to secure new ones, and achieved in their lessons are sometimes boys are compelled to taken away. go barefoot. Parents must often send additional protection. Hospital Criticized Grades, he said, begin at the tenth and climb up to the first. After that stage comes the first honor and then second honor, provided the boys don't get in trouble. If they do, they have It was also stated that the hos- pital procedure at the institution, is lax. If a boy gets a broken leg, for example, according to to begin all over again in the ! tenth grade, making it harder, of course, to get out of the school. title: AFRO Staff Artist Is Father of Son
full_text: AFRO Staff Artist Is Father of Son BALTIMORE Mr. and Mrs. Francis L. Yan- cey of 719 N. Carrollton Avenue became the parents of an 8-pound son, early Sunday morning. The baby, who is to be chris- tened Francis Edward Yancey, was born in Woburn, Mass.. where Mrs. Yancey had been vis- iting her aunt, Mrs. Susan Saun- ders. Mrs. Yancey, a member of the Smart Set Social Club, is expect- ed to return to Baltimore in May. The new father is staff artist for the AFRO. Writ Frees Boy, 17 from Cheltenham BALTIMORE On the contention that he was not given a proper trial when sentenced to Cheltenham School for Boys on July 7, 1937, Ed- ward E. Wesley, 17, of 1710 N. Mount Street was released from the institution at a writ of habeas corpus hearing in the Court of Common Pleas, Thurs- day. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

The baby, who is to be chris- tened Francis Edward Yancey, was born in Woburn, Mass.. where Mrs. Yancey had been vis- iting her aunt, Mrs. Susan Saun- ders. Mrs. Yancey, a member of the Smart Set Social Club, is expect- ed to return to Baltimore in May. The new father is staff artist for the AFRO. Writ Frees Boy, 17 from Cheltenham BALTIMORE On the contention that he was not given a proper trial when sentenced to Cheltenham School for Boys on July 7, 1937, Ed- ward E. Wesley, 17, of 1710 N. Mount Street was released from the institution at a writ of habeas corpus hearing in the Court of Common Pleas, Thurs- day. . .

title: Cheltenham Gets $80,000 Gym
full_text: Cheltenham Gets $80,000 Gym Here's a view of the new $80,000 gym- nasium at the Cheltenham School for Boys, which will be dedicated on Sun- day afternoon, October 20. This build- ing has a floor space 94 by 53 feet and is equipped with facilities for the popu- lar indoor games. It has showers, lava- tories and dressing rooms on the base- ment floor, which is not complete, pend- ing the granting of additional funds for a swimming pool. Here's an interior view of the new gym at the Chelten- ham School for Boys in Prince Georges County, Md. Note the balcony which, besides providing for a few spectators, also has a stand for the support of a small moving-picture camera. The gym floor is 94 by 53 feet and is suitable for basketball, boxing, volley ball, bad- minton and trapeze exercises. The basement will have showers, lavatories, dressing rooms, and a swimming pool. The State corrective school for boys at Cheltenham, Md., is a place where 450 "Dead End Kids" live under conditions that are many times worse than conditions portrayed in the movie of that name, according to Raymond Hobson, until re- cently an instructor there. Hobson says that he was dis- missed from his post the first of January, because: He spoke to Superintendent Benjamin Sunderland, white, without addressing him by name; He has constantly refused to humble himself before officials and the 60 or 70 white instruc- tors there; He was too popular with the boys imprisoned there. "Rotten Set-up" "I spoke up when things seemed wrong to me," he said Thursday. "They told me to mind my own business and they would mind theirs. "It is a rotten set-up," he con- tinued. "The boys are supposed to be prepared to re-enter society with a new point of view. Actual- ly they are prepared for nothing. They are encouraged to lie and cheat. No control is exercised over them. They are cruelly beaten. "One boy was beaten and con- fined to the hospital nine months. He was struck in the face with a bunch of keys, but the official report was that he was hurt play- ing football. On the road to re- covery he was beaten again with the result that both of his eyes were closed. His nose bled for four days. No Report Made There was no report on his condition from the hospital. title: Turner Gets Cheltenham Cottage Post
full_text: Turner Gets Cheltenham Cottage Post BALTIMORE Another step toward the rehabilitation of the Chel- tenham School for Boys, at Cheltenham, Md., was tak- en this week, when for the first time in the recent his- tory of that institution a colored man was placed on its staff of officials as a cot- tage master. This newly appointed cottage master is Milton Turner, 29, of 2220 Madison Avenue, a 1934 graduate of Morgan College and a former county school teacher. Mr. Turner assumed his new duties at Cheltenham on Monday. Не isresponsible, under direction, for the custody, sa fekeeping, training and Milton Turner well-being of the boys at that institution. The salary range of this position is $600 to $720 a year, with full maintenance. Personnel Revised The appointment of Mr. Tur- ner, who stood third on the list in a State employment service examination last fall, is said to have been achieved as a direct result of Governor Herbert R. O'Conor's recent revision of the personnel of the board of man- agers at the school. While colored cottage masters were not uncommon many years ago, no colored have been ap- pointed since the State took over the institution in 1937. Concerning this new appoint- ment, Edward S. Lewis, execu- tive secretary of the Baltimore Urban League and member of the executive committee of the board of managers at Chelten- ham, had this to say on Wednes- day: In Line with Agreement "We feel that this appointment of Mr. Turner is significant, in that it marks the beginning of a policy agreed upon, that those employees at Cheltenham deal- ing intimately with the personnel should be colored." Prior to his appointment, Mr. Turner, who is unmarried, but said to be engaged to Miss Mary Spriggs, of 2413 McCulloh Street, taught at high schools in Ches- tertown, Rockville and Princess Anne, Md. Other colored board members at Cheltenham include: Joseph C. Briscoe, the Rev. Albert J. Mitch- ell, Mrs. Bertha B. Proctor, Miss Ida R. Cummings and John L. Berry.

title: Rev. Geo. Bragg Dies; Served One Church 49 Years
full_text: Rev. Geo. Bragg Dies; Served One Church 49 Years BALTIMORE - The Rev. George F. Bragg, Jr., 77, editor, author, civic figure and for the past 49 years rector of St. James P.E. Church, died in his sleep at 7:15 a.m., Tuesday, at Provident Hospital, where he was taken on Saturday. The Rev. Mr. Bragg was taken ill two weeks ago with a cold and was confined to his home. Al- though absent from his pulpit on March 3, and the past Sunday morning, he ventured out that (Continued on Page 6, Col. 6) Brief Illness Fatal to Rev. George F. Bragg (Continued from Page 1) evening and conducted the serv- ices. Funeral services will be held at the church on Friday at 2:30 p.m.. with Bishop Edward T. Helfenstein, white, bishop of Maryland, presiding. The body will rest at the home. 827 N. Arlington Avenue, until 10 a.m. Thursday, after which time it will lie in State at the church. Baltimore's Oldest in Service The Rev. Mr. Bragg claimed the honor of being the oldest min- ister in Baltimore, in point of service. He has been in the min- istry fifty-two years, having been ordained in 1888, the twelfth Episcopal clergyman of his race. He was considered an authority on the history of the colored church and on the history of col- ored Americans, Marylanders in particular. One of his best-known works is his "Men of Maryland." which is considered authoritative. Educated in Virginia He was born in Warrenton. N.C., January 25, 1863, the son of George F. and Mary Bragg. who soon after his birth moved to Petersburg, Va. He studied first at the Episco- pal Parochial School, then in the St. Stephen Normal School and in the Bishop Payne Divinity School, all in Petersburg. His education was supplement- ed by private instruction from a master of languages, under whom he studied Latin, Greek. Hebrew and philosophy. In 1881, he was appointed a page in the Virginia Legislature and a little later was promoted to the postmastership of the House of Delegates. Pioneer Publisher In 1882 when just 18 years old. he founded and edited the Vir- ginia Lancet, one of the first col- ored weeklies published in Vir- ginia. He conducted this news- paper for five years and on Janu- агу 12, 1887, in St. Stephen Church, Petersburg, he was or- dained deacon by Bishop Whittle of Virginia. He immediately left for Nor- folk. where he began his min- istry as head of a small Episco- pal mission. He remained there for nearly five years, during which time he formally organized Grace Church, secured the lot. built a new church and rectory and improved the cld school building. He also carried on a day indus- trial school in connection with his work. Was Hampton Trustee During this time, Governor Fitzhugh Lee of Virginia appoint- ed him one of the State's trustees of the Hampton Institute, where he served for four years. In December 1888, he was ad- vanced to the priesthood by Bish- op Whittle in St. Luke Church, Norfolk, and in the fall of 1891, he accepted the call to come to St. James Church, Baltimore. which at that time was located on High Street in East Baltimore and had a congregation "ready to die." Growth Steady The Rev. Mr. Bragg set to work, increased the communicant list from 63 to over 200, and erected a new church building at Park Avenue and Preston Street, in 1901. ! In 1931. the church had prog- ressed to the point where it had over 300 members and was: able to give nearly $1,000 a year to charities, missions and bene- volence. In 1932, the old St. James prop- erty on Park Avenue was pur- chased by the city and the church moved into the edifice formerly occupied by the white Church of Ascension. at Arlington and Lafayette Avenues and has re- mained there ever since. Founded Friendless Home Soon after coming to Baiti- more in 1899, the Rev. Mr. Bragg founded the Maryland Home for Friendless Colored Children, which is now known as the Friendless Home for Colored Boys and is located in Catons- ville. For three decades, the Rev. Mr. Bragg was secretary of the An- nual Conference of the Episcopal Church Workers, and edited the Ledger. which afterward merged with the Baltimore AMERICAN. AFRO- He also published a church or- gan, the Church Advocate, for the past 34 years, as well as the Maryland Home, monthly publi- cation for the Friendless Home. For a number of years he served on the board of managers of Cheltenham. For the past half century, the Rev. Mr. Bragg has been a well- known figure in all public move- ments, but was never active in politics. He frequently wrote letters for publication in newspapers and carried on a large correspondence with men in publie office. In September 1937, the Rev. Mr. Bragg and his wife. the former Miss Nellie Hill. odebra:- ed the fiftieth anniversary e their marriage. On this necasion, the Rev. Mr. Bragg recalled that in 1909, in St. James Church. hs mother and father also celebrated the fiftieth anniversary of their marriage and he administered communion to his parents, hs wife and each of his children. Surviving him. besides Mrs. Bragg, are two daughters. Miss Mary Bragg and Mrs. Nellie Sor- rell, and two sons. Harry Bragg of New York and Arthu: Bragg. Among the Rev. Mr Bragg's many works are the following books and pamphlets: "History of the Afro-American Gro of the Epi-copal Church "The Fi St. Stephens. Petersburg." title: Dedicate Cheltenham Gymnasium in Oct.
full_text: Dedicate Cheltenham Gymnasium in Oct.  BALTIMORE Dedication of the new $80,-000 gymnasium at the Cheltenham School for Boys, originally scheduled for Sunday afternoon, has been postponed to one day early next month, it was learned on Thursday.  The new brick building is one-story high, with a basement equipped with showers and wash rooms. The main floor is suitable for indoor games and may also be used as an auditorium.  Swimming Pool Held Up Addition of a swimming pool to the building is being held up pending granting of additional funds by the next legislature, it was reported.  While the dedication date has tentatively been set for October 12, it is expected that a definite date will be set at a meeting of the board of managers on next Thursday.  N.C. Holds Guards after Convicts Flee  GOLDSBORO, N.C.—Following the escape of two long-term convicts, James Morgan and Richard Brooks, both 35, from the State Hospital for the insane, last week, J. F. Green and Elmore Morrissey, both white watchmen at the hospital, are being held pending an investigation.  Morgan and Brooks, sentenced for robbery, sawed through bars in a second-story cell block and climbed down a rope made of sheets. The two, recaptured five miles south of this city, had made an attempt to escape three weeks previously.

title: Track Greats, New and Old, Headline Penn Carnival
full_text: Track Greats, New and Old, Headline Penn Carnival By GEORGE LYLE, JR. SATURDAY.-Weather doubt- ful, track heavy, high winds sweeping across the track. The faithful devotees of the cinderpath are up betimes this morning to see the running of the forty-sixth annual Pennsylvania Relay Carnival, the No. 1 sports event of the day, which gets off to a flying start with the heats in the collegiate half-mile relay championship. North Texas Teachers win and there is joy in the stands. Then NYU, with George Hagans doing a fine job, takes a first. Xavier, turning in the best time in the trials, easily takes Rutgers and Columbia. In Full Regalia In the finals it appears that the Nordic quartets have been scared by the dark menace and Texas wins, with Pitt and Xavier elcse behind. Up in the stards, a lanky Texan, completely garbed in ten-gallon hat and cowboy boots (minus spurs) attracts plenty of attention. Lunches are being brought out of bags and boxes now and the hotdog man gets lots of takers as the clock reg- 1sters 11:30 and the next re- lay is still an hour away. Walt Arrington, Michigan State field man, has just completed a try in the broad jump, spanning 23 feet 11 5-8 inches to top the field. His triumph is short- lived though. Don Blount, white, of Dartmouth, nosed him out with a leap that carried him a frac- tion over an inch farther. Photographers appear from ev- erywhere and began getting ac- tion shots of the winner and sec- ond-place man. Nice racket that. Lock at Marshall Wilson, lining up that trio of smartly turned-out women. Wonder whether he got their phone num- bers? I'll ask him when he comes over. They seem anxious to speak to Arrington; what's he got that ch! here comes Wilson. They're his sisters? Oh, you mean Walter's, There's another one around? Gee, lucky guy, isn't he? Oh, well, Threadgill on Hand Who's that over there in front of the high jump pit. Al Thread- gill, guess he's thinking of days when he was the big shot in that event. One thing, he won't miss any of the boys' form today; his seat is almost on top of the standards. Goodness, the place is begin- ning to show signs of life; there come some of the high schcol runners out for a little warming up work. People are beginning to come in, too. One thing about this affair it is really colorful, literally and figuratively. Lock around in the stands. These girls are really coming on with their fine reds, blues, yellows and stuff. Out on the field, which inci- dentally is a beautiful green car- pet without a mark on it, the radiant-hued sweat suits vie with the colors in the stands. Near tragedy: two girls are conversing when suddenly one turns to go back down the ramp. The wind blow- ing at just the right minute, blows the second girl's veil so that it is caught in the de- parter's coat, A scream, "Wait Camilla!", averts more serious damage and the day is safe. What's this? the races have started. Who's that boy? Har- ry Dickens, from Cheltenham; want to watch him in the next few years he's got possibilities. Ho, hum. Norcum High from Portsmouth is going through its usual routine. First at the 100- yard mark and steadily retro- gressing from there in. Too bad, wish I could help that anchor man close up about half that gap anyway. Few It's 1:30! My, how time flies. The stands are pretty well filled and still they come. people are on the upper deck, most of the crowd being concen- trated in the north and east stands. Douglass Third Nott Terrace shows a good pair of tracksters in Salley and Holland, while Douglass High of Baltimore squeezes out a third place to Media in one of the fastest races of the day. Here come the college miles, now we'll see something. Look at that fellow go; that's Dean Cummings of West Chester; is he closing up that gap? Come on, boy! (He did.) Union is scratched in its event but Howard is still in. First I ever heard of a sepia trackman at LaSalle, Eddie Brown according to the program. Oh, yes, Howard WAS in this race. It took Lincoln to break into the medal class, with a third place over Morgan State and Virginia State. Great Finish Talk about opportunity, Cam- eraman Joe Sewell, of Washing- ton, just missed a swell shot at the finish of the Tuskegee event. Uly Sams, ace anchor man, ran his heart out to close up a twen- ty-yard gap and, in his desperate lunge at the tape, fell a heart- beat behind Doan of Wayne Uni- versity to cop second-place honors. Wonder why some athletes distort their faces as if in agony while others show no more expression than the well-known cow. There's Tarzan Cooper, star of the Renaissance five, seated just behind Threadgill. And there is Dick Moultrie, ex-captain of the Lincoln team, who put on many a fine show for the Frank- lin Field goers during his stay at the Lions' lair. Juan Luyanda of Lincoln is warming up for the high jump. He's a Puerto Rican and is the South American AAU titleholder. Walt Arrington and Joshua (Josh) Williamson join him. clean_text: Track Greats, New and Old, Headline Penn Carnival By GEORGE LYLE, JR. SATURDAY.-Weather doubt- ful, track heavy, high winds sweeping across the track. The faithful devotees of the cinderpath are up betimes this morning to see the running of the forty-sixth annual Pennsylvania Relay Carnival, the No. 1 sports event of the day, which gets off to a flying start with the heats in the collegiate half-mile relay championship. North Texas Teachers win and there is joy in the stands. Then NYU, with George Hagans doing a fine job, takes a first. Xavier, turning in the best time in the trials, easily takes Rutgers and Columbia. In Full Regalia In the finals it appears that the Nordic quartets have been scared by the dark menace and Texas wins, with Pitt and Xavier elcse behind. Up in the stards, a lanky Texan, completely garbed in ten-gallon hat and cowboy boots (minus spurs) attracts plenty of attention. Lunches are being brought out of bags and boxes now and the hotdog man gets lots of takers as the clock reg- 1sters 11:30 and the next re- lay is still an hour away. Walt Arrington, Michigan State field man, has just completed a try in the broad jump, spanning 23 feet 11 5-8 inches to top the field. His triumph is short- lived though. Don Blount, white, of Dartmouth, nosed him out with a leap that carried him a frac- tion over an inch farther. Photographers appear from ev- erywhere and began getting ac- tion shots of the winner and sec- ond-place man. Nice racket that. Lock at Marshall Wilson, lining up that trio of smartly turned-out women. Wonder whether he got their phone num- bers? I'll ask him when he comes over. title: Suspicions of Teacher Aid in Arrest of Seven
full_text: Suspicions of Teacher Aid in Arrest of Seven  BALTIMORE During most of last week, four young boys lavishly treated themselves; their chums, and jingled coins in their pockets while in school until a suspicious teacher reported the matter to Policewoman Violet H. Whyte who solved the mystery of a missing $90.  As a result, four boys, all un- der 15 years of age, were given a hearing on Tuesday in Ju- venile Court. The ringleader was committed to Cheltenham, one of the boys was held for further investigation, while the other two were placed on probation.  3 Face Jury Three persons, Mrs. Mary Richards, 50, her son, Charles, 18, of 1843 N. Gilmor Street, and Nathaniel E. Toyer, 19, of 1026 Lexington Street, who were charged with receiving some of the stolen money, were placed under $250 bail each by Mag- istrate J. H. Stanford for grand jury action.  On Monday, January 13, James Sandy, white, of 1601 Westmin- ster Avenue, told police that while eating in a store near Fulton Av- enue and Baker Street, someone had stolen $90 from a compart- ment in his truck which had been parked nearby. The money rep- resented collections for the bakery for which he worked.  Sandy said he did not discover the loss until after he had re- ported at the bakery office. Returning to the scene, he searched for several small boys he had observed loitering about, but failed to find them.  Teacher Gives Clue A few days later, Policewom- an Whyte was told by a school- teacher of the unusual amount of money several of her pupils had in their possession. Her subsequent investigation led to the roundup of the boys and the persons charged with receiving the money. Sergeant Thomas Joyce aided in the roundup.  In signed statements, the boys admitted stealing the money. They said they gave Mrs. Richards, $1 for allowing them to divide the cash on her kitchen table, and gave some money to her son and young daughter and to Toyer.  Sandy was unable to identify the boys at the hearing. He said they appeared to be of the same size as the ones he had observed when he parked his truck.

title: 8 Cheltenham Boys Get 3 to 9 Months, 1 Freed
full_text: 8 Cheltenham Boys Get 3 to 9 Months, 1 Freed UPPER MARLBORO, Md. One of the nine inmates of the Cheltenham School for Boys, who were tried here on Friday before Magistrate Walter Green on charges of riotous assembly, was freed in the custody of his par- ents, while the others received sentences of from three to nine months in the Maryland house of correction. At their first trial before him on December 5, Magistrate Green found the boys not guilty on the original charges of assault and at- tempting to escape and held them in jail until Friday when the charge was changed to riotous as- sembly so that their conviction and a light sentence might be pos- sible. Jesse Wells, 16, Freed The freed youth is Jesse Wells, 16, of 434 Orchard Street, Bal- timore, who was given a sus- pended sentence of six months in the house of correction and placed on probation for a year in the custody of his mother, Mrs. Martha Wells. He was represented by Ben- jamin Kline, white, attorney of Baltimore, and was the only one of the accused youths with coun- sel. This disposition of the case was predicted in an AFRO story in Friday's paper and was made be- cause B. B. Sunderland, white, acting superintendent, and Jo- seph H. Blandford, president of the board of managers, asked that the boys not be returned to the school because they were too dif- ficult to manage. Boys Spurn Cheltenham The boys, themselves, at the earlier hearing, expressed their desire to remain in the Upper Marlboro jail until their case was disposed of rather than be re- turned to Cheltenham. ☐ George Crawford, 17, of Bal- timore, who was given a three- month sentence in the house of ☐ correction because the where- abouts of his parents was not known, was the only found not guilty of these new charges. However, Magistrate Green stated that he will ask Governor Herbert R. O'Conor to commute Crawford's sentence as soon as the youth's parents are located and he will be released in their custody. Others Sentenced The other inmates who were sentenced to the house of cor- rection and the terms they re- ceived are: Charles Jackson, 18, of Wood- bine, nine months; Charles No- land, 17, of Rockville, nine months; George Johns, 16, of 1128 Whatcoat Street, Baltimore, six months; Benjaminin Hardy, 18, ☐ of 1427 Webb Street, Baltimore, six months; William Brooks, 18, of 1414 Ν. Gilmor Street, Baltimore, six months; Charles Jordan, 17, of 1106 Whatcoat Street, Baltimore, six months, and Christopher Thomspon, 16, of 437 Orchard Street, Baltimore, six months, 2 Years for 3 Others Three other inmates of Chel- tenham, who were arrested on December 5 and charged with as- saulting Owen Strong, white, also a cottage master, pleaded guilty at an earlier trial here, Friday, before Magistrate Alan Bowie, ☐☐ and received sentences of two years each in the Maryland house of correction. ! They are Daniel Brown, 17, of Annapolis; William Gardner, 17, of Towson, and Henry Solomon, 18, of Baltimore, According to the testimony, these youths, who were assigned to another cottage, resented Strong's ordering them out of No. 2 cottage at the institution, and struck him several times with wooden clubs. 31 YEARS AS CANAL ZONE TEACHER CRISTOBAL, Canal Zone (ANP) Completing 31 years of unbroken service with the Canal Zone government as teacher and principal in the colored schools, sT. S. Johnston, prominent West - Indian educator, will be retired yat the end of this month and may Isail soon after to Jamaica, his is- land home, to spend his declining years with his wife and relatives. title: $2,686 Estate Given to Heirs
full_text: $2,686 Estate Given to Heirs  BALTIMORE The $2,686 estate of Mrs. Charity McKee, widow of the late president of Florida A. and M. College was distributed to her heirs here, this week.  Beneficiaries included:  Mrs. Philly Mason of St. Louis, $1,283; Mrs. Eudora Tucker, $1283.  Boys’ Home Gets $150  Home for Friendless Boys, $150, by way the Virgin Council No. 42, Daughters of Elks; $150 from the Neighbors in Training Club; Miss Alice Price, $25.12; Bishop Norman Bishop and Ruth Bishop, $100; Miss Elizabeth Smith, $100; and the Rev. Shel- ton Bishop, Hutchins C. Bishop, Vivian Bishop, and Andrew Bishop, $10.62 each.  Mrs. Augusta Boothe, a niece, was omitted from the list of ben- eficiaries because the widow said she had made a settlement on her before her death.  Jones Fired by Recreation Dept.  BALTIMORE J. Alvin Jones, Jr., supervisor in the recreational department of Baltimore, was recently dis- missed $22 in charges of disorderly conduct and assaulting an officer, was dismissed from his job on Saturday.  At a meeting of the seven-man board of the recreation depart- ment, it was apparent that Mr. Jones, former Lexington Market clerk, was guilty of conduct un- becoming a recreational leader and city employee.  They had reference to the so- licitations at a dance given for the benefit of a Social Club on last February 28.  At that time, Mr. Jones, who lives at 1633 W. Baltimore Street, was involved in a disturb- ance in the Odd Fellows’ Hall, Park and Lanvale Streets, which subsequently resulted by charges of Clara Butler and other officers.  6 Vacancies Now at Cheltenham  BALTIMORE The recent death of Dr. Wil- liam H. Debnam, trustee, of Durham, brings to six the num- ber of vacancies on the board of managers of the Cheltenham School for Boys.  The terms of Enoch Harlan, Edward J. Hall, Dr. J. B. John- son, Alpheus K. Williams and James L. Scrimair, expired last June, but Governor O’Conor has not named anyone to fill the vacancies.  Trial of Shore Suspects Shifted  BALTIMORE Although it is expected that the two men indicted for the murder of Harvey Pritchard, white, Stockton, Md., farmer, will go to trial next week, the case has not been entered on the dock- et of the Baltimore County Cir- cuit Court at Towson, where it was moved last week, the Afro learned on Friday morning October.  The change of venue from Worcester County on the Eastern Shore was granted by a judge of the First Judicial Circuit Court. Charged with the murder of the farmer are Arthur Collick, 28, and Charles Absurd, 17, both of Stockton.

title: Ask State for $20,000 More for Cheltenham
full_text: Ask State for $20,000 More for Cheltenham BALTIMORE A committee interested in the Cheltenham School for Boys ap- peared before the ways and means committee of the Mary- land Legislature on Tuesday and requested that a supplemental budget of $20,700 be approved for the institution. A total of $11,700 of the 'amount requested was for operat- ing expenses, including $7,600 for food supplies and $4,100 for wearing apparel. The remaining $9,020 re- quested was for salaries and wages, including a $300 increase | in the salaries of the two colored parole officers and other em- ployees. Governor Asked $500.000 In his budget submitted to the legislature in January, Governor Herbert R. O'Conor requested over a half-million dollars for the institution, including $20,000 for additional salaries; $5,000 for operating expenses; and $225,000 for new buildings. This amount, however, did not include sufficient funds to allow purchase of more adequate food supplies and kitchen equipment. wearing apparel or increase in salaries for some employees, in- cluding the parole officers. Those who made the request for a supplmenetal budget in- cluded Edward S. Lewis, a mem- ber of the board of managers of the school; Joseph H. Blandford, president of the board; Colonel H. C. Jones, State employment commission; J. Abner Hall and Sidney Charles. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Snatch Artist Charges Hold 6 Boys under 13
full_text: Snatch Artist Charges Hold 6 Boys under 13  BALTIMORE Alarmed by the series of six pocketbook snatchings reported in the district over a five-day pe- riod Northwestern district police stepped up their investigation and as a result arrested six boys, all under 13 years of age, over the week-end.  Subsequent investigation linked the boys with two of the cases. Given a hearing in Ju- venile court, on Monday, the al- leged ring leader was committed to the Cheltenham School for Boys; another was dismissed. Jailly future of the other four were held under advisement.  Linked with 2 Snatches The following two cases are the ones in which the sextette were named by police: On Tuesday afternoon, Jan- uary 21, Mrs. Beatrice Banton, of 2007 Madison Avenue, was walking near on Presstman Street when as she approached the corner of Irving Street she was accosted by a young boy who, she said, snatched her pocketbook and fled through a nearby alley. The total value of the purse was estimated at $1.52.  The second case was reported by Mrs. Julia Brown, of 2246 Madison Avenue, who while leaving a group of young boys at Bloom and Brunt Streets Satur- day evening, said her bag was snatched by one of them. Among miscellaneous valuables in the purse was $7 in cash and a $25 watch.  With these two cases cleared up officers on Thursday were re- ported endeavoring to find the guilty parties in four other cases still open on their complaint books. The cases were filed by: Mrs. Hazel Street, of 1811 Ful- ton Street, who, while entering the back yard of her home, Fri- day, reported being yoked by an unidentified man who threw one arm around her neck and snatched her purse with the free hand. A pack of cigarettes and eighteen cents in change was the extent of her loss.  The sure sight Mrs. Winifred Robertson, of 1613 Division Street, became a victim when her purse was snatched while she was walking through the 2100 block of Division Street.  The other two victims were Mrs. Emma Weaver, of 623 Lan- vale Street, whose purse contain- ing $3.50 in cash and a $12 check- book was stolen while she was lea- ving a truck from her home, and Mrs. Nettie Bowling, of 2315 Mc- Culloh Street, whose purse con- taining $23 in cash and a $3 handbag was taken at Harlem and Caroline Avenues, Satur- day.

title: Cheltenham Fight Lost
full_text: Cheltenham Fight Lost BALTIMORE Voting as a bloc, the six white members of the board of management of Chelten- ham School for Boys elect- Led Claude D. Jones, 57- year-old white applicant, educational director for that institution on Thurs- day. The five colored members pres- ent voted for Martin J. Brennan, 37, for seventeen years connected with Hampton Institute, for the post. The action of the board, still dominated by the same group under which Cheltenham won the reputation of being one of the! most shamefully mismanaged re- form institutions in the country, dissipates hope, leaders declared Thursday night, that the O'Conor administration will develop a capable colored staff at the insti- tution. White members who voted for Mr. Jones were Enoch Harlan, Joseph H. Blandford, Hal B. Cla- gett, Blossie K. Miller, James E. i Steuart and J. Amos Davis. i Colored members who voted for Mr. Brennan were John L. Berry, the Rev. Albert J. Mitchell, Miss Ida R. Cummings and Mrs. Ber tha B. Proctor. Not present at the meeting were Dr. Harry C. Byrd, Howard C. Hill, Dr. William H. Gibbons, M. Hampton Magruder, white, and Dr. J. L. Johnson. Effort Blocked The election of Mr. Jones fol- lowed an exhaustive personnel report by Col. Harry C. Jones, State employment commissioner, in which he gave the three appli- cants ratings. These ratings, it was said, placed the applicants in the following order: Jones, Bren- non and Charles Kempton. Superintendent Sunderland, it was believed, favored the selec- tion of Mr. Brennon. The action of the controlling bloc, therefore, led some of the colored members to believe that the selection of the white educa- tional director meant that at some future date he would be elevated to the post of superintendent, now held by Sunderland. Second Battle Pending The white bloc also pegged an effort on the part of the colored members to insure that the physical and recreational direc- tor, yet to be named, would be colored. They voted solidly against a resolution offered by the Rev. Mr. Mitchell to the effect that preference be given a colored applicant for this post. Blame O'Conor Leaders interested in reforms at Cheltenham who commented on the selection of the white edu- cational director on Thursday blamed Governor Herbert O'Con- or for the outcome. They pointed out that his fail- ure to name new members to the board when the terms of five members expired last May left in control the same group under which the institution has been so long mismanaged. Whether or not, they say, this action on the part of the Gover- nor is deliberate, the result has been to block the appointment of colored applicants to posts insur- ing efficient and sympathetic ad- ministration of the institution in the interest of the inmates instead of job-seeking whites. Promise Unfulfilled They also state that this action does not square with the Gover- nor's promise to give colored citi- zens a chance to conduct their own institutions. The members whose terms ex- pired last May were Enoch Har- lan, Howard C. Hill, Dr. J. L. Johnson, Blossie K. Miller, James E. Steuart. Miller, Steuart, Har- lan and Blandford voted for Mr. Jones on Thursday. Howard C. Hill has voted generally with the reform group. : Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Yuletide Made Happy for Boys at Cheltenham
full_text: Yuletide Made Happy for Boys at Cheltenham - CHELTENHAM, Md. The holiday season was a happy. one for the boys at the Cheltenham School because many Baltimore men and women, at the suggestion of the school's auxiliary, made contributions of money and gifts. The auxiliary of the school in- cludes Mrs. Bertha Proctor, Miss Ida Cummings, Miss Vivian Young, Mrs. Rose D. Hill, Mrs. Fannie Tibbs, Policewoman Violet Hill Whyte and Edward S. Lewis. Miss Cummings furnished fifty- two bags of candy and peanuts for the boys and collected $51 which was turned over to the school for the purchase of gifts for those boys who have no fam- ilies. Sorority Gives Money Miss Young, representing the Pi Lambda Psi honorary society of Morgan State College, made a donation of $35 with which games and toys for smaller boys were purchased. Policewoman Whyte secured a contribution of nine dozen fleece- lined gloves from Captain Joseph Wallace and Lieutenant Edward Coughlin of the Northwestern police district, and other mem- bers of the committee made per- sonal donations with which toys. games, clothing and gifts were purchased. On Christmas Day, each of the boys were treated to a turkey dinner given by the school and also received fruits and candy. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Morgan Will Be Host to Bucknell Students
full_text: Hampton Grad New Teacher BALTIMORE An appointment and a resig- nation at the Cheltenham School for Boys were announced during a meeting of the school's Board of Managers on Thursday. Jacob Thomas Stewart, 25, Hampton Institute graduate, has been named instructor in tailor- ing. He began his duties this week. His appointment marks the first time within recent years that a colored person has been named to teach tailoring. The job carries with it a sal- ary of $900 a year. Mr. Stewart is a native of DeWitt, Va., but has been residing in Riverhead, N.Y., most of his life. He grad- uated from Hampton in May, 1940. White Supervisor Quits The resignation was that of O. C. Lott, white, recreational supervisor. An examination will be offered by the State employ- ment commission to fill this vacancy. Morgan Will Be Host to Bucknell Students BALTIMORE Twelve Bucknell College stu- dents white, who are interested in studying needs of colored people in Baltimore, will visit the city on Friday and Saturday, March 21 and 22, at which time they will be the guests of Mor- gan State College. clean_text: Hampton Grad New Teacher BALTIMORE An appointment and a resig- nation at the Cheltenham School for Boys were announced during a meeting of the school's Board of Managers on Thursday. Jacob Thomas Stewart, 25, Hampton Institute graduate, has been named instructor in tailor- ing. He began his duties this week. His appointment marks the first time within recent years that a colored person has been named to teach tailoring. The job carries with it a sal- ary of $900 a year. Mr. Stewart is a native of DeWitt, Va., but has been residing in Riverhead, N.Y., most of his life. He grad- uated from Hampton in May, 1940. White Supervisor Quits The resignation was that of O. C. Lott, white, recreational supervisor. An examination will be offered by the State employ- ment commission to fill this vacancy. title: Army, Navy Agreed on Blood Ban
full_text: Army, Navy Agreed on Blood Ban  WASHINGTON Two high defense officials, Ad- miral R. Ross Mcintyre and Sur- geon General James C. Magee, of the navy and army, respec- tively, were both unusually wary of an AP/RG reporter here to- day, when pressed for verification of rumors circulating within dis- tricts Red Cross affiliates to re- fuse to accept colored donors at the volunteer Blood Bank in Baltimore. Through his secretary, Admiral Mcintyre pleaded that the AP permit allow him time in which to contact the Washington Red (Continued on Page 2, Col. 2)

title: New Cheltenham Head Takes Over
full_text: New Cheltenham Head Takes Over BALTIMORE Vance E. Thomas, white, for- mer parole officer at the Fed- eral Penitentiary at Atlanta, Ga., took over his new duties as su- perintendent at the Cheltenham School for Boys on Saturday, it was announced this week. Mr. Thomas was selected from a list of eligible persons who passed a State exam early this year. He succeeds B. B. Sunder- land, also white, who has re- turned to his former position as parole officer of the school. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner.
