title: Gov. Asks HalfMillion More for 7 Institutions: Lives Up to Promises Made in Inaugural Address 2 Years Ago; $123,400 Increase for Morgan
full_text: Gov. Asks Half- Million More for 7 Institutions Lives Up to Promises Made in Inaugural Address 2 Years Ago; $123,400 Increase for Morgan BALTIMORE If Governor Herbert R. O'Conor's budget as pre- sented to the Maryland Legislature on Tuesday evening is adopted by that body, seven institutions for colored people will get an increase of over a half million dollars for next year. In recommending these increases, which total ap- He did not allow funds for ad- e proximately $630,000, Gov- ministration building, three ad- ernor O'Conor lived up to ditional cottages, an 1 e S t f academic his inaugural address prom- building, addition to gymnasium ise of two years ago to pro- vide increased facilities for many of the educational and reform institutions of the State. Morgan's Increase $123,400 Morgan State College, which was taken over by the State in 1939, will get $123,400 additional next year. Of this sum, $122,000 will be for the following items: $80,000 for a new power plant. $27,000 to complete and furnish the new girls' dormitory. $15,000 to remodel the service building and apartments. These latter are the stone structures now used as girls' dormitories, which may become vacant when the new dormitory is completed. girls' According to the governor's report to the legislature, Mor- gan's budget for the year 1942- 1943 will be $198,900. Of this 2 amount, the college will raise $110,000 from students' fees and charges for board and lodging. The appropriation from the State will amount to $88,900 which is $1400 more than Mor- ▸gan received from the State last year for salaries and operating expenses. For salaries in 1940, Morgan expended $105,934. The governor allowed $120,195 for 1942 and 1943. This will be an increase of nearly $15,000, most of which goes for new teachers, Operating Cost $91,000 in 1940 On the other hand, Morgan spent in 1940, $91,844 for operat- ing expenses and the governor allowed $78,705 for the next two years. The fact that the college re- ceives for salaries and op- erating expenses only $1,400 more than last year and still will have nearly $15,000 more to spend for new teachers, indicates that mon-: ey allowed for operating expen- ses in 1940 will go into salaries in 1942 and 1943. Appropriation $30,000 Short In its request to the governor, Morgan asked for an appropria- tion of $229,780 from the State for salaries and operating ex- penses. It actually received $198,- 900, so that the governor's allow- ance is $30,880 less than request- ed. In its request for new build- ings, it asked for a $100,000 pow- er plant and a $220,000 gymnas- ium, and $40,000 for teachers' cottages. The power plant was granted. Morgan also asked $8,000 for library equipment and $8,000 for scientific equipment. Nothing was allowed for these two items. Mor- gan asked the State for $50,000 for heat tunnels, water lines, and campus lights. Nothing was al- lowed for these items. Cheltenham Increased $280,000 Cheltenham School for Boys was allowed $20,000 additional for salaries; $5,000 additional for operating expenses, and $255,000 for new buildings. and an $80,000 employees' hous- ing project. No Raise for Princess Anne Princess Anne College, a col- ored branch of the University of Maryland, was recommended for the same appropriation as last year, $25,983. It had requested $46,368. 1 1 1 1 I 8 It had requested new build- ings to cost $549,300. The gover- nor allowed a power plant, vo- cational school, new cottage and F sewerage and water system, F Bowie's Increase $38,731 Bowie State Teachers' College spent $57,294 in 1940 for salaries and operating expenses. The governor allowed an in- crease of $8,731 for next year. allowed to remodel; the gym, The sum of $30,000 was also $15,000; extend the water supply, $10,000, and to purchase library equipment, $5,000. Md. School Gets $78,000 Raise The Maryland Training School for Girls at Glenburnie received an increase of $78,000. It spent $38,484 for salaries and operating expenses in 1940, and the gover- nor allower $41,145 for these items next year. A total of $50,000 was granted for an addition to the adminis- tration building and $10,000 for an extension of the sewerage system. A request for $26,700 for a new farm center was not al- lowed. Maryland Scholarships The $30,000 scholarship funds providing State aid to stu- dents who study outside Mary- land was reduced by $5,000 which was the amount previously used for operating expenses. Morgan State College trustees administer the funds now without additional cost. Crownsville Gets $89,980 Boost Crownsville State Hospital re- ceives an increase of $89,980 for the next two years. Last year, it spent $334,420 for operating ex- penses and salaries. It was given $398,400 for next year. A total of $26,000 was granted for new equipment, including hydrotherapy equip- ment ($6,500); porch grills gr ($6,500); pasteurization equip- ment ($10,000); and poultry house ($3,000). Henryton Gets $17,000 Henryton Sanatorium had re- quested $17,150 for beds and fur- nishings for doctors' and nurses' cottages, attendants' cottages and for the hospital and the governor allowed $17,000. Home for Friendless Boyв Increased $700 Only one of eight institutions for colored people which receive partial aid from the State were allowed increases for 1942 and 1943. This was the Home for Friendless Colored Boys at Ca- tonsville, which was granted $700 more than the $2,000 it received last year. Other State-aided institutions and the amount they received are as follows: Provident Hospital, $20,000; AME Home for the Aged, $1,500; N. M. Carroll Home for the Aged, $500; Maryland School for the Blind (colored division), $13,000; $1,500; House of Good Shepherd St. Peter Claver School for Girls, for Girls, $3,000. The Shelter for Aged and Infirm Colored People which received $500 in 1940, was for 1942 not recommended for any grant 1942 and 1943. in detail the amounts recommend- The accompanying table shows ed by the governor for various institutions, the amounts they spent in 1940, and the amounts requested in 1942-1943. title: New Head Is Selected for Cheltenham
full_text: New Head Is Selected for Cheltenham BALTIMORE Vance E. Thomas, 49, white, chief parole officer of the Federal penitentiary at Atlanta, was selected as the next superintendent of the Cheltenham School for Boys at a meeting of the board of managers of the school on Thursday. Mr. Thomas, who will take over his new duties sometime t- within the next sixty days, suc- Le ceeds B. B. Sunderland, also - of white, acting superintendent of the institution, who will return to his former post as parole of- ficer. Native of Sunflower State The new superintendent is a native of Kansas; was educated at Coter College in Nebraska; did his graduate work at the Uni- versity of Nebraska and was at one time administrator of emer- gency relief in Lincoln, Neb. Prior to going to Atlanta, he served as head of the re-employ- ment service in Lincoln and al- "so worked with the Lincoln Ur- rban League as interviewer of 5 colored workers. ون A colored clerk served on his staff of fifteen at the Atlanta e prison and he worked in co- operation with Forrester B. 1 Washington at the Atlanta School r of Social Work, and encouraged his workers to study there. Colored Members for Him It is understood that the col- ored members of the Cheltenham board cast an unanimous vote for the appointment of Mr. Thomas a and that all but four of the white ,members, Hal B. Clagett, Josephj r H. Blandford, Howard C. Hill and James A. Pratt, were inj agreement on the appointment. Edward S. Lewis, a member of 1 the Cheltenham executive com- mittee, in commenting on the ap- › pointment of Mr. Thomas, said that he was particularly im- pressed with him. 1 1 1 1 "He is unusually straight-for- award, clean-cut, honest and pos- sesses an extra-human touch," t Mr. Lewis said. 5 ٤ 1 "New Deal" Is Seen The new superintendent is married and the father of a 7- year-old daughter. His appoint- ament is believed to be another tstep in the "new deal" program I ☐☐ for Cheltenham, which has long 1 been branded as one of the State's t 1 worst correctional institutions. t Governor O'Conor's naming of trained social workers to serve on the board early last year and v Mr. Sunderland's relinquishing of the superintendency to return to , his former post as parole officer, C were the first two steps in the t "new deal" program, i t Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Mr. Thomas, who will take over his new duties sometime t- within the next sixty days, suc- Le ceeds B. B. Sunderland, also - of white, acting superintendent of the institution, who will return to his former post as parole of- ficer. Native of Sunflower State The new superintendent is a native of Kansas; was educated at Coter College in Nebraska; did his graduate work at the Uni- versity of Nebraska and was at one time administrator of emer- gency relief in Lincoln, Neb. Prior to going to Atlanta, he served as head of the re-employ- ment service in Lincoln and al- "so worked with the Lincoln Ur- rban League as interviewer of 5 colored workers. ون A colored clerk served on his staff of fifteen at the Atlanta e prison and he worked in co- operation with Forrester B. 1 Washington at the Atlanta School r of Social Work, and encouraged his workers to study there. Colored Members for Him It is understood that the col- ored members of the Cheltenham board cast an unanimous vote for the appointment of Mr. Thomas a and that all but four of the white ,members, Hal B. Clagett, Josephj r H. Blandford, Howard C. Hill and James A. Pratt, were inj agreement on the appointment. Edward S. Lewis, a member of 1 the Cheltenham executive com- mittee, in commenting on the ap- › pointment of Mr. Thomas, said that he was particularly im- pressed with him. 1 1 1 1 "He is unusually straight-for- award, clean-cut, honest and pos- sesses an extra-human touch," t Mr. Lewis said. 5 ٤ 1 "New Deal" Is Seen The new superintendent is married and the father of a 7- year-old daughter. His appoint- ament is believed to be another tstep in the "new deal" program I ☐☐ for Cheltenham, which has long 1 been branded as one of the State's t 1 worst correctional institutions. t Governor O'Conor's naming of trained social workers to serve on the board early last year and v Mr. Sunderland's relinquishing of the superintendency to return to , his former post as parole officer, C were the first two steps in the t "new deal" program, i t . .

title: Gov. Allows 3 Million for Institutions
full_text: Gov. Allows 3 Million for Institutions BALTIMORE Seven State institutions for colored people and nine State- aided institutions will receive in excess of $3,000,000 during the next two years as a result of the State Legislature's recent ap- proval of Governor Herbert R. O'Conor's budget recommenda- tions, an AFRO survey shows this week. In addition, $350,000 of this amount will go toward equaliza- tion of teachers' salaries in the Maryland counties and $50,000 will provide scholarships for Maryland students who desire to study at institutions of higher learning outside the State. A comparison of the O'Conor appropriations with those during the years 1938-39, when the late Harry W. Nice was Governor, shows that Governor O'Conor has allotted twice as much to our in- stitutions as did his predecessor. Mr. Nice appropriated only $1,392,204 as compared with the present governor's three million. $1,372,434 to Four Schools Largest increases over the Nice administration provided by Gov- ernor O'Conor are for the Chel- tenham School for Boys (which during the Nice administration was in process of becoming a State institution), which during Mr. Nice's term received only $90,000 for that two-year period as compared with the $558,378 provided by Mr. O'Conor for the next two years. Morgan State College (which was not State institution during the Nice regime) will receive a total of $519,800 during the next two years as compared with the $120,000 it received during the previous administration. Princess Anne College, which was denied appropriations for new buildings during the Nice term, which allowed only $51,966, will be able to add at least one new building with the $151,966 allowed by the present governor. The Maryland School for Girls at Glenburnie will receive twice as much ($142,290) during the next two years as it received under Mr. Nice, when it was allowed $77,280. Henryton Figure Lower The remainder of the seven State-supported institutions all get considerably more than they received under the Nice adminis- tration except the Henryton Sanatoruim, whose appropriation during the next two years is slightly less than that awarded by Mr. Nice, [Inasmuch as this institution comes under the general admin- istration of the Maryland Tuber- culosis Sanatoria there might be a slight discrepancy in this figure.] Under the Nice administration,; this institution received $228.110, and during the next two years under Mr. O'Conor it will receive $215,340. Bowie State Teachers' College, which received $100,028 under Mr. Nice, will receive $162,050 under Mr. O'Conor; Crownsville State Hospital, which received $573,820 under Mr. Nice, will get $822,800 under Mr. O'Conor. Other Allotments Maintained Of the State-aided institutions, only one will receive increased appropriations over those granted by Mr. Nice. This is the Home for Friendless Colored Boys at Catonsville, which received total of $4.000 under Nice but will get $5,400 during the next years. a 1 Five of the State-aided insti- tutions will receive the same ap- propriation for the next two years they were allowed under the Nice regime. They are: Shelter for Aged and Infirm Colored Peo- ple, St Peter Claver School for Girls, Provident Hospital, the A.M.E. Home for Aged, and the Maryland School for the Blind.; Only Three Take Cuts which is getting $2.000 less than The remaining three have been the $8,000 allotted by Mr. Nice, allotted less than they received and the N. M. Carroll Home for during the Nice regime. They Aged, which is getting $1,000 for are the Mission Helpers' Day the next two years as compared Nursery, which will get $2,000 as compared with $4,000 it received from Mr. Nice; the House of Good Shepherd for Colored Girls, with the $2,000 awarded it by Mr. Nice. The following table shows the comparative appropriations for the two administrations: Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Cheltenham Head to Take Over March 15
full_text: Cheltenham Head to Take Over March 15 BALTIMORE Vance E. Thomas, white, pa- role officer of the Federal prison at Atlanta, who was named su- perintendent of Cheltenham School for Boys last month, is expected to take over his new duties around March 15, it was announced this week. Mr. Thomas will succeed B. B. Sunderland, at present acting su- perintendent of the insitution, who will return to his former position of parole officer. Father and Son Burned Fighting Fire BALTIMORE Benjamin Johnson, 56, and his son, Charles, 26, were burned slightly, while fighting a fire in their home at 1110 Pennsylvania Avenue, late Tuesday. Taken to Provident Hospital, the father was treated for burns of the right hand, arm and neck, while the son received treatment for burns of the hands. title: 174 Receive $14,000 in Scholarships: 20 of Group Given State Aid Are on Cheltenham Staff
full_text: 174 Receive $14,000 in Scholarships 20 of Group Given State Aid Are on Cheltenham Staff BALTIMORE-A total of 174 persons, including twenty em- ployees of the Cheltenham School for Boys, were granted State scholarships to study in colleges and universities outside the State by the scholarship committee of the Morgan State College Board of Trustees last week. The scholarships are made pos- sible by a $25,000 grant from the State to enable students desiring higher education in fields not available at Morgan or Princess Anne to secure it in other ac- credited schools at State ex- pense. Recommendations for the awards totaling $14,172 were made after the committee, com- posed of Dr. Ivan E. McDougle, white, Josiah Henry, Jr., and Carl Murphy, held interviews with the new applicants. 40 Select H.U. Largest single group-forty- to select any one school, chose Howard University. abstract: A total of 174 persons, including twenty employees of the Cheltenham School for Boys, were granted State scholarships to study in colleges...
clean_text: 174 Receive $14,000 in Scholarships 20 of Group Given State Aid Are on Cheltenham Staff BALTIMORE-A total of 174 persons, including twenty em- ployees of the Cheltenham School for Boys, were granted State scholarships to study in colleges and universities outside the State by the scholarship committee of the Morgan State College Board of Trustees last week. The scholarships are made pos- sible by a $25,000 grant from the State to enable students desiring higher education in fields not available at Morgan or Princess Anne to secure it in other ac- credited schools at State ex- pense. Recommendations for the awards totaling $14,172 were made after the committee, com- posed of Dr. Ivan E. McDougle, white, Josiah Henry, Jr., and Carl Murphy, held interviews with the new applicants. 40 Select H.U. Largest single group-forty- to select any one school, chose Howard University. This includes the twenty Cheltenham employees who will pursue a special course to be sponsored by the univer- sity's extension department at the request of the institution's new supervisor of education, Daniel P. title: Ex-Cheltenham Inmate Gets Twenty-Year Term
full_text: Ex-Cheltenham Inmate Gets Twenty-Year Term BALTIMORE A 15-year-old former Chelten- ham inmate, convicted in Crim- inal Court Thursday of robbing and criminally assaulting at gun- point two teen-age girls and at- tempting to attack another girl, was sentenced by Judge Row- land K. Adams to twenty years in the house of correction. The youth was actually given a total of fifty-two years, twenty years on each of two rape charges, ten years on the assault to rape charge and a year each ☐on the two robbery charges, but ☐ Judge Adams ruled that the sen- tences would run concurrently. Testimony revealed that the first attack occurred on January 10, when the boy accosted Miss Dorothy Beane, 16, of 2521 Wood- brook Avenue, in the 1800 block Madison Avenue and forced her to accompany him to a yard in the rear of a house in the 1900 block of the same street. The other attack is said to have occurred on January 18, when Miss Dorothy Barney, 18, of 1344 Druid Hill Avenue, was stopped by the 'accused youth at Eutaw Place and Lanvale Street and forced into a nearby yard. With her at the time was Miss Ruth Howell, 17, same address, whom he attempted to attack. Miss Beane accused the boy of taking $3.50 from her at the time of the attack on her and Miss Howell said he took her watch and about $10.60. Gregory Haw- kins represented the boy. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: White Man Wins Honor for 1st Time
full_text: White Man Wins Honor for 1st Time BALTIMORE. Vance E. Thomas, white, su. perintendent of the Cheltenham School for Boys, five women and five men were selected this week for places on the AFRO's 1941 Honor Roll, This year marks the first time a white man has been selected for this honor. Basis of the selec- tion was superlative service to the community during the year 1941. The five women selceted are Miss Janet Green, president of the East Baltimore Residential Pro- tective Association; Mrs. Nellie Moore, Girl Scout field advisor; Mrs. Enolia P. McMillan, presi- dent of the Maryland State NAACP; Mrs. Florence Snowden, president of the Northwest Resi dential Protective Association; and Mrs. Violet Hill White, po- licewoman. Templeton Chosen The men on the honor rol, in addition to Mr. Thomas, are Fur- man L. Templeton, now connect. ed with the Office of Civilian Defense, but selected for his work as industrial secretary of the Ur- ban League; Joseph H. Neal, or- ganizer for the CIO's Steel Work. ers' Organizing Committee; De- tective Patrolman J. Hiram But- ler; Avon B. Collins, housing project manager; and Leroy A. Coles, Sr., Boy Scout leace. Those named on the honor roll were chosen from a list of per- sons suggested by AFRO readers and members of the city depart- ment staff of the AFRO. Another Precedent Broken Another deviation from what has been the case in the past is the fact that the 1941 horroil includes a person, Policewoman Whyte, who has previously been on the honor roll. However, be- cause of the fact that she was the first woman in recent years to be cited for meritorious service with the police department, it was felt that she should be included. She is sharing honors with De- tective Patrolman Butler, who was similarly honored. Honorable Mention Those awarded honorable men- tion are: Mrs. Sayde George, su- perintendent of the Maryland Training School for Girls at Glenburnie, Md., Raymond A. C. Young, postal employce and NAACP worker; The Rev. C. Baker Pearle, pas- tor of Payne AME Church: Father Cedric E. Mills, rector of St. James P.E. Churchn; Miss Anita Williams, senior social worker with the Cathore Chari- ties; Reginald Gardner, pres.dent of the Maryland Association for Promotion of Business; Dr. Maceo Williams, director of the Druid Health Center, and Chales Key, Morgan professor and di..ector of the Baltimore Negro Health Week celebration.

title: 400 at Urban League Dinner Honor Lewis
full_text: 400 at Urban League Dinner Honor Lewis BALTIMORE More than 400 persons of both races joined in paying tribute to Edward S. Lewis at the Baltimore Urban League's twelfth annual dinner meeting Thursday evening at the Cadoa, 118 W. Franklin Street. Mr. Lewis was executive secre- tary of the local branch for eleven year, leaving the first of the month for a similar post in New York.  Optimism Expressed The participating speakers all struck notes of optimism in re- counting the gains for workers ob- tained by the league during 1942, which augur even more work opportunities in the future. Before reviewing his administra- tion, Mr. Lewis asked the guests to join with him in a silent tri- bute to the late Father John T. Gillard, a board member, who made it possible for the dinner to be held in the Cadoa several years ago after Johns Hopkins had refused to open its doors: Credits Staff, Board He also paid special tribute to the members of his staff and board and announced that Dr. Bruce McDonald, white, president of the board, had agreed to serve another year. Mr. Lewis cited the increase in the league's annual budget, dur- ing the past eleven years, from $4,000 to a proposed $14,000 for 1943, and said there still remain the following problems to be solved: (1) department store discrim- ination, (2) uniforms for colored police, and (3) equalization of school facilities which, he esti- mated will cost a million dollars. His insistence that a colored person's appointment to the school board would help solve the latter problem brought sus- tained applause Not Discouraged The New York secretary said, however, that he is not discour- aged by the total picture - not even by the FEPC reversals. "We gathered here tonight to represent one of the elements of a new world society not based on race, but on opportunities, democ- racy, and freedom," he said in closing. "I believe that this really is a people's war and that the gates of hell shall not prevail against the new day now dawning." Allen Sees New Hope A. J. Allen, league industrial secretary, in his annual report, de- clared that the most significant factor in the opening of new em- ployment opportunities for col- ored workers is that their chil- dren, likewise, have a reasonable hope for future employment. He revealed that league efforts have made $750,000 in earnings available to members of the build- ing trades heretofore denied em- ployment. Hepbron Praises Lewis James M. Hepbron, white di- rector of the Criminal Justice Commission and the Community Fund, paid a personal tribute to Mr. Lewis, whom he termed his "friend, counsellor and co- worker.". He discussed the work of the Governor's Commission on Juve- nile Delinquency and indicated that its recommendations would be made quite soon. J. Harvey Kerns of Cincinnati, who succeeds Mr. Lewis as ex- ecutive secretary, was present. He will take up his duties March 1. Mr. and Mrs. Lewis received gifts from the Social Workers' Roundtable, the Book-of-the- Month Club, the Painters' Asso- ciation and the Urban League Women's Auxiliary. Several musical numbers were rendered by the Baltimore Sing- ers, directed by Gerald B. Wilson. Among those present were Congress- man Daniel Ellison; Walter Sondheim, director of U.S. Employment Service in Maryland; Kenneth Douty, War Man- power Commission assistant State direc- tor; Mr. and Mrs. Vance Thomas of Cheltenham School for Boys; G. Laird Ferguson, and Saul Purdue, Glenn L. Martin Company's Canton Di- vision; Harry Cohen, Baltimore Federa- tion of Labor: James Drury, post agent, National Maritime Union; Councilman Jacob Edelman, Bert Hanner, WFBR program manager; Sid- ney Katz, Md. CIO Industrial Council; Harold Buchman, local attorney; and Max Orlov, painters' union representa- tive. Bob McGuinn, lawyer for the carpen- ters' union, which now has more than 500 members; Dallas Nicholas, presi- dent, Monumental Bar Association; Mrs. Violet Hill Whyte, policewoman. ! title: The Week: War Training Week? Separate, Unequal Jobs at Cheltenham Collins a Pioneer
full_text: The Week War Training Week? Separate, Unequal Jobs at Cheltenham Collins a Pioneer By B. M. PHILLIPS This is War Training Week. Emphasis is on increasing enroll- ment in the city's war vocational training schools to prepare for vital jobs in war industries. The campaign is sponsored by the vocational war produc- tion training division of the city's schools, and it ought to be conducted without regard to race, color or creed. But it isn't. Advertisements on streetcars and trolleys through- out the city urge trainees to en- roll at Poly and Boys' Vocational Schools, both white. Ads Seek Whites Only Radio ads beg women to sign up at the Howard Street Re- cruiting Center, "white." Nowhere is mention made of similar training at Schools No. 453 at 775 Waesche Street; No. 456. 1315 Druid Hill Av- caue, or Carver Vocational No. 454 at Lajayette and Carroll- ton Avenues. The average person would presume, therefore, that Bal- timore was a democratic city and that training schools plug- ged over the air and on pub- lic vehicles were open to all. But Baltimore has a dual school ystem which is supposed to pro- ide equal and equitable facilities, which of course it does not. Nevertheless, under this dual system, if training classes for whites are advertised during a city-wide campaign, those for col- ored should likewise be pushed. James O. Proctor, supervisor of training, indicated, Thursday, that absence of radio advertising for colored schools was an over- sight and promised to do bet- ter. You can't win a war with mis- takes and promises. To put over the program of advertising, the colored school principals solicited funds from merchants to sponsor paid ad- vertising in colored newspa- pers. A vocational fund used to print advertising signs for white schools was not available to them. Symptom of Fascism This matter of promotion of a special drive in war vocational schools may seem a small matter on the surface, but it is a part of the vicious circle which has sur- rounded the war training pro- gram in our schools for three years. If this war continues to get i tougher, the Baltimore school officials will curse the day they allowed the color of a man to interfere with the war effort. Cheltenham has long been a hell-hole and col- ored people have been buffeted about too much. They want to be sure they'll get a square deal and no segregation. Securing people to fill jobs at institutions of this sort is, after all, the job of the super- intendent. If Cheltenham has at its head someone who is inter- ested in filling those jobs, then it will be done in short order. In the past month, a few girls who have been branded as "un- desirable" by fellow hostesses have been eliminated. The USO invites as hostesses only young women who have high standards. says Mrs. Marian Banfield, assist- ant program director in charge of girls' activities. Avon B. title: HATS OFF DEPARTMENT
full_text: HATS OFF DEPARTMENT AN EDITORIAL Hats off to Brigadier General P. L. Sadler, new commander of U.S. troops in Liberia, who last week put an end to all jim crow mess halls and quarters for officers. To the Board of Managers of Cheltenham, Mary- land's State reform school for colored boys, which did the same thing for its colored and white personnel. To Billy Curran and C. Arthur Eby, who know discrimination in selecting juries when they see it. To Darryl Zanuck, Hollywood producer, who was delighted to share a North African trench with the big- gest Senegalese soldier he had ever seen. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. full_text: The Monumental City Weekly News Summary Cut this out and send it to your soldier boy in your next letter. A new school to replace Chel- raising exercises. tenham School for Boys was rec- ommended by the Governor's Committee on Juvenile Delin- quency. The Baltimore Transit Company denied training colored men as streetcar motormen, A bill outlawing smoking in public places was introduced in the city council. Richard Clark, 22, war work trainee, was fatally shot. Stevedores' Pay Raised Stevedores working on the Bal timore waterfront were granted a five-cent-per-lhour wage increase. Western Union Telegraph Com- pany announced its employment of sixty-seven colored messeng- ers. Luther M. Hardin was ap- pointed physics instructor at Mor- gan State College. Robert T. McGuinn, attorney, › was appointed to the OPA board to decide ration violation. cases. Beautician Fined $10 Mrs. Henrietta Scott, beautician, was fined $10 for not having a shop-owner's license in case test- ing the validity of State Beauty Parlor Code. The Maryland Court of Ap- peals heard debate on the State's authority to refuse a license to a tavern near a church and a high school. The estranged husband of Mrs. Pearl Smith, public health nurse, got a six-month jail term for cut- ting her. The city recreation department was denied permission to set off a Stricker Street block for super- vised roller skating. Bethel Sexton Gets Decree Benjamin Roberts, sexton of Bethel AME Church, was divorced from Mrs. Blanche Roberts. Deaths: Miss Sarah H. Madden, sister of Elmer Madden, filling station operator; William J. Alli- son, 50, killed by railroad train at Middle River, Md. Graduated: 41 from Dunbar High School; 120 from Douglass High School. Church News The Rev. A. J. Payne, Enon Baptist Church, received the Sil- ver Beaver award for dis- Mingushed service to boyhood at a Boy Scout dinner. Mrs. Bertha Winston spoke on History Week to the Baptist Min- isters' Wives' Alliance. St. Paul Baptist Church hon- ored eleven service men at flag- Dr. Henry J. McGuinn, Union University sociology head, spoke at Enon Baptist Church, closing the dedication program. "My People," radio program, directed by Dr. G. Lake Imes, be- gan nation-wide broadcasts. The recreation department's chorus is seeking 100 voices to sing on a nation-wide symphonic program. Social Notes Married: Anita Edwards to Col- lis Bryant, Laura M. Singleton to Lt. Christopher Waller, vora King to H. Clay Evans, and Rosa lind Rogers to Carol Lee. Miss Rose T.. Kinney was re- elected president of the Junior Colleagues. The YWCA Mothers' Club en- tertained at its annual buffet sup- per. The Activities' Fund of Cop- pin Teachers' College gave a vic tory ball. Sports The YMCA boxing committee is arranging exhibition contests with schools, service teams, and defense industrial groups. Basketball scores: High 47, Annapolis 26; Baltimore Mets 39, New York Celtics 37; Morgan 71, Hampton 48; YMCA 61, Glenn Martin 39; USO Sailors 49, Rustless 23.

title: Drop Charges Against Clarke at Cheltenham: White Superintendent Lifts Suspension of Educational Director
full_text: Drop Charges Against Clarke af Cheltenham White Superintendent Lifts Suspension of Educational Director BALTIMORE Charges of insubordination against Daniel Clarke, education- al supervisor at Cheltenham School for Boys, have been with- drawn and his suspension lifted, it was announced Thursday. Col. Harry C. Jones, head of the State Employment Commis- sion which must pass on suspen- sions and dismissals at State in- stitutions, told the AFRO that his office had received notice of withdrawal of the charges by Vance E. Thomas, white, superin- tendent of the school. Howard C. Hill, white, acting president of the school's board of managers, said that Mr. Clarke returned to work Monday and that he and Mr. Thomas had reached a working agreement. Suspended on May 17 Mr. Clarke, the only colored ad- ministrative employee at the in- stitution, was suspended May 17 by Mr. Thomas for refusing to teach a class. However, those close to the Cheltenham situation saw in the suspension an attempt by the ad- ministration to punish Mr. Clarke for joining with other employees who have insisted upon an end to segregation of colored and white staff members at the school. It is also known that Mr. Clarke and other employees have insist- ed that the administration should carry out the order of the board of managers issued March 25, ending segregation in the cafe- teria and housing facilities and a subsequent order urging replace- ment of white employees with colored. Board in 7-Hour Session The board held a seven-hour meeting last Tuesday at which time some of the difficulties were ironed out, but no statement was made. It is expected that the board will hold another meet- ing this week. Eight colored employees have resigned from the school staff in the past two months on account of what they term "bungling" of the question of equal facilities for staff members by Mr. Thomas. Announce List of 20 Jobs Meantime, twenty jobs to be filled by col- ored workers was made this week. They include: announcement of Eight cottage masters at $900 a year, or four married couples, in which case the husband would earn cottage master's salary and his wife, as matron, would be paid, $720; On vocational instructor in tailoring at $1200 a year, whose wife, as seamstress, could earn $720. abstract: Charges of insubordination against Daniel Clarke, educational supervisor at Cheltenham School for Boys, have been withdrawn and his suspension lifted, it was announced Thursday.
clean_text: Drop Charges Against Clarke af Cheltenham White Superintendent Lifts Suspension of Educational Director BALTIMORE Charges of insubordination against Daniel Clarke, education- al supervisor at Cheltenham School for Boys, have been with- drawn and his suspension lifted, it was announced Thursday. Col. Harry C. Jones, head of the State Employment Commis- sion which must pass on suspen- sions and dismissals at State in- stitutions, told the AFRO that his office had received notice of withdrawal of the charges by Vance E. Thomas, white, superin- tendent of the school. Howard C. Hill, white, acting president of the school's board of managers, said that Mr. Clarke returned to work Monday and that he and Mr. Thomas had reached a working agreement. Suspended on May 17 Mr. Clarke, the only colored ad- ministrative employee at the in- stitution, was suspended May 17 by Mr. Thomas for refusing to teach a class. However, those close to the Cheltenham situation saw in the suspension an attempt by the ad- ministration to punish Mr. Clarke for joining with other employees who have insisted upon an end to segregation of colored and white staff members at the school. It is also known that Mr. Clarke and other employees have insist- ed that the administration should carry out the order of the board of managers issued March 25, ending segregation in the cafe- teria and housing facilities and a subsequent order urging replace- ment of white employees with colored. Board in 7-Hour Session The board held a seven-hour meeting last Tuesday at which time some of the difficulties were ironed out, but no statement was made. It is expected that the board will hold another meet- ing this week. Eight colored employees have resigned from the school staff in the past two months on account of what they term "bungling" of the question of equal facilities for staff members by Mr. Thomas. title: The Monumental City: Weekly News Summary
full_text: The Monumental City Weekly News Summary Cut this out and send it to your soldier boy in your next letter. Colored golfers agreed with rep- resentatives of the city to play on the three municipal courses until after $20,000 improvements are made on the Carroll Park course which has been set aside for them. This agreement follows Maryland Court of Appeals' re- versal of lower court decision opening all city-owned courses to colored golfers. The Federal Public Housing Authority has indicated its will ingness to build a permanent sub- urban housing project rather than temporary homes for war work ers. Since eight million dollars was appropriated two months ago for 2,000 such units, the Baltimore Housing Authority has not yet , agreed on a site for the location of 1,600 of them. ! Three Die in Fires Three persons, Mrs. Elizabeth Cooper, 37; Mrs. Beulah Robin- son, 28. and William Anderson, 43, died as the result of week- end fires. The two women were fatally burned in a blaze at the Cat's Paw Rubber Heel Factory. An- derson sustained a fractured skull by jumping from a third- floor window when fire began in his home. Five men who won places on the eligible list for appointment to the police department are Leon King, Clifton H. James, Clarence J. Stevens, Queener E. Willis and Lloyd G. Johnson. Walter C. Jones, teacher for five years at the Cheltenham School for Boys, resigned in pro- test against the administration of Vance E. Thomas, white, super- intendent, whom he blamed for racial tension at the school. Teachers Meet Here Region 5, American Teachers' Association discussed "Education During and After Wartime" at its annual one-day conference. Judge Joseph N. Ulman, noted liberal, died. 1 The Rev. John T. Colbert was named a trustee of the Eastern Sunrise Services, Inc., which held services Sunday at the municipal stadium. Women's Activities Mrs. Ruth Gregory was re- elected president of the women's auxiliary of the Baltimore Urban League. Engaged: Theodosia Johnson to Pfc. Edward V. Stokes; Genevieve Walker to Cassius Mason. Married: Wilhelmina C. Allen to William M. Gardner; Elsie Perkins to Lieut. Thomas W. Har- ris, Jr., of Annapolis: Cammie Payne to Henry Sanders. Sports Notes The Baltimore Elite Giants journey to Newport News, Va., for Easter week-end series with the Cincinnati Clowns. The Sugar Hill baseball nine completed spring training under the management of Odell Cully. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Cheltenham Head, 5 Women on Honor Roll: New Superintendent of Boys' Institution Is First White Man on AFRO's Honor Roll; Policewoman Whyte and Detective Butler Share Mythical Honors
full_text: Cheltenham Head, 5 Women on Honor Roll New Superintendent of Boys' Institution Is First White Man on AFRO's Honor Roll; Policewoman Whyte and Detective Butler Share Mythical Honors Cheltenham Superintendent Vance E. Thomas, new superintendent of the Cheltenham School for Boys, is the first white person to gain a place on the Baltimore AFRO's honor roll for superior service. When he took over the management of the Prince Georges County school in March, it was VANCE E. THOMAS branded as one of the State's worst correctional institutions. In the nine months he has been there, Mr. Thomas has improved the spirit and morale of the school, done away with severe corporal punishment and substituted, in his own words, "the heaven principle of privileges-hope for reward-rather than the hell principle." In 1941, for the first time in many years, the Baltimore City grand jury report was able to praise the institution and point out the effective and progressive measures of discipline, training and parole. Since March, Mr. Thomas has been able to release at least 300 boys on parole, and under supervision of a parole officer many of them have been able to return to a normal life, taking an active part in the YMCA activities. When he took over, there were only two colored cottage masters; today there are fifteen cottage masters and teachers out of the staff of sixty and every step is being made to include colored persons on the staff of this institution, which is operated for colored youth. Law-Enforcement Officers Mrs. Violet Hill Whyte, policewoman, and Detective Patrolman J. Hiram Butler are sharing a berth on the honor roll this year for their outstanding police work, which won them gold bars for meritorious service and commendation from Governor Herbert R. O'Conor two weeks ago. MRS. VIOLET HILL WHYTE Mrs. Whyte was not only the lone policewoman cited in 1941. but she is also one of the few policewom- en to be so honored in the history of the Batlimore Police Department. She was appointed in 1937 by the late Governor Harry W. Nice and was the first colored person in that city department. Policewoman Whyte divides her time between routine police duties, which are many, rearing four chil- dren, the youngest of whom is in high school, and participating in the acti- vities of numerous civic and welfare organizations. In achieving his second consecu- tive commendation, Detective Butler DETECTIVE BUTLER displayed the same type of persistency that resulted in his appointment with two other officers in 1938. His superior officers have nothing but praise for his outstanding police work and thorough investigations. Butler and Levi Jolley, manager of the Philadelphia AFRO-AMERICAN newspaper, were the first two colored persons to take the examination for probationary patrolmen in 1934. Officer Butler took the examination every year after that until he was appointed. While it is true that Police Commissioner Stanton has failed to appoint any other officers in addition to the four now on the force and has also failed to put any of them in uniform, the high type of efficinecy as demonstrated by Policewoman Whyte and Detective Butler is one of the strongest arguments we have in our fight for uniformed policemen. Boy Scout Leader By winning the coveted Silver Beaver, national Boy Scout council award for noteworthy service of exceptional character to boyhood in his community, during the annual Boy Scout meeting in February, 1941, Leroy A. Coles, Sr., clinched his place on the honor roll. Despite his duties as a mail carrier, Mr. Coles has found time to take an active part in Boy Scout activities during the past twelve years. He helped form the first colored troop in the city, in1928. Troop No. 275, located at the Sharp Street Community House. He then served as its assistant scoutmaster, scoutmaster, and then was elevated to neighborhood commissioner in 1938. Two years later he left this post to serve as Monumental District commissioner, his duties included the supervision of all the troops in Baltimore. His area at the end of the year had a total of 879 boys enrolled in forty-five troops, an increase of nine troops and 157 boys over 1940. On December 31, because of pressing duties and the need for building up his original troop, Mr. Coles resigned this post, but his work during the year, coupled with his winning the coveted scout award, recommended him for a place on the honor roll. Steel Workers' Organizer LEROY A. COLES, Sr. As an organizer for the CIO's Steel Workers' Organizing Committee. Joseph H. Neal was largely responsible for the unionizing of the Bethlehem Steel Company plant at Sparrows Point, and subsequent pay increases which placed about $1,040,000 additional in the pockets JOSEPH H. NEAL of approximately 6,000 colored workers there. Mr. Neal joined forces with the union as one of its first local organizers the early part of 1941, when the SWOC began its intensified drive on the steel plant and imported John Thornton from Pittsburgh and ten white organizers to work under Nicholas Fontecchio of Chicago. The pay increase came in April, when the company, under pressure from the union, granted basic wage increases of from 62½ cents to 72½ cents an hour. Mr. Neal, an employee of the company for about seven years, also enjoys the distinction of being the only colored official in the SWOC's union local No. 224, set up at the plant in July. He is vice-president. His election to this office was unanimous in appreciation and acknowledgement for his work with the union in the early stages of organizing the steel plant. Mr. Neal makes his home at 613 Baker Street. Girl Scout Secretary Increased participation by colored Girl Scouts in city-wide scouting projects, and sub- stantial representation of colored scout leaders on the standing city-wide committees which run Baltimore's scouting affairs are the tangible results of Mrs. Nellie Moore's first term as colored field secretary. MRS. NELLIE MOORE Since her appointment in January, 1941, this nominee for the AFRO's 1941 roll of honor, has invested six Girl Scout troops, bringing the total number of troops to fifteen and putting Balti- more in third place, with Washington and Richmond only a few troops ahead. The new troops are located at the Mt. Washington Baptist Church, Pimlico, Md., Troop 506; Trinity AME Church, Troops 507 and 514; Public School 100, Troop 513; Union Baptist Church, Troop 501, and Metropolitan Baptist Church, Troop 516. Mrs. Moore resigned from a Baltimore parent-education teaching position to take the Girl Scout appointment, although she had worked for five years previously as a volunteer member of the colored district committee. This year, for the first time, Girl Scout leaders have been taught by a colored instructor, and Mrs. Moore's success in obtaining leader co-operation has been outstanding. Housing Project Manager Avon B. Collins wins his place on the honor roll because of his excellent work in set- ting up and maintaining high community standards as a manager of the city's low-rent housing projects. Although he had done similar work at the Langston Project in Washing- ton, his work as resident manager in Baltimore was that of a pioneer, and has won respect and admiration from his tenants and from housing officials of both races. Last week, he repre- sented the local housing authority at the regional conference of the National Association of Housing Officials, which met in Louisville, Ky. Mr. Collins has served as manager at each of the three colored projects now in operation, but is most closely associated with the Edgar Allan Poe Homes, which was the first of seven such projects erected in the city. Under his administration, there has been established at the Pue Homes, a tenants' council, numerous civic clubs, a nursery school, a Boy Scout troop, and an extensive adult and junior recreation program, which is also being developed at the other projects. State Ν.Α.Α.C.P. President AVON B. COLLINS Organized in May. 1941, the Maryland State branch of the NAACP, under the efficient leadership of its president. Mrs. Enolia P. McMillan, now includes nine member branches, and has as its aim an active NAACP unit in each county of the State in which a number of MRS. ENOLIA P. MCMILLAN colored people reside. By virtue of a generous contribution of her time and energies, Mrs. McMillan qualifies for nomination on the 1941 AFRO roll of honor. This quiet-spoken woman keeps up a personal contact with each of the Maryland branches, which include Baltimore, Balnew, Catonsville, Frederick, and the Counties of Prince Georges, Calvert, Charles, Montgomery and Kent. Mrs. McMillan was president of the Maryland Teachers' As- sociation in 1936, when that body joined with the NAACP in the court fight for equalization of teachers' salaries. She is the mother of a 13-month-old son and at present is on leave from the Baltimore school system. Her findings in a Columbia University master's thesis reported to the Teachers' Association on inequalities in teachers' pay, school equipment, length of terms, transportation, and courses of study, were used by that body as a basis of the salary equalization fight. Improvement Group Head Mrs. Florence Snowden was selected for the honor roll because of her persevering, untiring and successful work as president of the Northwest Residential Protective Asso- ciation in its efforts to improve and protect residential neighborhoods. After battling for almost a year, this community group was successful last April in getting the Board of Liquor License Com- missioners to close two taverns at Biddle Street and Druid Hill Avenue, both of which were eyesores and the cause of disorderly conduct and unfavorable influences in the community, where three schools and a church are located. Previous to that, the organization won the support of Mayor Jackson, who vetoed a proposed spot-zoning ordinance which would have changed the 1200 block of McCulloh Street from residential to a first commercial district so that a white grocer could establish his business there. In addition. Mrs. Snowden served as captain of one of the teams in the NAACP campaign and is active in various other civic endeavors. MRS. FLORENCE SNOWDEN Urban League Secretary Taking over the newly created position of industrial secretary of the Baltimore Urban League on January 2, 1941, Furman L. Templeton has led a successful fight this year for jobs for colored skilled workers in various unionized trades formerly closed to them and has broken jim crow barriers in many companies and industries. Mr. Templeton was responsible for the hiring of nine boys as messengers by the local Postal Telegraph Company. He was instrumental in getting the State Plumbing Board to accept the applications of colored plumbers for consideration toward grant- ing them licenses-something never before accomplished here, as there are no colored licensed plumbers in Baltimore and but one in the State. He has helped to break down barriers in local defense industries, was one of the leaders in the fight for jobs at the Glenn L. Martin Aircraft Plant, and has secured the promise of officials at the Bethlehem-Fairfield Shipyard that they will hire and train colored men as soon as a building is completed there within the near future. So outstanding has Mr. Templeton's work been that through- out the year he has reecived job offers from various government agencies, notably the Office of Production Management, and last Monday he resigned his post to be regional racial relations staff advisor with the Office of Civilian Defense at a salary of $4,600. Eastside Civic Worker FURMAN TEMPLETON Miss Janet Green is heralded as East Baltimore's most militant community and civic worker, diligently taking part in all community betterment campaigns, despite the fact that she has to travel fifty-two miles a day to teach school in Hereford, Md. Miss Green is MISS JANET GREEN president of the East Baltimore Residential Protective Associa- tion, which will soon observe its first anniversary. This group appeared before the Legislature last spring and succeeded in having a bill passed for the appointment of an extra policewoman. in East Baltimore. It has also been instrumental in ridding the neighborhood of annoying taverns. As chairman of the NAACP campaign in October, Miss Green induced East Baltimoreans to triple their efforts of co-operation, with the result that to date 6,000 memberships and more than $6,000 has been reported in the campaign. In addition to these activities, she finds time to be active at the First Baptist Church, serving as assistant church clerk and working with the young people's choir. She is a native Balti- morean, the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. John C. Green, and grand- daughter of the late Bishop Alexander Wayman of the AME Church. title: YOUR NEIGHBOR: BRIEF SKETCHES OF FELLOW BALTIMOREANS YOU SHOULD KNOW
full_text: YOUR NEIGHBOR Brief Sketches of Fellow Baltimoreans You Should Know [EDITOR'S NOTE: This is the elev- enth in a series of thumbnail sketches of Baltimoreans of both races, designed to acquaint AFRO readers with each other.] By E. A. HARLESTON BALTIMORE | Joseph C. Briscoe, principal of Carver Vocational School, has a full-time job trying to gear his  JOSEPH C. BRISCOE school's curriculum to meet the war-time interests and needs of his pupils. Fully 55 per cent of his student body is engaged in part-time work after school hours. Working Hours Vary In most cases it's an individual matter. Some go to their jobs immediately after school and work until 1 and 2 a.m. Still oth- ers start work at 2 a.m. and quit at 7 in the morning. Health and recreational prob- lems must be considered, Mr. Bris- coe pointed out, and the task of revamping the school schedule to benefit all concerned is a tremen- dous one. "We have litle time for extra- curricula activities now," the prin- cipal asserted. "Most of our boys and girls feel a serious obligation to their work which precludes ev- en such activities as a victory corps." Mr. Briscoe, with a refined de meanor, fights for what he believes is right. On Cheltenham Board A member of the board of the Cheltenham School for Boys eight years, he has been diligent in ex (ercising his powers, and has main tained that segregation at the school be abolished, unequivo cably. When affairs at Provident Hos pital, of which he has been a board member for many years, were not being managed to meet his standards, he refused to go along "for the sake of peace" and sent in his resignation. In adidtion the Vocational prin cipal is an active board member of the Druid Hill YMCA, is on the Boy Scout district committee, and still finds time to continue ſteaching Sunday school at St. Katherine PE Church after thirty years' service. Mr. Briscoe is married to the former Mrs. Virginia Hall. a di rector in the Department of Pub- lic Welfare. They live at 1301 Madison Avenue. More Fowl, Seafood The war hasn't seriously affect- ed his personal life to any extent although Mr. Briscoe admits that he is eating more fowl and sea. food than formerly. He'll still be able to see his favorite teams play baseball, a sport about which he is quite enthusiastic. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: The Week: Official Spring Without a Vest ""Nice"" Medals End of Jim Crow Cars
full_text: The Week Official Spring Without a Vest "Nice" Medals End of Jim Crow Cars By CARL MURPHY Forsythia and crocuses are in bloom, barberry and bush honey- suckles have put out baby leaves, green has come back into lawns, and last week I heard the first mocking bird. It's official-spring is here. Ollie Stewart has promised to send me the words of the new N. African soldiers' song called "Dirty Gertie of Bizerte." That is, the words that may be printed. Without a Vest Vincent Tubbs, second member of the AFRO's editorial depart- ment to leave the U.S. to go abroad as a war correspondent, couldn't get released from his Norfolk draft board until P. B. Young, Sr. (Journal and Guide), put in a good word for him. Up to the time I saw Vincent in uniform with his coat open, I didn't realize that U.S. Army officers wear no vests. But they have on long underwear. The church has no rules for keeping Lent, says Archdeacon Bravrid Harris of Norfolk. All forms of self-denial-giving up amusements, smoking, or even fasting are not in themselves right or wrong. But you commit a sin if you set up for yourself any form of self-denial and then fail to carry it out. - Maryland's reform school for boys at Cheltenham is having a tempest in a teapot because some colored and white staff members want to sleep and eat together. Some twenty white members of the staff are con- scientious objectors to whom race hate is as bad as war. Seems funny that anything like this could happen in a colored State school. But it isn't funny to those who believe in white supremacy. Charming Jimmy Readers from all over the U.S. wrote to smiling Sgt. Jimmy Stewart in Hawaii after his pic appeared in the AFRO the week of January 19. Says Jimmie, whose sinile is indeed charming: "My morale is above 100 per cent. For my social aspirations, the fu- ture looks quite bright." If you rent out a room in your home and haven't registered with the OPA rent bureau, you may still do so without being penal- ized. "Nice" Medals Citations to seven members of 41st Singing Engineers for their fine work in building airfields, roads and bridges in Liberia are printed in detail in this week's AFRO. According to President Bar- clay, two of the Americans were honored because they were "nice to Liberians." This may not be the first time soldiers were decorated for being "nice," (Continued on Page 2, Col. 3) The Week (Continued from Page 1) 7 but it is certainly the first time we have seen anybody quite so frank about it as the Liberian President. Bill Jones, 41st band leader and the man most responsible for the kind of music that makes everybody want to sing, impressed the Africans too. Naive Mr. Barclay pinned a medal on Bill for "recreational services rendered." Until the going got tough in the Civil War, Union soldiers of the 100th Indiana Volunteers declared, "This is a white man's war." How does it feel to win $1,000? Mrs. Ethel H. Grubbs of Wash- ington, that much richer by rea- son of the AFRO's post office game, said three days afterwards that she still was as in a dream up in the clouds. Quiet as It's Kept The 76th Coast Artillery, which was organized at Fort Bragg, N.C., fought with the Marines at Guad- alcanal and was withdrawn when the army later took over. With these men were George Cox of Durham and Leroy Haynes of Chicago, both Morehouse College athletes. Colored troops in the South Pacific are now located in the New Hebrides, New Caledonia, the Solomons, and New Guinea. Copies of Monsignor Ryan's ad- dress at Howard U. distributed to the press contained the epithet nr. President Mordecai Johnson says the Catholic U. professor did not read that part of his speech. ■Congressman Will Rogers, Jr., of the 16th District, California, and son of the great humorist, writes me: "One reason for my coming to Congress was my great concern over race prejudice. When there is an opportunity on the House floor to fight against race discrimination I will cer- tainly be there doing it." End of J.C. Cars Seen Speaker Conlon of the Mary- land Legislature is fumbling with the jim-crow repeal bill. That happens every session. A How- ard U. law school professor named Hart personally had the courts abolish jim crow on inter-State traffic arteries, but not on trans- port facilities within the State. He fought his case through the lower court to the court of ap- peals and won. If we had more lawyers like the late Professor Hart, jim crow on all inter-State railroads would soon end. As the war lasts two years more (and I predict it will), jim crow on trains will be dealt its heaviest blow by car riders themselves. car. Smoking by law is restricted to smoking cars on trains, but so many people disobey the law that virtually every car is a smoking The trains will continue to be crowded with soldiers. Civilians will be glad to ride anywhere, and conductors won't be able to keep whites out of the jim-crow cars when there are seats there. We have already arrived at that point. E. A. Gannon, passenger agent for the Baltimore and An- napolis Electric R.R. (B. & Ο. owned), told the Legislature last week that war-time travel makes enforcement of the separate car law in Maryland impossible. He asked that it be abolished. Sergeant William S. Medley of the 377th Engineers, was taken from a bus between Clover and Camp Pickett, Va., because he sat with a white sailor. The sailor didn't object; the conductor did. When white soldiers and sailors join colored comrades in insisting on throwing jim crow out of the window, bus conductors will not be such smart elecks. Pop Summers Dr. H. H. (Pop) Summers, Wil- berforce U. professor of Greek and theology, who was buried last week in Harrisburg, Pa., was a remarkable man. Thirty-six years ago we attended Howard Uni- versity together. Most of us were 18 then; Pop was 43. He had been a teacher and principal of a pub- lic school in Steelton, Pa., for seventeen years, and with the late Dr. Numa P. G. Adams, decided he wanted a college education. Being twice as old as the rest of us didn't bother Pop, neither did he mind the nickname we gave him. He was hungry for knowledge and went on to finish college, theological school, and to earn two master's degrees final- ly at the age of 65. He thus spent some twenty- two years in college-teaching in the winter and studying in the summer. At the age when most men are retiring from ac- tive life, Pop Summers was just getting ready to do his best work as a teacher at Wil- berforce University. They don't come any more dur- able and gentlemanly than Pop. He was a credit to his profession.

title: Jim Crow Foe Gets 4 Years
full_text: 17 Teach Plane Care at N.C. Field GOLDSBORO, N. C. (ANP) - Seventeen colored civilian avia- tion instructors and now on duty at Seymour Johnson Field here, teaching engine repairing, pro- pelller care and other phases of airplane maintenance. Their classes, usually contain- ing from five to ten persons, in- clue both soldiers and civilians. All supervisors are white but the instructors say their treatment has been excellent. All received their original train- ing at Chanute Field, Ill., Jim Crow Foe Gets 4 Years | BALTIMORE. -- Charles Butcher, 26, white, ousted em- ployee of the Cheltenham School for Boys, on Monday received a four-year sentence in the Federal Court for refusing to obey an or- der to report to a conscientious objectors' camp. A Harvard graduate, Butcher began working at Cheltenham in November. He was released from the institution in January on three charges, one of them being his insistence on eating in a din- ing hall reserved for colored em- ployees. Butcher stated that he had been offered several jobs since his dis- missal from Cheltenham, but de- clined them because he wanted to do what he could in straighten- ing out the racial situation at the institution. He said that the case is a "test" for clarification of Selective Serv- ice regulations concerning con- scientious objectors and would be carried to the United States Su- preme Court, if necessary. Judge Coleman refused to grant Butcher bail, pending a ruling on his appeal. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

Speaking of his work with the Cheltenham board, Mr. Lewis said: "One of Judge Ulman's mos important contributions was the reorganization ham board. "His tireless work on this body was responsible for most of the administrative reform of the pas two years." Mr. Lewis con. tinued. "His vision. courage, en. gaging sense of humor and his matchless ability to resolve tense issues were always sources of in spiration to me." Judge Ulman was a native Bal. timorean, educated at Johns Hop kins Universities. Funeral services were held Mon- while the general board meeting day. was to have been held today (Tuesday). Noted for Fair Dealings Judge Ulman was noted for being as fair in his dealings with his fellow citizens in his non- professional life as he tried to be on the bench. During his term as president of the Urban League board, he led a delegation to protest a lynching on the Eastern Shore to Governor Albert Ritchie in spite of the fact that the Governor had appointed him. When the Van Nuys Antilynch- ing Bill was before the United States Senate in 1936, Judge Ul- man made a private appeal to the Senate for its passing. He also lodged a protest with, the Postal Telegraph Company in 1941 when the company failed to assign to duty six colored youths who had been hired for service. Wrote Own Epitaph Judge Ulman's personal phi- losophy of interracial relations can be summed up in an excerpt from a speech which he delivered in 1935 at an annual meeting of the Baltimore Urban League: "If the white Baltimore City and the colored city are ever to become one city, a city in which white citizens and colored citi- zens will enjoy equal rights of citizenship, in which colored babies will have the same chance to grow into useful and healthful manhood, then white men and Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Wrote Own Epitaph Judge Ulman's personal phi- losophy of interracial relations can be summed up in an excerpt from a speech which he delivered in 1935 at an annual meeting of the Baltimore Urban League: "If the white Baltimore City and the colored city are ever to become one city, a city in which white citizens and colored citi- zens will enjoy equal rights of citizenship, in which colored babies will have the same chance to grow into useful and healthful manhood, then white men and . .

title: The Monumental City: Weekly News Summary cut this out and send it to your soldier boy in your next letter.
full_text: The Monumental City Weekly News Summary Cut this out and send it to your soldier boy in your next letter. BALTIMORE-Dr. Francis M. Wood, 65, director of colored schools since 1927, died Friday night at Johns Hopkins Hospital. Six munitions workers were killed and fifty-two others injured in an explosion at the Triumph Explosives Company in Elkton, Md. The dead are: Mrs. Christine Erby, Wilmington; Mrs. Shirley Gilmor, Coatesville, Pa.; Melvin Cole, Stillpond, Pa.; Willie Crad- dock, Dover, Del.; Harry Rian, Dover; and an unidentified man. The Cheltenham School for Boys was promised an all-colored staff in a resolution passed by the interracial board of managers. Provident Medic Resigns Dr. Sarah E. Lewis, house physician at Provident Hospital for six years, resigned. The Maryland State Conference of Social Welfare discussed hous- ing and delinquency problems in a two-day meet at the Lord Balti- more Hotel. The war housing project to be built at Fairfield, Md., was ap proved to contain 248 instead of 400 units. Church News The annual conference of the AME Church was held at Trinity AME Church, with Bishop M. H. Davis presiding, assisted by Bish- op David H. Sims of Philadel- phia. Theodore R. McKeldin, new mayor of Baltimore, made his first public address since his elec- tion Tuesday, to this conference. Social Activities Mrs. Mary Church Terrell, lec- turer and author, was guest speak- er for Alpna Gamma Chapter of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority at Morgan State College, opening May Week services. Miss Kathleen Mumby, Morgan senior, reigned as queen at May Day exercises. Dooley Wilson, singing star of "Casablanca," was entertained by Mr. and Mrs. Noah Thompson. Mrs. Dorothy N. Washington, 21, widow of Carl M. Washington, who was drowned in March while on duty with the U.S. Coast Guard at Fort McHenry, became the bride of Earl Lyons, 21, another Coast Guardsman at the same sta- tion. Entertainment Notes The opera study group of the Studio of Creative Arts appeared at Grace Memorial Baptist Church. The Association of Colored Musicians presented Miss Carol Brice, contralto, in recital at Sharp Street Methodist Church. A symphony orchestra, directed by A. Jack Thomas, presented a concert at Dunbar High School. Sports Events Cocoa Kid of New Haven, Conn., won a 15-round decision over Johnny Brown of Baltimore. title: Hearing Set in Fatal Gas Blast
full_text: [ Hearing Set in Fatal Gas Blast BALTIMORE. The pre- liminary hearing for John Lemon, 27, of 515 Archer Street, held without bail at Southwestern po- lice station in connection with the death of James Walker of 512 W. West Street, has been set for August 16. Mr. Walker died on July 19 as the result of burns received from a gasoline explosion in Lemon's! cellar the night before. According to Cain Ballard, 24, of 904 S. Sharp Street, he and Mr. Walker were visiting Lemon when the three went to the cel- lar to get coal oil for a lamp from a drum there. Lamp Ignites Oil Unable to siphon the oil from the drum, Ballard said Walker picked it up and began to pour the contents into a five-gallon can when, suddenly, the lamp flared up, igniting the coal oil and their clothing. Police report that Ballard took Walker and Lemon to the hos pital in the latter's car, where Mr. Walker died the next morning, ninety per cent of his body char- red by the fire. The other two men suffered burns of the arms and legs, but were later released from the hos- pital. Old Dominion Richmond News Digest Cut this out and send it to your soldier boy in your next letter. RICHMOND. - Herbert Bland, 52, prominent coal dealer, was in St. Philip Hospital, suffering from serious stab wounds, alleged- ly inflicted by Seaman Edward Miller, sald to be AWOL from Yorktown Naval Mine Depot. Lieut. R. A. B. Crump, for six- teen years assistant superintend. ent of Hanover Industrial School, resigned that position to become head of the Cheltenham (Md.) School for Boys. The Rev. A. A. Hector, for the past eight years pastor of Mizpah Presbyterian Church, South Bos- ton, Va., announced his retire- ment from the ministry, effective August 30. Holman B. Thomas, of Lunen- burg County, sentence to die for the slaying of the police chief of Kenbridge. was granted a writ of error by Justice Browning 2 Women Stabbed; Man Under Bail BALTIMORE Two women, one of whom is now in a serious condition, were treated at Johns Hopkins Hospital Friday, for stab wounds inflicted by Noah Johnson, 128 S. Caroline Street. Johnson is being held on assault charges by Eastern district police under bail totaling $1,000 pending further trial. According to police reports, the two women were walking north on Caroline Street when Johnson crept behind them and stabbed Mrs. Anna Fitzgerald, 50, 134 Car- oline Street, in the back, and Mrs. Ruth Wells, 114 S. Caroline Street, in the left elbow. Mrs. Fitzgerald is still in the hospital while the other womar I was released after treatment.. ]

title: To-day: Cheltenham Dilemma Menaces to Morale
full_text: -To-day- Cheltenham Dilemma Menaces to Morale By B. M. PHILLIPS BALTIMORE The reason a new superinten- dent has not been appointed at the Cheltenham School for Boys' is that technically no vacancy ex- ists! While it is true that in Janu- ary the Governor named the pres- ent superintendent, Vance E. Thomas, white, to a new post at the Maryland Training School for white boys at Loch Raven, reports are that he has NOT accepted it. The board of managers of Chel- tenham has already picked the new head, reportedly a Virginia man, but it appears that Thomas won't budge. The fact that Thomas is balk- ing on moving is said in some quarters to be due to his reluc- tance to work under the Loch Raven superintendent, James A. Pratt, a former member of the Cheltenham board. That's where the matter stands now and that's why the delay in announcing the new colored super- intendent as promised sometime ago by the board. Incidentally, the Cheltenham board has ordered $8,000 worth of new equipment, including knives and forks, so that at long last the inmmates can eat like human beings instead of savages. A move is also on foot to in crease the salaries of employees at the juvenile institution. That white matron from whom $3,000 in cash and an equali amount in securities was stolent last week is reportedly the aunt of Wally Simpson. New Divorce Grounds Under terms of a case decided in the Circuit Court here recent-. ly, when either party refuses to agree to the hearing of children, that constitutes marital desertion. This was established in the case of Ann C. Malley vs. Bernard Mal- ley, in which the wife testified that her husband insisted on use of contraceptives, although they had agreed before marriage to have three children. The story is being whispered about that Northwestern District lieutenant who has a colored girl-. friend of some years' standing. For a real insight into Hitler's! character, read Dr. Kurt Krueger's "I Was Hitler's Doctor." This is the group which was formed soon after the nation-wide riots and is concerned with rais- ing morale. Dr. Holmes related that the greatest menaces to morale are the "disheartening things that happen to put 13 million colored people in the dumps." He cited ill-treatment of soldiers who receive the same brand of discrimination in the army as they did in civilian life, and very little protection from the army when they get in trouble. title: Girls Riot at School in Md.: President of Board, Police Restore Quiet 18 GIRLS INVOLVED Ringleader Returned to Home of Parents
full_text: Girls Riot at School in Md. President of Board, Police Restore Quiet 18 GIRLS INVOLVED Ringleader Returned to Home of Parents BALTIMORE-A riot at the Maryland Training School for Girls at Glenburnie on Saturday was quelled only when police and the board president put in an ap- pearance. Before their arrival, however, the girls had broken window panes and furniture, doing damage amounting to over $200. Cause for the rampage, it is re- ported, was an order that they "turn in"-go to bed at 8 p.m. Too Early to Go to Bed The girls said that 8 p.m. was too early and, since it was still light, they would not obey. When attempts were made to get them to retire, some of them fled the school grounds. Others went to the room of Miss Mae Rideout, the matron who had given the order, and pro- ceeded to damage her clothing, the furniture, and break out win- dow panes. About eighteen girls were in- volved in the disturbance, which was finally brought under control when nearby Ferndale police and H. H. Murphy, president of the board of managers, were sum- moned. Mr. Murphy reported that the ringleader, a 17-year-old, who is too old for the type of training at the school, has been returned to her parents. School Problems Cited He indicated that the school is faced with the same problem which obtains at Cheltenham School for Boys: namely, that the Institution is given all types of girls to care for when in reality it can only accommodate those who are amenable to training. He pointed out that the rioting was led by girls with mental de- ficiences and incorrigibles for which it has no provision, adding that members of his board are meeting with the State Depart- ment of Public Welfare with a view towards discovering some method of segregating them. Extra Work" for Rioters Restrictions and extra work have been ordered for all the par- ticipants in Saturday's disturbance, Mr. Murphy declared. Mrs. Sadye P. George, superin- tendent of the institution, said that the attack on Miss Rideout's property occurred the following morning while she (Mrs. George) was bringing the leader of the disturbance into town, and was apparently an attempt to "get even" with Miss Rideout for fore- ing the girl to leave the school. Mrs. George reported that most of the damage was done in the music room, but that the trouble had subsided by Monday. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

title: Citizens Tell How They're Paying Their 1942 Taxes
full_text: Citizens Tell How They're Paying Their 1942 Taxes By RUTH JENKINS BALTIMORE Despite the fact that the largest crowds in many years paid income taxes daily at the Custom House, inquiries last week revealed that hundreds of taxpayers were likely to be caught in the last-minute rush on the March 15 deadline. The Custom House at Gay and Lombard Streets has been kept open until 9 p.m. to meet the de- mands of the thousands paying their taxes in person. But in order to accommodate the deadline crowd, it remained open until midnight on Saturday and Monday. Taxes were also paid by check or by money order, through the mails. With the government still con- sidering phases of the pay-as-you- go and collection-at-the-souce systems of taxation, the major- ity of wage-earners have adopted the quarterly installment plan for the payments now due. Must Meet Deadline Custom House officials warned this week that where the quar- ly payment plan is used, the first installment or one-fourth of the tax due must be paid in full by March 15. Otherwise, a notice will be sent the taxpayer, demanding full pay- ment of the total tax within ten days, plus a 6 per cent interest charge. Taxpayers questioned last week in regard to their taxes made the following replies: MRS. VIRGIE WATERS, presi- dent, Master Beauticians' As- sociation, 527 umberland Street: "My hus- band and I filed our returns to- gether and have already paid the first install- ment." To Pay Quarterly ROBERT SAMPLE, foreman at the Maryland Drydock, Fairfield, Md., 1209 Wilmer Court: "I just got my return worked out on Wednesday. I'm paying my taxes in installments." FURMAN TEMPLETON, OCD racial relations adviser: "I'm going to use the installment plan, But I wish the government would adopt that pay-as-you- go system be- cause people usually get caught short when the install- ments are due anyway." JOHN W. SMITH, carpenter at Port Deposit, Md., 1318 Мс- Culloh Street: "Since I suf- fered dent which kept: me out ill for five months last. year, my tax is going to be very small. I'm still an acci- waiting for my salary statement from my employer. If I collect compensation from the State In- dustrial Accident Commission in a lump sum, I'll be able to pay the tax in full." "Foolish" to Pay All MRS. VIRGINIA H. BRISCOE, director of the Samuel Cole- ridge-Taylor re- ception center: "With the state of affairs now existing in Con gress in regard to taxation, it would seem foolish to pay 1942 taxes oth- erwise than in quarterly installments." J. EDGAR STANLEY, cottage master at Cheltenham School for Boys: "My income was relatively small last year since my main- tenance was included at the school and since I have a young daughter as a dependant." WILLIAM I. GOSNELL, attor ney, 1151 Myrtle Avenue: "I will most likely get caught in the deadline rush. And as broke as I am, my pay- ments will cer- tainly be made in install- ments." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.
